def:axiom.title: Axiom
def:axiom.statement_preview: Let $\mathcal{L}$ be a first-order language, and let $\operatorname{Sent}(\mathcal{L})$ denote the set of sentences of $\mathcal{L}$. If $T\subseteq \operatorname{Sent}(\mathcal{L})$ is a theory, then an \emph{axiom of $T$} is an element $\alpha\in T$.
def:axiom.statement_display:
Let $\mathcal{L}$ be a first-order language, and let
$\operatorname{Sent}(\mathcal{L})$ denote the set of sentences of
$\mathcal{L}$. If $T\subseteq \operatorname{Sent}(\mathcal{L})$ is a theory,
then an \emph{axiom of $T$} is an element $\alpha\in T$.
def:axiom.statement_tex:
Let $\mathcal{L}$ be a first-order language, and let
$\operatorname{Sent}(\mathcal{L})$ denote the set of sentences of
$\mathcal{L}$. If $T\subseteq \operatorname{Sent}(\mathcal{L})$ is a theory,
then an \emph{axiom of $T$} is an element $\alpha\in T$.

def:axiom-system.title: Axiom System
def:axiom-system.statement_preview: An \emph{axiom system} is a collection of axioms expressed in a common language.
def:axiom-system.statement_display:
An \emph{axiom system} is a collection of axioms expressed in a common
language.
def:axiom-system.statement_tex:
An \emph{axiom system} is a collection of axioms expressed in a common
language.

def:assumption.title: Assumption
def:assumption.statement_preview: An \emph{assumption} is a statement temporarily accepted for purposes of reasoning.
def:assumption.statement_display:
An \emph{assumption} is a statement temporarily accepted for purposes of
reasoning.
def:assumption.statement_tex:
An \emph{assumption} is a statement temporarily accepted for purposes of
reasoning.

def:axiom-systems-contradiction.title: Contradiction
def:axiom-systems-contradiction.statement_preview: A \emph{contradiction} is a statement that cannot be true under a given interpretation.
def:axiom-systems-contradiction.statement_display:
A \emph{contradiction} is a statement that cannot be true under a given
interpretation.
def:axiom-systems-contradiction.statement_tex:
A \emph{contradiction} is a statement that cannot be true under a given
interpretation.

def:axiom-systems-consistent-axiom-syste.title: Consistent Axiom System
def:axiom-systems-consistent-axiom-syste.statement_preview: A \emph{consistent axiom system} is an axiom system that does not lead to contradiction.
def:axiom-systems-consistent-axiom-syste.statement_display:
A \emph{consistent axiom system} is an axiom system that does not lead to
contradiction.
def:axiom-systems-consistent-axiom-syste.statement_tex:
A \emph{consistent axiom system} is an axiom system that does not lead to
contradiction.

def:axiom-systems-inconsistent-axiom-sys.title: Inconsistent Axiom System
def:axiom-systems-inconsistent-axiom-sys.statement_preview: An \emph{inconsistent axiom system} is an axiom system containing or producing contradictory assertions.
def:axiom-systems-inconsistent-axiom-sys.statement_display:
An \emph{inconsistent axiom system} is an axiom system containing or producing
contradictory assertions.
def:axiom-systems-inconsistent-axiom-sys.statement_tex:
An \emph{inconsistent axiom system} is an axiom system containing or producing
contradictory assertions.

def:axiom-systems-model-existence-criter.title: Model Existence Criterion (Informal)
def:axiom-systems-model-existence-criter.statement_preview: Informally, if an axiom system has a model, then the axioms are mutually compatible.
def:axiom-systems-model-existence-criter.statement_display:
Informally, if an axiom system has a model, then the axioms are mutually
compatible.
def:axiom-systems-model-existence-criter.statement_tex:
Informally, if an axiom system has a model, then the axioms are mutually
compatible.

def:axiom-systems-relative-consistency.title: Relative Consistency
def:axiom-systems-relative-consistency.statement_preview: Let $T_0$ and $T_1$ be theories. The theory $T_1$ is \emph{consistent relative to $T_0$} if \[ \operatorname{Con}(T_0)\implies \operatorname{Con}(T_1). \] Equivalently, every contradiction in $T_1$ would imply a contradiction in $T_0$.
def:axiom-systems-relative-consistency.statement_display:
Let $T_0$ and $T_1$ be theories. The theory $T_1$ is
\emph{consistent relative to $T_0$} if
\[
  \operatorname{Con}(T_0)\implies \operatorname{Con}(T_1).
\]
Equivalently, every contradiction in $T_1$ would imply a contradiction in
$T_0$.
def:axiom-systems-relative-consistency.statement_tex:
Let $T_0$ and $T_1$ be theories. The theory $T_1$ is
\emph{consistent relative to $T_0$} if
\[
  \operatorname{Con}(T_0)\implies \operatorname{Con}(T_1).
\]
Equivalently, every contradiction in $T_1$ would imply a contradiction in
$T_0$.

def:axiom-systems-interpretation.title: Interpretation
def:axiom-systems-interpretation.statement_preview: Let $\Sigma=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar})$ be a first-order signature, and let $M$ be a nonempty set. An \emph{interpretation of $\Sigma$ on $M$} is an assignment $\mathcal{I}$ that maps each symbol of $\Sigma$ to concrete data over $M$: constants are assigned elements of $M$, function symbols are assigned operations on $M$, and re...
def:axiom-systems-interpretation.statement_display:
Let $\Sigma=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar})$ be a
first-order signature, and let $M$ be a nonempty set. An
\emph{interpretation of $\Sigma$ on $M$} is an assignment $\mathcal{I}$ that
maps each symbol of $\Sigma$ to concrete data over $M$: constants are assigned
elements of $M$, function symbols are assigned operations on $M$, and relation
symbols are assigned relations on $M$.
def:axiom-systems-interpretation.statement_tex:
Let $\Sigma=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar})$ be a
first-order signature, and let $M$ be a nonempty set. An
\emph{interpretation of $\Sigma$ on $M$} is an assignment $\mathcal{I}$ that
maps each symbol of $\Sigma$ to concrete data over $M$: constants are assigned
elements of $M$, function symbols are assigned operations on $M$, and relation
symbols are assigned relations on $M$.

def:axiom-systems-structure.title: $\mathcal{L}$-Structure
def:axiom-systems-structure.statement_preview: Let $\mathcal{L}$ be a first-order signature. An \emph{$\mathcal{L}$-structure} is a pair \[ \mathfrak{M}=(M,\mathcal{I}), \] where $M$ is a nonempty domain and $\mathcal{I}$ is an interpretation of the symbols of $\mathcal{L}$ on $M$.
def:axiom-systems-structure.statement_display:
Let $\mathcal{L}$ be a first-order signature. An
\emph{$\mathcal{L}$-structure} is a pair
\[
  \mathfrak{M}=(M,\mathcal{I}),
\]
where $M$ is a nonempty domain and $\mathcal{I}$ is an interpretation of the
symbols of $\mathcal{L}$ on $M$.
def:axiom-systems-structure.statement_tex:
Let $\mathcal{L}$ be a first-order signature. An
\emph{$\mathcal{L}$-structure} is a pair
\[
  \mathfrak{M}=(M,\mathcal{I}),
\]
where $M$ is a nonempty domain and $\mathcal{I}$ is an interpretation of the
symbols of $\mathcal{L}$ on $M$.

def:axiom-systems-model.title: Model
def:axiom-systems-model.statement_preview: Let $\mathcal{L}$ be a first-order signature, let $T$ be a theory over $\mathcal{L}$, and let $\mathfrak{M}=(M,\mathcal{I})$ be an $\mathcal{L}$-structure. The structure $\mathfrak{M}$ is a \emph{model of $T$}, written $\mathfrak{M}\vDash T$, if every sentence in $T$ is true in $\mathfrak{M}$.
def:axiom-systems-model.statement_display:
Let $\mathcal{L}$ be a first-order signature, let $T$ be a theory over
$\mathcal{L}$, and let $\mathfrak{M}=(M,\mathcal{I})$ be an
$\mathcal{L}$-structure. The structure $\mathfrak{M}$ is a \emph{model of
$T$}, written $\mathfrak{M}\vDash T$, if every sentence in $T$ is true in
$\mathfrak{M}$.
def:axiom-systems-model.statement_tex:
Let $\mathcal{L}$ be a first-order signature, let $T$ be a theory over
$\mathcal{L}$, and let $\mathfrak{M}=(M,\mathcal{I})$ be an
$\mathcal{L}$-structure. The structure $\mathfrak{M}$ is a \emph{model of
$T$}, written $\mathfrak{M}\vDash T$, if every sentence in $T$ is true in
$\mathfrak{M}$.

def:axiom-systems-satisfaction.title: Satisfaction
def:axiom-systems-satisfaction.statement_preview: Let $\mathfrak{M}=(M,\mathcal{I})$ be an $\mathcal{L}$-structure. A \emph{variable assignment} is a function $s:\operatorname{Var}\to M$. The assignment extends recursively to a term-evaluation map $\bar{s}$ by \[ \bar{s}(x)=s(x),\qquad \bar{s}(c)=\mathcal{I}(c), \] and \[ \bar{s}\bigl(f(t_1,\ldots,t_n)\bigr) = \mathcal{I}(f)\bigl(\bar{s}(t_1),\ldots,\bar{s...
def:axiom-systems-satisfaction.statement_display:
Let $\mathfrak{M}=(M,\mathcal{I})$ be an $\mathcal{L}$-structure. A
\emph{variable assignment} is a function $s:\operatorname{Var}\to M$. The
assignment extends recursively to a term-evaluation map $\bar{s}$ by
\[
  \bar{s}(x)=s(x),\qquad
  \bar{s}(c)=\mathcal{I}(c),
\]
and
\[
  \bar{s}\bigl(f(t_1,\ldots,t_n)\bigr)
  =
  \mathcal{I}(f)\bigl(\bar{s}(t_1),\ldots,\bar{s}(t_n)\bigr).
\]
The satisfaction relation $\mathfrak{M}\vDash\varphi[s]$ is then defined by
induction on formulas: atomic relations are tested inside the interpreted
relation, equality is interpreted as equality in $M$, Boolean connectives are
interpreted by the corresponding truth operations, and quantifiers range over
all elements of $M$.
def:axiom-systems-satisfaction.statement_tex:
Let $\mathfrak{M}=(M,\mathcal{I})$ be an $\mathcal{L}$-structure. A
\emph{variable assignment} is a function $s:\operatorname{Var}\to M$. The
assignment extends recursively to a term-evaluation map $\bar{s}$ by
\[
  \bar{s}(x)=s(x),\qquad
  \bar{s}(c)=\mathcal{I}(c),
\]
and
\[
  \bar{s}\bigl(f(t_1,\ldots,t_n)\bigr)
  =
  \mathcal{I}(f)\bigl(\bar{s}(t_1),\ldots,\bar{s}(t_n)\bigr).
\]
The satisfaction relation $\mathfrak{M}\vDash\varphi[s]$ is then defined by
induction on formulas: atomic relations are tested inside the interpreted
relation, equality is interpreted as equality in $M$, Boolean connectives are
interpreted by the corresponding truth operations, and quantifiers range over
all elements of $M$.

def:axiom-systems-countermodel.title: Countermodel
def:axiom-systems-countermodel.statement_preview: Let $\Gamma\subseteq\operatorname{Sent}(\mathcal{L})$ be a set of premises, let $\varphi\in\operatorname{Sent}(\mathcal{L})$, and let $\mathfrak{M}$ be an $\mathcal{L}$-structure. A \emph{countermodel to the entailment $\Gamma\vDash\varphi$} is a structure $\mathfrak{M}$ that satisfies every sentence in $\Gamma$ but does not satisfy $\varphi$.
def:axiom-systems-countermodel.statement_display:
Let $\Gamma\subseteq\operatorname{Sent}(\mathcal{L})$ be a set of premises,
let $\varphi\in\operatorname{Sent}(\mathcal{L})$, and let $\mathfrak{M}$ be
an $\mathcal{L}$-structure. A \emph{countermodel to the entailment
$\Gamma\vDash\varphi$} is a structure $\mathfrak{M}$ that satisfies every
sentence in $\Gamma$ but does not satisfy $\varphi$.
def:axiom-systems-countermodel.statement_tex:
Let $\Gamma\subseteq\operatorname{Sent}(\mathcal{L})$ be a set of premises,
let $\varphi\in\operatorname{Sent}(\mathcal{L})$, and let $\mathfrak{M}$ be
an $\mathcal{L}$-structure. A \emph{countermodel to the entailment
$\Gamma\vDash\varphi$} is a structure $\mathfrak{M}$ that satisfies every
sentence in $\Gamma$ but does not satisfy $\varphi$.

def:signature.title: First-Order Signature
def:signature.statement_preview: A \emph{first-order signature} is a tuple \[ \Sigma=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar}), \] where $\mathcal{C}$ is a set of constant symbols, $\mathcal{F}$ is a set of function symbols, $\mathcal{R}$ is a set of relation symbols, and \[ \operatorname{ar}:\mathcal{F}\cup\mathcal{R}\to \mathbb{Z}_{>0} \] assigns to each function or relatio...
def:signature.statement_display:
A \emph{first-order signature} is a tuple
\[
  \Sigma=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar}),
\]
where $\mathcal{C}$ is a set of constant symbols, $\mathcal{F}$ is a set of
function symbols, $\mathcal{R}$ is a set of relation symbols, and
\[
  \operatorname{ar}:\mathcal{F}\cup\mathcal{R}\to \mathbb{Z}_{>0}
\]
assigns to each function or relation symbol its number of arguments.
def:signature.statement_tex:
A \emph{first-order signature} is a tuple
\[
  \Sigma=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar}),
\]
where $\mathcal{C}$ is a set of constant symbols, $\mathcal{F}$ is a set of
function symbols, $\mathcal{R}$ is a set of relation symbols, and
\[
  \operatorname{ar}:\mathcal{F}\cup\mathcal{R}\to \mathbb{Z}_{>0}
\]
assigns to each function or relation symbol its number of arguments.

def:arity.title: Arity
def:arity.statement_preview: Let $\Sigma=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar})$ be a first-order signature. The \emph{arity function} is the map \[ \operatorname{ar}:\mathcal{F}\cup\mathcal{R}\to\mathbb{Z}_{>0} \] that assigns to each function or relation symbol the number of arguments it requires. When constants are treated as $0$-ary function symbols, the codomain i...
def:arity.statement_display:
Let $\Sigma=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar})$ be a
first-order signature. The \emph{arity function} is the map
\[
  \operatorname{ar}:\mathcal{F}\cup\mathcal{R}\to\mathbb{Z}_{>0}
\]
that assigns to each function or relation symbol the number of arguments it
requires. When constants are treated as $0$-ary function symbols, the codomain
is enlarged to include $0$.
def:arity.statement_tex:
Let $\Sigma=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar})$ be a
first-order signature. The \emph{arity function} is the map
\[
  \operatorname{ar}:\mathcal{F}\cup\mathcal{R}\to\mathbb{Z}_{>0}
\]
that assigns to each function or relation symbol the number of arguments it
requires. When constants are treated as $0$-ary function symbols, the codomain
is enlarged to include $0$.

def:language.title: Language
def:language.statement_preview: A \emph{first-order language} has two standard readings. As vocabulary, it is the same data as a signature, \[ \mathcal{L}=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar}). \] As a formal language generated by a signature $\Sigma$, it is the set of well-formed strings built from the alphabet \[ A=\Sigma\cup V\cup\Lambda, \] where $V$ is a countable s...
def:language.statement_display:
A \emph{first-order language} has two standard readings. As vocabulary, it is
the same data as a signature,
\[
  \mathcal{L}=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar}).
\]
As a formal language generated by a signature $\Sigma$, it is the set of
well-formed strings built from the alphabet
\[
  A=\Sigma\cup V\cup\Lambda,
\]
where $V$ is a countable set of variables and $\Lambda$ is the fixed stock of
logical symbols and punctuation. In this second reading,
\[
  \mathcal{L}(\Sigma)
  =\{s\in A^* \mid s\in\operatorname{Terms}(\Sigma)
    \text{ or } s\in\operatorname{WFF}(\Sigma)\}.
\]
def:language.statement_tex:
A \emph{first-order language} has two standard readings. As vocabulary, it is
the same data as a signature,
\[
  \mathcal{L}=(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{F},\mathcal{R},\operatorname{ar}).
\]
As a formal language generated by a signature $\Sigma$, it is the set of
well-formed strings built from the alphabet
\[
  A=\Sigma\cup V\cup\Lambda,
\]
where $V$ is a countable set of variables and $\Lambda$ is the fixed stock of
logical symbols and punctuation. In this second reading,
\[
  \mathcal{L}(\Sigma)
  =\{s\in A^* \mid s\in\operatorname{Terms}(\Sigma)
    \text{ or } s\in\operatorname{WFF}(\Sigma)\}.
\]

def:signature-constant-symbol.title: Constant Symbol
def:signature-constant-symbol.statement_preview: A \emph{constant symbol} is a non-logical symbol that behaves syntactically as a $0$-ary function symbol. In the streamlined convention where constants are included among function symbols, this is the condition \[ \operatorname{Constant}(c) \iff c\in\mathcal{F}\text{ and }\operatorname{ar}(c)=0. \] Because its arity is $0$, the symbol $c$ is already a well-...
def:signature-constant-symbol.statement_display:
A \emph{constant symbol} is a non-logical symbol that behaves syntactically as
a $0$-ary function symbol. In the streamlined convention where constants are
included among function symbols, this is the condition
\[
  \operatorname{Constant}(c)
  \iff c\in\mathcal{F}\text{ and }\operatorname{ar}(c)=0.
\]
Because its arity is $0$, the symbol $c$ is already a well-formed term and
takes no input terms.
def:signature-constant-symbol.statement_tex:
A \emph{constant symbol} is a non-logical symbol that behaves syntactically as
a $0$-ary function symbol. In the streamlined convention where constants are
included among function symbols, this is the condition
\[
  \operatorname{Constant}(c)
  \iff c\in\mathcal{F}\text{ and }\operatorname{ar}(c)=0.
\]
Because its arity is $0$, the symbol $c$ is already a well-formed term and
takes no input terms.

def:signature-function-symbol.title: Function Symbol
def:signature-function-symbol.statement_preview: A \emph{function symbol} is a non-logical symbol $f\in\mathcal{F}$ whose assigned arity determines how many terms it must take as inputs. If $\operatorname{ar}(f)=n$, then $f$ is an $n$-ary term constructor: it takes exactly $n$ terms and produces a new term, not a truth value.
def:signature-function-symbol.statement_display:
A \emph{function symbol} is a non-logical symbol $f\in\mathcal{F}$ whose
assigned arity determines how many terms it must take as inputs. If
$\operatorname{ar}(f)=n$, then $f$ is an $n$-ary term constructor: it takes
exactly $n$ terms and produces a new term, not a truth value.
def:signature-function-symbol.statement_tex:
A \emph{function symbol} is a non-logical symbol $f\in\mathcal{F}$ whose
assigned arity determines how many terms it must take as inputs. If
$\operatorname{ar}(f)=n$, then $f$ is an $n$-ary term constructor: it takes
exactly $n$ terms and produces a new term, not a truth value.

def:relation-symbol.title: Relation Symbol
def:relation-symbol.statement_preview: A \emph{relation symbol}, or predicate symbol, is a non-logical symbol $R\in\mathcal{R}$ whose assigned arity determines how many terms it must take as inputs. If $\operatorname{ar}(R)=n$, then $R$ is an $n$-ary formula constructor: it takes exactly $n$ terms and produces an atomic formula, hence something with a truth value rather than an object.
def:relation-symbol.statement_display:
A \emph{relation symbol}, or predicate symbol, is a non-logical symbol
$R\in\mathcal{R}$ whose assigned arity determines how many terms it must take
as inputs. If $\operatorname{ar}(R)=n$, then $R$ is an $n$-ary formula
constructor: it takes exactly $n$ terms and produces an atomic formula, hence
something with a truth value rather than an object.
def:relation-symbol.statement_tex:
A \emph{relation symbol}, or predicate symbol, is a non-logical symbol
$R\in\mathcal{R}$ whose assigned arity determines how many terms it must take
as inputs. If $\operatorname{ar}(R)=n$, then $R$ is an $n$-ary formula
constructor: it takes exactly $n$ terms and produces an atomic formula, hence
something with a truth value rather than an object.

def:axiom-systems-theory.title: Theory
def:axiom-systems-theory.statement_preview: A \emph{theory} over a first-order language $\mathcal{L}$ is a collection of closed sentences in that language: \[ T\subseteq\operatorname{Sentences}(\mathcal{L}). \] If $\Gamma$ is a chosen set of axioms, the theory generated by $\Gamma$ is the deductive closure \[ T=\{\varphi\in\operatorname{Sentences}(\mathcal{L})\mid \Gamma\vdash\varphi\}. \]
def:axiom-systems-theory.statement_display:
A \emph{theory} over a first-order language $\mathcal{L}$ is a collection of
closed sentences in that language:
\[
  T\subseteq\operatorname{Sentences}(\mathcal{L}).
\]
If $\Gamma$ is a chosen set of axioms, the theory generated by $\Gamma$ is the
deductive closure
\[
  T=\{\varphi\in\operatorname{Sentences}(\mathcal{L})\mid
      \Gamma\vdash\varphi\}.
\]
def:axiom-systems-theory.statement_tex:
A \emph{theory} over a first-order language $\mathcal{L}$ is a collection of
closed sentences in that language:
\[
  T\subseteq\operatorname{Sentences}(\mathcal{L}).
\]
If $\Gamma$ is a chosen set of axioms, the theory generated by $\Gamma$ is the
deductive closure
\[
  T=\{\varphi\in\operatorname{Sentences}(\mathcal{L})\mid
      \Gamma\vdash\varphi\}.
\]

def:axiom-systems-theorem.title: Theorem
def:axiom-systems-theorem.statement_preview: A \emph{theorem} of a theory $T$ is a sentence $\varphi$ for which there is a finite formal derivation from $T$, written \[ T\vdash\varphi. \] Equivalently, in proof-theoretic terms, $\varphi$ is a theorem of $T$ exactly when some derivation has leaves drawn from logical axioms or sentences of $T$, uses valid inference rules, and has $\varphi$ as its root.
def:axiom-systems-theorem.statement_display:
A \emph{theorem} of a theory $T$ is a sentence $\varphi$ for which there is a
finite formal derivation from $T$, written
\[
  T\vdash\varphi.
\]
Equivalently, in proof-theoretic terms, $\varphi$ is a theorem of $T$ exactly
when some derivation has leaves drawn from logical axioms or sentences of
$T$, uses valid inference rules, and has $\varphi$ as its root.
def:axiom-systems-theorem.statement_tex:
A \emph{theorem} of a theory $T$ is a sentence $\varphi$ for which there is a
finite formal derivation from $T$, written
\[
  T\vdash\varphi.
\]
Equivalently, in proof-theoretic terms, $\varphi$ is a theorem of $T$ exactly
when some derivation has leaves drawn from logical axioms or sentences of
$T$, uses valid inference rules, and has $\varphi$ as its root.

def:axiom-systems-consequence.title: Consequence
def:axiom-systems-consequence.statement_preview: A sentence $\varphi$ is a \emph{semantic consequence} of a set of premises $\Gamma$, written $\Gamma\vDash\varphi$, if every model of $\Gamma$ is also a model of $\varphi$. Equivalently, there is no structure in which all sentences of $\Gamma$ are true while $\varphi$ is false.
def:axiom-systems-consequence.statement_display:
A sentence $\varphi$ is a \emph{semantic consequence} of a set of premises
$\Gamma$, written $\Gamma\vDash\varphi$, if every model of $\Gamma$ is also a
model of $\varphi$. Equivalently, there is no structure in which all sentences
of $\Gamma$ are true while $\varphi$ is false.
def:axiom-systems-consequence.statement_tex:
A sentence $\varphi$ is a \emph{semantic consequence} of a set of premises
$\Gamma$, written $\Gamma\vDash\varphi$, if every model of $\Gamma$ is also a
model of $\varphi$. Equivalently, there is no structure in which all sentences
of $\Gamma$ are true while $\varphi$ is false.

def:deductive-closure.title: Deductive Closure
def:deductive-closure.statement_preview: Let $\Gamma\subseteq\operatorname{Sentences}(\mathcal{L})$ be a set of premises. The \emph{deductive closure} of $\Gamma$, denoted $\operatorname{Cn}(\Gamma)$, is the set of all sentences derivable from $\Gamma$: \[ \operatorname{Cn}(\Gamma) = \{\varphi\in\operatorname{Sentences}(\mathcal{L})\mid \Gamma\vdash\varphi\}. \]
def:deductive-closure.statement_display:
Let $\Gamma\subseteq\operatorname{Sentences}(\mathcal{L})$ be a set of
premises. The \emph{deductive closure} of $\Gamma$, denoted
$\operatorname{Cn}(\Gamma)$, is the set of all sentences derivable from
$\Gamma$:
\[
  \operatorname{Cn}(\Gamma)
  =
  \{\varphi\in\operatorname{Sentences}(\mathcal{L})\mid
    \Gamma\vdash\varphi\}.
\]
def:deductive-closure.statement_tex:
Let $\Gamma\subseteq\operatorname{Sentences}(\mathcal{L})$ be a set of
premises. The \emph{deductive closure} of $\Gamma$, denoted
$\operatorname{Cn}(\Gamma)$, is the set of all sentences derivable from
$\Gamma$:
\[
  \operatorname{Cn}(\Gamma)
  =
  \{\varphi\in\operatorname{Sentences}(\mathcal{L})\mid
    \Gamma\vdash\varphi\}.
\]

ax:predicate-logic-universal-instantiati.title: Universal Instantiation
ax:predicate-logic-universal-instantiati.statement_preview: For every formula $\varphi(x)$ and every term $t$ that is free for $x$ in $\varphi$, \[ \forall x\,\varphi(x) \Longrightarrow \varphi(t). \]
ax:predicate-logic-universal-instantiati.statement_display:
For every formula $\varphi(x)$ and every term $t$ that is free for $x$ in
$\varphi$,
\[
\forall x\,\varphi(x) \Longrightarrow \varphi(t).
\]
ax:predicate-logic-universal-instantiati.statement_tex:
For every formula $\varphi(x)$ and every term $t$ that is free for $x$ in
$\varphi$,
\[
\forall x\,\varphi(x) \Longrightarrow \varphi(t).
\]

ax:predicate-logic-quantifier-distributi.title: Quantifier Distribution
ax:predicate-logic-quantifier-distributi.statement_preview: For formulas $\varphi$ and $\psi(x)$, if $x$ is not free in $\varphi$, then \[ \forall x\,(\varphi \Longrightarrow \psi(x)) \Longrightarrow \bigl(\varphi \Longrightarrow \forall x\,\psi(x)\bigr). \]
ax:predicate-logic-quantifier-distributi.statement_display:
For formulas $\varphi$ and $\psi(x)$, if $x$ is not free in $\varphi$, then
\[
\forall x\,(\varphi \Longrightarrow \psi(x))
\Longrightarrow
\bigl(\varphi \Longrightarrow \forall x\,\psi(x)\bigr).
\]
ax:predicate-logic-quantifier-distributi.statement_tex:
For formulas $\varphi$ and $\psi(x)$, if $x$ is not free in $\varphi$, then
\[
\forall x\,(\varphi \Longrightarrow \psi(x))
\Longrightarrow
\bigl(\varphi \Longrightarrow \forall x\,\psi(x)\bigr).
\]

def:eq-refl.title: Equality Introduction — Reflexivity
def:eq-refl.statement_preview: For any term $t$, one may infer $t = t$.
def:eq-refl.statement_display: For any term $t$, one may infer $t = t$.
def:eq-refl.statement_tex: For any term $t$, one may infer $t = t$.

def:eq-elim.title: Equality Elimination — Substitution of Equals
def:eq-elim.statement_preview: From $t_1 = t_2$ and $\varphi[t_1/x]$, one may infer $\varphi[t_2/x]$.
def:eq-elim.statement_display: From $t_1 = t_2$ and $\varphi[t_1/x]$, one may infer $\varphi[t_2/x]$.
def:eq-elim.statement_tex: From $t_1 = t_2$ and $\varphi[t_1/x]$, one may infer $\varphi[t_2/x]$.

thm:eq-sym.title: Symmetry of Equality
thm:eq-sym.statement_preview: From $t_1 = t_2$, one may infer $t_2 = t_1$.
thm:eq-sym.statement_display: From $t_1 = t_2$, one may infer $t_2 = t_1$.
thm:eq-sym.statement_tex: From $t_1 = t_2$, one may infer $t_2 = t_1$.

thm:eq-trans.title: Transitivity of Equality
thm:eq-trans.statement_preview: From $t_1 = t_2$ and $t_2 = t_3$, one may infer $t_1 = t_3$.
thm:eq-trans.statement_display: From $t_1 = t_2$ and $t_2 = t_3$, one may infer $t_1 = t_3$.
thm:eq-trans.statement_tex: From $t_1 = t_2$ and $t_2 = t_3$, one may infer $t_1 = t_3$.

thm:eq-term.title: Term Substitution under Equality
thm:eq-term.statement_preview: If $t_1 = t_2$, then for any function symbol $f$, $f(\dots,t_1,\dots) = f(\dots,t_2,\dots)$.
thm:eq-term.statement_display:
If $t_1 = t_2$, then for any function symbol $f$,
$f(\dots,t_1,\dots) = f(\dots,t_2,\dots)$.
thm:eq-term.statement_tex:
If $t_1 = t_2$, then for any function symbol $f$,
$f(\dots,t_1,\dots) = f(\dots,t_2,\dots)$.

thm:eq-pred.title: Predicate Substitution under Equality
thm:eq-pred.statement_preview: If $t_1 = t_2$ and $P$ is an $n$-ary predicate symbol, then $P(\dots,t_1,\dots) \;\Leftrightarrow\; P(\dots,t_2,\dots)$.
thm:eq-pred.statement_display:
If $t_1 = t_2$ and $P$ is an $n$-ary predicate symbol, then
$P(\dots,t_1,\dots) \;\Leftrightarrow\; P(\dots,t_2,\dots)$.
thm:eq-pred.statement_tex:
If $t_1 = t_2$ and $P$ is an $n$-ary predicate symbol, then
$P(\dots,t_1,\dots) \;\Leftrightarrow\; P(\dots,t_2,\dots)$.

def:ui.title: Universal Instantiation — UI
def:ui.statement_preview: From a universally quantified statement, infer any instance obtained by substituting a term for the bound variable: \[ \forall x\,\varphi \;\Rightarrow\; \varphi[t/x]. \]
def:ui.statement_display:
From a universally quantified statement, infer any instance obtained by
substituting a term for the bound variable:
\[
\forall x\,\varphi \;\Rightarrow\; \varphi[t/x].
\]
def:ui.statement_tex:
From a universally quantified statement, infer any instance obtained by
substituting a term for the bound variable:
\[
\forall x\,\varphi \;\Rightarrow\; \varphi[t/x].
\]

def:ug.title: Universal Generalization — UG
def:ug.statement_preview: From a formula, infer a universally quantified statement: \[ \varphi \;\Rightarrow\; \forall x\,\varphi. \]
def:ug.statement_display:
From a formula, infer a universally quantified statement:
\[
\varphi \;\Rightarrow\; \forall x\,\varphi.
\]
def:ug.statement_tex:
From a formula, infer a universally quantified statement:
\[
\varphi \;\Rightarrow\; \forall x\,\varphi.
\]

def:ei.title: Existential Instantiation — EI
def:ei.statement_preview: From an existentially quantified statement, infer an instance with a fresh constant (witness): \[ \exists x\,\varphi \;\Rightarrow\; \varphi[c/x]. \]
def:ei.statement_display:
From an existentially quantified statement, infer an instance with a fresh
constant (witness):
\[
\exists x\,\varphi \;\Rightarrow\; \varphi[c/x].
\]
def:ei.statement_tex:
From an existentially quantified statement, infer an instance with a fresh
constant (witness):
\[
\exists x\,\varphi \;\Rightarrow\; \varphi[c/x].
\]

def:eg.title: Existential Generalization — EG
def:eg.statement_preview: From a formula containing a term, infer an existential statement: \[ \varphi[t/x] \;\Rightarrow\; \exists x\,\varphi. \]
def:eg.statement_display:
From a formula containing a term, infer an existential statement:
\[
\varphi[t/x] \;\Rightarrow\; \exists x\,\varphi.
\]
def:eg.statement_tex:
From a formula containing a term, infer an existential statement:
\[
\varphi[t/x] \;\Rightarrow\; \exists x\,\varphi.
\]

def:fol-soundness.title: Soundness
def:fol-soundness.statement_preview: A proof system is \emph{sound} if every provable formula is valid: \[ \Gamma \vdash \varphi \implies \Gamma \models \varphi. \]
def:fol-soundness.statement_display:
A proof system is \emph{sound} if every provable formula is valid:
\[
\Gamma \vdash \varphi \implies \Gamma \models \varphi.
\]
def:fol-soundness.statement_tex:
A proof system is \emph{sound} if every provable formula is valid:
\[
\Gamma \vdash \varphi \implies \Gamma \models \varphi.
\]

def:fol-completeness.title: Completeness
def:fol-completeness.statement_preview: A proof system is \emph{complete} if every valid formula is provable: \[ \Gamma \models \varphi \implies \Gamma \vdash \varphi. \]
def:fol-completeness.statement_display:
A proof system is \emph{complete} if every valid formula is provable:
\[
\Gamma \models \varphi \implies \Gamma \vdash \varphi.
\]
def:fol-completeness.statement_tex:
A proof system is \emph{complete} if every valid formula is provable:
\[
\Gamma \models \varphi \implies \Gamma \vdash \varphi.
\]

thm:fol-sound.title: Soundness of First-Order Logic
thm:fol-sound.statement_preview: The standard inference rules for first-order logic (UI, UG, EI, EG, and the propositional rules) are sound: they preserve truth in all structures. If $\Gamma \vdash \varphi$, then $\Gamma \models \varphi$.
thm:fol-sound.statement_display:
The standard inference rules for first-order logic (UI, UG, EI, EG, and the
propositional rules) are sound: they preserve truth in all structures.

If $\Gamma \vdash \varphi$, then $\Gamma \models \varphi$.
thm:fol-sound.statement_tex:
The standard inference rules for first-order logic (UI, UG, EI, EG, and the
propositional rules) are sound: they preserve truth in all structures.

If $\Gamma \vdash \varphi$, then $\Gamma \models \varphi$.

def:universal-q.title: Universal Formula
def:universal-q.statement_preview: A \emph{universal formula} is a well-formed formula of the form \[ \forall x\,\varphi, \] where \(x\) is a variable and \(\varphi\) is a well-formed formula.
def:universal-q.statement_display:
A \emph{universal formula} is a well-formed formula of the form
\[
\forall x\,\varphi,
\]
where \(x\) is a variable and \(\varphi\) is a well-formed formula.
def:universal-q.statement_tex:
A \emph{universal formula} is a well-formed formula of the form
\[
\forall x\,\varphi,
\]
where \(x\) is a variable and \(\varphi\) is a well-formed formula.

def:existential-q.title: Existential Formula
def:existential-q.statement_preview: An \emph{existential formula} is a well-formed formula of the form \[ \exists x\,\varphi, \] where \(x\) is a variable and \(\varphi\) is a well-formed formula.
def:existential-q.statement_display:
An \emph{existential formula} is a well-formed formula of the form
\[
\exists x\,\varphi,
\]
where \(x\) is a variable and \(\varphi\) is a well-formed formula.
def:existential-q.statement_tex:
An \emph{existential formula} is a well-formed formula of the form
\[
\exists x\,\varphi,
\]
where \(x\) is a variable and \(\varphi\) is a well-formed formula.

def:bounded-q.title: Bounded Quantifier
def:bounded-q.statement_preview: A \emph{bounded quantifier} is an abbreviation that restricts a quantifier to a specified set or predicate: \[ \forall x\in A\,\varphi \quad\coloneqq\quad \forall x\,(x\in A\to\varphi), \] \[ \exists x\in A\,\varphi \quad\coloneqq\quad \exists x\,(x\in A\land\varphi). \]
def:bounded-q.statement_display:
A \emph{bounded quantifier} is an abbreviation that restricts a quantifier to a
specified set or predicate:
\[
\forall x\in A\,\varphi
\quad\coloneqq\quad
\forall x\,(x\in A\to\varphi),
\]
\[
\exists x\in A\,\varphi
\quad\coloneqq\quad
\exists x\,(x\in A\land\varphi).
\]
def:bounded-q.statement_tex:
A \emph{bounded quantifier} is an abbreviation that restricts a quantifier to a
specified set or predicate:
\[
\forall x\in A\,\varphi
\quad\coloneqq\quad
\forall x\,(x\in A\to\varphi),
\]
\[
\exists x\in A\,\varphi
\quad\coloneqq\quad
\exists x\,(x\in A\land\varphi).
\]

def:sentence-predicate-logic.title: Sentence
def:sentence-predicate-logic.statement_preview: A \emph{sentence} is a well-formed formula with no free variables.
def:sentence-predicate-logic.statement_display: A \emph{sentence} is a well-formed formula with no free variables.
def:sentence-predicate-logic.statement_tex: A \emph{sentence} is a well-formed formula with no free variables.

def:open-formula-quantifiers.title: Open Formula
def:open-formula-quantifiers.statement_preview: An \emph{open formula} is a well-formed formula with at least one free variable.
def:open-formula-quantifiers.statement_display:
An \emph{open formula} is a well-formed formula with at least one free
variable.
def:open-formula-quantifiers.statement_tex:
An \emph{open formula} is a well-formed formula with at least one free
variable.

def:quantifier-scope.title: Quantifier Scope
def:quantifier-scope.statement_preview: In a formula \(Qx\,\varphi\), where \(Q\in\{\forall,\exists\}\), the \emph{scope} of the quantifier \(Qx\) is the formula \(\varphi\). Occurrences of \(x\) inside that scope are bound by the quantifier unless an inner quantifier for \(x\) intervenes.
def:quantifier-scope.statement_display:
In a formula \(Qx\,\varphi\), where \(Q\in\{\forall,\exists\}\), the
\emph{scope} of the quantifier \(Qx\) is the formula \(\varphi\). Occurrences
of \(x\) inside that scope are bound by the quantifier unless an inner
quantifier for \(x\) intervenes.
def:quantifier-scope.statement_tex:
In a formula \(Qx\,\varphi\), where \(Q\in\{\forall,\exists\}\), the
\emph{scope} of the quantifier \(Qx\) is the formula \(\varphi\). Occurrences
of \(x\) inside that scope are bound by the quantifier unless an inner
quantifier for \(x\) intervenes.

thm:neg-bounded.title: Negation of Bounded Quantifiers
thm:neg-bounded.statement_preview:
Let \(A\) be a set and let \(\varphi\) be a formula. Then \[ 
eg( orall x\in A\,\varphi)\equiv \exists x\in A\,
eg\varphi, \qquad 
eg(\exists x\in A\,\varphi)\equiv  orall x\in A\,
eg\varphi. \]
thm:neg-bounded.statement_display:
Let \(A\) be a set and let \(\varphi\) be a formula. Then
\[
\neg(\forall x\in A\,\varphi)\equiv \exists x\in A\,\neg\varphi,
\qquad
\neg(\exists x\in A\,\varphi)\equiv \forall x\in A\,\neg\varphi.
\]
thm:neg-bounded.statement_tex:
Let \(A\) be a set and let \(\varphi\) be a formula. Then
\[
\neg(\forall x\in A\,\varphi)\equiv \exists x\in A\,\neg\varphi,
\qquad
\neg(\exists x\in A\,\varphi)\equiv \forall x\in A\,\neg\varphi.
\]

def:strength.title: Stronger Formula
def:strength.statement_preview: A sentence \(A\) is \emph{stronger than} a sentence \(B\) exactly when \[ A\models B \quad\text{and}\quad B\not\models A. \]
def:strength.statement_display:
A sentence \(A\) is \emph{stronger than} a sentence \(B\) exactly when
\[
A\models B
\quad\text{and}\quad
B\not\models A.
\]
def:strength.statement_tex:
A sentence \(A\) is \emph{stronger than} a sentence \(B\) exactly when
\[
A\models B
\quad\text{and}\quad
B\not\models A.
\]

def:weaker-formula.title: Weaker Formula
def:weaker-formula.statement_preview: A sentence \(B\) is \emph{weaker than} a sentence \(A\) exactly when \(A\) is stronger than \(B\).
def:weaker-formula.statement_display:
A sentence \(B\) is \emph{weaker than} a sentence \(A\) exactly when \(A\) is
stronger than \(B\).
def:weaker-formula.statement_tex:
A sentence \(B\) is \emph{weaker than} a sentence \(A\) exactly when \(A\) is
stronger than \(B\).

def:q-alt.title: Quantifier Order
def:q-alt.statement_preview: The \emph{quantifier order} of a prenex formula is the ordered list of quantifiers in its prefix. A change of order is significant when it changes which witnesses may depend on earlier variables.
def:q-alt.statement_display:
The \emph{quantifier order} of a prenex formula is the ordered list of
quantifiers in its prefix. A change of order is significant when it changes
which witnesses may depend on earlier variables.
def:q-alt.statement_tex:
The \emph{quantifier order} of a prenex formula is the ordered list of
quantifiers in its prefix. A change of order is significant when it changes
which witnesses may depend on earlier variables.

thm:uniform-witness-implies-dependent-wi.title: Uniform Witness Implies Dependent Witness
thm:uniform-witness-implies-dependent-wi.statement_preview: For every formula \(\varphi(x,y)\), \[ \exists y\,\forall x\,\varphi(x,y) \models \forall x\,\exists y\,\varphi(x,y). \]
thm:uniform-witness-implies-dependent-wi.statement_display:
For every formula \(\varphi(x,y)\),
\[
\exists y\,\forall x\,\varphi(x,y)
\models
\forall x\,\exists y\,\varphi(x,y).
\]
thm:uniform-witness-implies-dependent-wi.statement_tex:
For every formula \(\varphi(x,y)\),
\[
\exists y\,\forall x\,\varphi(x,y)
\models
\forall x\,\exists y\,\varphi(x,y).
\]

thm:dependent-witness-not-uniform.title: Dependent Witness Need Not Be Uniform
thm:dependent-witness-not-uniform.statement_preview: In general, \[ \forall x\,\exists y\,\varphi(x,y) \not\models \exists y\,\forall x\,\varphi(x,y). \]
thm:dependent-witness-not-uniform.statement_display:
In general,
\[
\forall x\,\exists y\,\varphi(x,y)
\not\models
\exists y\,\forall x\,\varphi(x,y).
\]
thm:dependent-witness-not-uniform.statement_tex:
In general,
\[
\forall x\,\exists y\,\varphi(x,y)
\not\models
\exists y\,\forall x\,\varphi(x,y).
\]

def:quantifier-prefix.title: Quantifier Prefix
def:quantifier-prefix.statement_preview: A \emph{quantifier prefix} is a finite string \[ Q_1x_1\,Q_2x_2\cdots Q_nx_n, \] where each \(Q_i\in\{\forall,\exists\}\) and each \(x_i\) is a variable.
def:quantifier-prefix.statement_display:
A \emph{quantifier prefix} is a finite string
\[
Q_1x_1\,Q_2x_2\cdots Q_nx_n,
\]
where each \(Q_i\in\{\forall,\exists\}\) and each \(x_i\) is a variable.
def:quantifier-prefix.statement_tex:
A \emph{quantifier prefix} is a finite string
\[
Q_1x_1\,Q_2x_2\cdots Q_nx_n,
\]
where each \(Q_i\in\{\forall,\exists\}\) and each \(x_i\) is a variable.

def:matrix-prenex.title: Matrix
def:matrix-prenex.statement_preview: In a formula \(Q_1x_1\cdots Q_nx_n\,\psi\), the \emph{matrix} is the trailing formula \(\psi\). For prenex normal form, the matrix is required to be quantifier-free.
def:matrix-prenex.statement_display:
In a formula \(Q_1x_1\cdots Q_nx_n\,\psi\), the \emph{matrix} is the trailing
formula \(\psi\). For prenex normal form, the matrix is required to be
quantifier-free.
def:matrix-prenex.statement_tex:
In a formula \(Q_1x_1\cdots Q_nx_n\,\psi\), the \emph{matrix} is the trailing
formula \(\psi\). For prenex normal form, the matrix is required to be
quantifier-free.

def:prenex-formula.title: Prenex Formula
def:prenex-formula.statement_preview: A \emph{prenex formula} is a formula of the form \[ Q_1x_1\,Q_2x_2\cdots Q_nx_n\,\psi, \] where \(Q_1x_1\cdots Q_nx_n\) is a quantifier prefix and \(\psi\) is quantifier-free.
def:prenex-formula.statement_display:
A \emph{prenex formula} is a formula of the form
\[
Q_1x_1\,Q_2x_2\cdots Q_nx_n\,\psi,
\]
where \(Q_1x_1\cdots Q_nx_n\) is a quantifier prefix and \(\psi\) is
quantifier-free.
def:prenex-formula.statement_tex:
A \emph{prenex formula} is a formula of the form
\[
Q_1x_1\,Q_2x_2\cdots Q_nx_n\,\psi,
\]
where \(Q_1x_1\cdots Q_nx_n\) is a quantifier prefix and \(\psi\) is
quantifier-free.

def:pnf.title: Prenex Normal Form
def:pnf.statement_preview: A formula \(\psi\) is a \emph{prenex normal form} of a formula \(\varphi\) exactly when \(\psi\) is prenex and \(\varphi\equiv\psi\).
def:pnf.statement_display:
A formula \(\psi\) is a \emph{prenex normal form} of a formula \(\varphi\)
exactly when \(\psi\) is prenex and \(\varphi\equiv\psi\).
def:pnf.statement_tex:
A formula \(\psi\) is a \emph{prenex normal form} of a formula \(\varphi\)
exactly when \(\psi\) is prenex and \(\varphi\equiv\psi\).

thm:pnf.title: Existence of Prenex Normal Form
thm:pnf.statement_preview: Every first-order formula is logically equivalent to some formula in prenex normal form.
thm:pnf.statement_display:
Every first-order formula is logically equivalent to some formula in prenex
normal form.
thm:pnf.statement_tex:
Every first-order formula is logically equivalent to some formula in prenex
normal form.

thm:qneg.title: Quantifier Negation Laws
thm:qneg.statement_preview:
For every formula \(\varphi\), \[ 
eg orall x\,\varphi \equiv \exists x\,
eg\varphi, \qquad 
eg\exists x\,\varphi \equiv  orall x\,
eg\varphi. \]
thm:qneg.statement_display:
For every formula \(\varphi\),
\[
\neg\forall x\,\varphi \equiv \exists x\,\neg\varphi,
\qquad
\neg\exists x\,\varphi \equiv \forall x\,\neg\varphi.
\]
thm:qneg.statement_tex:
For every formula \(\varphi\),
\[
\neg\forall x\,\varphi \equiv \exists x\,\neg\varphi,
\qquad
\neg\exists x\,\varphi \equiv \forall x\,\neg\varphi.
\]

thm:quantifier-double-negation.title: Double Negation and Quantifiers
thm:quantifier-double-negation.statement_preview:
For every formula \(\varphi\), \[ 
eg
eg orall x\,\varphi \equiv  orall x\,\varphi, \qquad 
eg
eg\exists x\,\varphi \equiv \exists x\,\varphi. \]
thm:quantifier-double-negation.statement_display:
For every formula \(\varphi\),
\[
\neg\neg\forall x\,\varphi \equiv \forall x\,\varphi,
\qquad
\neg\neg\exists x\,\varphi \equiv \exists x\,\varphi.
\]
thm:quantifier-double-negation.statement_tex:
For every formula \(\varphi\),
\[
\neg\neg\forall x\,\varphi \equiv \forall x\,\varphi,
\qquad
\neg\neg\exists x\,\varphi \equiv \exists x\,\varphi.
\]

thm:qcomm.title: Same-Type Quantifier Commutation
thm:qcomm.statement_preview: Quantifiers of the same type commute: \[ \forall x\,\forall y\,\varphi \equiv \forall y\,\forall x\,\varphi, \qquad \exists x\,\exists y\,\varphi \equiv \exists y\,\exists x\,\varphi. \]
thm:qcomm.statement_display:
Quantifiers of the same type commute:
\[
\forall x\,\forall y\,\varphi \equiv \forall y\,\forall x\,\varphi,
\qquad
\exists x\,\exists y\,\varphi \equiv \exists y\,\exists x\,\varphi.
\]
thm:qcomm.statement_tex:
Quantifiers of the same type commute:
\[
\forall x\,\forall y\,\varphi \equiv \forall y\,\forall x\,\varphi,
\qquad
\exists x\,\exists y\,\varphi \equiv \exists y\,\exists x\,\varphi.
\]

thm:mixed-quantifiers-do-not-commute.title: Mixed Quantifiers Do Not Commute
thm:mixed-quantifiers-do-not-commute.statement_preview: In general, \[ \forall x\,\exists y\,\varphi \not\equiv \exists y\,\forall x\,\varphi. \]
thm:mixed-quantifiers-do-not-commute.statement_display:
In general,
\[
\forall x\,\exists y\,\varphi
\not\equiv
\exists y\,\forall x\,\varphi.
\]
thm:mixed-quantifiers-do-not-commute.statement_tex:
In general,
\[
\forall x\,\exists y\,\varphi
\not\equiv
\exists y\,\forall x\,\varphi.
\]

thm:qdist.title: Valid Quantifier Distribution
thm:qdist.statement_preview: For all formulas \(\varphi,\psi\), \[ \forall x\,(\varphi\land\psi) \equiv (\forall x\,\varphi)\land(\forall x\,\psi), \] \[ \exists x\,(\varphi\lor\psi) \equiv (\exists x\,\varphi)\lor(\exists x\,\psi). \]
thm:qdist.statement_display:
For all formulas \(\varphi,\psi\),
\[
\forall x\,(\varphi\land\psi)
\equiv
(\forall x\,\varphi)\land(\forall x\,\psi),
\]
\[
\exists x\,(\varphi\lor\psi)
\equiv
(\exists x\,\varphi)\lor(\exists x\,\psi).
\]
thm:qdist.statement_tex:
For all formulas \(\varphi,\psi\),
\[
\forall x\,(\varphi\land\psi)
\equiv
(\forall x\,\varphi)\land(\forall x\,\psi),
\]
\[
\exists x\,(\varphi\lor\psi)
\equiv
(\exists x\,\varphi)\lor(\exists x\,\psi).
\]

thm:invalid-quantifier-distribution.title: Invalid Quantifier Distribution
thm:invalid-quantifier-distribution.statement_preview: In general, \[ \forall x\,(\varphi\lor\psi) \not\equiv (\forall x\,\varphi)\lor(\forall x\,\psi), \] \[ \exists x\,(\varphi\land\psi) \not\equiv (\exists x\,\varphi)\land(\exists x\,\psi). \]
thm:invalid-quantifier-distribution.statement_display:
In general,
\[
\forall x\,(\varphi\lor\psi)
\not\equiv
(\forall x\,\varphi)\lor(\forall x\,\psi),
\]
\[
\exists x\,(\varphi\land\psi)
\not\equiv
(\exists x\,\varphi)\land(\exists x\,\psi).
\]
thm:invalid-quantifier-distribution.statement_tex:
In general,
\[
\forall x\,(\varphi\lor\psi)
\not\equiv
(\forall x\,\varphi)\lor(\forall x\,\psi),
\]
\[
\exists x\,(\varphi\land\psi)
\not\equiv
(\exists x\,\varphi)\land(\exists x\,\psi).
\]

thm:vacuous.title: Vacuous Quantification
thm:vacuous.statement_preview: If \(x\) does not occur free in \(\varphi\), then \[ \forall x\,\varphi\equiv\varphi, \qquad \exists x\,\varphi\equiv\varphi. \]
thm:vacuous.statement_display:
If \(x\) does not occur free in \(\varphi\), then
\[
\forall x\,\varphi\equiv\varphi,
\qquad
\exists x\,\varphi\equiv\varphi.
\]
thm:vacuous.statement_tex:
If \(x\) does not occur free in \(\varphi\), then
\[
\forall x\,\varphi\equiv\varphi,
\qquad
\exists x\,\varphi\equiv\varphi.
\]

thm:rename.title: Renaming Bound Variables
thm:rename.statement_preview: If \(y\) is free for \(x\) in \(\varphi\) and no capture is introduced, then \[ \forall x\,\varphi\equiv\forall y\,\varphi[y/x], \qquad \exists x\,\varphi\equiv\exists y\,\varphi[y/x]. \]
thm:rename.statement_display:
If \(y\) is free for \(x\) in \(\varphi\) and no capture is introduced, then
\[
\forall x\,\varphi\equiv\forall y\,\varphi[y/x],
\qquad
\exists x\,\varphi\equiv\exists y\,\varphi[y/x].
\]
thm:rename.statement_tex:
If \(y\) is free for \(x\) in \(\varphi\) and no capture is introduced, then
\[
\forall x\,\varphi\equiv\forall y\,\varphi[y/x],
\qquad
\exists x\,\varphi\equiv\exists y\,\varphi[y/x].
\]

def:model.title: Model of a Sentence
def:model.statement_preview: Let \(\varphi\) be a sentence and let \(\mathcal M\) be a structure for the language of \(\varphi\). The structure \(\mathcal M\) is a \emph{model of} \(\varphi\) exactly when \(\mathcal M\models\varphi\).

def:model-theory-set.title: Model of a Set of Sentences
def:model-theory-set.statement_preview: Let \(T\) be a set of sentences and let \(\mathcal M\) be a structure for their common language. The structure \(\mathcal M\) is a \emph{model of} \(T\), written \(\mathcal M\models T\), exactly when \(\mathcal M\models\theta\) for every \(\theta\in T\).
def:model-theory-set.statement_display:
Let \(T\) be a set of sentences and let \(\mathcal M\) be a structure for their
common language. The structure \(\mathcal M\) is a \emph{model of} \(T\),
written \(\mathcal M\models T\), exactly when
\(\mathcal M\models\theta\) for every \(\theta\in T\).
def:model-theory-set.statement_tex:
Let \(T\) be a set of sentences and let \(\mathcal M\) be a structure for their
common language. The structure \(\mathcal M\) is a \emph{model of} \(T\),
written \(\mathcal M\models T\), exactly when
\(\mathcal M\models\theta\) for every \(\theta\in T\).

def:theory.title: Theory
def:theory.statement_preview: In Volume I, a \emph{theory} is a set of sentences in a fixed first-order language.
def:theory.statement_display:
In Volume I, a \emph{theory} is a set of sentences in a fixed first-order
language.
def:theory.statement_tex:
In Volume I, a \emph{theory} is a set of sentences in a fixed first-order
language.

def:log-consequence.title: Logical Consequence
def:log-consequence.statement_preview: Let \(T\) be a set of sentences and let \(\varphi\) be a sentence in the same language. The sentence \(\varphi\) is a \emph{logical consequence} of \(T\), written \(T\models\varphi\), exactly when every model of \(T\) is a model of \(\varphi\).
def:log-consequence.statement_display:
Let \(T\) be a set of sentences and let \(\varphi\) be a sentence in the same
language. The sentence \(\varphi\) is a \emph{logical consequence} of \(T\),
written \(T\models\varphi\), exactly when every model of \(T\) is a model of
\(\varphi\).
def:log-consequence.statement_tex:
Let \(T\) be a set of sentences and let \(\varphi\) be a sentence in the same
language. The sentence \(\varphi\) is a \emph{logical consequence} of \(T\),
written \(T\models\varphi\), exactly when every model of \(T\) is a model of
\(\varphi\).

def:log-equiv.title: Logical Equivalence
def:log-equiv.statement_preview: Two sentences \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are \emph{logically equivalent}, written \(\varphi\equiv\psi\), exactly when each is a logical consequence of the other: \[ \varphi\models\psi \quad\text{and}\quad \psi\models\varphi. \]
def:log-equiv.statement_display:
Two sentences \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are \emph{logically equivalent}, written
\(\varphi\equiv\psi\), exactly when each is a logical consequence of the other:
\[
\varphi\models\psi
\quad\text{and}\quad
\psi\models\varphi.
\]
def:log-equiv.statement_tex:
Two sentences \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are \emph{logically equivalent}, written
\(\varphi\equiv\psi\), exactly when each is a logical consequence of the other:
\[
\varphi\models\psi
\quad\text{and}\quad
\psi\models\varphi.
\]

def:predicate.title: Open Formula
def:predicate.statement_preview: An \emph{open formula} is a well-formed formula with at least one free variable. When the free variables are among \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\), it may be displayed as \(\varphi(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\).
def:predicate.statement_display:
An \emph{open formula} is a well-formed formula with at least one free
variable. When the free variables are among \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\), it may be
displayed as \(\varphi(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\).
def:predicate.statement_tex:
An \emph{open formula} is a well-formed formula with at least one free
variable. When the free variables are among \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\), it may be

def:definable-relation.title: Definable Relation
def:definable-relation.statement_preview: Let \(\mathcal M=\langle D,I\rangle\) be a structure and let \(\varphi(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\) be an open formula. The \emph{relation defined by} \(\varphi\) in \(\mathcal M\) is the set \[ R_{\varphi}^{\mathcal M} = \{(a_1,\ldots,a_n)\in D^n: \mathcal M,s[x_1\mapsto a_1,\ldots,x_n\mapsto a_n]\models\varphi\}. \]
def:definable-relation.statement_display:
Let \(\mathcal M=\langle D,I\rangle\) be a structure and let
\(\varphi(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\) be an open formula. The \emph{relation defined by}
\(\varphi\) in \(\mathcal M\) is the set
\[
R_{\varphi}^{\mathcal M}
=
\{(a_1,\ldots,a_n)\in D^n:
\mathcal M,s[x_1\mapsto a_1,\ldots,x_n\mapsto a_n]\models\varphi\}.
\]

lem:subst-terms.title: Substitution Lemma for Terms
lem:subst-terms.statement_preview: Let \(\mathcal M\) be a structure, let \(s\) be a variable assignment, let \(x\) be a variable, and let \(t\) and \(u\) be terms. Then \[ \llbracket t[u/x]\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s} = \llbracket t\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,\, s[x\mapsto\llbracket u\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}]}. \]
lem:subst-terms.statement_display:
Let \(\mathcal M\) be a structure, let \(s\) be a variable assignment, let
\(x\) be a variable, and let \(t\) and \(u\) be terms. Then
\[
\llbracket t[u/x]\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}
=
\llbracket t\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,\,
s[x\mapsto\llbracket u\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}]}.
\]
lem:subst-terms.statement_tex:

=
\llbracket t\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,\,
s[x\mapsto\llbracket u\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}]}.
\]

lem:subst-formulas.title: Substitution Lemma for Formulas
lem:subst-formulas.statement_preview: Let \(\varphi\) be a formula and let \(u\) be a term free for \(x\) in \(\varphi\). For every structure \(\mathcal M\) and assignment \(s\), \[ \mathcal M,s\models\varphi[u/x] \Longleftrightarrow \mathcal M,s[x\mapsto\llbracket u\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}]\models\varphi. \]
lem:subst-formulas.statement_display:
Let \(\varphi\) be a formula and let \(u\) be a term free for \(x\) in
\(\varphi\). For every structure \(\mathcal M\) and assignment \(s\),
\[
\mathcal M,s\models\varphi[u/x]
\Longleftrightarrow
\mathcal M,s[x\mapsto\llbracket u\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}]\models\varphi.
\]

def:fol-satisfaction.title: Satisfaction
def:fol-satisfaction.statement_preview: Let \(\mathcal M=\langle D,I\rangle\) be a structure, let \(s\) be a variable assignment, and let \(\varphi\) be a well-formed formula. The relation \(\mathcal M,s\models\varphi\) is defined recursively by the usual clauses: atomic formulas are evaluated by term values and interpreted predicate relations; equality compares term values; connectives follow th...
def:fol-satisfaction.statement_display:
Let \(\mathcal M=\langle D,I\rangle\) be a structure, let \(s\) be a variable
assignment, and let \(\varphi\) be a well-formed formula. The relation
\(\mathcal M,s\models\varphi\) is defined recursively by the usual clauses:
atomic formulas are evaluated by term values and interpreted predicate
relations; equality compares term values; connectives follow the truth
conditions for \(\neg,\land,\lor,\to,\leftrightarrow\); and
\[
\mathcal M,s\models\forall x\,\psi
\Longleftrightarrow
\forall d\in D\;(\mathcal M,s[x\mapsto d]\models\psi),
\]
\[
\mathcal M,s\models\exists x\,\psi
\Longleftrightarrow
\exists d\in D\;(\mathcal M,s[x\mapsto d]\models\psi).
\]
def:fol-satisfaction.statement_tex:
Let \(\mathcal M=\langle D,I\rangle\) be a structure, let \(s\) be a variable
assignment, and let \(\varphi\) be a well-formed formula. The relation
\(\mathcal M,s\models\varphi\) is defined recursively by the usual clauses:
atomic formulas are evaluated by term values and interpreted predicate
relations; equality compares term values; connectives follow the truth
conditions for \(\neg,\land,\lor,\to,\leftrightarrow\); and
\[
\mathcal M,s\models\forall x\,\psi
\Longleftrightarrow
\forall d\in D\;(\mathcal M,s[x\mapsto d]\models\psi),
\]
\[
\mathcal M,s\models\exists x\,\psi
\Longleftrightarrow
\exists d\in D\;(\mathcal M,s[x\mapsto d]\models\psi).
\]

def:truth-structure.title: Truth in a Structure
def:truth-structure.statement_preview: Let \(\varphi\) be a sentence and let \(\mathcal M\) be a structure. The sentence \(\varphi\) is \emph{true in} \(\mathcal M\), written \(\mathcal M\models\varphi\), exactly when \(\mathcal M,s\models\varphi\) for every variable assignment \(s\) for \(\mathcal M\).
def:truth-structure.statement_display:
Let \(\varphi\) be a sentence and let \(\mathcal M\) be a structure. The
sentence \(\varphi\) is \emph{true in} \(\mathcal M\), written
\(\mathcal M\models\varphi\), exactly when
\(\mathcal M,s\models\varphi\) for every variable assignment \(s\) for
\(\mathcal M\).
def:truth-structure.statement_tex:
Let \(\varphi\) be a sentence and let \(\mathcal M\) be a structure. The

def:validity.title: Validity
def:validity.statement_preview: A formula \(\varphi\) is \emph{valid}, or \emph{logically true}, exactly when \(\mathcal M,s\models\varphi\) for every structure \(\mathcal M\) for the language of \(\varphi\) and every variable assignment \(s\) for \(\mathcal M\).
def:validity.statement_display:
A formula \(\varphi\) is \emph{valid}, or \emph{logically true}, exactly when
\(\mathcal M,s\models\varphi\) for every structure \(\mathcal M\) for the
language of \(\varphi\) and every variable assignment \(s\) for \(\mathcal M\).
def:validity.statement_tex:

language of \(\varphi\) and every variable assignment \(s\) for \(\mathcal M\).

def:fol-satisfiability.title: Satisfiability
def:fol-satisfiability.statement_preview: A formula \(\varphi\) is \emph{satisfiable} exactly when there are a structure \(\mathcal M\) and a variable assignment \(s\) such that \(\mathcal M,s\models\varphi\).
def:fol-satisfiability.statement_display:
A formula \(\varphi\) is \emph{satisfiable} exactly when there are a structure
\(\mathcal M\) and a variable assignment \(s\) such that
\(\mathcal M,s\models\varphi\).

def:fo-language.title: First-Order Language
def:fo-language.statement_preview: A \emph{first-order language} \(\mathcal L\) consists of variables, logical symbols, and a signature of non-logical symbols: constant symbols, function symbols with assigned arities, and predicate symbols with assigned arities. Equality may be included as a distinguished logical predicate.
def:fo-language.statement_display:
A \emph{first-order language} \(\mathcal L\) consists of variables, logical
symbols, and a signature of non-logical symbols: constant symbols, function
symbols with assigned arities, and predicate symbols with assigned arities.
Equality may be included as a distinguished logical predicate.
def:fo-language.statement_tex:

symbols, and a signature of non-logical symbols: constant symbols, function
symbols with assigned arities, and predicate symbols with assigned arities.
Equality may be included as a distinguished logical predicate.

def:structure.title: Structure
def:structure.statement_preview: Let \(\mathcal L\) be a first-order language. A \emph{structure} for \(\mathcal L\) is a pair \[ \mathcal M=\langle D,I\rangle \] where \(D\) is a nonempty set and \(I\) assigns each constant symbol an element of \(D\), each \(n\)-ary function symbol a function \(D^n\to D\), and each \(n\)-ary predicate symbol a relation on \(D^n\).
def:structure.statement_display:
Let \(\mathcal L\) be a first-order language. A \emph{structure} for
\(\mathcal L\) is a pair
\[
\mathcal M=\langle D,I\rangle
\]
where \(D\) is a nonempty set and \(I\) assigns each constant symbol an element
of \(D\), each \(n\)-ary function symbol a function \(D^n\to D\), and each
\(n\)-ary predicate symbol a relation on \(D^n\).
def:structure.statement_tex:
Let \(\mathcal L\) be a first-order language. A \emph{structure} for
\(\mathcal L\) is a pair
\[
\mathcal M=\langle D,I\rangle
\]
where \(D\) is a nonempty set and \(I\) assigns each constant symbol an element
of \(D\), each \(n\)-ary function symbol a function \(D^n\to D\), and each
\(n\)-ary predicate symbol a relation on \(D^n\).

def:var-assign.title: Variable Assignment
def:var-assign.statement_preview: Let \(\mathcal M=\langle D,I\rangle\) be a structure for \(\mathcal L\). A \emph{variable assignment} for \(\mathcal M\) is a function \[ s:\mathsf{Var}_{\mathcal L}\to D. \]
def:var-assign.statement_display:
Let \(\mathcal M=\langle D,I\rangle\) be a structure for \(\mathcal L\).
A \emph{variable assignment} for \(\mathcal M\) is a function
\[
s:\mathsf{Var}_{\mathcal L}\to D.
\]

A \emph{variable assignment} for \(\mathcal M\) is a function
\[
s:\mathsf{Var}_{\mathcal L}\to D.
\]

def:mod-assign.title: Modified Assignment
def:mod-assign.statement_preview: Let \(s:\mathsf{Var}_{\mathcal L}\to D\), let \(x\in\mathsf{Var}_{\mathcal L}\), and let \(d\in D\). The \emph{modified assignment} \(s[x\mapsto d]\) is the assignment defined by \[ s[x\mapsto d](y)= \begin{cases} d, & y=x,\\ s(y), & y\neq x. \end{cases} \]
def:mod-assign.statement_display:
Let \(s:\mathsf{Var}_{\mathcal L}\to D\), let \(x\in\mathsf{Var}_{\mathcal L}\),
and let \(d\in D\). The \emph{modified assignment} \(s[x\mapsto d]\) is the
assignment defined by
\[
s[x\mapsto d](y)=
\begin{cases}
d, & y=x,\\
s(y), & y\neq x.
\end{cases}
\]
def:mod-assign.statement_tex:
Let \(s:\mathsf{Var}_{\mathcal L}\to D\), let \(x\in\mathsf{Var}_{\mathcal L}\),

assignment defined by
\[
s[x\mapsto d](y)=
\begin{cases}

s(y), & y\neq x.
\end{cases}
\]

def:term-interp.title: Term Evaluation
def:term-interp.statement_preview: Let \(\mathcal M=\langle D,I\rangle\) be a structure and \(s\) a variable assignment. The \emph{value} of a term \(t\), denoted \(\llbracket t\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}\), is defined recursively by \[ \llbracket x\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}=s(x),\qquad \llbracket c\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}=I(c), \] and \[ \llbracket f(t_1,\ldots,t_n)\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s} =...
def:term-interp.statement_display:
Let \(\mathcal M=\langle D,I\rangle\) be a structure and \(s\) a variable
assignment. The \emph{value} of a term \(t\), denoted
\(\llbracket t\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}\), is defined recursively by
\[
\llbracket x\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}=s(x),\qquad
\llbracket c\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}=I(c),
\]
and
\[
\llbracket f(t_1,\ldots,t_n)\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}
=
I(f)\bigl(\llbracket t_1\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s},\ldots,
\llbracket t_n\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}\bigr).
\]
def:term-interp.statement_tex:
Let \(\mathcal M=\langle D,I\rangle\) be a structure and \(s\) a variable
assignment. The \emph{value} of a term \(t\), denoted
\(\llbracket t\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}\), is defined recursively by
\[
\llbracket x\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}=s(x),\qquad
\llbracket c\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}=I(c),
\]
and
\[
\llbracket f(t_1,\ldots,t_n)\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}
=
I(f)\bigl(\llbracket t_1\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s},\ldots,
\llbracket t_n\rrbracket_{\mathcal M,s}\bigr).
\]

def:formula-depth.title: Formula Depth
def:formula-depth.statement_preview: The \emph{depth} (or \emph{complexity}) of a formula $\varphi$, denoted $\mathrm{depth}(\varphi)$, is a natural number defined recursively: \begin{enumerate} \item \textbf{Atomic case.} If $\varphi$ is atomic, then $\mathrm{depth}(\varphi) = 0$. \item \textbf{Negation.} If $\varphi = \neg \psi$, then $\mathrm{depth}(\varphi) = \mathrm{depth}(\psi) + 1$. \it...
def:formula-depth.statement_display:
The \emph{depth} (or \emph{complexity}) of a formula $\varphi$, denoted
$\mathrm{depth}(\varphi)$, is a natural number defined recursively:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Atomic case.} If $\varphi$ is atomic, then $\mathrm{depth}(\varphi) = 0$.

  \item \textbf{Negation.} If $\varphi = \neg \psi$, then
  $\mathrm{depth}(\varphi) = \mathrm{depth}(\psi) + 1$.

  \item \textbf{Binary connectives.} If $\varphi = (\psi \circ \chi)$
  for $\circ \in \{\wedge,\vee,\rightarrow,\leftrightarrow\}$, then
  \[
  \mathrm{depth}(\varphi) = \max\{\mathrm{depth}(\psi),\,\mathrm{depth}(\chi)\} + 1.
  \]

  \item \textbf{Quantifiers.} If $\varphi = \forall x\,\psi$ or $\varphi = \exists x\,\psi$,
  then $\mathrm{depth}(\varphi) = \mathrm{depth}(\psi) + 1$.
\end{enumerate}
def:formula-depth.statement_tex:
The \emph{depth} (or \emph{complexity}) of a formula $\varphi$, denoted
$\mathrm{depth}(\varphi)$, is a natural number defined recursively:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Atomic case.} If $\varphi$ is atomic, then $\mathrm{depth}(\varphi) = 0$.

  \item \textbf{Negation.} If $\varphi = \neg \psi$, then
  $\mathrm{depth}(\varphi) = \mathrm{depth}(\psi) + 1$.

  \item \textbf{Binary connectives.} If $\varphi = (\psi \circ \chi)$
  for $\circ \in \{\wedge,\vee,\rightarrow,\leftrightarrow\}$, then
  \[
  \mathrm{depth}(\varphi) = \max\{\mathrm{depth}(\psi),\,\mathrm{depth}(\chi)\} + 1.
  \]

  \item \textbf{Quantifiers.} If $\varphi = \forall x\,\psi$ or $\varphi = \exists x\,\psi$,
  then $\mathrm{depth}(\varphi) = \mathrm{depth}(\psi) + 1$.
\end{enumerate}

def:variable.title: Variable
def:variable.statement_preview: For a first-order language \(\mathcal L\), a \emph{variable} is a symbol drawn from the distinguished variable stock of \(\mathcal L\). Variables are usually written \[ x,y,z,x_0,x_1,x_2,\ldots . \]
def:variable.statement_display:

written
\[
x,y,z,x_0,x_1,x_2,\ldots .
\]

def:constant-symbol.title: Constant Symbol
def:constant-symbol.statement_preview: For a first-order language \(\mathcal L\), a \emph{constant symbol} is a non-logical symbol of arity \(0\). A constant symbol may occur as a term without taking input terms.
def:constant-symbol.statement_display:
For a first-order language \(\mathcal L\), a \emph{constant symbol} is a
non-logical symbol of arity \(0\). A constant symbol may occur as a term without
taking input terms.
def:constant-symbol.statement_tex:

non-logical symbol of arity \(0\). A constant symbol may occur as a term without
taking input terms.

def:function-symbol.title: Function Symbol
def:function-symbol.statement_preview: For a first-order language \(\mathcal L\), an \(n\)-ary \emph{function symbol} is a non-logical symbol of arity \(n\geq 1\). If \(f\) has arity \(n\), then it forms terms by the pattern \[ f(t_1,\ldots,t_n). \]

forms terms by the pattern
\[
f(t_1,\ldots,t_n).
\]

def:predicate-symbol.title: Predicate Symbol
def:predicate-symbol.statement_preview: For a first-order language \(\mathcal L\), an \(n\)-ary \emph{predicate symbol} is a non-logical symbol of arity \(n\geq 1\). If \(P\) has arity \(n\), then it forms atomic formulas by the pattern \[ P(t_1,\ldots,t_n). \]

For a first-order language \(\mathcal L\), an \(n\)-ary \emph{predicate symbol}

forms atomic formulas by the pattern
\[
P(t_1,\ldots,t_n).
\]

def:term.title: Term
def:term.statement_preview: The set of \emph{terms} of a first-order language \(\mathcal L\), denoted \(\mathsf{Term}_{\mathcal L}\), is the smallest set of strings satisfying: \begin{enumerate} \item every variable is a term; \item every constant symbol is a term; \item if \(f\) is an \(n\)-ary function symbol and \(t_1,\ldots,t_n\) are terms, then \(f(t_1,\ldots,t_n)\) is a term; \i...

def:atomic-formula.title: Atomic Formula
def:atomic-formula.statement_preview: An \emph{atomic formula} of a first-order language \(\mathcal L\) is a string of the form \[ P(t_1,\ldots,t_n), \] where \(P\) is an \(n\)-ary predicate symbol and \(t_1,\ldots,t_n\) are terms.
def:atomic-formula.statement_display:

P(t_1,\ldots,t_n),
\]
where \(P\) is an \(n\)-ary predicate symbol and
\(t_1,\ldots,t_n\) are terms.

def:formula.title: Formula
def:formula.statement_preview: For a first-order language \(\mathcal L\), a \emph{formula} is an expression formed by the formula grammar of \(\mathcal L\). The grammar starts from atomic formulas and closes under the logical connectives and quantifiers of the language.
def:formula.statement_display:

formed by the formula grammar of \(\mathcal L\). The grammar starts from atomic
formulas and closes under the logical connectives and quantifiers of the
language.
