def:literal-propositional-logic.title: Literal
def:literal-propositional-logic.statement_preview: A \emph{literal} is either a propositional variable \(P\) or the negation \(\neg P\) of a propositional variable \(P\).
def:literal-propositional-logic.statement_display:
A \emph{literal} is either a propositional variable \(P\) or the negation
\(\neg P\) of a propositional variable \(P\).
def:literal-propositional-logic.statement_tex:
A \emph{literal} is either a propositional variable \(P\) or the negation
\(\neg P\) of a propositional variable \(P\).

def:positive-literal-propositional-logic.title: Positive Literal
def:positive-literal-propositional-logic.statement_preview: A \emph{positive literal} is a literal of the form \(P\), where \(P\) is a propositional variable.
def:positive-literal-propositional-logic.statement_display:
A \emph{positive literal} is a literal of the form \(P\), where \(P\) is a
propositional variable.
def:positive-literal-propositional-logic.statement_tex:
A \emph{positive literal} is a literal of the form \(P\), where \(P\) is a
propositional variable.

def:negative-literal-propositional-logic.title: Negative Literal
def:negative-literal-propositional-logic.statement_preview: A \emph{negative literal} is a literal of the form \(\neg P\), where \(P\) is a propositional variable.
def:negative-literal-propositional-logic.statement_display:
A \emph{negative literal} is a literal of the form \(\neg P\), where \(P\) is a
propositional variable.
def:negative-literal-propositional-logic.statement_tex:
A \emph{negative literal} is a literal of the form \(\neg P\), where \(P\) is a
propositional variable.

def:complementary-literals-propositional.title: Complementary Literals
def:complementary-literals-propositional.statement_preview: Two literals are \emph{complementary} if they are \(P\) and \(\neg P\) for the same propositional variable \(P\).
def:complementary-literals-propositional.statement_display:
Two literals are \emph{complementary} if they are \(P\) and \(\neg P\) for the
same propositional variable \(P\).
def:complementary-literals-propositional.statement_tex:
Two literals are \emph{complementary} if they are \(P\) and \(\neg P\) for the
same propositional variable \(P\).

def:clause-propositional-logic.title: Clause
def:clause-propositional-logic.statement_preview: A \emph{clause} is a finite disjunction of literals.
def:clause-propositional-logic.statement_display: A \emph{clause} is a finite disjunction of literals.
def:clause-propositional-logic.statement_tex: A \emph{clause} is a finite disjunction of literals.

def:term-propositional-logic.title: Term
def:term-propositional-logic.statement_preview: A \emph{term}, also called a \emph{conjunction term}, is a finite conjunction of literals.
def:term-propositional-logic.statement_display:
A \emph{term}, also called a \emph{conjunction term}, is a finite conjunction
of literals.
def:term-propositional-logic.statement_tex:
A \emph{term}, also called a \emph{conjunction term}, is a finite conjunction
of literals.

def:empty-clause-propositional-logic.title: Empty Clause
def:empty-clause-propositional-logic.statement_preview: The \emph{empty clause} is the empty disjunction of literals. Semantically, it is read as false.
def:empty-clause-propositional-logic.statement_display:
The \emph{empty clause} is the empty disjunction of literals. Semantically, it
is read as false.
def:empty-clause-propositional-logic.statement_tex:
The \emph{empty clause} is the empty disjunction of literals. Semantically, it
is read as false.

def:empty-conjunction-propositional-logi.title: Empty Conjunction
def:empty-conjunction-propositional-logi.statement_preview: The \emph{empty conjunction} is the conjunction over no literals. Semantically, it is read as true.
def:empty-conjunction-propositional-logi.statement_display:
The \emph{empty conjunction} is the conjunction over no literals. Semantically,
it is read as true.
def:empty-conjunction-propositional-logi.statement_tex:
The \emph{empty conjunction} is the conjunction over no literals. Semantically,
it is read as true.

def:negation-normal-form-propositional-l.title: Negation Normal Form
def:negation-normal-form-propositional-l.statement_preview: A formula is in \emph{negation normal form} if it uses only propositional variables, negated propositional variables, conjunction, and disjunction, and every negation occurs immediately before a propositional variable.
def:negation-normal-form-propositional-l.statement_display:
A formula is in \emph{negation normal form} if it uses only propositional
variables, negated propositional variables, conjunction, and disjunction, and
every negation occurs immediately before a propositional variable.
def:negation-normal-form-propositional-l.statement_tex:
A formula is in \emph{negation normal form} if it uses only propositional
variables, negated propositional variables, conjunction, and disjunction, and
every negation occurs immediately before a propositional variable.

def:nnf-conversion-procedure-proposition.title: NNF Conversion Procedure
def:nnf-conversion-procedure-proposition.statement_preview: The \emph{NNF conversion procedure} converts a propositional formula to an equivalent formula in negation normal form by the following steps: \begin{enumerate} \item eliminate biconditionals using biconditional equivalence laws; \item eliminate conditionals using conditional equivalence laws; \item eliminate double negations; \item push negations inward usi...

thm:existence-negation-normal-form-propo.title: Existence of Negation Normal Form
thm:existence-negation-normal-form-propo.statement_preview: For every formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\), there exists a formula \(\psi\in\WFF\) such that \(\psi\) is in negation normal form and \[ \varphi\equiv\psi. \]
thm:existence-negation-normal-form-propo.statement_display:
For every formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\), there exists a formula \(\psi\in\WFF\)
such that \(\psi\) is in negation normal form and
\[
\varphi\equiv\psi.
\]
thm:existence-negation-normal-form-propo.statement_tex:
For every formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\), there exists a formula \(\psi\in\WFF\)
such that \(\psi\) is in negation normal form and
\[
\varphi\equiv\psi.
\]

thm:normal-form-semantic-classification-.title: Semantic Classification by Normal Forms
thm:normal-form-semantic-classification-.statement_preview: Normal forms give finite certificates for semantic classification. For any formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\): \begin{enumerate} \item \(\varphi\) is \hyperref[def:satisfiable-formula-propositional-logic]{satisfiable} if and only if some \hyperref[def:disjunctive-normal-form-propositional-logic]{DNF} formula equivalent to \(\varphi\) has a satisfiable conjunction t...
thm:normal-form-semantic-classification-.statement_display:
Normal forms give finite certificates for semantic classification. For any
formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\):
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \(\varphi\) is
  \hyperref[def:satisfiable-formula-propositional-logic]{satisfiable} if and
  only if some
  \hyperref[def:disjunctive-normal-form-propositional-logic]{DNF} formula
  equivalent to \(\varphi\) has a satisfiable conjunction term.
  \item \(\varphi\) is a
  \hyperref[def:contradiction-propositional-logic]{contradiction} if and only
  if some DNF formula equivalent to \(\varphi\) has no satisfiable conjunction
  term.
  \item \(\varphi\) is a
  \hyperref[def:tautology-propositional-logic]{tautology} if and only if some
  \hyperref[def:conjunctive-normal-form-propositional-logic]{CNF} formula
  equivalent to \(\varphi\) has no falsifiable clause.
  \item \(\varphi\) is a
  \hyperref[def:contingency-propositional-logic]{contingency} if and only if it
  is satisfiable and not a tautology.
\end{enumerate}
thm:normal-form-semantic-classification-.statement_tex:
Normal forms give finite certificates for semantic classification. For any
formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\):
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \(\varphi\) is
  \hyperref[def:satisfiable-formula-propositional-logic]{satisfiable} if and
  only if some
  \hyperref[def:disjunctive-normal-form-propositional-logic]{DNF} formula
  equivalent to \(\varphi\) has a satisfiable conjunction term.
  \item \(\varphi\) is a
  \hyperref[def:contradiction-propositional-logic]{contradiction} if and only
  if some DNF formula equivalent to \(\varphi\) has no satisfiable conjunction
  term.
  \item \(\varphi\) is a
  \hyperref[def:tautology-propositional-logic]{tautology} if and only if some
  \hyperref[def:conjunctive-normal-form-propositional-logic]{CNF} formula
  equivalent to \(\varphi\) has no falsifiable clause.
  \item \(\varphi\) is a
  \hyperref[def:contingency-propositional-logic]{contingency} if and only if it
  is satisfiable and not a tautology.
\end{enumerate}

def:truth-assignment-propositional-logic.title: Truth Assignment
def:truth-assignment-propositional-logic.statement_preview: A \emph{truth assignment} is a function \[ v:\Prop\to\mathbb{B}. \] For each propositional variable \(P\in\Prop\), the value \(v(P)\) is the truth value assigned to \(P\).
def:truth-assignment-propositional-logic.statement_display:
A \emph{truth assignment} is a function
\[
v:\Prop\to\mathbb{B}.
\]
For each propositional variable \(P\in\Prop\), the value \(v(P)\) is the truth
value assigned to \(P\).
def:truth-assignment-propositional-logic.statement_tex:
A \emph{truth assignment} is a function
\[
v:\Prop\to\mathbb{B}.
\]
For each propositional variable \(P\in\Prop\), the value \(v(P)\) is the truth
value assigned to \(P\).

def:truth-assignment-domain-propositiona.title: Domain of a Truth Assignment
def:truth-assignment-domain-propositiona.statement_preview: The \emph{domain} of a truth assignment \[ v:\Prop\to\mathbb{B} \] is the set \(\Prop\) of propositional variables.
def:truth-assignment-domain-propositiona.statement_display:
The \emph{domain} of a truth assignment
\[
v:\Prop\to\mathbb{B}
\]
is the set \(\Prop\) of propositional variables.
def:truth-assignment-domain-propositiona.statement_tex:
The \emph{domain} of a truth assignment
\[
v:\Prop\to\mathbb{B}
\]
is the set \(\Prop\) of propositional variables.

def:extended-truth-assignment-propositio.title: Extension of a Truth Assignment to Formulas
def:extended-truth-assignment-propositio.statement_preview: Let \[ v:\Prop\to\mathbb{B} \] be a truth assignment. The \emph{extension} of \(v\) to well-formed formulas is the function \[ \widehat v:\WFF\to\mathbb{B} \] defined recursively as follows: \[ \widehat v(P)=v(P) \quad\text{for every }P\in\Prop, \] \[ \widehat v(\neg\varphi)=\True \Longleftrightarrow \widehat v(\varphi)=\False, \] \[ \widehat v(\varphi\land...
def:extended-truth-assignment-propositio.statement_display:
Let
\[
v:\Prop\to\mathbb{B}
\]
be a truth assignment. The \emph{extension} of \(v\) to well-formed formulas is
the function
\[
\widehat v:\WFF\to\mathbb{B}
\]
defined recursively as follows:
\[
\widehat v(P)=v(P)
\quad\text{for every }P\in\Prop,
\]
\[
\widehat v(\neg\varphi)=\True
\Longleftrightarrow
\widehat v(\varphi)=\False,
\]
\[
\widehat v(\varphi\land\psi)=\True
\Longleftrightarrow
\widehat v(\varphi)=\True
\text{ and }
\widehat v(\psi)=\True,
\]
\[
\widehat v(\varphi\lor\psi)=\True
\Longleftrightarrow
\widehat v(\varphi)=\True
\text{ or }
\widehat v(\psi)=\True,
\]
\[
\widehat v(\varphi\to\psi)=\False
\Longleftrightarrow
\widehat v(\varphi)=\True
\text{ and }
\widehat v(\psi)=\False,
\]
and
\[
\widehat v(\varphi\leftrightarrow\psi)=\True
\Longleftrightarrow
\widehat v(\varphi)=\widehat v(\psi).
\]
def:extended-truth-assignment-propositio.statement_tex:
Let
\[
v:\Prop\to\mathbb{B}
\]
be a truth assignment. The \emph{extension} of \(v\) to well-formed formulas is
the function
\[
\widehat v:\WFF\to\mathbb{B}
\]
defined recursively as follows:
\[
\widehat v(P)=v(P)
\quad\text{for every }P\in\Prop,
\]
\[
\widehat v(\neg\varphi)=\True
\Longleftrightarrow
\widehat v(\varphi)=\False,
\]
\[
\widehat v(\varphi\land\psi)=\True
\Longleftrightarrow
\widehat v(\varphi)=\True
\text{ and }
\widehat v(\psi)=\True,
\]
\[
\widehat v(\varphi\lor\psi)=\True
\Longleftrightarrow
\widehat v(\varphi)=\True
\text{ or }
\widehat v(\psi)=\True,
\]
\[
\widehat v(\varphi\to\psi)=\False
\Longleftrightarrow
\widehat v(\varphi)=\True
\text{ and }
\widehat v(\psi)=\False,
\]
and
\[
\widehat v(\varphi\leftrightarrow\psi)=\True
\Longleftrightarrow
\widehat v(\varphi)=\widehat v(\psi).
\]

thm:unique-extension-truth-assignment-pr.title: Unique Extension of a Truth Assignment
thm:unique-extension-truth-assignment-pr.statement_preview: Every truth assignment \[ v:\Prop\to\mathbb{B} \] has a unique extension \[ \widehat v:\WFF\to\mathbb{B} \] satisfying the recursive truth clauses for propositional variables and the primitive connectives.
thm:unique-extension-truth-assignment-pr.statement_display:
Every truth assignment
\[
v:\Prop\to\mathbb{B}
\]
has a unique extension
\[
\widehat v:\WFF\to\mathbb{B}
\]
satisfying the recursive truth clauses for propositional variables and the
primitive connectives.
thm:unique-extension-truth-assignment-pr.statement_tex:
Every truth assignment
\[
v:\Prop\to\mathbb{B}
\]
has a unique extension
\[
\widehat v:\WFF\to\mathbb{B}
\]
satisfying the recursive truth clauses for propositional variables and the
primitive connectives.

def:satisfaction-propositional-formula.title: Satisfaction of a Formula
def:satisfaction-propositional-formula.statement_preview: Let \(v\) be a truth assignment and let \(\varphi\in\WFF\). We say that \(v\) satisfies \(\varphi\), written \[ v\models\varphi, \] if \[ \widehat v(\varphi)=\mathrm{True}. \]
def:satisfaction-propositional-formula.statement_display:
Let \(v\) be a truth assignment and let \(\varphi\in\WFF\). We say that \(v\) satisfies \(\varphi\), written
\[
v\models\varphi,
\]
if
\[
\widehat v(\varphi)=\mathrm{True}.
\]
def:satisfaction-propositional-formula.statement_tex:
Let \(v\) be a truth assignment and let \(\varphi\in\WFF\). We say that \(v\) satisfies \(\varphi\), written
\[
v\models\varphi,
\]
if
\[
\widehat v(\varphi)=\mathrm{True}.
\]

def:satisfaction-set-propositional-logic.title: Satisfaction of a Set of Formulas
def:satisfaction-set-propositional-logic.statement_preview: Let \(\Gamma\subseteq\WFF\). A truth assignment \(v\) satisfies \(\Gamma\), written \[ v\models\Gamma, \] if \(v\models\gamma\) for every \(\gamma\in\Gamma\).
def:satisfaction-set-propositional-logic.statement_display:
Let \(\Gamma\subseteq\WFF\). A truth assignment \(v\) satisfies \(\Gamma\), written
\[
v\models\Gamma,
\]
if \(v\models\gamma\) for every \(\gamma\in\Gamma\).
def:satisfaction-set-propositional-logic.statement_tex:
Let \(\Gamma\subseteq\WFF\). A truth assignment \(v\) satisfies \(\Gamma\), written
\[
v\models\Gamma,
\]
if \(v\models\gamma\) for every \(\gamma\in\Gamma\).

def:truth-table-propositional-logic.title: Truth Table
def:truth-table-propositional-logic.statement_preview: Let \(\varphi\in\WFF\) and suppose the variables occurring in \(\varphi\) are among \(P_1,\ldots,P_n\). A truth table for \(\varphi\) is a finite table with one row for each assignment \[ a:\{P_1,\ldots,P_n\}\to\mathbb{B} \] and a final column recording the value of \(\widehat v(\varphi)\) for any truth assignment \(v\) extending \(a\).
def:truth-table-propositional-logic.statement_display:
Let \(\varphi\in\WFF\) and suppose the variables occurring in \(\varphi\) are among \(P_1,\ldots,P_n\). A truth table for \(\varphi\) is a finite table with one row for each assignment
\[
a:\{P_1,\ldots,P_n\}\to\mathbb{B}
\]
and a final column recording the value of \(\widehat v(\varphi)\) for any truth assignment \(v\) extending \(a\).
def:truth-table-propositional-logic.statement_tex:
Let \(\varphi\in\WFF\) and suppose the variables occurring in \(\varphi\) are among \(P_1,\ldots,P_n\). A truth table for \(\varphi\) is a finite table with one row for each assignment
\[
a:\{P_1,\ldots,P_n\}\to\mathbb{B}
\]
and a final column recording the value of \(\widehat v(\varphi)\) for any truth assignment \(v\) extending \(a\).

def:tautology-propositional-logic.title: Tautology
def:tautology-propositional-logic.statement_preview: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is a tautology, written \[ \models\varphi, \] if every truth assignment satisfies \(\varphi\).
def:tautology-propositional-logic.statement_display:
A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is a tautology, written
\[
\models\varphi,
\]
if every truth assignment satisfies \(\varphi\).
def:tautology-propositional-logic.statement_tex:
A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is a tautology, written
\[
\models\varphi,
\]
if every truth assignment satisfies \(\varphi\).

def:contradiction-propositional-logic.title: Contradiction
def:contradiction-propositional-logic.statement_preview: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is a contradiction if no truth assignment satisfies \(\varphi\).
def:contradiction-propositional-logic.statement_display: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is a contradiction if no truth assignment satisfies \(\varphi\).
def:contradiction-propositional-logic.statement_tex: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is a contradiction if no truth assignment satisfies \(\varphi\).

def:satisfiable-formula-propositional-lo.title: Satisfiable Formula
def:satisfiable-formula-propositional-lo.statement_preview: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is satisfiable if at least one truth assignment satisfies it.
def:satisfiable-formula-propositional-lo.statement_display: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is satisfiable if at least one truth assignment satisfies it.
def:satisfiable-formula-propositional-lo.statement_tex: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is satisfiable if at least one truth assignment satisfies it.

def:contingency-propositional-logic.title: Contingency
def:contingency-propositional-logic.statement_preview: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is a contingency if it is satisfiable but not a tautology.
def:contingency-propositional-logic.statement_display: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is a contingency if it is satisfiable but not a tautology.
def:contingency-propositional-logic.statement_tex: A formula \(\varphi\in\WFF\) is a contingency if it is satisfiable but not a tautology.

def:logical-consequence-propositional-lo.title: Logical Consequence
def:logical-consequence-propositional-lo.statement_preview: Let \(\Gamma\subseteq\WFF\) and \(\varphi\in\WFF\). The formula \(\varphi\) is a logical consequence of \(\Gamma\), written \[ \Gamma\models\varphi, \] if every truth assignment satisfying \(\Gamma\) also satisfies \(\varphi\).
def:logical-consequence-propositional-lo.statement_display:
Let \(\Gamma\subseteq\WFF\) and \(\varphi\in\WFF\). The formula \(\varphi\) is a logical consequence of \(\Gamma\), written
\[
\Gamma\models\varphi,
\]
if every truth assignment satisfying \(\Gamma\) also satisfies \(\varphi\).
def:logical-consequence-propositional-lo.statement_tex:
Let \(\Gamma\subseteq\WFF\) and \(\varphi\in\WFF\). The formula \(\varphi\) is a logical consequence of \(\Gamma\), written
\[
\Gamma\models\varphi,
\]
if every truth assignment satisfying \(\Gamma\) also satisfies \(\varphi\).

def:logical-equivalence-propositional-lo.title: Logical Equivalence
def:logical-equivalence-propositional-lo.statement_preview: Formulas \(\varphi,\psi\in\WFF\) are logically equivalent, written \[ \varphi\equiv\psi, \] if they have the same truth value under every truth assignment.
def:logical-equivalence-propositional-lo.statement_display:
Formulas \(\varphi,\psi\in\WFF\) are logically equivalent, written
\[
\varphi\equiv\psi,
\]
if they have the same truth value under every truth assignment.
def:logical-equivalence-propositional-lo.statement_tex:
Formulas \(\varphi,\psi\in\WFF\) are logically equivalent, written
\[
\varphi\equiv\psi,
\]
if they have the same truth value under every truth assignment.

lem:coincidence-lemma-propositional-logi.title: Coincidence Lemma for Propositional Logic
lem:coincidence-lemma-propositional-logi.statement_preview: Let \(v,w:\Prop\to\mathbb{B}\) be truth assignments. If \(v\) and \(w\) agree on every propositional variable occurring in \(\varphi\), then they assign the same truth value to \(\varphi\): \[ v|_{\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)} = w|_{\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)} \Longrightarrow \widehat v(\varphi)=\widehat w(\varphi). \]
lem:coincidence-lemma-propositional-logi.statement_display:
Let \(v,w:\Prop\to\mathbb{B}\) be truth assignments. If \(v\) and \(w\) agree on every propositional variable occurring in \(\varphi\), then they assign the same truth value to \(\varphi\):
\[
v|_{\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)}
=
w|_{\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)}
\Longrightarrow
\widehat v(\varphi)=\widehat w(\varphi).
\]
lem:coincidence-lemma-propositional-logi.statement_tex:
Let \(v,w:\Prop\to\mathbb{B}\) be truth assignments. If \(v\) and \(w\) agree on every propositional variable occurring in \(\varphi\), then they assign the same truth value to \(\varphi\):
\[
v|_{\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)}
=
w|_{\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)}
\Longrightarrow
\widehat v(\varphi)=\widehat w(\varphi).
\]

thm:finite-truth-table-propositional-log.title: Finite Truth-Table Theorem
thm:finite-truth-table-propositional-log.statement_preview: Every propositional formula has a finite truth table. More precisely, if \(\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)\) has \(n\) elements, then \(\varphi\) has a truth table with \(2^n\) rows.
thm:finite-truth-table-propositional-log.statement_display: Every propositional formula has a finite truth table. More precisely, if \(\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)\) has \(n\) elements, then \(\varphi\) has a truth table with \(2^n\) rows.
thm:finite-truth-table-propositional-log.statement_tex: Every propositional formula has a finite truth table. More precisely, if \(\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)\) has \(n\) elements, then \(\varphi\) has a truth table with \(2^n\) rows.

def:boolean-function-propositional-logic.title: Boolean Function
def:boolean-function-propositional-logic.statement_preview: An \(n\)-ary Boolean function is a function \[ f:\mathbb{B}^n\to\mathbb{B}. \]
def:boolean-function-propositional-logic.statement_display:
An \(n\)-ary Boolean function is a function
\[
f:\mathbb{B}^n\to\mathbb{B}.
\]
def:boolean-function-propositional-logic.statement_tex:
An \(n\)-ary Boolean function is a function
\[
f:\mathbb{B}^n\to\mathbb{B}.
\]

def:truth-function-induced-by-formula.title: Truth Function Induced by a Formula
def:truth-function-induced-by-formula.statement_preview: Let \(\varphi\in\WFF\), and suppose \(\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)\subseteq\{P_1,\ldots,P_n\}\). The truth function induced by \(\varphi\) is the Boolean function \[ f_\varphi:\mathbb{B}^n\to\mathbb{B} \] defined by \[ f_\varphi(b_1,\ldots,b_n)=\widehat v(\varphi), \] where \(v(P_i)=b_i\) for each \(i\).
def:truth-function-induced-by-formula.statement_display:

\]
defined by
\[
f_\varphi(b_1,\ldots,b_n)=\widehat v(\varphi),
\]
where \(v(P_i)=b_i\) for each \(i\).

def:truth-value-propositional-logic.title: Truth Value
def:truth-value-propositional-logic.statement_preview: The set of truth values is \[ \mathbb{B} := \{\mathrm{False},\mathrm{True}\}. \] Some sources identify this set with \(\{0,1\}\), with \(0\) read as false and \(1\) read as true.
def:truth-value-propositional-logic.statement_display:
The set of truth values is
\[
\mathbb{B} := \{\mathrm{False},\mathrm{True}\}.
\]
Some sources identify this set with \(\{0,1\}\), with \(0\) read as false and \(1\) read as true.
def:truth-value-propositional-logic.statement_tex:
The set of truth values is
\[
\mathbb{B} := \{\mathrm{False},\mathrm{True}\}.
\]

def:propositional-language.title: Propositional Language
def:propositional-language.statement_preview: A propositional language consists of: \begin{enumerate} \item a set \(\Prop\) of propositional variables; \item logical connectives; \item punctuation symbols such as parentheses; \item recursive formation rules specifying the well-formed formulas. \end{enumerate}

def:propositional-variable.title: Propositional Variable
def:propositional-variable.statement_preview: A propositional variable is an atomic symbol intended to stand for a proposition. A standard supply of variables is \[ \Prop=\{P_1,P_2,P_3,\ldots\}. \] Informally, variables are often written \(P,Q,R,S,\ldots\).
def:propositional-variable.statement_display:
A propositional variable is an atomic symbol intended to stand for a proposition. A standard supply of variables is
\[
\Prop=\{P_1,P_2,P_3,\ldots\}.
\]
Informally, variables are often written \(P,Q,R,S,\ldots\).
def:propositional-variable.statement_tex:
A propositional variable is an atomic symbol intended to stand for a proposition. A standard supply of variables is
\[
\Prop=\{P_1,P_2,P_3,\ldots\}.
\]
Informally, variables are often written \(P,Q,R,S,\ldots\).

def:logical-connective.title: Logical Connective
def:logical-connective.statement_preview:
The standard primitive propositional connectives are \[ 
eg,\qquad \land,\qquad \lor,\qquad 	o,\qquad \leftrightarrow. \] The connective \(
eg\) is unary. The connectives \(\land,\lor,	o,\leftrightarrow\) are binary. \[  egin{array}{c|c|c|c|c} 	ext{Symbol} & 	ext{Name} & 	ext{Arity} & 	ext{Formation pattern} & 	ext{Reading}\ \hline 
eg & 	ext{n...
def:logical-connective.statement_display:
The standard primitive propositional connectives are
\[
\neg,\qquad \land,\qquad \lor,\qquad \to,\qquad \leftrightarrow.
\]
The connective \(\neg\) is unary. The connectives \(\land,\lor,\to,\leftrightarrow\) are binary.

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c}
\text{Symbol} & \text{Name} & \text{Arity} & \text{Formation pattern} & \text{Reading}\\
\hline
\neg & \text{negation} & \text{unary} & \neg\varphi & \text{not }\varphi\\
\land & \text{conjunction} & \text{binary} & (\varphi\land\psi) & \varphi\text{ and }\psi\\
\lor & \text{disjunction} & \text{binary} & (\varphi\lor\psi) & \varphi\text{ or }\psi\\
\to & \text{conditional} & \text{binary} & (\varphi\to\psi) & \text{if }\varphi,\text{ then }\psi\\
\leftrightarrow & \text{biconditional} & \text{binary} & (\varphi\leftrightarrow\psi) & \varphi\text{ iff }\psi
\end{array}
\]
def:logical-connective.statement_tex:
The standard primitive propositional connectives are
\[
\neg,\qquad \land,\qquad \lor,\qquad \to,\qquad \leftrightarrow.
\]
The connective \(\neg\) is unary. The connectives \(\land,\lor,\to,\leftrightarrow\) are binary.

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c}
\text{Symbol} & \text{Name} & \text{Arity} & \text{Formation pattern} & \text{Reading}\\
\hline
\neg & \text{negation} & \text{unary} & \neg\varphi & \text{not }\varphi\\
\land & \text{conjunction} & \text{binary} & (\varphi\land\psi) & \varphi\text{ and }\psi\\
\lor & \text{disjunction} & \text{binary} & (\varphi\lor\psi) & \varphi\text{ or }\psi\\
\to & \text{conditional} & \text{binary} & (\varphi\to\psi) & \text{if }\varphi,\text{ then }\psi\\
\leftrightarrow & \text{biconditional} & \text{binary} & (\varphi\leftrightarrow\psi) & \varphi\text{ iff }\psi
\end{array}
\]

def:well-formed-formula.title: Well-Formed Formula
def:well-formed-formula.statement_preview: The set \(\WFF\) of well-formed formulas is the smallest set of strings satisfying the following formation rules: \begin{enumerate} \item Every propositional variable \(P\in\Prop\) belongs to \(\WFF\). \item If \(\varphi\in\WFF\), then \(\neg\varphi\in\WFF\). \item If \(\varphi,\psi\in\WFF\) and \(\circ\in\{\land,\lor,\to,\leftrightarrow\}\), then \((\varph...
def:well-formed-formula.statement_display:
The set \(\WFF\) of well-formed formulas is the smallest set of strings satisfying the following formation rules:
\begin{enumerate}
\item Every propositional variable \(P\in\Prop\) belongs to \(\WFF\).
\item If \(\varphi\in\WFF\), then \(\neg\varphi\in\WFF\).
\item If \(\varphi,\psi\in\WFF\) and \(\circ\in\{\land,\lor,\to,\leftrightarrow\}\), then \((\varphi\circ\psi)\in\WFF\).
\item No string is a well-formed formula unless it is obtained by finitely many applications of the previous clauses.
\end{enumerate}
def:well-formed-formula.statement_tex:
The set \(\WFF\) of well-formed formulas is the smallest set of strings satisfying the following formation rules:
\begin{enumerate}
\item Every propositional variable \(P\in\Prop\) belongs to \(\WFF\).
\item If \(\varphi\in\WFF\), then \(\neg\varphi\in\WFF\).
\item If \(\varphi,\psi\in\WFF\) and \(\circ\in\{\land,\lor,\to,\leftrightarrow\}\), then \((\varphi\circ\psi)\in\WFF\).
\item No string is a well-formed formula unless it is obtained by finitely many applications of the previous clauses.
\end{enumerate}

lem:closure-under-formation-propositiona.title: Closure Under Formation
lem:closure-under-formation-propositiona.statement_preview: The set \(\WFF\) is closed under the propositional formation rules. That is, if \(\varphi\in\WFF\), then \[ \neg\varphi\in\WFF. \] If \(\varphi,\psi\in\WFF\) and \[ \circ\in\{\land,\lor,\to,\leftrightarrow\}, \] then \[ (\varphi\circ\psi)\in\WFF. \]
lem:closure-under-formation-propositiona.statement_display:
The set \(\WFF\) is closed under the propositional formation rules.

That is, if \(\varphi\in\WFF\), then
\[
\neg\varphi\in\WFF.
\]
If \(\varphi,\psi\in\WFF\) and
\[
\circ\in\{\land,\lor,\to,\leftrightarrow\},
\]
then
\[
(\varphi\circ\psi)\in\WFF.
\]
lem:closure-under-formation-propositiona.statement_tex:
The set \(\WFF\) is closed under the propositional formation rules.

That is, if \(\varphi\in\WFF\), then
\[
\neg\varphi\in\WFF.
\]
If \(\varphi,\psi\in\WFF\) and
\[
\circ\in\{\land,\lor,\to,\leftrightarrow\},
\]
then
\[
(\varphi\circ\psi)\in\WFF.
\]

thm:minimality-of-well-formed-formulas.title: Minimality of Well-Formed Formulas
thm:minimality-of-well-formed-formulas.statement_preview: Let \(S\) be a set of strings over the propositional alphabet. Suppose: \begin{enumerate} \item \(\Prop\subseteq S\); \item if \(\varphi\in S\), then \(\neg\varphi\in S\); \item if \(\varphi,\psi\in S\) and \(\circ\in\{\land,\lor,\to,\leftrightarrow\}\), then \((\varphi\circ\psi)\in S\). \end{enumerate} Then \[ \WFF\subseteq S. \]

def:atomic-formula-propositional-logic.title: Atomic Formula
def:atomic-formula-propositional-logic.statement_preview: An atomic formula is a well-formed formula that is a propositional variable.
def:atomic-formula-propositional-logic.statement_display: An atomic formula is a well-formed formula that is a propositional variable.
def:atomic-formula-propositional-logic.statement_tex: An atomic formula is a well-formed formula that is a propositional variable.

def:molecular-formula-propositional-logi.title: Molecular Formula
def:molecular-formula-propositional-logi.statement_preview: A molecular formula is a well-formed formula that is not atomic. Equivalently, it is a well-formed formula formed using at least one logical connective.
def:molecular-formula-propositional-logi.statement_display: A molecular formula is a well-formed formula that is not atomic. Equivalently, it is a well-formed formula formed using at least one logical connective.
def:molecular-formula-propositional-logi.statement_tex: A molecular formula is a well-formed formula that is not atomic. Equivalently, it is a well-formed formula formed using at least one logical connective.

def:main-connective-propositional-logic.title: Main Connective
def:main-connective-propositional-logic.statement_preview: The main connective of a molecular formula is the connective introduced at the final formation step: \begin{enumerate} \item for \(\neg\varphi\), the main connective is \(\neg\); \item for \((\varphi\circ\psi)\), the main connective is \(\circ\). \end{enumerate} Atomic formulas have no main connective.

thm:structural-induction-propositional-f.title: Structural Induction for Propositional Formulas
thm:structural-induction-propositional-f.statement_preview: Let \(A(\varphi)\) be a property of well-formed formulas. Suppose: \begin{enumerate} \item \(A(P)\) holds for every \(P\in\Prop\); \item if \(A(\varphi)\) holds, then \(A(\neg\varphi)\) holds; \item if \(A(\varphi)\) and \(A(\psi)\) hold, then \(A((\varphi\circ\psi))\) holds for every \(\circ\in\{\land,\lor,\to,\leftrightarrow\}\). \end{enumerate} Then \(A(...

thm:structural-recursion-propositional-f.title: Structural Recursion for Propositional Formulas
thm:structural-recursion-propositional-f.statement_preview: To define a function \(F\) on \(\WFF\), it suffices to specify: \begin{enumerate} \item \(F(P)\) for every \(P\in\Prop\); \item \(F(\neg\varphi)\) in terms of \(F(\varphi)\); \item \(F((\varphi\circ\psi))\) in terms of \(F(\varphi)\), \(F(\psi)\), and \(\circ\). \end{enumerate} When these clauses respect the formation rules, they determine a unique function...

lem:constructor-disjointness-proposition.title: Constructor Disjointness for Propositional Formulas
lem:constructor-disjointness-proposition.statement_preview: The three outer formation cases for propositional formulas are disjoint: \begin{enumerate} \item no propositional variable is a negation formula; \item no propositional variable is a binary formula; \item no negation formula is a binary formula. \end{enumerate}

lem:constructor-injectivity-propositiona.title: Constructor Injectivity for Propositional Formulas
lem:constructor-injectivity-propositiona.statement_preview: The propositional formula constructors are injective: \begin{enumerate} \item if \(\neg\varphi=\neg\psi\), then \(\varphi=\psi\); \item if \((\varphi\circ\psi)=(\alpha\star\beta)\), then \(\varphi=\alpha\), \(\psi=\beta\), and \(\circ=\star\). \end{enumerate}

thm:unique-decomposition-propositional-f.title: Unique Decomposition for Propositional Formulas
thm:unique-decomposition-propositional-f.statement_preview: Every \(\varphi\in\WFF\) has exactly one outermost form: \begin{enumerate} \item \(\varphi=P\) for a unique \(P\in\Prop\); \item \(\varphi=\neg\psi\) for a unique \(\psi\in\WFF\); \item \(\varphi=(\psi\circ\chi)\) for unique \(\psi,\chi\in\WFF\) and a unique binary connective \(\circ\in\{\land,\lor,\to,\leftrightarrow\}\). \end{enumerate}

def:parse-tree-propositional-logic.title: Parse Tree
def:parse-tree-propositional-logic.statement_preview: The parse tree, or syntax tree, of a well-formed formula is the tree representation of its recursive construction: \begin{enumerate} \item a propositional variable is represented by a single leaf; \item a formula \(\neg\varphi\) is represented by a root labeled \(\neg\) with one child, the parse tree of \(\varphi\); \item a formula \((\varphi\circ\psi)\) is...

thm:unique-syntax-tree-propositional-for.title: Unique Syntax Tree for Propositional Formulas
thm:unique-syntax-tree-propositional-for.statement_preview: Every propositional well-formed formula has a unique parse tree.
thm:unique-syntax-tree-propositional-for.statement_display: Every propositional well-formed formula has a unique parse tree.
thm:unique-syntax-tree-propositional-for.statement_tex: Every propositional well-formed formula has a unique parse tree.

def:formula-variable-set-propositional-l.title: Variable Set of a Formula
def:formula-variable-set-propositional-l.statement_preview: The variable set \(\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)\) of a formula \(\varphi\) is defined recursively: \begin{enumerate} \item if \(\varphi=P\in\Prop\), then \(\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)=\{P\}\); \item if \(\varphi=\neg\psi\), then \(\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)=\operatorname{Var}(\psi)\); \item if \(\varphi=(\psi\circ\chi)\), then \[ \operatorname{Var}(\varphi)...

def:subformula-propositional-logic.title: Subformula
def:subformula-propositional-logic.statement_preview: The set \(\operatorname{Sub}(\varphi)\) of subformulas of a formula \(\varphi\) is defined recursively: \begin{enumerate} \item if \(\varphi\) is atomic, then \(\operatorname{Sub}(\varphi)=\{\varphi\}\); \item if \(\varphi=\neg\psi\), then \[ \operatorname{Sub}(\varphi)=\{\varphi\}\cup\operatorname{Sub}(\psi); \] \item if \(\varphi=(\psi\circ\chi)\), then \...

def:proper-subformula.title: Proper Subformula
def:proper-subformula.statement_preview: Let \(\varphi\in\WFF\). A \emph{proper subformula} of \(\varphi\) is a formula \(\psi\) such that \[ \psi\in\operatorname{Sub}(\varphi) \quad\text{and}\quad \psi\neq\varphi. \]
def:proper-subformula.statement_display:
Let \(\varphi\in\WFF\). A \emph{proper subformula} of \(\varphi\) is a
formula \(\psi\) such that
\[
\psi\in\operatorname{Sub}(\varphi)
\quad\text{and}\quad
\psi\neq\varphi.
\]
def:proper-subformula.statement_tex:
Let \(\varphi\in\WFF\). A \emph{proper subformula} of \(\varphi\) is a
formula \(\psi\) such that
\[
\psi\in\operatorname{Sub}(\varphi)
\quad\text{and}\quad
\psi\neq\varphi.
\]

def:formula-depth-propositional-logic.title: Formula Depth
def:formula-depth-propositional-logic.statement_preview: The depth of a formula \(\varphi\), denoted \(\operatorname{depth}(\varphi)\), is defined recursively: \begin{enumerate} \item if \(\varphi\) is atomic, then \(\operatorname{depth}(\varphi)=0\); \item if \(\varphi=\neg\psi\), then \(\operatorname{depth}(\varphi)=\operatorname{depth}(\psi)+1\); \item if \(\varphi=(\psi\circ\chi)\), then \[ \operatorname{dept...

def:connective-count-propositional-logic.title: Connective Count
def:connective-count-propositional-logic.statement_preview: The connective count of a formula \(\varphi\), denoted \(\operatorname{conn}(\varphi)\), is defined recursively: \begin{enumerate} \item if \(\varphi\) is atomic, then \(\operatorname{conn}(\varphi)=0\); \item if \(\varphi=\neg\psi\), then \(\operatorname{conn}(\varphi)=\operatorname{conn}(\psi)+1\); \item if \(\varphi=(\psi\circ\chi)\), then \[ \operatorna...

lem:proper-subformula-depth-propositiona.title: Proper Subformula Depth
lem:proper-subformula-depth-propositiona.statement_preview: If \(\psi\) is a proper subformula of \(\varphi\), then \[ \operatorname{depth}(\psi)<\operatorname{depth}(\varphi). \]
lem:proper-subformula-depth-propositiona.statement_display:
If \(\psi\) is a proper subformula of \(\varphi\), then
\[
\operatorname{depth}(\psi)<\operatorname{depth}(\varphi).
\]
lem:proper-subformula-depth-propositiona.statement_tex:
If \(\psi\) is a proper subformula of \(\varphi\), then
\[
\operatorname{depth}(\psi)<\operatorname{depth}(\varphi).
\]

lem:finiteness-of-variables-propositiona.title: Finiteness of Variables in a Formula
lem:finiteness-of-variables-propositiona.statement_preview: For every \(\varphi\in\WFF\), the set \(\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)\) is finite.
lem:finiteness-of-variables-propositiona.statement_display: For every \(\varphi\in\WFF\), the set \(\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)\) is finite.
lem:finiteness-of-variables-propositiona.statement_tex: For every \(\varphi\in\WFF\), the set \(\operatorname{Var}(\varphi)\) is finite.

lem:finiteness-of-subformulas-propositio.title: Finiteness of Subformulas
lem:finiteness-of-subformulas-propositio.statement_preview: For every \(\varphi\in\WFF\), the set \(\operatorname{Sub}(\varphi)\) is finite.
lem:finiteness-of-subformulas-propositio.statement_display: For every \(\varphi\in\WFF\), the set \(\operatorname{Sub}(\varphi)\) is finite.
lem:finiteness-of-subformulas-propositio.statement_tex: For every \(\varphi\in\WFF\), the set \(\operatorname{Sub}(\varphi)\) is finite.

def:same-cardinality.title: Same Cardinality
def:same-cardinality.statement_preview: Two sets $A$ and $B$ have the \emph{same cardinality}, written $A \sim B$, if there exists a bijection $f : A \to B$.
def:same-cardinality.statement_display:
Two sets $A$ and $B$ have the \emph{same cardinality}, written $A \sim B$,
if there exists a bijection $f : A \to B$.
def:same-cardinality.statement_tex:
Two sets $A$ and $B$ have the \emph{same cardinality}, written $A \sim B$,
if there exists a bijection $f : A \to B$.

def:finite-infinite.title: Finite and Infinite Sets
def:finite-infinite.statement_preview: A set $A$ is \emph{finite} if $A = \varnothing$ or $A \sim \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$ for some $n \in \mathbb{N}$. A set is \emph{infinite} if it is not finite.
def:finite-infinite.statement_display:
A set $A$ is \emph{finite} if $A = \varnothing$ or $A \sim \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$
for some $n \in \mathbb{N}$. A set is \emph{infinite} if it is not finite.
def:finite-infinite.statement_tex:
A set $A$ is \emph{finite} if $A = \varnothing$ or $A \sim \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$
for some $n \in \mathbb{N}$. A set is \emph{infinite} if it is not finite.

def:countable.title: Countable and Uncountable Sets
def:countable.statement_preview: A set $A$ is \emph{countably infinite} if $A \sim \mathbb{N}$. A set is \emph{countable} if it is finite or countably infinite. A set is \emph{uncountable} if it is infinite but not countable.
def:countable.statement_display:
A set $A$ is \emph{countably infinite} if $A \sim \mathbb{N}$. A set is
\emph{countable} if it is finite or countably infinite. A set is
\emph{uncountable} if it is infinite but not countable.
def:countable.statement_tex:
A set $A$ is \emph{countably infinite} if $A \sim \mathbb{N}$. A set is
\emph{countable} if it is finite or countably infinite. A set is
\emph{uncountable} if it is infinite but not countable.

def:cardinality-order.title: Comparing Cardinalities
def:cardinality-order.statement_preview: Write $|A| \leq |B|$ if there exists an injection $f : A \hookrightarrow B$. Write $|A| < |B|$ if $|A| \leq |B|$ but $A \not\sim B$.
def:cardinality-order.statement_display:
Write $|A| \leq |B|$ if there exists an injection $f : A \hookrightarrow B$.
Write $|A| < |B|$ if $|A| \leq |B|$ but $A \not\sim B$.
def:cardinality-order.statement_tex:
Write $|A| \leq |B|$ if there exists an injection $f : A \hookrightarrow B$.
Write $|A| < |B|$ if $|A| \leq |B|$ but $A \not\sim B$.

thm:Q-countable.title: $\mathbb{Q}$ is countable
thm:Q-countable.statement_preview: The set $\mathbb{Q}$ of rational numbers is countable.
thm:Q-countable.statement_display: The set $\mathbb{Q}$ of rational numbers is countable.
thm:Q-countable.statement_tex: The set $\mathbb{Q}$ of rational numbers is countable.

thm:countable-union.title: Countable union of countable sets is countable
thm:countable-union.statement_preview: Let $\{A_n\}_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ be a countable family of countable sets. Then $\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n$ is countable.
thm:countable-union.statement_display:
Let $\{A_n\}_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ be a countable family of countable sets.
Then $\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n$ is countable.
thm:countable-union.statement_tex:
Let $\{A_n\}_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ be a countable family of countable sets.
Then $\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n$ is countable.

thm:R-uncountable.title: $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable
thm:R-uncountable.statement_preview: The set $\mathbb{R}$ of real numbers is not countable.
thm:R-uncountable.statement_display: The set $\mathbb{R}$ of real numbers is not countable.
thm:R-uncountable.statement_tex: The set $\mathbb{R}$ of real numbers is not countable.

thm:cantor.title: Cantor's Theorem
thm:cantor.statement_preview: For any set $A$, there is no surjection $A \to \mathcal{P}(A)$. In particular, $|A| < |\mathcal{P}(A)|$.
thm:cantor.statement_display:
For any set $A$, there is no surjection $A \to \mathcal{P}(A)$. In
particular, $|A| < |\mathcal{P}(A)|$.
thm:cantor.statement_tex:
For any set $A$, there is no surjection $A \to \mathcal{P}(A)$. In
particular, $|A| < |\mathcal{P}(A)|$.

def:function-space.title: Function Space $B^A$
def:function-space.statement_preview: For sets $A$ and $B$, the \emph{function space} $B^A$ denotes the set of all functions from $A$ to $B$: \[ B^A \;:=\; \{\, f : A \to B \,\}. \]
def:function-space.statement_display:
For sets $A$ and $B$, the \emph{function space} $B^A$ denotes the set of
all functions from $A$ to $B$:
\[
B^A \;:=\; \{\, f : A \to B \,\}.
\]
def:function-space.statement_tex:
For sets $A$ and $B$, the \emph{function space} $B^A$ denotes the set of
all functions from $A$ to $B$:
\[
B^A \;:=\; \{\, f : A \to B \,\}.
\]

thm:schroder-bernstein.title: Schröder--Bernstein Theorem
thm:schroder-bernstein.statement_preview: If $|A| \leq |B|$ and $|B| \leq |A|$, then $A \sim B$. Equivalently: if there exist injections $f : A \hookrightarrow B$ and $g : B \hookrightarrow A$, then there exists a bijection $h : A \to B$.
thm:schroder-bernstein.statement_display:
If $|A| \leq |B|$ and $|B| \leq |A|$, then $A \sim B$.

Equivalently: if there exist injections $f : A \hookrightarrow B$ and
$g : B \hookrightarrow A$, then there exists a bijection $h : A \to B$.
thm:schroder-bernstein.statement_tex:
If $|A| \leq |B|$ and $|B| \leq |A|$, then $A \sim B$.

Equivalently: if there exist injections $f : A \hookrightarrow B$ and
$g : B \hookrightarrow A$, then there exists a bijection $h : A \to B$.

def:equiv-class.title: Equivalence Class
def:equiv-class.statement_preview: Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. For $a \in A$, the \emph{equivalence class} of $a$ is: \[ [a]_R \;:=\; \{\, x \in A \mid (a,x) \in R \,\}. \] When $R$ is clear from context, we write $[a]$.
def:equiv-class.statement_display:
Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. For $a \in A$, the
\emph{equivalence class} of $a$ is:
\[
[a]_R \;:=\; \{\, x \in A \mid (a,x) \in R \,\}.
\]
When $R$ is clear from context, we write $[a]$.
def:equiv-class.statement_tex:
Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. For $a \in A$, the
\emph{equivalence class} of $a$ is:
\[
[a]_R \;:=\; \{\, x \in A \mid (a,x) \in R \,\}.
\]
When $R$ is clear from context, we write $[a]$.

def:quotient-set.title: Quotient Set
def:quotient-set.statement_preview: The \emph{quotient set} of $A$ by $R$ is the set of all equivalence classes: \[ A / R \;:=\; \{\, [a]_R \mid a \in A \,\}. \]
def:quotient-set.statement_display:
The \emph{quotient set} of $A$ by $R$ is the set of all equivalence classes:
\[
A / R \;:=\; \{\, [a]_R \mid a \in A \,\}.
\]
def:quotient-set.statement_tex:
The \emph{quotient set} of $A$ by $R$ is the set of all equivalence classes:
\[
A / R \;:=\; \{\, [a]_R \mid a \in A \,\}.
\]

def:index.title: Index of an Equivalence Relation
def:index.statement_preview: The \emph{index} of $R$ on $A$ is the cardinality $|A/R|$, i.e.\ the number of equivalence classes.
def:index.statement_display:
The \emph{index} of $R$ on $A$ is the cardinality $|A/R|$, i.e.\ the number
of equivalence classes.
def:index.statement_tex:
The \emph{index} of $R$ on $A$ is the cardinality $|A/R|$, i.e.\ the number
of equivalence classes.

def:canonical-surj.title: Canonical Surjection
def:canonical-surj.statement_preview: The \emph{canonical surjection} (quotient map) is the function \[ \pi : A \to A/R, \qquad \pi(a) := [a]. \]
def:canonical-surj.statement_display:
The \emph{canonical surjection} (quotient map) is the function
\[
\pi : A \to A/R,
\qquad
\pi(a) := [a].
\]
def:canonical-surj.statement_tex:
The \emph{canonical surjection} (quotient map) is the function
\[
\pi : A \to A/R,
\qquad
\pi(a) := [a].
\]

def:partition.title: Partition
def:partition.statement_preview: A \emph{partition} of $A$ is a collection $\mathcal{P}$ of subsets of $A$ such that: \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item every block is nonempty: $\forall P \in \mathcal{P},\; P \neq \varnothing$; \item distinct blocks are disjoint: $\forall P,Q \in \mathcal{P},\; P \neq Q \rightarrow P \cap Q = \varnothing$; \item the blocks cover $A$: $\bigcup_{P \in...

lem:rep-independence.title: Representative Independence Lemma
lem:rep-independence.statement_preview: Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. For any $a,b \in A$, \[ [a] = [b] \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; (a,b) \in R. \]
lem:rep-independence.statement_display:
Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. For any $a,b \in A$,
\[
[a] = [b]
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
(a,b) \in R.
\]
lem:rep-independence.statement_tex:
Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. For any $a,b \in A$,
\[
[a] = [b]
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
(a,b) \in R.
\]

thm:equiv-partition.title: Equivalence Relations and Partitions
thm:equiv-partition.statement_preview: Let $A$ be a set. \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item If $R$ is an equivalence relation on $A$, then $A/R$ is a partition of $A$. \item If $\mathcal{P}$ is a partition of $A$, then the relation $R_{\mathcal{P}}$ defined by \[ (a,b) \in R_{\mathcal{P}} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \exists P \in \mathcal{P} \text{ with } a \in P \text{ and } b \in P \] is an...
thm:equiv-partition.statement_display:
Let $A$ be a set.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item If $R$ is an equivalence relation on $A$, then $A/R$ is a partition of $A$.
\item If $\mathcal{P}$ is a partition of $A$, then the relation
$R_{\mathcal{P}}$ defined by
\[
(a,b) \in R_{\mathcal{P}} \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\exists P \in \mathcal{P} \text{ with } a \in P \text{ and } b \in P
\]
is an equivalence relation on $A$.
\item These constructions are inverse: $R_{A/R} = R$ and $A/R_{\mathcal{P}} = \mathcal{P}$.
\end{enumerate}
thm:equiv-partition.statement_tex:
Let $A$ be a set.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item If $R$ is an equivalence relation on $A$, then $A/R$ is a partition of $A$.
\item If $\mathcal{P}$ is a partition of $A$, then the relation
$R_{\mathcal{P}}$ defined by
\[
(a,b) \in R_{\mathcal{P}} \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\exists P \in \mathcal{P} \text{ with } a \in P \text{ and } b \in P
\]
is an equivalence relation on $A$.
\item These constructions are inverse: $R_{A/R} = R$ and $A/R_{\mathcal{P}} = \mathcal{P}$.
\end{enumerate}

thm:quotient-universal.title: Universal Property of the Quotient Map
thm:quotient-universal.statement_preview: Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$, let $\pi : A \to A/R$ be the canonical surjection, and let $f : A \to B$ be any function. Then $f$ is \emph{constant on equivalence classes} — meaning $(a,b) \in R$ implies $f(a) = f(b)$ — if and only if there exists a unique function $\bar{f} : A/R \to B$ such that \[ f = \bar{f} \circ \pi. \] When it exists, $\ba...
thm:quotient-universal.statement_display:
Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$, let $\pi : A \to A/R$ be the
canonical surjection, and let $f : A \to B$ be any function. Then $f$ is
\emph{constant on equivalence classes} — meaning $(a,b) \in R$ implies
$f(a) = f(b)$ — if and only if there exists a unique function
$\bar{f} : A/R \to B$ such that
\[
f = \bar{f} \circ \pi.
\]
When it exists, $\bar{f}$ is defined by $\bar{f}([a]) = f(a)$.
thm:quotient-universal.statement_tex:
Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$, let $\pi : A \to A/R$ be the
canonical surjection, and let $f : A \to B$ be any function. Then $f$ is
\emph{constant on equivalence classes} — meaning $(a,b) \in R$ implies
$f(a) = f(b)$ — if and only if there exists a unique function
$\bar{f} : A/R \to B$ such that
\[
f = \bar{f} \circ \pi.
\]
When it exists, $\bar{f}$ is defined by $\bar{f}([a]) = f(a)$.

def:cover-full.title: Cover
def:cover-full.statement_preview: Let $A$ be a set and $\mathcal{C}$ a collection of sets. $\mathcal{C}$ is a \emph{cover} of $A$ if every element of $A$ belongs to at least one member of $\mathcal{C}$: \[ A \;\subseteq\; \bigcup_{C \in \mathcal{C}} C. \]
def:cover-full.statement_display:
Let $A$ be a set and $\mathcal{C}$ a collection of sets.
$\mathcal{C}$ is a \emph{cover} of $A$ if every element of $A$ belongs to
at least one member of $\mathcal{C}$:
\[
A \;\subseteq\; \bigcup_{C \in \mathcal{C}} C.
\]
def:cover-full.statement_tex:
Let $A$ be a set and $\mathcal{C}$ a collection of sets.
$\mathcal{C}$ is a \emph{cover} of $A$ if every element of $A$ belongs to
at least one member of $\mathcal{C}$:
\[
A \;\subseteq\; \bigcup_{C \in \mathcal{C}} C.
\]

def:subcover.title: Subcover
def:subcover.statement_preview: $\mathcal{C}'$ is a \emph{subcover} of $\mathcal{C}$ if $\mathcal{C}' \subseteq \mathcal{C}$ and $\mathcal{C}'$ is still a cover of $A$.
def:subcover.statement_display:
$\mathcal{C}'$ is a \emph{subcover} of $\mathcal{C}$ if $\mathcal{C}'
\subseteq \mathcal{C}$ and $\mathcal{C}'$ is still a cover of $A$.
def:subcover.statement_tex:
$\mathcal{C}'$ is a \emph{subcover} of $\mathcal{C}$ if $\mathcal{C}'
\subseteq \mathcal{C}$ and $\mathcal{C}'$ is still a cover of $A$.

def:finite-cover.title: Finite Cover
def:finite-cover.statement_preview: A cover is \emph{finite} if it has finitely many members. A \emph{finite subcover} is a subcover $\mathcal{C}' \subseteq \mathcal{C}$ with $|\mathcal{C}'| < \infty$.
def:finite-cover.statement_display:
A cover is \emph{finite} if it has finitely many members. A \emph{finite
subcover} is a subcover $\mathcal{C}' \subseteq \mathcal{C}$ with
$|\mathcal{C}'| < \infty$.
def:finite-cover.statement_tex:
A cover is \emph{finite} if it has finitely many members. A \emph{finite
subcover} is a subcover $\mathcal{C}' \subseteq \mathcal{C}$ with
$|\mathcal{C}'| < \infty$.

def:open-cover.title: Open Cover
def:open-cover.statement_preview: In a topological space $(X, \tau)$, an \emph{open cover} of a subset $A \subseteq X$ is a cover $\mathcal{C}$ of $A$ all of whose members are open sets: $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \tau$.
def:open-cover.statement_display:
In a topological space $(X, \tau)$, an \emph{open cover} of a subset $A
\subseteq X$ is a cover $\mathcal{C}$ of $A$ all of whose members are open
sets: $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \tau$.
def:open-cover.statement_tex:
In a topological space $(X, \tau)$, an \emph{open cover} of a subset $A
\subseteq X$ is a cover $\mathcal{C}$ of $A$ all of whose members are open
sets: $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \tau$.

def:fip.title: Finite Intersection Property
def:fip.statement_preview: A collection $\mathcal{F}$ of sets has the \emph{finite intersection property} (FIP) if every finite subcollection has nonempty intersection: for all finite $\mathcal{F}' \subseteq \mathcal{F}$, \[ \bigcap_{F \in \mathcal{F}'} F \;\neq\; \varnothing. \]
def:fip.statement_display:
A collection $\mathcal{F}$ of sets has the \emph{finite intersection
property} (FIP) if every finite subcollection has nonempty intersection:
for all finite $\mathcal{F}' \subseteq \mathcal{F}$,
\[
\bigcap_{F \in \mathcal{F}'} F \;\neq\; \varnothing.
\]
def:fip.statement_tex:
A collection $\mathcal{F}$ of sets has the \emph{finite intersection
property} (FIP) if every finite subcollection has nonempty intersection:
for all finite $\mathcal{F}' \subseteq \mathcal{F}$,
\[
\bigcap_{F \in \mathcal{F}'} F \;\neq\; \varnothing.
\]

prop:fip-duality.title: FIP--Cover Duality
prop:fip-duality.statement_preview: Let $X$ be a set, $A \subseteq X$, and $\{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in I}$ a family of subsets of $X$. Let $F_\alpha = U_\alpha^c = X \setminus U_\alpha$. Then: \[ \{U_\alpha\} \text{ covers } A \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \{F_\alpha \cap A\}_{\alpha \in I} \text{ does \emph{not} have the FIP}. \] More precisely: the family $\{U_\alpha\}$ does \emph{not} cover $A...
prop:fip-duality.statement_display:
Let $X$ be a set, $A \subseteq X$, and $\{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in I}$ a
family of subsets of $X$. Let $F_\alpha = U_\alpha^c = X \setminus U_\alpha$.
Then:
\[
\{U_\alpha\} \text{ covers } A
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
\{F_\alpha \cap A\}_{\alpha \in I} \text{ does \emph{not} have the FIP}.
\]
More precisely: the family $\{U_\alpha\}$ does \emph{not} cover $A$ if and
only if the family of closed complements $\{F_\alpha\}$ has the FIP relative
to $A$.
prop:fip-duality.statement_tex:
Let $X$ be a set, $A \subseteq X$, and $\{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in I}$ a
family of subsets of $X$. Let $F_\alpha = U_\alpha^c = X \setminus U_\alpha$.
Then:
\[
\{U_\alpha\} \text{ covers } A
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
\{F_\alpha \cap A\}_{\alpha \in I} \text{ does \emph{not} have the FIP}.
\]
More precisely: the family $\{U_\alpha\}$ does \emph{not} cover $A$ if and
only if the family of closed complements $\{F_\alpha\}$ has the FIP relative
to $A$.

def:indexed-family.title: Indexed Family of Sets
def:indexed-family.statement_preview: Let $I$ be a set (the \emph{index set}) and $U$ a universe. An \emph{indexed family of sets} is a function \[ F : I \to \mathcal{P}(U), \] with $F(i)$ typically written $A_i$. The family is denoted $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$.
def:indexed-family.statement_display:
Let $I$ be a set (the \emph{index set}) and $U$ a universe. An
\emph{indexed family of sets} is a function
\[
F : I \to \mathcal{P}(U),
\]
with $F(i)$ typically written $A_i$. The family is denoted $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$.
def:indexed-family.statement_tex:
Let $I$ be a set (the \emph{index set}) and $U$ a universe. An
\emph{indexed family of sets} is a function
\[
F : I \to \mathcal{P}(U),
\]
with $F(i)$ typically written $A_i$. The family is denoted $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$.

def:indexed-union.title: Indexed Union
def:indexed-union.statement_preview: The \emph{union} of the indexed family $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$ is: \[ \bigcup_{i \in I} A_i \;:=\; \{\, x \mid \exists i \in I \text{ such that } x \in A_i \,\}. \]
def:indexed-union.statement_display:
The \emph{union} of the indexed family $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$ is:
\[
\bigcup_{i \in I} A_i
\;:=\;
\{\, x \mid \exists i \in I \text{ such that } x \in A_i \,\}.
\]
def:indexed-union.statement_tex:
The \emph{union} of the indexed family $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$ is:
\[
\bigcup_{i \in I} A_i
\;:=\;
\{\, x \mid \exists i \in I \text{ such that } x \in A_i \,\}.
\]

def:indexed-intersection.title: Indexed Intersection
def:indexed-intersection.statement_preview: For $I \neq \varnothing$, the \emph{intersection} of $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$ is: \[ \bigcap_{i \in I} A_i \;:=\; \{\, x \mid \forall i \in I,\; x \in A_i \,\}. \]
def:indexed-intersection.statement_display:
For $I \neq \varnothing$, the \emph{intersection} of $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$ is:
\[
\bigcap_{i \in I} A_i
\;:=\;
\{\, x \mid \forall i \in I,\; x \in A_i \,\}.
\]
def:indexed-intersection.statement_tex:
For $I \neq \varnothing$, the \emph{intersection} of $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$ is:
\[
\bigcap_{i \in I} A_i
\;:=\;
\{\, x \mid \forall i \in I,\; x \in A_i \,\}.
\]

def:pairwise-disjoint.title: Pairwise Disjoint Family
def:pairwise-disjoint.statement_preview: The family $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$ is \emph{pairwise disjoint} if \[ \forall i,j \in I,\; i \neq j \rightarrow A_i \cap A_j = \varnothing. \]
def:pairwise-disjoint.statement_display:
The family $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$ is \emph{pairwise disjoint} if
\[
\forall i,j \in I,\;
i \neq j \rightarrow A_i \cap A_j = \varnothing.
\]
def:pairwise-disjoint.statement_tex:
The family $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$ is \emph{pairwise disjoint} if
\[
\forall i,j \in I,\;
i \neq j \rightarrow A_i \cap A_j = \varnothing.
\]

def:set-cover.title: Cover
def:set-cover.statement_preview: A collection $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{P}(A)$ is a \emph{cover} of $A$ if \[ \bigcup_{C \in \mathcal{C}} C = A. \]
def:set-cover.statement_display:
A collection $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{P}(A)$ is a \emph{cover} of $A$ if
\[
\bigcup_{C \in \mathcal{C}} C = A.
\]
def:set-cover.statement_tex:
A collection $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{P}(A)$ is a \emph{cover} of $A$ if
\[
\bigcup_{C \in \mathcal{C}} C = A.
\]

def:arbitrary-product.title: Arbitrary Cartesian Product
def:arbitrary-product.statement_preview: Let $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$ be an indexed family of sets. The \emph{Cartesian product} is: \[ \prod_{i \in I} A_i \;:=\; \Bigl\{ f : I \to \bigcup_{i \in I} A_i \;\Bigm|\; \forall i \in I,\; f(i) \in A_i \Bigr\}. \]
def:arbitrary-product.statement_display:
Let $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$ be an indexed family of sets. The \emph{Cartesian
product} is:
\[
\prod_{i \in I} A_i
\;:=\;
\Bigl\{
f : I \to \bigcup_{i \in I} A_i
\;\Bigm|\;
\forall i \in I,\; f(i) \in A_i
\Bigr\}.
\]
def:arbitrary-product.statement_tex:
Let $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$ be an indexed family of sets. The \emph{Cartesian
product} is:
\[
\prod_{i \in I} A_i
\;:=\;
\Bigl\{
f : I \to \bigcup_{i \in I} A_i
\;\Bigm|\;
\forall i \in I,\; f(i) \in A_i
\Bigr\}.
\]

def:composition.title: Composition
def:composition.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to B$ and $g : B \to C$. The \emph{composition} $g \circ f : A \to C$ is: \[ (g \circ f)(a) := g\bigl(f(a)\bigr) \quad\text{for all } a \in A. \]
def:composition.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $g : B \to C$. The \emph{composition} $g \circ f : A \to C$ is:
\[
(g \circ f)(a) := g\bigl(f(a)\bigr)
\quad\text{for all } a \in A.
\]
def:composition.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $g : B \to C$. The \emph{composition} $g \circ f : A \to C$ is:
\[
(g \circ f)(a) := g\bigl(f(a)\bigr)
\quad\text{for all } a \in A.
\]

thm:comp-assoc.title: Associativity of Composition
thm:comp-assoc.statement_preview: For $f : A \to B$, $g : B \to C$, $h : C \to D$: \[ h \circ (g \circ f) = (h \circ g) \circ f. \]
thm:comp-assoc.statement_display:
For $f : A \to B$, $g : B \to C$, $h : C \to D$:
\[
h \circ (g \circ f) = (h \circ g) \circ f.
\]
thm:comp-assoc.statement_tex:
For $f : A \to B$, $g : B \to C$, $h : C \to D$:
\[
h \circ (g \circ f) = (h \circ g) \circ f.
\]

thm:comp-id.title: Identity and Composition
thm:comp-id.statement_preview: For any $f : A \to B$: \[ f \circ \id_A = f \quad\text{and}\quad \id_B \circ f = f. \]
thm:comp-id.statement_display:
For any $f : A \to B$:
\[
f \circ \id_A = f
\quad\text{and}\quad
\id_B \circ f = f.
\]
thm:comp-id.statement_tex:
For any $f : A \to B$:
\[
f \circ \id_A = f
\quad\text{and}\quad
\id_B \circ f = f.
\]

thm:comp-inj-surj.title: Injectivity and Surjectivity Under Composition
thm:comp-inj-surj.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to B$ and $g : B \to C$. \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item If $f$ and $g$ are injective, then $g \circ f$ is injective. \item If $f$ and $g$ are surjective, then $g \circ f$ is surjective. \item If $g \circ f$ is injective, then $f$ is injective. \item If $g \circ f$ is surjective, then $g$ is surjective. \end{enumerate}
thm:comp-inj-surj.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $g : B \to C$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item If $f$ and $g$ are injective, then $g \circ f$ is injective.
\item If $f$ and $g$ are surjective, then $g \circ f$ is surjective.
\item If $g \circ f$ is injective, then $f$ is injective.
\item If $g \circ f$ is surjective, then $g$ is surjective.
\end{enumerate}
thm:comp-inj-surj.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $g : B \to C$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item If $f$ and $g$ are injective, then $g \circ f$ is injective.
\item If $f$ and $g$ are surjective, then $g \circ f$ is surjective.
\item If $g \circ f$ is injective, then $f$ is injective.
\item If $g \circ f$ is surjective, then $g$ is surjective.
\end{enumerate}

def:inverse.title: Inverse Function
def:inverse.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to B$ be bijective. The \emph{inverse function} is $f^{-1} : B \to A$ defined by: $f^{-1}(b) = a$ iff $f(a) = b$.
def:inverse.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ be bijective. The \emph{inverse function} is
$f^{-1} : B \to A$ defined by: $f^{-1}(b) = a$ iff $f(a) = b$.
def:inverse.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to B$ be bijective. The \emph{inverse function} is
$f^{-1} : B \to A$ defined by: $f^{-1}(b) = a$ iff $f(a) = b$.

thm:inverse-char.title: Characterization of Inverse Functions
thm:inverse-char.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to B$ be bijective. Then \[ f^{-1} \circ f = \id_A \quad\text{and}\quad f \circ f^{-1} = \id_B. \] Conversely, a function admits an inverse iff it is bijective.
thm:inverse-char.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ be bijective. Then
\[
f^{-1} \circ f = \id_A
\quad\text{and}\quad
f \circ f^{-1} = \id_B.
\]
Conversely, a function admits an inverse iff it is bijective.
thm:inverse-char.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to B$ be bijective. Then
\[
f^{-1} \circ f = \id_A
\quad\text{and}\quad
f \circ f^{-1} = \id_B.
\]
Conversely, a function admits an inverse iff it is bijective.

thm:inverse-comp.title: Inverse of a Composition
thm:inverse-comp.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to B$ and $g : B \to C$ be bijective. Then $g \circ f$ is bijective and \[ (g \circ f)^{-1} = f^{-1} \circ g^{-1}. \]
thm:inverse-comp.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $g : B \to C$ be bijective. Then $g \circ f$ is
bijective and
\[
(g \circ f)^{-1} = f^{-1} \circ g^{-1}.
\]
thm:inverse-comp.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $g : B \to C$ be bijective. Then $g \circ f$ is
bijective and
\[
(g \circ f)^{-1} = f^{-1} \circ g^{-1}.
\]

def:left-right-inverse.title: Left and Right Inverses
def:left-right-inverse.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to B$. A \emph{left inverse} of $f$ is a function $g : B \to A$ with $g \circ f = \id_A$. A \emph{right inverse} (or \emph{section}) of $f$ is a function $h : B \to A$ with $f \circ h = \id_B$.
def:left-right-inverse.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$.

A \emph{left inverse} of $f$ is a function $g : B \to A$ with $g \circ f = \id_A$.

A \emph{right inverse} (or \emph{section}) of $f$ is a function $h : B \to A$
with $f \circ h = \id_B$.
def:left-right-inverse.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to B$.

A \emph{left inverse} of $f$ is a function $g : B \to A$ with $g \circ f = \id_A$.

A \emph{right inverse} (or \emph{section}) of $f$ is a function $h : B \to A$
with $f \circ h = \id_B$.

thm:one-sided.title: One-Sided Inverses and Function Properties
thm:one-sided.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to B$. \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item $f$ has a left inverse $\iff$ $f$ is injective (and $A \neq \varnothing$ or $A=B=\varnothing$). \item $f$ has a right inverse $\iff$ $f$ is surjective. \item If $f$ has both a left inverse $g$ and a right inverse $h$, then $g = h$ and $f$ is bijective. \end{enumerate}
thm:one-sided.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f$ has a left inverse $\iff$ $f$ is injective (and $A \neq \varnothing$ or $A=B=\varnothing$).
\item $f$ has a right inverse $\iff$ $f$ is surjective.
\item If $f$ has both a left inverse $g$ and a right inverse $h$, then $g = h$
and $f$ is bijective.
\end{enumerate}
thm:one-sided.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to B$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f$ has a left inverse $\iff$ $f$ is injective (and $A \neq \varnothing$ or $A=B=\varnothing$).
\item $f$ has a right inverse $\iff$ $f$ is surjective.
\item If $f$ has both a left inverse $g$ and a right inverse $h$, then $g = h$
and $f$ is bijective.
\end{enumerate}

def:restriction.title: Restriction and Extension
def:restriction.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to B$ and $C \subseteq A$. The \emph{restriction} of $f$ to $C$ is $f|_C : C \to B$, $f|_C(a) = f(a)$ for all $a \in C$. Let $A \subseteq A'$, and let $f : A \to B$. A function $g : A' \to B$ is an \emph{extension} of $f$ if $g|_A = f$.
def:restriction.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $C \subseteq A$. The \emph{restriction} of $f$ to $C$
is $f|_C : C \to B$, $f|_C(a) = f(a)$ for all $a \in C$.

Let $A \subseteq A'$, and let $f : A \to B$. A function $g : A' \to B$ is an
\emph{extension} of $f$ if $g|_A = f$.
def:restriction.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $C \subseteq A$. The \emph{restriction} of $f$ to $C$
is $f|_C : C \to B$, $f|_C(a) = f(a)$ for all $a \in C$.

Let $A \subseteq A'$, and let $f : A \to B$. A function $g : A' \to B$ is an
\emph{extension} of $f$ if $g|_A = f$.

thm:preimage-ops.title: Preimages Preserve Set Operations
thm:preimage-ops.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to B$ and $S, T \subseteq B$. Then \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item $f^{-1}(S \cup T) = f^{-1}(S) \cup f^{-1}(T)$, \item $f^{-1}(S \cap T) = f^{-1}(S) \cap f^{-1}(T)$, \item $f^{-1}(S \setminus T) = f^{-1}(S) \setminus f^{-1}(T)$, \item $f^{-1}(S^c) = (f^{-1}(S))^c$. \end{enumerate}
thm:preimage-ops.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $S, T \subseteq B$. Then
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f^{-1}(S \cup T) = f^{-1}(S) \cup f^{-1}(T)$,
\item $f^{-1}(S \cap T) = f^{-1}(S) \cap f^{-1}(T)$,
\item $f^{-1}(S \setminus T) = f^{-1}(S) \setminus f^{-1}(T)$,
\item $f^{-1}(S^c) = (f^{-1}(S))^c$.
\end{enumerate}
thm:preimage-ops.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $S, T \subseteq B$. Then
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f^{-1}(S \cup T) = f^{-1}(S) \cup f^{-1}(T)$,
\item $f^{-1}(S \cap T) = f^{-1}(S) \cap f^{-1}(T)$,
\item $f^{-1}(S \setminus T) = f^{-1}(S) \setminus f^{-1}(T)$,
\item $f^{-1}(S^c) = (f^{-1}(S))^c$.
\end{enumerate}

thm:image-ops.title: Images and Set Operations
thm:image-ops.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to B$ and $S, T \subseteq A$. Then \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item $f(S \cup T) = f(S) \cup f(T)$, \item $f(S \cap T) \subseteq f(S) \cap f(T)$, \item $f(S \setminus T) \supseteq f(S) \setminus f(T)$. \end{enumerate} Equality holds in (ii) and (iii) for all $S,T$ iff $f$ is injective.
thm:image-ops.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $S, T \subseteq A$. Then
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f(S \cup T) = f(S) \cup f(T)$,
\item $f(S \cap T) \subseteq f(S) \cap f(T)$,
\item $f(S \setminus T) \supseteq f(S) \setminus f(T)$.
\end{enumerate}
Equality holds in (ii) and (iii) for all $S,T$ iff $f$ is injective.
thm:image-ops.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $S, T \subseteq A$. Then
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f(S \cup T) = f(S) \cup f(T)$,
\item $f(S \cap T) \subseteq f(S) \cap f(T)$,
\item $f(S \setminus T) \supseteq f(S) \setminus f(T)$.
\end{enumerate}
Equality holds in (ii) and (iii) for all $S,T$ iff $f$ is injective.

def:function.title: Function
def:function.statement_preview: Let $A$ and $B$ be sets. A \emph{function} from $A$ to $B$ is a relation $f \subseteq A \times B$ such that: \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item (\emph{Existence / left-total}) for every $a \in A$, there exists $b \in B$ with $(a,b) \in f$; \item (\emph{Uniqueness / right-unique}) if $(a,b_1) \in f$ and $(a,b_2) \in f$ then $b_1 = b_2$. \end{enumerate}...
def:function.statement_display:
Let $A$ and $B$ be sets. A \emph{function} from $A$ to $B$ is a relation
$f \subseteq A \times B$ such that:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item (\emph{Existence / left-total}) for every $a \in A$, there exists $b \in B$
  with $(a,b) \in f$;
\item (\emph{Uniqueness / right-unique}) if $(a,b_1) \in f$ and $(a,b_2) \in f$
  then $b_1 = b_2$.
\end{enumerate}
If $(a,b) \in f$ we write $f(a) = b$.
def:function.statement_tex:
Let $A$ and $B$ be sets. A \emph{function} from $A$ to $B$ is a relation
$f \subseteq A \times B$ such that:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item (\emph{Existence / left-total}) for every $a \in A$, there exists $b \in B$
  with $(a,b) \in f$;
\item (\emph{Uniqueness / right-unique}) if $(a,b_1) \in f$ and $(a,b_2) \in f$
  then $b_1 = b_2$.
\end{enumerate}
If $(a,b) \in f$ we write $f(a) = b$.

def:domain-codomain.title: Domain and Codomain
def:domain-codomain.statement_preview: If $f$ is a function from $A$ to $B$, we write $f : A \to B$, where $A$ is the \emph{domain} $\dom(f)$ and $B$ the \emph{codomain} $\cod(f)$.
def:domain-codomain.statement_display:
If $f$ is a function from $A$ to $B$, we write $f : A \to B$, where $A$ is
the \emph{domain} $\dom(f)$ and $B$ the \emph{codomain} $\cod(f)$.
def:domain-codomain.statement_tex:
If $f$ is a function from $A$ to $B$, we write $f : A \to B$, where $A$ is
the \emph{domain} $\dom(f)$ and $B$ the \emph{codomain} $\cod(f)$.

def:image-function.title: Image of a Function
def:image-function.statement_preview: The \emph{image} (or \emph{range}) of $f : A \to B$ is: \[ \im(f) \;:=\; \{\, b \in B \mid \exists a \in A,\ f(a)=b \,\}. \] The image may be a proper subset of the codomain.
def:image-function.statement_display:
The \emph{image} (or \emph{range}) of $f : A \to B$ is:
\[
\im(f) \;:=\; \{\, b \in B \mid \exists a \in A,\ f(a)=b \,\}.
\]
The image may be a proper subset of the codomain.
def:image-function.statement_tex:
The \emph{image} (or \emph{range}) of $f : A \to B$ is:
\[
\im(f) \;:=\; \{\, b \in B \mid \exists a \in A,\ f(a)=b \,\}.
\]
The image may be a proper subset of the codomain.

def:image-set.title: Image of a Set
def:image-set.statement_preview: For $S \subseteq A$, the \emph{image of $S$ under $f$} is: \[ f(S) \;:=\; \{\, f(a) \mid a \in S \,\}. \] Note $f(A) = \im(f)$.
def:image-set.statement_display:
For $S \subseteq A$, the \emph{image of $S$ under $f$} is:
\[
f(S) \;:=\; \{\, f(a) \mid a \in S \,\}.
\]
Note $f(A) = \im(f)$.
def:image-set.statement_tex:
For $S \subseteq A$, the \emph{image of $S$ under $f$} is:
\[
f(S) \;:=\; \{\, f(a) \mid a \in S \,\}.
\]
Note $f(A) = \im(f)$.

def:preimage.title: Preimage
def:preimage.statement_preview: For $T \subseteq B$, the \emph{preimage} (inverse image) of $T$ under $f$ is: \[ f^{-1}(T) \;:=\; \{\, a \in A \mid f(a) \in T \,\}. \]
def:preimage.statement_display:
For $T \subseteq B$, the \emph{preimage} (inverse image) of $T$ under $f$ is:
\[
f^{-1}(T) \;:=\; \{\, a \in A \mid f(a) \in T \,\}.
\]
def:preimage.statement_tex:
For $T \subseteq B$, the \emph{preimage} (inverse image) of $T$ under $f$ is:
\[
f^{-1}(T) \;:=\; \{\, a \in A \mid f(a) \in T \,\}.
\]

def:fiber.title: Fiber
def:fiber.statement_preview: The \emph{fiber} of $f$ over $b \in B$ is the preimage of the singleton: \[ f^{-1}(\{b\}) \;=\; \{\, a \in A \mid f(a) = b \,\}. \]
def:fiber.statement_display:
The \emph{fiber} of $f$ over $b \in B$ is the preimage of the singleton:
\[
f^{-1}(\{b\}) \;=\; \{\, a \in A \mid f(a) = b \,\}.
\]
def:fiber.statement_tex:
The \emph{fiber} of $f$ over $b \in B$ is the preimage of the singleton:
\[
f^{-1}(\{b\}) \;=\; \{\, a \in A \mid f(a) = b \,\}.
\]

def:graph.title: Graph of a Function
def:graph.statement_preview: The \emph{graph} of $f : A \to B$ is: \[ \operatorname{Graph}(f) \;:=\; \{\, (a,b) \in A \times B \mid b = f(a) \,\}. \]
def:graph.statement_display:
The \emph{graph} of $f : A \to B$ is:
\[
\operatorname{Graph}(f)
\;:=\;
\{\, (a,b) \in A \times B \mid b = f(a) \,\}.
\]
def:graph.statement_tex:
The \emph{graph} of $f : A \to B$ is:
\[
\operatorname{Graph}(f)
\;:=\;
\{\, (a,b) \in A \times B \mid b = f(a) \,\}.
\]

def:injective.title: Injective Function
def:injective.statement_preview: $f : A \to B$ is \emph{injective} (one-to-one) if distinct elements of $A$ have distinct images: \[ \forall a_1,a_2 \in A,\; f(a_1)=f(a_2) \;\Longrightarrow\; a_1=a_2. \]
def:injective.statement_display:
$f : A \to B$ is \emph{injective} (one-to-one) if distinct elements of $A$
have distinct images:
\[
\forall a_1,a_2 \in A,\;
f(a_1)=f(a_2) \;\Longrightarrow\; a_1=a_2.
\]
def:injective.statement_tex:
$f : A \to B$ is \emph{injective} (one-to-one) if distinct elements of $A$
have distinct images:
\[
\forall a_1,a_2 \in A,\;
f(a_1)=f(a_2) \;\Longrightarrow\; a_1=a_2.
\]

def:surjective.title: Surjective Function
def:surjective.statement_preview: $f : A \to B$ is \emph{surjective} (onto) if every element of $B$ is achieved: \[ \forall b \in B,\; \exists a \in A \text{ such that } f(a)=b. \] Equivalently, $\im(f) = B$.
def:surjective.statement_display:
$f : A \to B$ is \emph{surjective} (onto) if every element of $B$ is achieved:
\[
\forall b \in B,\; \exists a \in A \text{ such that } f(a)=b.
\]
Equivalently, $\im(f) = B$.
def:surjective.statement_tex:
$f : A \to B$ is \emph{surjective} (onto) if every element of $B$ is achieved:
\[
\forall b \in B,\; \exists a \in A \text{ such that } f(a)=b.
\]
Equivalently, $\im(f) = B$.

def:bijective.title: Bijective Function
def:bijective.statement_preview: $f : A \to B$ is \emph{bijective} if it is both injective and surjective.
def:bijective.statement_display: $f : A \to B$ is \emph{bijective} if it is both injective and surjective.
def:bijective.statement_tex: $f : A \to B$ is \emph{bijective} if it is both injective and surjective.

def:identity.title: Identity and Inclusion
def:identity.statement_preview: The \emph{identity function} on $A$ is $\id_A : A \to A$, $\id_A(a) = a$. If $A \subseteq B$, the \emph{inclusion map} is $\iota : A \hookrightarrow B$, $\iota(a) = a$.
def:identity.statement_display:
The \emph{identity function} on $A$ is $\id_A : A \to A$, $\id_A(a) = a$.

If $A \subseteq B$, the \emph{inclusion map} is $\iota : A \hookrightarrow B$,
$\iota(a) = a$.
def:identity.statement_tex:
The \emph{identity function} on $A$ is $\id_A : A \to A$, $\id_A(a) = a$.

If $A \subseteq B$, the \emph{inclusion map} is $\iota : A \hookrightarrow B$,
$\iota(a) = a$.

def:constant.title: Constant Function
def:constant.statement_preview: $f : A \to B$ is \emph{constant} if there exists $b_0 \in B$ with $f(a) = b_0$ for all $a \in A$.
def:constant.statement_display:
$f : A \to B$ is \emph{constant} if there exists $b_0 \in B$ with
$f(a) = b_0$ for all $a \in A$.
def:constant.statement_tex:
$f : A \to B$ is \emph{constant} if there exists $b_0 \in B$ with
$f(a) = b_0$ for all $a \in A$.

def:sym-asym.title: Symmetric and Asymmetric Parts
def:sym-asym.statement_preview: For any binary relation $R$ on $X$, define: \begin{itemize} \item the \emph{symmetric part}: $xIy \iff xRy \text{ and } yRx$ \item the \emph{asymmetric part}: $xPy \iff xRy \text{ but not } yRx$ \end{itemize} Note that $R = P \cup I$.

def:maximal-upper.title: Maximal Elements and Upper Bounds
def:maximal-upper.statement_preview: Let $\succsim$ be a binary relation on $X$. \begin{itemize} \item The set of \emph{maximal elements} of $X$ is \[ \mathrm{Max}(X, \succsim) = \{ x \in X \mid y \succsim x \text{ for no } y \in X \text{ with } y \neq x \}. \] \item The set of \emph{upper bounds} of $X$ is \[ M(X, \succsim) = \{ x \in X \mid x \succsim y \text{ for all } y \in X \}. \] \end{i...
def:maximal-upper.statement_display:
Let $\succsim$ be a binary relation on $X$.
\begin{itemize}
  \item The set of \emph{maximal elements} of $X$ is
    \[
      \mathrm{Max}(X, \succsim)
      = \{ x \in X \mid y \succsim x \text{ for no } y \in X \text{ with }
          y \neq x \}.
    \]
  \item The set of \emph{upper bounds} of $X$ is
    \[
      M(X, \succsim)
      = \{ x \in X \mid x \succsim y \text{ for all } y \in X \}.
    \]
\end{itemize}
def:maximal-upper.statement_tex:
Let $\succsim$ be a binary relation on $X$.
\begin{itemize}
  \item The set of \emph{maximal elements} of $X$ is
    \[
      \mathrm{Max}(X, \succsim)
      = \{ x \in X \mid y \succsim x \text{ for no } y \in X \text{ with }
          y \neq x \}.
    \]
  \item The set of \emph{upper bounds} of $X$ is
    \[
      M(X, \succsim)
      = \{ x \in X \mid x \succsim y \text{ for all } y \in X \}.
    \]
\end{itemize}

def:trans-closure.title: Transitive Closure
def:trans-closure.statement_preview: The \emph{transitive closure} $T(R)$ of a binary relation $R$ on $X$ is the smallest transitive relation containing $R$: that is, $T(R)$ is transitive, $xRy \Rightarrow xT(R)y$, and no strictly smaller relation is both transitive and contains $R$.
def:trans-closure.statement_display:
The \emph{transitive closure} $T(R)$ of a binary relation $R$ on $X$ is the
smallest transitive relation containing $R$: that is, $T(R)$ is transitive,
$xRy \Rightarrow xT(R)y$, and no strictly smaller relation is both transitive
and contains $R$.
def:trans-closure.statement_tex:
The \emph{transitive closure} $T(R)$ of a binary relation $R$ on $X$ is the
smallest transitive relation containing $R$: that is, $T(R)$ is transitive,
$xRy \Rightarrow xT(R)y$, and no strictly smaller relation is both transitive
and contains $R$.

def:preorder-extension.title: Extension of a Preorder
def:preorder-extension.statement_preview: Let $\succsim$ be a preorder on $X$. A preorder $\succsim'$ is an \emph{extension} of $\succsim$ if \[ x \succsim y \;\Rightarrow\; x \succsim' y, \qquad x \succ y \;\Rightarrow\; x \succ' y. \] A \emph{complete extension} is an extension that is also complete.
def:preorder-extension.statement_display:
Let $\succsim$ be a preorder on $X$. A preorder $\succsim'$ is an
\emph{extension} of $\succsim$ if
\[
  x \succsim y \;\Rightarrow\; x \succsim' y,
  \qquad
  x \succ y \;\Rightarrow\; x \succ' y.
\]
A \emph{complete extension} is an extension that is also complete.
def:preorder-extension.statement_tex:
Let $\succsim$ be a preorder on $X$. A preorder $\succsim'$ is an
\emph{extension} of $\succsim$ if
\[
  x \succsim y \;\Rightarrow\; x \succsim' y,
  \qquad
  x \succ y \;\Rightarrow\; x \succ' y.
\]
A \emph{complete extension} is an extension that is also complete.

cor:complete-preorder-extension.title: Complete preorder extension
