on $A/{\sim}$ is well-defined if and only if $O$ respects $\sim$.

def:strict-partial-order.title: Strict Partial Order
def:strict-partial-order.statement_preview: A \emph{strict partial order} is an irreflexive and transitive relation.
def:strict-partial-order.statement_display: A \emph{strict partial order} is an irreflexive and transitive relation.
def:strict-partial-order.statement_tex: A \emph{strict partial order} is an irreflexive and transitive relation.

def:strict-total-order.title: Strict Total Order
def:strict-total-order.statement_preview: A \emph{strict total order} is a strict partial order satisfying trichotomy.
def:strict-total-order.statement_display: A \emph{strict total order} is a strict partial order satisfying trichotomy.
def:strict-total-order.statement_tex: A \emph{strict total order} is a strict partial order satisfying trichotomy.

prop:sign-rule.title: Sign Rule
prop:sign-rule.statement_preview: In a ring, \[ (-a)\cdot b=-(a\cdot b) \qquad\text{and}\qquad a\cdot(-b)=-(a\cdot b). \]
prop:sign-rule.statement_display:
In a ring,
\[
(-a)\cdot b=-(a\cdot b)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
a\cdot(-b)=-(a\cdot b).
\]
prop:sign-rule.statement_tex:
In a ring,
\[
(-a)\cdot b=-(a\cdot b)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
a\cdot(-b)=-(a\cdot b).
\]

thm:no-zero-divisors.title: No Zero Divisors
thm:no-zero-divisors.statement_preview: In an integral domain, \[ a\cdot b=0 \Longrightarrow a=0 \lor b=0. \]
thm:no-zero-divisors.statement_display:
In an integral domain,
\[
a\cdot b=0 \Longrightarrow a=0 \lor b=0.
\]
thm:no-zero-divisors.statement_tex:
In an integral domain,
\[
a\cdot b=0 \Longrightarrow a=0 \lor b=0.
\]

thm:group-cancellation.title: Group Cancellation
thm:group-cancellation.statement_preview: In a group, cancellation holds on both sides: \[ a\ast c=b\ast c\Longrightarrow a=b \qquad\text{and}\qquad c\ast a=c\ast b\Longrightarrow a=b. \]
thm:group-cancellation.statement_display:
In a group, cancellation holds on both sides:
\[
a\ast c=b\ast c\Longrightarrow a=b
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
c\ast a=c\ast b\Longrightarrow a=b.
\]
thm:group-cancellation.statement_tex:
In a group, cancellation holds on both sides:
\[
a\ast c=b\ast c\Longrightarrow a=b
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
c\ast a=c\ast b\Longrightarrow a=b.
\]

thm:group-uniqueness-of-inverse.title: Group Uniqueness of Inverse
thm:group-uniqueness-of-inverse.statement_preview: In a group, each element has a unique inverse.
thm:group-uniqueness-of-inverse.statement_display: In a group, each element has a unique inverse.
thm:group-uniqueness-of-inverse.statement_tex: In a group, each element has a unique inverse.

thm:field-zero-product.title: Field Zero Product
thm:field-zero-product.statement_preview: In a field, \[ a\cdot b=0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad a=0\ \text{or}\ b=0. \]
thm:field-zero-product.statement_display:
In a field,
\[
a\cdot b=0
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
a=0\ \text{or}\ b=0.
\]
thm:field-zero-product.statement_tex:
In a field,
\[
a\cdot b=0
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
a=0\ \text{or}\ b=0.
\]

thm:ring-negation-of-product.title: Ring Negation of Product
thm:ring-negation-of-product.statement_preview: In a ring, \[ (-a)\cdot b=-(a\cdot b) \qquad\text{and}\qquad a\cdot(-b)=-(a\cdot b). \]
thm:ring-negation-of-product.statement_display:
In a ring,
\[
(-a)\cdot b=-(a\cdot b)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
a\cdot(-b)=-(a\cdot b).
\]
thm:ring-negation-of-product.statement_tex:
In a ring,
\[
(-a)\cdot b=-(a\cdot b)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
a\cdot(-b)=-(a\cdot b).
\]

thm:field-multiplicative-cancellation.title: Field Multiplicative Cancellation
thm:field-multiplicative-cancellation.statement_preview: In a field, \[ c\ne0\land a\cdot c=b\cdot c \Longrightarrow a=b. \]
thm:field-multiplicative-cancellation.statement_display:
In a field,
\[
c\ne0\land a\cdot c=b\cdot c
\Longrightarrow
a=b.
\]
thm:field-multiplicative-cancellation.statement_tex:
In a field,
\[
c\ne0\land a\cdot c=b\cdot c
\Longrightarrow
a=b.
\]

thm:field-uniqueness-of-inverse.title: Field Uniqueness of Inverse
thm:field-uniqueness-of-inverse.statement_preview: In a field, each nonzero element has a unique multiplicative inverse.
thm:field-uniqueness-of-inverse.statement_display: In a field, each nonzero element has a unique multiplicative inverse.
thm:field-uniqueness-of-inverse.statement_tex: In a field, each nonzero element has a unique multiplicative inverse.

thm:field-inverse-of-product.title: Field Inverse of Product
thm:field-inverse-of-product.statement_preview: In a field, if $a\ne0$ and $b\ne0$, then \[ (a\cdot b)^{-1}=a^{-1}\cdot b^{-1}. \]
thm:field-inverse-of-product.statement_display:
In a field, if $a\ne0$ and $b\ne0$, then
\[
(a\cdot b)^{-1}=a^{-1}\cdot b^{-1}.
\]
thm:field-inverse-of-product.statement_tex:
In a field, if $a\ne0$ and $b\ne0$, then
\[
(a\cdot b)^{-1}=a^{-1}\cdot b^{-1}.
\]

def:field-integer-power.title: Field Integer Power
def:field-integer-power.statement_preview: Let $F$ be a field. For $a\in F$, powers $a^n$ with $n\in\mathbb{W}$ are defined recursively. If $a\ne0$ and $n\in\mathbb{Z}$ is negative, define \[ a^n:=(a^{-1})^{-n}. \]
def:field-integer-power.statement_display:
Let $F$ be a field. For $a\in F$, powers $a^n$ with $n\in\mathbb{W}$ are
defined recursively. If $a\ne0$ and $n\in\mathbb{Z}$ is negative, define
\[
a^n:=(a^{-1})^{-n}.
\]
def:field-integer-power.statement_tex:
Let $F$ be a field. For $a\in F$, powers $a^n$ with $n\in\mathbb{W}$ are
defined recursively. If $a\ne0$ and $n\in\mathbb{Z}$ is negative, define
\[
a^n:=(a^{-1})^{-n}.
\]

thm:field-exponent-addition.title: Field Exponent Addition
thm:field-exponent-addition.statement_preview: In a field, if $a\ne0$ and $m,n\in\mathbb{Z}$, then \[ a^{m+n}=a^m\cdot a^n. \]
thm:field-exponent-addition.statement_display:
In a field, if $a\ne0$ and $m,n\in\mathbb{Z}$, then
\[
a^{m+n}=a^m\cdot a^n.
\]
thm:field-exponent-addition.statement_tex:
In a field, if $a\ne0$ and $m,n\in\mathbb{Z}$, then
\[
a^{m+n}=a^m\cdot a^n.
\]

thm:field-power-of-power.title: Field Power of Power
thm:field-power-of-power.statement_preview: In a field, if $a\ne0$ and $m,n\in\mathbb{Z}$, then \[ (a^m)^n=a^{m\cdot n}. \]
thm:field-power-of-power.statement_display:
In a field, if $a\ne0$ and $m,n\in\mathbb{Z}$, then
\[
(a^m)^n=a^{m\cdot n}.
\]
thm:field-power-of-power.statement_tex:
In a field, if $a\ne0$ and $m,n\in\mathbb{Z}$, then
\[
(a^m)^n=a^{m\cdot n}.
\]

thm:field-power-of-product.title: Field Power of Product
thm:field-power-of-product.statement_preview: In a field, if $a\ne0$, $b\ne0$, and $n\in\mathbb{Z}$, then \[ (a\cdot b)^n=a^n\cdot b^n. \]
thm:field-power-of-product.statement_display:
In a field, if $a\ne0$, $b\ne0$, and $n\in\mathbb{Z}$, then
\[
(a\cdot b)^n=a^n\cdot b^n.
\]
thm:field-power-of-product.statement_tex:
In a field, if $a\ne0$, $b\ne0$, and $n\in\mathbb{Z}$, then
\[
(a\cdot b)^n=a^n\cdot b^n.
\]

cor:generic-field-laws.title: Generic Field Laws
cor:generic-field-laws.statement_preview: Every field satisfies the zero-product law, cancellation laws, uniqueness of inverses, inverse laws, ring sign laws, and integer-exponent laws listed in this topic.
cor:generic-field-laws.statement_display:
Every field satisfies the zero-product law, cancellation laws, uniqueness of
inverses, inverse laws, ring sign laws, and integer-exponent laws listed in
this topic.
cor:generic-field-laws.statement_tex:
Every field satisfies the zero-product law, cancellation laws, uniqueness of
inverses, inverse laws, ring sign laws, and integer-exponent laws listed in
this topic.

thm:ordered-field-positive-multiplicatio.title: Ordered Field Positive Multiplication Preserves Order
thm:ordered-field-positive-multiplicatio.statement_preview: In an ordered field, \[ 0<c\land a<b \Longrightarrow c\cdot a<c\cdot b. \] The corresponding non-strict implication also holds.
thm:ordered-field-positive-multiplicatio.statement_display:
In an ordered field,
\[
0<c\land a<b
\Longrightarrow
c\cdot a<c\cdot b.
\]
The corresponding non-strict implication also holds.
thm:ordered-field-positive-multiplicatio.statement_tex:
In an ordered field,
\[
0<c\land a<b
\Longrightarrow
c\cdot a<c\cdot b.
\]
The corresponding non-strict implication also holds.

thm:ordered-field-negative-multiplicatio.title: Ordered Field Negative Multiplication Reverses Order
thm:ordered-field-negative-multiplicatio.statement_preview: In an ordered field, \[ c<0\land a<b \Longrightarrow c\cdot b<c\cdot a. \] The corresponding non-strict implication also holds.
thm:ordered-field-negative-multiplicatio.statement_display:
In an ordered field,
\[
c<0\land a<b
\Longrightarrow
c\cdot b<c\cdot a.
\]
The corresponding non-strict implication also holds.
thm:ordered-field-negative-multiplicatio.statement_tex:
In an ordered field,
\[
c<0\land a<b
\Longrightarrow
c\cdot b<c\cdot a.
\]
The corresponding non-strict implication also holds.

thm:ordered-field-fraction-comparison.title: Ordered Field Fraction Comparison
thm:ordered-field-fraction-comparison.statement_preview: In an ordered field, if $b\cdot d>0$, then \[ \frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad a\cdot d<c\cdot b. \]
thm:ordered-field-fraction-comparison.statement_display:
In an ordered field, if $b\cdot d>0$, then
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
a\cdot d<c\cdot b.
\]
thm:ordered-field-fraction-comparison.statement_tex:
In an ordered field, if $b\cdot d>0$, then
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
a\cdot d<c\cdot b.
\]

cor:generic-ordered-field-laws.title: Generic Ordered-Field Laws
cor:generic-ordered-field-laws.statement_preview: Every ordered field satisfies the standard positivity, order-compatibility, inverse-order, sign, and comparison laws listed in this topic.
cor:generic-ordered-field-laws.statement_display:
Every ordered field satisfies the standard positivity, order-compatibility,
inverse-order, sign, and comparison laws listed in this topic.
cor:generic-ordered-field-laws.statement_tex:
Every ordered field satisfies the standard positivity, order-compatibility,
inverse-order, sign, and comparison laws listed in this topic.

def:two-sided-identity.title: Two-Sided Identity
def:two-sided-identity.statement_preview: An element $e$ is a \emph{two-sided identity} for $O$ if it is both a left identity and a right identity.
def:two-sided-identity.statement_display:
An element $e$ is a \emph{two-sided identity} for $O$ if it is both a left
identity and a right identity.
def:two-sided-identity.statement_tex:
An element $e$ is a \emph{two-sided identity} for $O$ if it is both a left
identity and a right identity.

lem:left-right-identities-coincide.title: Left and Right Identities Coincide
lem:left-right-identities-coincide.statement_preview: If $e_L$ is a left identity and $e_R$ is a right identity for the same operation, then $e_L=e_R$.
lem:left-right-identities-coincide.statement_display:
If $e_L$ is a left identity and $e_R$ is a right identity for the same
operation, then $e_L=e_R$.
lem:left-right-identities-coincide.statement_tex:
If $e_L$ is a left identity and $e_R$ is a right identity for the same
operation, then $e_L=e_R$.

thm:uniqueness-of-identity.title: Uniqueness of Identity
thm:uniqueness-of-identity.statement_preview: A two-sided identity for a binary operation is unique.
thm:uniqueness-of-identity.statement_display: A two-sided identity for a binary operation is unique.
thm:uniqueness-of-identity.statement_tex: A two-sided identity for a binary operation is unique.

def:left-inverse.title: Left Inverse
def:left-inverse.statement_preview: An element $x'$ is a left inverse of $x$ with respect to identity $e$ if \[ x'\mathbin{O}x=e. \]
def:left-inverse.statement_display:
An element $x'$ is a left inverse of $x$ with respect to identity $e$ if
\[
x'\mathbin{O}x=e.
\]
def:left-inverse.statement_tex:
An element $x'$ is a left inverse of $x$ with respect to identity $e$ if
\[
x'\mathbin{O}x=e.
\]

def:right-inverse.title: Right Inverse
def:right-inverse.statement_preview: An element $x''$ is a right inverse of $x$ with respect to identity $e$ if \[ x\mathbin{O}x''=e. \]
def:right-inverse.statement_display:
An element $x''$ is a right inverse of $x$ with respect to identity $e$ if
\[
x\mathbin{O}x''=e.
\]
def:right-inverse.statement_tex:
An element $x''$ is a right inverse of $x$ with respect to identity $e$ if
\[
x\mathbin{O}x''=e.
\]

def:two-sided-inverse.title: Two-Sided Inverse
def:two-sided-inverse.statement_preview: An element $y$ is a two-sided inverse of $x$ if \[ y\mathbin{O}x=e \quad\text{and}\quad x\mathbin{O}y=e. \]
def:two-sided-inverse.statement_display:
An element $y$ is a two-sided inverse of $x$ if
\[
y\mathbin{O}x=e
\quad\text{and}\quad
x\mathbin{O}y=e.
\]
def:two-sided-inverse.statement_tex:
An element $y$ is a two-sided inverse of $x$ if
\[
y\mathbin{O}x=e
\quad\text{and}\quad
x\mathbin{O}y=e.
\]

lem:left-right-inverses-coincide.title: Left and Right Inverses Coincide
lem:left-right-inverses-coincide.statement_preview: In an associative operation with identity, a left inverse and a right inverse of the same element are equal.
lem:left-right-inverses-coincide.statement_display:
In an associative operation with identity, a left inverse and a right inverse
of the same element are equal.
lem:left-right-inverses-coincide.statement_tex:
In an associative operation with identity, a left inverse and a right inverse
of the same element are equal.

thm:uniqueness-of-inverse.title: Uniqueness of Inverse
thm:uniqueness-of-inverse.statement_preview: In an associative operation with identity, inverses are unique.
thm:uniqueness-of-inverse.statement_display: In an associative operation with identity, inverses are unique.
thm:uniqueness-of-inverse.statement_tex: In an associative operation with identity, inverses are unique.

def:right-solvability.title: Right Solvability
def:right-solvability.statement_preview: Right solvability means \[ \forall x,y\,\exists z,\ x=y\mathbin{O}z, \]
def:right-solvability.statement_display:
Right solvability means
\[
\forall x,y\,\exists z,\ x=y\mathbin{O}z,
\]
def:right-solvability.statement_tex:
Right solvability means
\[
\forall x,y\,\exists z,\ x=y\mathbin{O}z,
\]

def:left-solvability.title: Left Solvability
def:left-solvability.statement_preview: Left solvability means \[ \forall x,y\,\exists z,\ x=z\mathbin{O}y. \]
def:left-solvability.statement_display:
Left solvability means
\[
\forall x,y\,\exists z,\ x=z\mathbin{O}y.
\]
def:left-solvability.statement_tex:
Left solvability means
\[
\forall x,y\,\exists z,\ x=z\mathbin{O}y.
\]

def:two-sided-solvability.title: Two-Sided Solvability
def:two-sided-solvability.statement_preview: Two-sided solvability means both left solvability and right solvability hold.
def:two-sided-solvability.statement_display: Two-sided solvability means both left solvability and right solvability hold.
def:two-sided-solvability.statement_tex: Two-sided solvability means both left solvability and right solvability hold.

prop:solvability-iff-inverses.title: Solvability and Inverses
prop:solvability-iff-inverses.statement_preview: In a monoid, solvability is equivalent to the existence of inverses.
prop:solvability-iff-inverses.statement_display: In a monoid, solvability is equivalent to the existence of inverses.
prop:solvability-iff-inverses.statement_tex: In a monoid, solvability is equivalent to the existence of inverses.

def:two-sided-monotony.title: Two-Sided Monotony
def:two-sided-monotony.statement_preview: Two-sided monotony means both left monotony and right monotony hold.
def:two-sided-monotony.statement_display: Two-sided monotony means both left monotony and right monotony hold.
def:two-sided-monotony.statement_tex: Two-sided monotony means both left monotony and right monotony hold.

def:two-sided-order-reflection.title: Two-Sided Order Reflection
def:two-sided-order-reflection.statement_preview: Two-sided order reflection means both left order reflection and right order reflection hold.
def:two-sided-order-reflection.statement_display:
Two-sided order reflection means both left order reflection and right order
reflection hold.
def:two-sided-order-reflection.statement_tex:
Two-sided order reflection means both left order reflection and right order
reflection hold.

prop:addition-order-compatibility-left.title: Addition Preserves Order on the Left
prop:addition-order-compatibility-left.statement_preview: \[ y<z \Longrightarrow x+y<x+z \] in ordered arithmetic systems where addition is strictly order-preserving.
prop:addition-order-compatibility-left.statement_display:
\[
y<z \Longrightarrow x+y<x+z
\]
in ordered arithmetic systems where addition is strictly order-preserving.
prop:addition-order-compatibility-left.statement_tex:
\[
y<z \Longrightarrow x+y<x+z
\]
in ordered arithmetic systems where addition is strictly order-preserving.

prop:addition-order-compatibility-right.title: Addition Preserves Order on the Right
prop:addition-order-compatibility-right.statement_preview: \[ y<z \Longrightarrow y+x<z+x \] in ordered arithmetic systems where addition is strictly order-preserving.
prop:addition-order-compatibility-right.statement_display:
\[
y<z \Longrightarrow y+x<z+x
\]
in ordered arithmetic systems where addition is strictly order-preserving.
prop:addition-order-compatibility-right.statement_tex:
\[
y<z \Longrightarrow y+x<z+x
\]
in ordered arithmetic systems where addition is strictly order-preserving.

prop:addition-order-compatibility.title: Addition Preserves Order
prop:addition-order-compatibility.statement_preview: In ordered arithmetic systems where addition is strictly order-preserving, addition preserves order on both sides.
prop:addition-order-compatibility.statement_display:
In ordered arithmetic systems where addition is strictly order-preserving,
addition preserves order on both sides.
prop:addition-order-compatibility.statement_tex:
In ordered arithmetic systems where addition is strictly order-preserving,
addition preserves order on both sides.

prop:multiplication-order-compatibility-.title: Multiplication Preserves Order on the Left
prop:multiplication-order-compatibility-.statement_preview: \[ 0<x \land y<z \Longrightarrow x\cdot y<x\cdot z \] in ordered arithmetic systems where multiplication by positive elements is strictly order-preserving.
prop:multiplication-order-compatibility-.statement_display:
\[
0<x \land y<z \Longrightarrow x\cdot y<x\cdot z
\]
in ordered arithmetic systems where multiplication by positive elements is
strictly order-preserving.
prop:multiplication-order-compatibility-.statement_tex:
\[
0<x \land y<z \Longrightarrow x\cdot y<x\cdot z
\]
in ordered arithmetic systems where multiplication by positive elements is
strictly order-preserving.

prop:multiplication-order-compatibility-.title: Multiplication Preserves Order on the Right
prop:multiplication-order-compatibility-.statement_preview: \[ 0<x \land y<z \Longrightarrow y\cdot x<z\cdot x \] in ordered arithmetic systems where multiplication by positive elements is strictly order-preserving.
prop:multiplication-order-compatibility-.statement_display:
\[
0<x \land y<z \Longrightarrow y\cdot x<z\cdot x
\]
in ordered arithmetic systems where multiplication by positive elements is
strictly order-preserving.
prop:multiplication-order-compatibility-.statement_tex:
\[
0<x \land y<z \Longrightarrow y\cdot x<z\cdot x
\]
in ordered arithmetic systems where multiplication by positive elements is
strictly order-preserving.

prop:multiplication-order-compatibility.title: Multiplication Preserves Order
prop:multiplication-order-compatibility.statement_preview: In ordered arithmetic systems where multiplication by positive elements is strictly order-preserving, multiplication preserves order on both sides.
prop:multiplication-order-compatibility.statement_display:
In ordered arithmetic systems where multiplication by positive elements is
strictly order-preserving, multiplication preserves order on both sides.
prop:multiplication-order-compatibility.statement_tex:
In ordered arithmetic systems where multiplication by positive elements is
strictly order-preserving, multiplication preserves order on both sides.

def:trichotomy.title: Trichotomy
def:trichotomy.statement_preview: The relation $<$ satisfies \emph{trichotomy} if exactly one of \[ x<y,\qquad x=y,\qquad y<x \] holds for every pair $x,y$.
def:trichotomy.statement_display:
The relation $<$ satisfies \emph{trichotomy} if exactly one of
\[
x<y,\qquad x=y,\qquad y<x
\]
holds for every pair $x,y$.
def:trichotomy.statement_tex:
The relation $<$ satisfies \emph{trichotomy} if exactly one of
\[
x<y,\qquad x=y,\qquad y<x
\]
holds for every pair $x,y$.

def:homomorphism.title: Homomorphism
def:homomorphism.statement_preview: A map $f$ is a \emph{homomorphism} for an operation $O$ when \[ f(x\mathbin{O}y)=f(x)\mathbin{O'}f(y). \] For ring-like structures it preserves both addition and multiplication, and preserves $1$ when the language includes a distinguished multiplicative identity.
def:homomorphism.statement_display:
A map $f$ is a \emph{homomorphism} for an operation $O$ when
\[
f(x\mathbin{O}y)=f(x)\mathbin{O'}f(y).
\]
For ring-like structures it preserves both addition and multiplication, and
preserves $1$ when the language includes a distinguished multiplicative
identity.
def:homomorphism.statement_tex:
A map $f$ is a \emph{homomorphism} for an operation $O$ when
\[
f(x\mathbin{O}y)=f(x)\mathbin{O'}f(y).
\]
For ring-like structures it preserves both addition and multiplication, and
preserves $1$ when the language includes a distinguished multiplicative
identity.

def:injective-homomorphism.title: Injective Homomorphism
def:injective-homomorphism.statement_preview: An \emph{injective homomorphism} is a homomorphism whose underlying function is injective.
def:injective-homomorphism.statement_display:
An \emph{injective homomorphism} is a homomorphism whose underlying function
is injective.
def:injective-homomorphism.statement_tex:
An \emph{injective homomorphism} is a homomorphism whose underlying function
is injective.

def:structure-embedding.title: Structure Embedding
def:structure-embedding.statement_preview: A \emph{structure embedding} is an injective homomorphism that also embeds the relevant order relation.
def:structure-embedding.statement_display:
A \emph{structure embedding} is an injective homomorphism that also embeds the
relevant order relation.
def:structure-embedding.statement_tex:
A \emph{structure embedding} is an injective homomorphism that also embeds the
relevant order relation.

prop:composition-of-homomorphisms.title: Composition of Homomorphisms
prop:composition-of-homomorphisms.statement_preview: The composition of two homomorphisms is a homomorphism.
prop:composition-of-homomorphisms.statement_display: The composition of two homomorphisms is a homomorphism.
prop:composition-of-homomorphisms.statement_tex: The composition of two homomorphisms is a homomorphism.

cor:identity-map-is-homomorphism.title: Identity Map Is a Homomorphism
cor:identity-map-is-homomorphism.statement_preview: The identity map on a structure is a homomorphism from that structure to itself.
cor:identity-map-is-homomorphism.statement_display:
The identity map on a structure is a homomorphism from that structure to
itself.
cor:identity-map-is-homomorphism.statement_tex:
The identity map on a structure is a homomorphism from that structure to
itself.

def:complex-prepair.title: Complex Prepair
def:complex-prepair.statement_preview: A \emph{complex prepair} is an ordered pair $(a,b)\in\mathbb{R}\times\mathbb{R}$, read informally as the expression $a+bi$.
def:complex-prepair.statement_display:
A \emph{complex prepair} is an ordered pair $(a,b)\in\mathbb{R}\times\mathbb{R}$,
read informally as the expression $a+bi$.
def:complex-prepair.statement_tex:
A \emph{complex prepair} is an ordered pair $(a,b)\in\mathbb{R}\times\mathbb{R}$,
read informally as the expression $a+bi$.

thm:complex-coordinate-equivalence-relat.title: Complex Coordinate Equivalence Is an Equivalence Relation
thm:complex-coordinate-equivalence-relat.statement_preview: The relation $\sim_{\mathbb{C}}$ is an equivalence relation on \(\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{C}\).
thm:complex-coordinate-equivalence-relat.statement_display:
The relation $\sim_{\mathbb{C}}$ is an equivalence relation on
\(\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{C}\).
thm:complex-coordinate-equivalence-relat.statement_tex:
The relation $\sim_{\mathbb{C}}$ is an equivalence relation on
\(\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{C}\).

def:complex-numbers.title: Complex Numbers
def:complex-numbers.statement_preview: The complex numbers are the quotient \[ \mathbb{C}:=\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{C}/{\sim_{\mathbb{C}}}. \]
def:complex-numbers.statement_display:
The complex numbers are the quotient
\[
\mathbb{C}:=\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{C}/{\sim_{\mathbb{C}}}.
\]
def:complex-numbers.statement_tex:
The complex numbers are the quotient
\[
\mathbb{C}:=\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{C}/{\sim_{\mathbb{C}}}.
\]

def:complex-class-notation.title: Complex Class Notation
def:complex-class-notation.statement_preview: The notation $[a,b]_{\mathbb{C}}$ denotes the \(\sim_{\mathbb{C}}\)-equivalence class of the prepair $(a,b)$.
def:complex-class-notation.statement_display:
The notation $[a,b]_{\mathbb{C}}$ denotes the
\(\sim_{\mathbb{C}}\)-equivalence class of the prepair $(a,b)$.
def:complex-class-notation.statement_tex:
The notation $[a,b]_{\mathbb{C}}$ denotes the
\(\sim_{\mathbb{C}}\)-equivalence class of the prepair $(a,b)$.

def:zero-one-i-in-c.title: Zero, One, and the Imaginary Unit in $\mathbb{C}$

thm:embedding-r-into-c-injective.title: The Real Embedding into $\mathbb{C}$ Is Injective

thm:embedding-r-into-c-preserves-operati.title: The Real Embedding Preserves Addition and Multiplication

thm:embedding-r-into-c-preserves-zero-on.title: The Real Embedding Preserves Zero and One

thm:real-imaginary-parts-well-defined.title: Real and Imaginary Parts Are Well-Defined

thm:zero-additive-identity-on-c.title: Zero Is an Additive Identity on $\mathbb{C}$

thm:every-complex-has-additive-inverse.title: Every Complex Number Has an Additive Inverse

thm:one-multiplicative-identity-on-c.title: One Is a Multiplicative Identity on $\mathbb{C}$

thm:every-nonzero-complex-has-inverse.title: Every Nonzero Complex Number Has a Multiplicative Inverse

thm:c-is-a-field.title: $\mathbb{C}$ Is a Field
thm:c-is-a-field.statement_preview: With the operations defined above, \[ (\mathbb{C},+,\,\cdot,\,0_{\mathbb{C}},\,1_{\mathbb{C}}) \] is a field.
thm:c-is-a-field.statement_display:
With the operations defined above,
\[
(\mathbb{C},+,\,\cdot,\,0_{\mathbb{C}},\,1_{\mathbb{C}})
\]
is a field.
thm:c-is-a-field.statement_tex:
With the operations defined above,
\[
(\mathbb{C},+,\,\cdot,\,0_{\mathbb{C}},\,1_{\mathbb{C}})
\]
is a field.

def:congruence-modulo-integer.title: Congruence Modulo an Integer
def:congruence-modulo-integer.statement_preview: Let \(n\in\mathbb{Z}\) be positive. For \(a,b\in\mathbb{Z}\), define \[ a\equiv b \pmod n \quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad n\mid (a-b). \]
def:congruence-modulo-integer.statement_display:
Let \(n\in\mathbb{Z}\) be positive. For \(a,b\in\mathbb{Z}\), define
\[
a\equiv b \pmod n
\quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad
n\mid (a-b).
\]
def:congruence-modulo-integer.statement_tex:
Let \(n\in\mathbb{Z}\) be positive. For \(a,b\in\mathbb{Z}\), define
\[
a\equiv b \pmod n
\quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad
n\mid (a-b).
\]

thm:congruence-modulo-integer-equivalenc.title: Congruence Modulo an Integer Is an Equivalence Relation
thm:congruence-modulo-integer-equivalenc.statement_preview: For each positive integer \(n\), congruence modulo \(n\) is an equivalence relation on \(\mathbb{Z}\).
thm:congruence-modulo-integer-equivalenc.statement_display:

relation on \(\mathbb{Z}\).
thm:congruence-modulo-integer-equivalenc.statement_tex:

def:integers-modulo-n.title: Integers Modulo \(n\)
def:integers-modulo-n.statement_preview: Let \(n\in\mathbb{Z}\) be positive. The \emph{integers modulo \(n\)} are the quotient set \[ \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}:=\mathbb{Z}/{\equiv \pmod n}. \]
def:integers-modulo-n.statement_display:
Let \(n\in\mathbb{Z}\) be positive. The \emph{integers modulo \(n\)} are the
quotient set
\[
\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}:=\mathbb{Z}/{\equiv \pmod n}.
\]
def:integers-modulo-n.statement_tex:

quotient set
\[

\]

def:residue-class-modulo-n.title: Residue Class Modulo \(n\)
def:residue-class-modulo-n.statement_preview: For \(a\in\mathbb{Z}\), the \emph{residue class of \(a\) modulo \(n\)} is \[ [a]_n:=\{b\in\mathbb{Z}: b\equiv a\pmod n\}. \]
def:residue-class-modulo-n.statement_display:
For \(a\in\mathbb{Z}\), the \emph{residue class of \(a\) modulo \(n\)} is
\[
[a]_n:=\{b\in\mathbb{Z}: b\equiv a\pmod n\}.
\]
def:residue-class-modulo-n.statement_tex:
For \(a\in\mathbb{Z}\), the \emph{residue class of \(a\) modulo \(n\)} is
\[
[a]_n:=\{b\in\mathbb{Z}: b\equiv a\pmod n\}.
\]

def:addition-modulo-n.title: Addition Modulo \(n\)
def:addition-modulo-n.statement_preview: For residue classes modulo a positive integer \(n\), define \[ [a]_n+[b]_n:=[a+b]_n. \]
def:addition-modulo-n.statement_display:
For residue classes modulo a positive integer \(n\), define
\[
[a]_n+[b]_n:=[a+b]_n.
\]
def:addition-modulo-n.statement_tex:

def:multiplication-modulo-n.title: Multiplication Modulo \(n\)
def:multiplication-modulo-n.statement_preview: For residue classes modulo a positive integer \(n\), define \[ [a]_n\cdot[b]_n:=[ab]_n. \]

thm:modular-operations-well-defined.statement_display:

def:finite-modular-number-system.title: Finite Modular Number System
def:finite-modular-number-system.statement_preview: For a positive integer \(n\), the \emph{finite modular number system modulo \(n\)} is \[ \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z} \] with addition and multiplication modulo \(n\), additive identity \([0]_n\), and multiplicative identity \([1]_n\).
def:finite-modular-number-system.statement_display:
For a positive integer \(n\), the \emph{finite modular number system modulo
\(n\)} is
\[
\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}
\]
with addition and multiplication modulo \(n\), additive identity \([0]_n\),
and multiplicative identity \([1]_n\).
def:finite-modular-number-system.statement_tex:

and multiplicative identity \([1]_n\).

thm:z-mod-four-has-zero-divisors.title: \(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\) Has Zero Divisors
thm:z-mod-four-has-zero-divisors.statement_preview: In \(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\), the nonzero class \([2]_4\) satisfies \[ [2]_4\cdot[2]_4=[0]_4. \] Therefore \(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\) is not a field.
thm:z-mod-four-has-zero-divisors.statement_display:
In \(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\), the nonzero class \([2]_4\) satisfies
\[
[2]_4\cdot[2]_4=[0]_4.
\]
Therefore \(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\) is not a field.

def:number-system-construction-target.title: Number-System Construction Target
def:number-system-construction-target.statement_preview: A \emph{number-system construction target} is the specified algebraic and order structure that a proposed system is meant to satisfy. The target fixes the required carrier, operations, relations, distinguished elements, and laws.
def:number-system-construction-target.statement_display:
A \emph{number-system construction target} is the specified algebraic and
order structure that a proposed system is meant to satisfy. The target fixes

def:number-system-construction-data.title: Construction Data
def:number-system-construction-data.statement_preview: The \emph{construction data} for a number system consists of the raw carrier objects, the intended equality or equivalence relation, the distinguished elements, and the operation formulas from which the system is built.
def:number-system-construction-data.statement_display:

def:number-system-construction-verificat.title: Construction Verification
def:number-system-construction-verificat.statement_preview: \emph{Construction verification} is the proof that the construction data actually satisfies the construction target. It includes, as applicable, closure of operations, equivalence-relation proofs, well-definedness of operations on classes, algebraic laws, order laws, and compatibility of embeddings with earlier systems.

def:addition-on-two-element-system.statement_preview: Addition on \(\mathbb{B}_2\) is the binary operation \(+_2\) determined by \[ \begin{array}{c|cc} +_2 & 0 & 1\\ \hline 0 & 0 & 1\\ 1 & 1 & 0 \end{array} \]

1 & 1 & 0
\end{array}
\]

classes, \([0]_2\) and \([1]_2\), and its addition and multiplication tables
are the tables of \(\mathbb{F}_2\).

thm:embedding-chain-compatibility.title: Compatibility of the Embedding Chain
thm:embedding-chain-compatibility.statement_preview: Let $\iota_{1}:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{W}$, $\iota_{2}:\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{Z}$, $\iota_{3}:\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Q}$, and $\iota_{4}:\mathbb{Q}\to\mathbb{R}$ be the canonical embeddings. For every $n\in\mathbb{N}$ the composite $(\iota_{4}\circ\iota_{3}\circ\iota_{2}\circ\iota_{1})(n)$ is the real number identified with $n$, and the composite preserves the ari...
thm:embedding-chain-compatibility.statement_display: Let $\iota_{1}:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{W}$, $\iota_{2}:\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{Z}$, $\iota_{3}:\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Q}$, and $\iota_{4}:\mathbb{Q}\to\mathbb{R}$ be the canonical embeddings. For every $n\in\mathbb{N}$ the composite $(\iota_{4}\circ\iota_{3}\circ\iota_{2}\circ\iota_{1})(n)$ is the real number identified with $n$, and the composite preserves the arithmetic and order carried by the source system. Hence arithmetic computed in any system agrees with arithmetic on its image in $\mathbb{R}$.
thm:embedding-chain-compatibility.statement_tex: Let $\iota_{1}:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{W}$, $\iota_{2}:\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{Z}$, $\iota_{3}:\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Q}$, and $\iota_{4}:\mathbb{Q}\to\mathbb{R}$ be the canonical embeddings. For every $n\in\mathbb{N}$ the composite $(\iota_{4}\circ\iota_{3}\circ\iota_{2}\circ\iota_{1})(n)$ is the real number identified with $n$, and the composite preserves the arithmetic and order carried by the source system. Hence arithmetic computed in any system agrees with arithmetic on its image in $\mathbb{R}$.

def:embedding-n-into-w.statement_preview: The \emph{canonical embedding} $\iota:\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{W}$ is defined by \[ \iota(n)=n \quad(\text{the copy of each natural number inside the whole numbers}). \]
def:embedding-n-into-w.statement_display:
The \emph{canonical embedding} $\iota:\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{W}$ is defined by
\[
\iota(n)=n \quad(\text{the copy of each natural number inside the whole numbers}).
\]
def:embedding-n-into-w.statement_tex:
The \emph{canonical embedding} $\iota:\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{W}$ is defined by
\[
\iota(n)=n \quad(\text{the copy of each natural number inside the whole numbers}).
\]

thm:n-embeds-in-w.statement_preview: The canonical map $\iota:\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{W}$ identifies $\mathbb{N}$ with the positive part $\mathbb{W}\setminus\{0\}$ and preserves successor, $1$, addition, multiplication, and order.
thm:n-embeds-in-w.statement_display:

thm:q-embeds-in-r.statement_preview: The canonical map $\iota:\mathbb{Q}\to \mathbb{R}$ is an embedding of ordered arithmetic systems: it is injective, preserves $0$, $1$, addition, and multiplication, and reflects the order.
thm:q-embeds-in-r.statement_display: The canonical map $\iota:\mathbb{Q}\to \mathbb{R}$ is an embedding of ordered arithmetic systems: it is injective, preserves $0$, $1$, addition, and multiplication, and reflects the order.

def:structure-preserving-map.title: Structure-Preserving Map
def:structure-preserving-map.statement_preview: Let $A$ and $B$ be ordered arithmetic systems with a specified common source structure: selected constants, selected operations, and a strict order. A map $\varphi:A\to B$ is \emph{structure-preserving} exactly when it preserves each selected constant and, for every selected binary operation $\star$ and all $a,a'\in A$, \[ \varphi(a\star_A a')=\varphi(a)\st...
def:structure-preserving-map.statement_display:
Let $A$ and $B$ be ordered arithmetic systems with a specified common source
structure: selected constants, selected operations, and a strict order. A map
$\varphi:A\to B$ is \emph{structure-preserving} exactly when it preserves each
selected constant and, for every selected binary operation $\star$ and all
$a,a'\in A$,
\[
\varphi(a\star_A a')=\varphi(a)\star_B\varphi(a'),
\]
and $a<_A a' \Longrightarrow \varphi(a)<_B\varphi(a')$.
def:structure-preserving-map.statement_tex:
Let $A$ and $B$ be ordered arithmetic systems with a specified common source
structure: selected constants, selected operations, and a strict order. A map
$\varphi:A\to B$ is \emph{structure-preserving} exactly when it preserves each
selected constant and, for every selected binary operation $\star$ and all
$a,a'\in A$,
\[
\varphi(a\star_A a')=\varphi(a)\star_B\varphi(a'),
\]
and $a<_A a' \Longrightarrow \varphi(a)<_B\varphi(a')$.

def:number-system-embedding.title: Embedding of Number Systems
def:number-system-embedding.statement_preview: Let $A$ and $B$ be ordered arithmetic systems. A map $\varphi:A\to B$ is an \emph{embedding} exactly when $\varphi$ is injective and structure-preserving and is an order embedding.
def:number-system-embedding.statement_display: Let $A$ and $B$ be ordered arithmetic systems. A map $\varphi:A\to B$ is an \emph{embedding} exactly when $\varphi$ is injective and structure-preserving and is an order embedding.
def:number-system-embedding.statement_tex: Let $A$ and $B$ be ordered arithmetic systems. A map $\varphi:A\to B$ is an \emph{embedding} exactly when $\varphi$ is injective and structure-preserving and is an order embedding.

def:image-of-embedding.title: Image of an Embedding
def:image-of-embedding.statement_preview: Let $\varphi:A\to B$ be an embedding. The \emph{image} of $\varphi$ is the set $\varphi(A)=\{\,\varphi(a) : a\in A\,\}\subseteq B$, equipped with the selected constants, operations, and order of $B$ restricted to $\varphi(A)$.
def:image-of-embedding.statement_display: Let $\varphi:A\to B$ be an embedding. The \emph{image} of $\varphi$ is the set $\varphi(A)=\{\,\varphi(a) : a\in A\,\}\subseteq B$, equipped with the selected constants, operations, and order of $B$ restricted to $\varphi(A)$.
def:image-of-embedding.statement_tex: Let $\varphi:A\to B$ be an embedding. The \emph{image} of $\varphi$ is the set $\varphi(A)=\{\,\varphi(a) : a\in A\,\}\subseteq B$, equipped with the selected constants, operations, and order of $B$ restricted to $\varphi(A)$.

thm:embedding-identifies-with-image.title: Embeddings Identify a System with Its Image
thm:embedding-identifies-with-image.statement_preview: Let $\varphi:A\to B$ be an embedding of ordered arithmetic systems relative to a selected source structure. Then the corestriction $\varphi:A\to\varphi(A)$ is an isomorphism onto its image: it is a bijection that preserves and reflects the selected constants, operations, and order. Consequently $A$ may be identified with the corresponding substructure $\var...
thm:embedding-identifies-with-image.statement_display:
Let $\varphi:A\to B$ be an embedding of ordered arithmetic systems relative to
a selected source structure. Then the corestriction $\varphi:A\to\varphi(A)$
is an isomorphism onto its image: it is a bijection that preserves and reflects
the selected constants, operations, and order. Consequently $A$ may be
identified with the corresponding substructure $\varphi(A)\subseteq B$.
thm:embedding-identifies-with-image.statement_tex:
Let $\varphi:A\to B$ be an embedding of ordered arithmetic systems relative to
a selected source structure. Then the corestriction $\varphi:A\to\varphi(A)$
is an isomorphism onto its image: it is a bijection that preserves and reflects
the selected constants, operations, and order. Consequently $A$ may be
identified with the corresponding substructure $\varphi(A)\subseteq B$.

def:identity-relation.title: Identity
def:identity-relation.statement_preview: Two expressions $x$ and $y$ are \emph{identical} when they denote the same object.
def:identity-relation.statement_display:
Two expressions $x$ and $y$ are \emph{identical} when they denote the same
object.
def:identity-relation.statement_tex:
Two expressions $x$ and $y$ are \emph{identical} when they denote the same
object.

ax:leibniz-law.title: Leibniz's Law
ax:leibniz-law.statement_preview: Identical objects have exactly the same properties. \[ \forall x\,\forall y\, \Bigl(x=y \Longrightarrow \forall P\,\bigl(P(x)\Longleftrightarrow P(y)\bigr)\Bigr). \]
ax:leibniz-law.statement_display:
Identical objects have exactly the same properties.
\[
\forall x\,\forall y\,
\Bigl(x=y \Longrightarrow \forall P\,\bigl(P(x)\Longleftrightarrow P(y)\bigr)\Bigr).
\]
ax:leibniz-law.statement_tex:
Identical objects have exactly the same properties.
\[
\forall x\,\forall y\,
\Bigl(x=y \Longrightarrow \forall P\,\bigl(P(x)\Longleftrightarrow P(y)\bigr)\Bigr).
\]

def:equality-relation.title: Equality on a Domain
def:equality-relation.statement_preview: Let $A$ be a domain. \emph{Equality on $A$} is the relation, written $=$, that holds exactly when two terms or constructions denote the same element of $A$.
def:equality-relation.statement_display:
Let $A$ be a domain. \emph{Equality on $A$} is the relation, written $=$,
that holds exactly when two terms or constructions denote the same element of
$A$.
def:equality-relation.statement_tex:
Let $A$ be a domain. \emph{Equality on $A$} is the relation, written $=$,
that holds exactly when two terms or constructions denote the same element of
$A$.

def:definitional-propositional-equality.title: Definitional and Propositional Equality
def:definitional-propositional-equality.statement_preview: \emph{Definitional equality} records an introduced meaning, written with $:=$. \emph{Propositional equality} is a statement of sameness inside the theory, written with $=$.
def:definitional-propositional-equality.statement_display:
\emph{Definitional equality} records an introduced meaning, written with
$:=$. \emph{Propositional equality} is a statement of sameness inside the
theory, written with $=$.
def:definitional-propositional-equality.statement_tex:
\emph{Definitional equality} records an introduced meaning, written with
$:=$. \emph{Propositional equality} is a statement of sameness inside the
theory, written with $=$.

def:equivalence-relation.title: Equivalence Relation
def:equivalence-relation.statement_preview: Let $A$ be a set. A relation $\sim$ on $A$ is an \emph{equivalence relation} if it is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
def:equivalence-relation.statement_display:
Let $A$ be a set. A relation $\sim$ on $A$ is an \emph{equivalence relation}
if it is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
def:equivalence-relation.statement_tex:
Let $A$ be a set. A relation $\sim$ on $A$ is an \emph{equivalence relation}
if it is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.

cor:equality-is-finest-equivalence.title: Equality Is the Finest Equivalence
cor:equality-is-finest-equivalence.statement_preview: Let $\sim$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. For all $a,b\in A$, \[ a=b \Longrightarrow a\sim b. \]
cor:equality-is-finest-equivalence.statement_display:
Let $\sim$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. For all $a,b\in A$,
\[
a=b \Longrightarrow a\sim b.
\]
cor:equality-is-finest-equivalence.statement_tex:
Let $\sim$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. For all $a,b\in A$,
\[
a=b \Longrightarrow a\sim b.
\]

def:equivalence-class.title: Equivalence Class
def:equivalence-class.statement_preview: Let $\sim$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. The \emph{equivalence class} of $a\in A$ is \[ [a] := \{x\in A \mid x\sim a\}. \]
def:equivalence-class.statement_display:
Let $\sim$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. The \emph{equivalence class}
of $a\in A$ is
\[
[a] := \{x\in A \mid x\sim a\}.
\]
def:equivalence-class.statement_tex:
Let $\sim$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. The \emph{equivalence class}
of $a\in A$ is
\[
[a] := \{x\in A \mid x\sim a\}.
\]

thm:equivalence-classes-partition-domain.title: Equivalence Classes Partition the Domain
thm:equivalence-classes-partition-domain.statement_preview: The equivalence classes of an equivalence relation on $A$ are nonempty, cover $A$, and are pairwise disjoint.
thm:equivalence-classes-partition-domain.statement_display:
The equivalence classes of an equivalence relation on $A$ are nonempty, cover
$A$, and are pairwise disjoint.
thm:equivalence-classes-partition-domain.statement_tex:
The equivalence classes of an equivalence relation on $A$ are nonempty, cover
$A$, and are pairwise disjoint.

def:canonical-projection.title: Canonical Projection
def:canonical-projection.statement_preview: Let $\sim$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. The \emph{canonical projection} is the map \[ \pi:A\to A/{\sim}, \qquad \pi(a):=[a]. \]
def:canonical-projection.statement_display:
Let $\sim$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. The \emph{canonical projection}
is the map
\[
\pi:A\to A/{\sim},
\qquad
\pi(a):=[a].
\]
def:canonical-projection.statement_tex:
Let $\sim$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. The \emph{canonical projection}
is the map
\[
\pi:A\to A/{\sim},
\qquad
\pi(a):=[a].
\]

prop:projection-is-surjective.title: Projection Is Surjective
prop:projection-is-surjective.statement_preview: The canonical projection $\pi:A\to A/{\sim}$ is surjective.
prop:projection-is-surjective.statement_display: The canonical projection $\pi:A\to A/{\sim}$ is surjective.
prop:projection-is-surjective.statement_tex: The canonical projection $\pi:A\to A/{\sim}$ is surjective.

prop:projection-identifies-equivalent-el.title: Projection Identifies Exactly the Equivalent Elements

thm:existence-of-a-quotient.title: Existence of a Quotient
thm:existence-of-a-quotient.statement_preview: For any equivalence relation $\sim$ on $A$, the quotient set $A/{\sim}$ exists as the set of equivalence classes, and the canonical projection \[ \pi:A\to A/{\sim} \] is well-defined.
thm:existence-of-a-quotient.statement_display:
For any equivalence relation $\sim$ on $A$, the quotient set $A/{\sim}$ exists
as the set of equivalence classes, and the canonical projection
\[
\pi:A\to A/{\sim}
\]
is well-defined.
thm:existence-of-a-quotient.statement_tex:
For any equivalence relation $\sim$ on $A$, the quotient set $A/{\sim}$ exists
as the set of equivalence classes, and the canonical projection
\[
\pi:A\to A/{\sim}
\]
is well-defined.

def:operation-respects-equivalence.title: Operation Respects an Equivalence
def:operation-respects-equivalence.statement_preview: Let $O:A\times A\to A$ be a binary operation and let $\sim$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. The operation $O$ \emph{respects} $\sim$ if \[ \forall a,a',b,b'\in A,\, \bigl(a\sim a'\land b\sim b'\bigr) \Longrightarrow \bigl(a\mathbin{O}b\bigr)\sim\bigl(a'\mathbin{O}b'\bigr). \]
def:operation-respects-equivalence.statement_display:
Let $O:A\times A\to A$ be a binary operation and let $\sim$ be an equivalence
relation on $A$. The operation $O$ \emph{respects} $\sim$ if
\[
\forall a,a',b,b'\in A,\,
\bigl(a\sim a'\land b\sim b'\bigr)
\Longrightarrow
\bigl(a\mathbin{O}b\bigr)\sim\bigl(a'\mathbin{O}b'\bigr).
\]
def:operation-respects-equivalence.statement_tex:
Let $O:A\times A\to A$ be a binary operation and let $\sim$ be an equivalence
relation on $A$. The operation $O$ \emph{respects} $\sim$ if
\[
\forall a,a',b,b'\in A,\,
\bigl(a\sim a'\land b\sim b'\bigr)
\Longrightarrow
\bigl(a\mathbin{O}b\bigr)\sim\bigl(a'\mathbin{O}b'\bigr).
\]

def:left-right-respect-equivalence.title: Left and Right Respect
def:left-right-respect-equivalence.statement_preview: The operation $O$ \emph{respects} $\sim$ on the left if \[ a\sim a'\Longrightarrow (a\mathbin{O}b)\sim(a'\mathbin{O}b), \] and respects $\sim$ on the right if \[ b\sim b'\Longrightarrow (a\mathbin{O}b)\sim(a\mathbin{O}b'). \]
def:left-right-respect-equivalence.statement_display:
The operation $O$ \emph{respects} $\sim$ on the left if
\[
a\sim a'\Longrightarrow (a\mathbin{O}b)\sim(a'\mathbin{O}b),
\]
and respects $\sim$ on the right if
\[
b\sim b'\Longrightarrow (a\mathbin{O}b)\sim(a\mathbin{O}b').
\]
def:left-right-respect-equivalence.statement_tex:
The operation $O$ \emph{respects} $\sim$ on the left if
\[
a\sim a'\Longrightarrow (a\mathbin{O}b)\sim(a'\mathbin{O}b),
\]
and respects $\sim$ on the right if
\[
b\sim b'\Longrightarrow (a\mathbin{O}b)\sim(a\mathbin{O}b').
\]

lem:respect-splits.title: Respect Splits
lem:respect-splits.statement_preview: A binary operation $O$ respects $\sim$ if and only if it respects $\sim$ on the left and on the right.
lem:respect-splits.statement_display:
A binary operation $O$ respects $\sim$ if and only if it respects $\sim$ on
the left and on the right.
lem:respect-splits.statement_tex:
A binary operation $O$ respects $\sim$ if and only if it respects $\sim$ on
the left and on the right.

thm:induced-operation-on-quotient.title: Induced Operation on a Quotient
thm:induced-operation-on-quotient.statement_preview: If $O:A\times A\to A$ respects $\sim$, then \[ [a]\mathbin{\overline{O}}[b]:=[a\mathbin{O}b] \] defines a well-defined binary operation on $A/{\sim}$.
thm:induced-operation-on-quotient.statement_display:
If $O:A\times A\to A$ respects $\sim$, then
\[
[a]\mathbin{\overline{O}}[b]:=[a\mathbin{O}b]
\]
defines a well-defined binary operation on $A/{\sim}$.
thm:induced-operation-on-quotient.statement_tex:
If $O:A\times A\to A$ respects $\sim$, then
\[
[a]\mathbin{\overline{O}}[b]:=[a\mathbin{O}b]
\]
defines a well-defined binary operation on $A/{\sim}$.

thm:induced-unary-operation-on-quotient.title: Induced Unary Operation
thm:induced-unary-operation-on-quotient.statement_preview: Let $f:A\to A$. If \[ a\sim a'\Longrightarrow f(a)\sim f(a'), \] then \[ \overline{f}([a]):=[f(a)] \] defines a well-defined unary operation on $A/{\sim}$.
thm:induced-unary-operation-on-quotient.statement_display:
Let $f:A\to A$. If
\[
a\sim a'\Longrightarrow f(a)\sim f(a'),
\]
then
\[
\overline{f}([a]):=[f(a)]
\]
defines a well-defined unary operation on $A/{\sim}$.
thm:induced-unary-operation-on-quotient.statement_tex:
Let $f:A\to A$. If
\[
a\sim a'\Longrightarrow f(a)\sim f(a'),
\]
then
\[
\overline{f}([a]):=[f(a)]
\]
defines a well-defined unary operation on $A/{\sim}$.

def:predicate-respects-equivalence.title: Predicate Respects an Equivalence
def:predicate-respects-equivalence.statement_preview: Let $P$ be a predicate on $A$. The predicate $P$ \emph{respects} $\sim$ if \[ \forall a,a'\in A,\, a\sim a'\Longrightarrow \bigl(P(a)\Longleftrightarrow P(a')\bigr). \]
def:predicate-respects-equivalence.statement_display:
Let $P$ be a predicate on $A$. The predicate $P$ \emph{respects} $\sim$ if
\[
\forall a,a'\in A,\,
a\sim a'\Longrightarrow \bigl(P(a)\Longleftrightarrow P(a')\bigr).
\]
def:predicate-respects-equivalence.statement_tex:
Let $P$ be a predicate on $A$. The predicate $P$ \emph{respects} $\sim$ if
\[
\forall a,a'\in A,\,
a\sim a'\Longrightarrow \bigl(P(a)\Longleftrightarrow P(a')\bigr).
\]

thm:induced-predicate-on-quotient.title: Induced Predicate on a Quotient
thm:induced-predicate-on-quotient.statement_preview: If $P$ respects $\sim$, then \[ \overline{P}([a]):\Longleftrightarrow P(a) \] defines a well-defined predicate on $A/{\sim}$.
thm:induced-predicate-on-quotient.statement_display:
If $P$ respects $\sim$, then
\[
\overline{P}([a]):\Longleftrightarrow P(a)
\]
defines a well-defined predicate on $A/{\sim}$.
thm:induced-predicate-on-quotient.statement_tex:
If $P$ respects $\sim$, then
\[
\overline{P}([a]):\Longleftrightarrow P(a)
\]
defines a well-defined predicate on $A/{\sim}$.

def:relation-respects-equivalence.title: Relation Respects an Equivalence
def:relation-respects-equivalence.statement_preview: Let $R$ be a binary relation on $A$. The relation $R$ \emph{respects} $\sim$ if \[ \forall a,a',b,b'\in A,\, \bigl(a\sim a'\land b\sim b'\bigr) \Longrightarrow \bigl(R(a,b)\Longleftrightarrow R(a',b')\bigr). \]
def:relation-respects-equivalence.statement_display:
Let $R$ be a binary relation on $A$. The relation $R$ \emph{respects} $\sim$
if
\[
\forall a,a',b,b'\in A,\,
\bigl(a\sim a'\land b\sim b'\bigr)
\Longrightarrow
\bigl(R(a,b)\Longleftrightarrow R(a',b')\bigr).
\]
def:relation-respects-equivalence.statement_tex:
Let $R$ be a binary relation on $A$. The relation $R$ \emph{respects} $\sim$
if
\[
\forall a,a',b,b'\in A,\,
\bigl(a\sim a'\land b\sim b'\bigr)
\Longrightarrow
\bigl(R(a,b)\Longleftrightarrow R(a',b')\bigr).
\]

thm:induced-relation-on-quotient.title: Induced Relation on a Quotient
thm:induced-relation-on-quotient.statement_preview: If $R$ respects $\sim$, then \[ \overline{R}([a],[b]):\Longleftrightarrow R(a,b) \] defines a well-defined binary relation on $A/{\sim}$.
thm:induced-relation-on-quotient.statement_display:
If $R$ respects $\sim$, then
\[
\overline{R}([a],[b]):\Longleftrightarrow R(a,b)
\]
defines a well-defined binary relation on $A/{\sim}$.
thm:induced-relation-on-quotient.statement_tex:
If $R$ respects $\sim$, then
\[
\overline{R}([a],[b]):\Longleftrightarrow R(a,b)
\]
defines a well-defined binary relation on $A/{\sim}$.

thm:induced-map-out-of-quotient.title: Induced Map out of a Quotient
thm:induced-map-out-of-quotient.statement_preview: Let $g:A\to B$. If \[ a\sim a'\Longrightarrow g(a)=g(a'), \] then there is a unique well-defined map \[ \overline{g}:A/{\sim}\to B \] such that \[ \overline{g}([a])=g(a) \qquad\text{and}\qquad \overline{g}\circ\pi=g, \] where $\pi:A\to A/{\sim}$ is the canonical projection.
thm:induced-map-out-of-quotient.statement_display:
Let $g:A\to B$. If
\[
a\sim a'\Longrightarrow g(a)=g(a'),
\]
then there is a unique well-defined map
\[
\overline{g}:A/{\sim}\to B
\]
such that
\[
\overline{g}([a])=g(a)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\overline{g}\circ\pi=g,
\]
where $\pi:A\to A/{\sim}$ is the canonical projection.
thm:induced-map-out-of-quotient.statement_tex:
Let $g:A\to B$. If
\[
a\sim a'\Longrightarrow g(a)=g(a'),
\]
then there is a unique well-defined map
\[
\overline{g}:A/{\sim}\to B
\]
such that
\[
\overline{g}([a])=g(a)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\overline{g}\circ\pi=g,
\]
where $\pi:A\to A/{\sim}$ is the canonical projection.

thm:induced-partial-operation-on-quotien.title: Induced Partial Operation on a Quotient
thm:induced-partial-operation-on-quotien.statement_preview: Let $D\subseteq A$ be saturated under $\sim$, meaning \[ a\in D\land a\sim a'\Longrightarrow a'\in D. \] If $f:D\to A$ respects $\sim$ on $D$, then \[ \overline{f}([a]):=[f(a)] \] is a well-defined partial operation on $A/{\sim}$ with domain \[ D/{\sim}:=\{[a]\in A/{\sim}:a\in D\}. \]
thm:induced-partial-operation-on-quotien.statement_display:
Let $D\subseteq A$ be saturated under $\sim$, meaning
\[
a\in D\land a\sim a'\Longrightarrow a'\in D.
\]
If $f:D\to A$ respects $\sim$ on $D$, then
\[
\overline{f}([a]):=[f(a)]
\]
is a well-defined partial operation on $A/{\sim}$ with domain
\[
D/{\sim}:=\{[a]\in A/{\sim}:a\in D\}.
\]
thm:induced-partial-operation-on-quotien.statement_tex:
Let $D\subseteq A$ be saturated under $\sim$, meaning
\[
a\in D\land a\sim a'\Longrightarrow a'\in D.
\]
If $f:D\to A$ respects $\sim$ on $D$, then
\[
\overline{f}([a]):=[f(a)]
\]
is a well-defined partial operation on $A/{\sim}$ with domain
\[
D/{\sim}:=\{[a]\in A/{\sim}:a\in D\}.
\]

prop:representative-independence-is-nece.title: Representative Independence Is Necessary
prop:representative-independence-is-nece.statement_preview: If a rule on classes \[ [a]\mathbin{\overline{O}}[b]:=[a\mathbin{O}b] \] is well-defined on $A/{\sim}$, then $O$ respects $\sim$.
prop:representative-independence-is-nece.statement_display:
If a rule on classes
\[
[a]\mathbin{\overline{O}}[b]:=[a\mathbin{O}b]
\]
is well-defined on $A/{\sim}$, then $O$ respects $\sim$.
prop:representative-independence-is-nece.statement_tex:
If a rule on classes
\[
[a]\mathbin{\overline{O}}[b]:=[a\mathbin{O}b]
\]
is well-defined on $A/{\sim}$, then $O$ respects $\sim$.

cor:equality-is-equivalence-relation.title: Equality Is an Equivalence Relation
cor:equality-is-equivalence-relation.statement_preview: The equality relation is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
cor:equality-is-equivalence-relation.statement_display: The equality relation is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
cor:equality-is-equivalence-relation.statement_tex: The equality relation is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.

ax:equality-substitution.title: Substitution of Equals
ax:equality-substitution.statement_preview: For every formula $\varphi(z)$ whose substitution is admissible, \[ x=y \Longrightarrow \bigl(\varphi(x)\Longleftrightarrow\varphi(y)\bigr). \]
ax:equality-substitution.statement_display:
For every formula $\varphi(z)$ whose substitution is admissible,
\[
x=y \Longrightarrow \bigl(\varphi(x)\Longleftrightarrow\varphi(y)\bigr).
\]
ax:equality-substitution.statement_tex:
For every formula $\varphi(z)$ whose substitution is admissible,
\[
x=y \Longrightarrow \bigl(\varphi(x)\Longleftrightarrow\varphi(y)\bigr).
\]

prop:substitution-preserves-relations-le.title: Substitution Preserves Relations on the Left

prop:substitution-preserves-relations-ri.title: Substitution Preserves Relations on the Right

prop:congruence-with-respect-to-equality.title: Congruence with Respect to Equality Is Automatic
prop:congruence-with-respect-to-equality.statement_preview: Every function, predicate, relation, and operation respects equality in its displayed arguments.
prop:congruence-with-respect-to-equality.statement_display:
Every function, predicate, relation, and operation respects equality in its
displayed arguments.
prop:congruence-with-respect-to-equality.statement_tex:
Every function, predicate, relation, and operation respects equality in its
displayed arguments.

thm:z-has-no-zero-divisors.title: $\mathbb{Z}$ Has No Zero Divisors
thm:z-has-no-zero-divisors.statement_preview: For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$, \[ x\cdot y=0 \Longrightarrow x=0 \text{ or } y=0. \]
thm:z-has-no-zero-divisors.statement_display:
For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
x\cdot y=0 \Longrightarrow x=0 \text{ or } y=0.
\]
thm:z-has-no-zero-divisors.statement_tex:
For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
x\cdot y=0 \Longrightarrow x=0 \text{ or } y=0.
\]

thm:z-integral-domain.title: $\mathbb{Z}$ Is an Integral Domain
thm:z-integral-domain.statement_preview: The integers form an integral domain: $\mathbb{Z}$ is a commutative ring, $0\ne 1$, and $\mathbb{Z}$ has no zero divisors.
thm:z-integral-domain.statement_display:
The integers form an integral domain: $\mathbb{Z}$ is a commutative ring,
$0\ne 1$, and $\mathbb{Z}$ has no zero divisors.
thm:z-integral-domain.statement_tex:
The integers form an integral domain: $\mathbb{Z}$ is a commutative ring,
$0\ne 1$, and $\mathbb{Z}$ has no zero divisors.

cor:multiplicative-cancellation-on-z.title: Multiplicative Cancellation on $\mathbb{Z}$
cor:multiplicative-cancellation-on-z.statement_preview: For all $x,y,z\in\mathbb{Z}$, \[ z\ne 0 \land z\cdot x=z\cdot y \Longrightarrow x=y. \]
cor:multiplicative-cancellation-on-z.statement_display:
For all $x,y,z\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
z\ne 0 \land z\cdot x=z\cdot y \Longrightarrow x=y.
\]
cor:multiplicative-cancellation-on-z.statement_tex:
For all $x,y,z\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
z\ne 0 \land z\cdot x=z\cdot y \Longrightarrow x=y.
\]

cor:sign-rule-on-z.title: Sign Rule on $\mathbb{Z}$
cor:sign-rule-on-z.statement_preview: For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$, \[ (-x)\cdot y=-(x\cdot y) \qquad\text{and}\qquad x\cdot(-y)=-(x\cdot y). \]
cor:sign-rule-on-z.statement_display:
For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
(-x)\cdot y=-(x\cdot y)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
x\cdot(-y)=-(x\cdot y).
\]
cor:sign-rule-on-z.statement_tex:
For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
(-x)\cdot y=-(x\cdot y)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
x\cdot(-y)=-(x\cdot y).
\]

def:integer-prepair.title: Integer Prepair
def:integer-prepair.statement_preview: An \emph{integer prepair} is an ordered pair $(a,b)\in\mathbb{W}\times\mathbb{W}$, read as the formal difference $a-b$.
def:integer-prepair.statement_display:
An \emph{integer prepair} is an ordered pair $(a,b)\in\mathbb{W}\times\mathbb{W}$,
read as the formal difference $a-b$.
def:integer-prepair.statement_tex:
An \emph{integer prepair} is an ordered pair $(a,b)\in\mathbb{W}\times\mathbb{W}$,
read as the formal difference $a-b$.

thm:formal-difference-equivalence-relati.title: Formal-Difference Equivalence Is an Equivalence Relation
thm:formal-difference-equivalence-relati.statement_preview: The relation $\sim_{\mathbb{Z}}$ is an equivalence relation on $\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{Z}$.
thm:formal-difference-equivalence-relati.statement_display:
The relation $\sim_{\mathbb{Z}}$ is an equivalence relation on
$\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{Z}$.
thm:formal-difference-equivalence-relati.statement_tex:
The relation $\sim_{\mathbb{Z}}$ is an equivalence relation on
$\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{Z}$.

def:integer-class-notation.title: Integer Class Notation
def:integer-class-notation.statement_preview: The notation $[a,b]$ denotes the $\sim_{\mathbb{Z}}$-equivalence class of the prepair $(a,b)$.
def:integer-class-notation.statement_display:
The notation $[a,b]$ denotes the $\sim_{\mathbb{Z}}$-equivalence class of the
prepair $(a,b)$.
def:integer-class-notation.statement_tex:

prepair $(a,b)$.

thm:zero-is-an-integer.title: Zero Is an Integer
thm:zero-is-an-integer.statement_preview: The class $0_{\mathbb{Z}}$ is an element of $\mathbb{Z}$.
thm:zero-is-an-integer.statement_display: The class $0_{\mathbb{Z}}$ is an element of $\mathbb{Z}$.
thm:zero-is-an-integer.statement_tex: The class $0_{\mathbb{Z}}$ is an element of $\mathbb{Z}$.

thm:one-is-an-integer.title: One Is an Integer
thm:one-is-an-integer.statement_preview: The class $1_{\mathbb{Z}}$ is an element of $\mathbb{Z}$.
thm:one-is-an-integer.statement_display: The class $1_{\mathbb{Z}}$ is an element of $\mathbb{Z}$.
thm:one-is-an-integer.statement_tex: The class $1_{\mathbb{Z}}$ is an element of $\mathbb{Z}$.

def:divisibility-in-z.title: Divisibility in $\mathbb{Z}$
def:divisibility-in-z.statement_preview: For $a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$, define \[ a\mid b \quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad \exists c\in\mathbb{Z}\;(b=a\cdot c). \]
def:divisibility-in-z.statement_display:
For $a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$, define
\[
a\mid b
\quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\exists c\in\mathbb{Z}\;(b=a\cdot c).
\]
def:divisibility-in-z.statement_tex:
For $a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$, define
\[
a\mid b
\quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\exists c\in\mathbb{Z}\;(b=a\cdot c).
\]

prop:divisibility-transitive-on-z.title: Divisibility Is Transitive on $\mathbb{Z}$
prop:divisibility-transitive-on-z.statement_preview: For all $a,b,c\in\mathbb{Z}$, \[ a\mid b\land b\mid c \Longrightarrow a\mid c. \]
prop:divisibility-transitive-on-z.statement_display:
For all $a,b,c\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
a\mid b\land b\mid c \Longrightarrow a\mid c.
\]
prop:divisibility-transitive-on-z.statement_tex:
For all $a,b,c\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
a\mid b\land b\mid c \Longrightarrow a\mid c.
\]

prop:units-divide-everything-on-z.title: One and Negative One Divide Every Integer

prop:divisibility-compatible-with-negati.title: Divisibility Is Compatible with Negation on the Divisor
Divisibility Is Compatible with Negation on the Dividend
Divisibility Is Compatible with Negation on $\mathbb{Z}$
prop:divisibility-compatible-with-negati.statement_preview: For all $a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$, \[ a\mid b \Longleftrightarrow (-a)\mid b \Longleftrightarrow a\mid(-b). \]
prop:divisibility-compatible-with-negati.statement_display:
For all $a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
a\mid b \Longleftrightarrow (-a)\mid b \Longleftrightarrow a\mid(-b).
\]
prop:divisibility-compatible-with-negati.statement_tex:
For all $a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
a\mid b \Longleftrightarrow (-a)\mid b \Longleftrightarrow a\mid(-b).
\]

prop:divisibility-compatible-with-additi.title: Divisibility Is Compatible with Addition on $\mathbb{Z}$
prop:divisibility-compatible-with-additi.statement_preview: For all $a,b,c\in\mathbb{Z}$, \[ a\mid b\land a\mid c \Longrightarrow a\mid(b+c). \]
prop:divisibility-compatible-with-additi.statement_display:
For all $a,b,c\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
a\mid b\land a\mid c \Longrightarrow a\mid(b+c).
\]
prop:divisibility-compatible-with-additi.statement_tex:
For all $a,b,c\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
a\mid b\land a\mid c \Longrightarrow a\mid(b+c).
\]

prop:divisibility-respects-linear-combin.title: Divisibility Respects Linear Combinations on $\mathbb{Z}$
prop:divisibility-respects-linear-combin.statement_preview: For all $a,b,c,x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$, \[ a\mid b\land a\mid c \Longrightarrow a\mid(b\cdot x+c\cdot y). \]
prop:divisibility-respects-linear-combin.statement_display:
For all $a,b,c,x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
a\mid b\land a\mid c \Longrightarrow a\mid(b\cdot x+c\cdot y).
\]
prop:divisibility-respects-linear-combin.statement_tex:
For all $a,b,c,x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
a\mid b\land a\mid c \Longrightarrow a\mid(b\cdot x+c\cdot y).
\]

def:unit-in-z.title: Unit in $\mathbb{Z}$
def:unit-in-z.statement_preview: An integer $u\in\mathbb{Z}$ is a \emph{unit} if \[ \exists v\in\mathbb{Z}\;(u\cdot v=1). \]
def:unit-in-z.statement_display:
An integer $u\in\mathbb{Z}$ is a \emph{unit} if
\[
\exists v\in\mathbb{Z}\;(u\cdot v=1).
\]
def:unit-in-z.statement_tex:
An integer $u\in\mathbb{Z}$ is a \emph{unit} if
\[
\exists v\in\mathbb{Z}\;(u\cdot v=1).
\]

thm:units-of-z-are-pm-one.title: The Units of $\mathbb{Z}$ Are Exactly $\pm1$
thm:units-of-z-are-pm-one.statement_preview: An integer $u$ is a unit if and only if \[ u=1\quad\text{or}\quad u=-1. \]
thm:units-of-z-are-pm-one.statement_display:
An integer $u$ is a unit if and only if
\[
u=1\quad\text{or}\quad u=-1.
\]
thm:units-of-z-are-pm-one.statement_tex:
An integer $u$ is a unit if and only if
\[
u=1\quad\text{or}\quad u=-1.
\]

def:associates-in-z.title: Associates in $\mathbb{Z}$
def:associates-in-z.statement_preview: Integers $a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$ are \emph{associates} if $a=u\cdot b$ for some unit $u\in\mathbb{Z}$.
def:associates-in-z.statement_display:
Integers $a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$ are \emph{associates} if $a=u\cdot b$ for some
unit $u\in\mathbb{Z}$.
def:associates-in-z.statement_tex:
Integers $a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$ are \emph{associates} if $a=u\cdot b$ for some
unit $u\in\mathbb{Z}$.

thm:division-algorithm-on-z.title: Division Algorithm on $\mathbb{Z}$
thm:division-algorithm-on-z.statement_preview: If $a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$ and $b\ne0$, then there exist unique $q,r\in\mathbb{Z}$ such that \[ a=bq+r \qquad\text{and}\qquad 0\le r<|b|. \]
thm:division-algorithm-on-z.statement_display:
If $a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$ and $b\ne0$, then there exist unique
$q,r\in\mathbb{Z}$ such that
\[
a=bq+r
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
0\le r<|b|.
\]
thm:division-algorithm-on-z.statement_tex:
If $a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$ and $b\ne0$, then there exist unique
$q,r\in\mathbb{Z}$ such that
\[
a=bq+r
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
0\le r<|b|.
\]

def:greatest-common-divisor-in-z.title: Greatest Common Divisor in $\mathbb{Z}$
def:greatest-common-divisor-in-z.statement_preview: A greatest common divisor $\gcd(a,b)$ is the positive common divisor of $a$ and $b$ divisible by every other common divisor.
def:greatest-common-divisor-in-z.statement_display:
A greatest common divisor $\gcd(a,b)$ is the positive common divisor of
$a$ and $b$ divisible by every other common divisor.
def:greatest-common-divisor-in-z.statement_tex:
A greatest common divisor $\gcd(a,b)$ is the positive common divisor of
$a$ and $b$ divisible by every other common divisor.

thm:bezout-identity-on-z.title: Bezout Identity on $\mathbb{Z}$
thm:bezout-identity-on-z.statement_preview: For $a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$, there exist $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$ such that \[ \gcd(a,b)=a\cdot x+b\cdot y. \]
thm:bezout-identity-on-z.statement_display:
For $a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$, there exist $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$ such that
\[
\gcd(a,b)=a\cdot x+b\cdot y.
\]
thm:bezout-identity-on-z.statement_tex:
For $a,b\in\mathbb{Z}$, there exist $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$ such that
\[
\gcd(a,b)=a\cdot x+b\cdot y.
\]

thm:w-embeds-into-z.title: $\mathbb{W}$ Embeds into $\mathbb{Z}$
thm:w-embeds-into-z.statement_preview: The map $\iota:\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{Z}$ is an injective order-preserving semiring homomorphism.
thm:w-embeds-into-z.statement_display:
The map $\iota:\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{Z}$ is an injective order-preserving
semiring homomorphism.
thm:w-embeds-into-z.statement_tex:
The map $\iota:\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{Z}$ is an injective order-preserving
semiring homomorphism.

thm:every-integer-has-additive-inverse.title: Every Integer Has an Additive Inverse
thm:every-integer-has-additive-inverse.statement_preview: For every $x\in\mathbb{Z}$ there exists $y\in\mathbb{Z}$ such that \[ x+y=0_{\mathbb{Z}} \qquad\text{and}\qquad y+x=0_{\mathbb{Z}}. \]

For every $x\in\mathbb{Z}$ there exists $y\in\mathbb{Z}$ such that
\[
x+y=0_{\mathbb{Z}}
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
y+x=0_{\mathbb{Z}}.
\]

thm:z-multiplicative-commutative-monoid.statement_preview: The structure $(\mathbb{Z},\cdot,1_{\mathbb{Z}})$ is a commutative monoid.
thm:z-multiplicative-commutative-monoid.statement_display: The structure $(\mathbb{Z},\cdot,1_{\mathbb{Z}})$ is a commutative monoid.

thm:z-commutative-ring.statement_preview: The structure $(\mathbb{Z},+,\cdot,0_{\mathbb{Z}},1_{\mathbb{Z}})$ is a commutative ring.
thm:z-commutative-ring.statement_display:
The structure $(\mathbb{Z},+,\cdot,0_{\mathbb{Z}},1_{\mathbb{Z}})$ is a
commutative ring.

def:positive-integer.title: Positive Integer
def:positive-integer.statement_preview: An integer $x\in\mathbb{Z}$ is \emph{positive} if $x=[a,b]$ for some $a,b\in\mathbb{W}$ with $b<a$.
def:positive-integer.statement_display:
An integer $x\in\mathbb{Z}$ is \emph{positive} if $x=[a,b]$ for some
$a,b\in\mathbb{W}$ with $b<a$.
def:positive-integer.statement_tex:
An integer $x\in\mathbb{Z}$ is \emph{positive} if $x=[a,b]$ for some
$a,b\in\mathbb{W}$ with $b<a$.

thm:positivity-on-z-well-defined.title: Positivity on $\mathbb{Z}$ Is Well-Defined
thm:positivity-on-z-well-defined.statement_preview: The predicate ``$x$ is positive'' is well-defined on equivalence classes in $\mathbb{Z}$.
thm:positivity-on-z-well-defined.statement_display:
The predicate ``$x$ is positive'' is well-defined on equivalence classes in
$\mathbb{Z}$.
thm:positivity-on-z-well-defined.statement_tex:
The predicate ``$x$ is positive'' is well-defined on equivalence classes in
$\mathbb{Z}$.

def:strict-order-on-z.title: Strict Order on $\mathbb{Z}$
def:strict-order-on-z.statement_preview: For $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$, define \[ x<y \quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad y-x \text{ is positive}. \]
def:strict-order-on-z.statement_display:
For $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$, define
\[
x<y \quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad y-x \text{ is positive}.
\]
def:strict-order-on-z.statement_tex:
For $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$, define
\[
x<y \quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad y-x \text{ is positive}.
\]

def:order-on-z.title: Order on $\mathbb{Z}$
def:order-on-z.statement_preview: For $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$, define \[ x\le y \quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad x<y \text{ or } x=y. \]
def:order-on-z.statement_display:
For $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$, define
\[
x\le y \quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad x<y \text{ or } x=y.
\]
def:order-on-z.statement_tex:
For $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$, define
\[
x\le y \quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad x<y \text{ or } x=y.
\]

thm:canonical-form-of-integers.title: Canonical Form of Integers
thm:canonical-form-of-integers.statement_preview: For every $x\in\mathbb{Z}$, exactly one of the following holds: \[ x=[n,0]\text{ for some nonzero }n\in\mathbb{W},\qquad x=[0,0],\qquad x=[0,n]\text{ for some nonzero }n\in\mathbb{W}. \]
thm:canonical-form-of-integers.statement_display:
For every $x\in\mathbb{Z}$, exactly one of the following holds:
\[
x=[n,0]\text{ for some nonzero }n\in\mathbb{W},\qquad
x=[0,0],\qquad
x=[0,n]\text{ for some nonzero }n\in\mathbb{W}.
\]
thm:canonical-form-of-integers.statement_tex:
For every $x\in\mathbb{Z}$, exactly one of the following holds:
\[
x=[n,0]\text{ for some nonzero }n\in\mathbb{W},\qquad
x=[0,0],\qquad
x=[0,n]\text{ for some nonzero }n\in\mathbb{W}.
\]

cor:integers-split-by-sign.title: Integers Split by Sign
cor:integers-split-by-sign.statement_preview: Every integer is nonnegative or the negative of a nonnegative integer, and the two descriptions overlap only at $0_{\mathbb{Z}}$.
cor:integers-split-by-sign.statement_display:
Every integer is nonnegative or the negative of a nonnegative integer, and
the two descriptions overlap only at $0_{\mathbb{Z}}$.
cor:integers-split-by-sign.statement_tex:
Every integer is nonnegative or the negative of a nonnegative integer, and
the two descriptions overlap only at $0_{\mathbb{Z}}$.

cor:image-of-embedding-is-nonnegatives.title: Image of the Embedding Is the Nonnegative Integers
cor:image-of-embedding-is-nonnegatives.statement_preview: The image of the canonical embedding $\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{Z}$ is exactly the set of nonnegative integers.
cor:image-of-embedding-is-nonnegatives.statement_display:
The image of the canonical embedding $\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{Z}$ is exactly
the set of nonnegative integers.
cor:image-of-embedding-is-nonnegatives.statement_tex:
The image of the canonical embedding $\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{Z}$ is exactly
the set of nonnegative integers.

thm:sign-trichotomy-on-z.title: Sign Trichotomy on $\mathbb{Z}$
thm:sign-trichotomy-on-z.statement_preview: For every $x\in\mathbb{Z}$, exactly one of \[ x<0,\qquad x=0,\qquad 0<x \] holds.
thm:sign-trichotomy-on-z.statement_display:
For every $x\in\mathbb{Z}$, exactly one of
\[
x<0,\qquad x=0,\qquad 0<x
\]
holds.
thm:sign-trichotomy-on-z.statement_tex:
For every $x\in\mathbb{Z}$, exactly one of
\[
x<0,\qquad x=0,\qquad 0<x
\]
holds.

thm:trichotomy-on-z.title: Trichotomy on $\mathbb{Z}$
thm:trichotomy-on-z.statement_preview: For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$, exactly one of \[ x<y,\qquad x=y,\qquad y<x \] holds.
thm:trichotomy-on-z.statement_display:
For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$, exactly one of
\[
x<y,\qquad x=y,\qquad y<x
\]
holds.
thm:trichotomy-on-z.statement_tex:
For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$, exactly one of
\[
x<y,\qquad x=y,\qquad y<x
\]
holds.

thm:order-on-z-antisymmetric.title: Order on $\mathbb{Z}$ Is Antisymmetric
thm:order-on-z-antisymmetric.statement_preview: For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$, \[ x\le y\land y\le x \Longrightarrow x=y. \]
thm:order-on-z-antisymmetric.statement_display:
For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
x\le y\land y\le x \Longrightarrow x=y.
\]
thm:order-on-z-antisymmetric.statement_tex:
For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
x\le y\land y\le x \Longrightarrow x=y.
\]

thm:order-on-z-transitive.title: Order on $\mathbb{Z}$ Is Transitive
thm:order-on-z-transitive.statement_preview: For all $x,y,z\in\mathbb{Z}$, \[ x\le y\land y\le z \Longrightarrow x\le z. \]
thm:order-on-z-transitive.statement_display:
For all $x,y,z\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
x\le y\land y\le z \Longrightarrow x\le z.
\]
thm:order-on-z-transitive.statement_tex:
For all $x,y,z\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
x\le y\land y\le z \Longrightarrow x\le z.
\]

thm:order-on-z-total.title: Order on $\mathbb{Z}$ Is Total
thm:order-on-z-total.statement_preview: For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$, \[ x\le y\text{ or }y\le x. \]
thm:order-on-z-total.statement_display:
For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
x\le y\text{ or }y\le x.
\]
thm:order-on-z-total.statement_tex:
For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
x\le y\text{ or }y\le x.
\]

thm:z-totally-ordered-set.statement_display: The ordered pair $(\mathbb{Z},\le)$ is a totally ordered set.
thm:z-totally-ordered-set.statement_tex: The ordered pair $(\mathbb{Z},\le)$ is a totally ordered set.

thm:left-addition-preserves-order-on-z.statement_tex:

For all $x,y,z\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
x\le y \Longrightarrow x+z\le y+z.

cor:right-addition-preserves-order-on-z.statement_tex:

def:addition-on-peano-system.title: Addition on a Peano System
def:addition-on-peano-system.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For each $m \in P$, define the function \[ f_m : P \to P \] to be the unique function satisfying \[ f_m(1)=S(m) \qquad\text{and}\qquad \forall n \in P\;(f_m(S(n))=S(f_m(n))). \] For $m,n \in P$, define \[ m+n \coloneqq f_m(n). \]
def:addition-on-peano-system.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For each $m \in P$, define the function
\[
f_m : P \to P
\]
to be the unique function satisfying
\[
f_m(1)=S(m)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(f_m(S(n))=S(f_m(n))).
\]
For $m,n \in P$, define
\[
m+n \coloneqq f_m(n).
\]
def:addition-on-peano-system.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For each $m \in P$, define the function
\[
f_m : P \to P
\]
to be the unique function satisfying
\[
f_m(1)=S(m)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(f_m(S(n))=S(f_m(n))).
\]
For $m,n \in P$, define
\[
m+n \coloneqq f_m(n).
\]

thm:addition-well-defined-on-peano-syste.title: Addition Is Well-Defined on a Peano System
thm:addition-well-defined-on-peano-syste.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m \in P$, there exists a unique function \[ f_m : P \to P \] such that \[ f_m(1)=S(m) \qquad\text{and}\qquad \forall n \in P\;(f_m(S(n))=S(f_m(n))). \] Consequently the rule \[ (m,n) \longmapsto m+n \coloneqq f_m(n) \] defines a unique binary operation on $P$.
thm:addition-well-defined-on-peano-syste.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m \in P$, there exists a unique
function
\[
f_m : P \to P
\]
such that
\[
f_m(1)=S(m)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(f_m(S(n))=S(f_m(n))).
\]
Consequently the rule
\[
(m,n) \longmapsto m+n \coloneqq f_m(n)
\]
defines a unique binary operation on $P$.
thm:addition-well-defined-on-peano-syste.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m \in P$, there exists a unique
function
\[
f_m : P \to P
\]
such that
\[
f_m(1)=S(m)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(f_m(S(n))=S(f_m(n))).
\]
Consequently the rule
\[
(m,n) \longmapsto m+n \coloneqq f_m(n)
\]
defines a unique binary operation on $P$.
