thm:n-additive-structure.title: $\mathbb{N}$ Is Closed, Associative, and Commutative Under Addition
thm:n-additive-structure.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. The binary operation $+$ on $P$ satisfies: \begin{enumerate} \item \textbf{Closure.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a+b \in P$. \item \textbf{Associativity.} For all $a,b,c \in P$, $(a+b)+c = a+(b+c)$. \item \textbf{Commutativity.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a+b = b+a$. \item \textbf{Left cancellation.} For all $a,b,c \in P$, if $a+b = a+...
thm:n-additive-structure.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. The binary operation $+$ on $P$ satisfies:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Closure.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a+b \in P$.
  \item \textbf{Associativity.} For all $a,b,c \in P$,
  $(a+b)+c = a+(b+c)$.
  \item \textbf{Commutativity.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a+b = b+a$.
  \item \textbf{Left cancellation.} For all $a,b,c \in P$, if $a+b = a+c$ then $b=c$.
  \item \textbf{Right cancellation.} For all $a,b,c \in P$, if $b+a = c+a$ then $b=c$.
\end{enumerate}
thm:n-additive-structure.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. The binary operation $+$ on $P$ satisfies:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Closure.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a+b \in P$.
  \item \textbf{Associativity.} For all $a,b,c \in P$,
  $(a+b)+c = a+(b+c)$.
  \item \textbf{Commutativity.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a+b = b+a$.
  \item \textbf{Left cancellation.} For all $a,b,c \in P$, if $a+b = a+c$ then $b=c$.
  \item \textbf{Right cancellation.} For all $a,b,c \in P$, if $b+a = c+a$ then $b=c$.
\end{enumerate}

thm:n-multiplicative-monoid.title: $\mathbb{N}$ Is a Commutative Monoid Under Multiplication
thm:n-multiplicative-monoid.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. The binary operation $\cdot$ on $P$ satisfies: \begin{enumerate} \item \textbf{Closure.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a \cdot b \in P$. \item \textbf{Associativity.} For all $a,b,c \in P$, $(a \cdot b) \cdot c = a \cdot (b \cdot c)$. \item \textbf{Commutativity.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a \cdot b = b \cdot a$. \item \textbf{Identity....
thm:n-multiplicative-monoid.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. The binary operation $\cdot$ on $P$ satisfies:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Closure.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a \cdot b \in P$.
  \item \textbf{Associativity.} For all $a,b,c \in P$,
  $(a \cdot b) \cdot c = a \cdot (b \cdot c)$.
  \item \textbf{Commutativity.} For all $a,b \in P$,
  $a \cdot b = b \cdot a$.
  \item \textbf{Identity.} For all $a \in P$,
  $1 \cdot a = a = a \cdot 1$.
\end{enumerate}
Consequently $(P, \cdot, 1)$ is a commutative monoid.
thm:n-multiplicative-monoid.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. The binary operation $\cdot$ on $P$ satisfies:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Closure.} For all $a,b \in P$, $a \cdot b \in P$.
  \item \textbf{Associativity.} For all $a,b,c \in P$,
  $(a \cdot b) \cdot c = a \cdot (b \cdot c)$.
  \item \textbf{Commutativity.} For all $a,b \in P$,
  $a \cdot b = b \cdot a$.
  \item \textbf{Identity.} For all $a \in P$,
  $1 \cdot a = a = a \cdot 1$.
\end{enumerate}
Consequently $(P, \cdot, 1)$ is a commutative monoid.

thm:n-is-semiring.title: $\mathbb{N}$ Is a Semiring
thm:n-is-semiring.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. Then $(P,+,\cdot)$ satisfies: \begin{enumerate} \item $(P,+)$ is a commutative semigroup. \item $(P,\cdot,1)$ is a commutative monoid. \item Multiplication distributes over addition: for all $a,b,c \in P$, \[ a \cdot (b+c) = (a \cdot b) + (a \cdot c). \] \end{enumerate}
thm:n-is-semiring.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. Then $(P,+,\cdot)$ satisfies:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item $(P,+)$ is a commutative semigroup.
  \item $(P,\cdot,1)$ is a commutative monoid.
  \item Multiplication distributes over addition: for all $a,b,c \in P$,
  \[
  a \cdot (b+c) = (a \cdot b) + (a \cdot c).
  \]
\end{enumerate}
thm:n-is-semiring.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. Then $(P,+,\cdot)$ satisfies:

  \item $(P,\cdot,1)$ is a commutative monoid.
  \item Multiplication distributes over addition: for all $a,b,c \in P$,

  \]
\end{enumerate}

thm:induction-from-arbitrary-base.title: Induction from an Arbitrary Base
thm:induction-from-arbitrary-base.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $m \in P$, and let $\varphi$ be a predicate on $P$. If \[ \varphi(m) \qquad\text{and}\qquad \forall n \in P\;\bigl(n \ge m \land \varphi(n) \Longrightarrow \varphi(S(n))\bigr), \] then \[ \forall n \in P\;\bigl(n \ge m \Longrightarrow \varphi(n)\bigr). \]

\]
then
\[
\forall n \in P\;\bigl(n \ge m \Longrightarrow \varphi(n)\bigr).
\]

def:multiplication-on-peano-system.title: Multiplication on a Peano System
def:multiplication-on-peano-system.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For each $m \in P$, define the function \[ g_m : P \to P \] to be the unique function satisfying \[ g_m(1)=m \qquad\text{and}\qquad \forall n \in P\;(g_m(S(n))=g_m(n)+m). \] For $m,n \in P$, define \[ m \cdot n \coloneqq g_m(n). \]

\]
thm:multiplication-with-one.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m \in P$,
\[
m \cdot 1 = m.
\]

thm:multiplication-successor-on-right.title: Multiplication Successor on the Right
thm:multiplication-successor-on-right.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m,n \in P$, \[ m \cdot S(n) = (m \cdot n) + m. \]
thm:multiplication-successor-on-right.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m,n \in P$,
\[
m \cdot S(n) = (m \cdot n) + m.
\]
thm:multiplication-successor-on-right.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $m,n \in P$,
\[
m \cdot S(n) = (m \cdot n) + m.
\]

thm:one-times-n.title: One Times $n$
thm:one-times-n.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $n \in P$, \[ 1 \cdot n = n. \]
thm:one-times-n.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $n \in P$,
\[
1 \cdot n = n.
\]
thm:one-times-n.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $n \in P$,
\[

\]

thm:multiplication-distributes-over-addi.title: Multiplication Distributes Over Addition
thm:multiplication-distributes-over-addi.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$, \[ a \cdot (b+c) = (a \cdot b) + (a \cdot c). \]
thm:multiplication-distributes-over-addi.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a \cdot (b+c) = (a \cdot b) + (a \cdot c).
\]
thm:multiplication-distributes-over-addi.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a \cdot (b+c) = (a \cdot b) + (a \cdot c).
\]

thm:left-distributivity-of-multiplicatio.title: Left Distributivity of Multiplication Over Addition
thm:left-distributivity-of-multiplicatio.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$, \[ (a+b)\cdot c=(a\cdot c)+(b\cdot c). \]
thm:left-distributivity-of-multiplicatio.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
(a+b)\cdot c=(a\cdot c)+(b\cdot c).
\]
thm:left-distributivity-of-multiplicatio.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
(a+b)\cdot c=(a\cdot c)+(b\cdot c).
\]

thm:multiplication-distributes-over-addi.title: Multiplication Distributes over Addition on Both Sides
thm:multiplication-distributes-over-addi.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$, \[ a\cdot(b+c)=(a\cdot b)+(a\cdot c) \qquad\text{and}\qquad (a+b)\cdot c=(a\cdot c)+(b\cdot c). \]
thm:multiplication-distributes-over-addi.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a\cdot(b+c)=(a\cdot b)+(a\cdot c)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
(a+b)\cdot c=(a\cdot c)+(b\cdot c).
\]
thm:multiplication-distributes-over-addi.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a\cdot(b+c)=(a\cdot b)+(a\cdot c)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
(a+b)\cdot c=(a\cdot c)+(b\cdot c).
\]

thm:multiplication-associative.title: Multiplication Is Associative
thm:multiplication-associative.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$, \[ (a \cdot b) \cdot c = a \cdot (b \cdot c). \]
thm:multiplication-associative.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
(a \cdot b) \cdot c = a \cdot (b \cdot c).
\]
thm:multiplication-associative.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
(a \cdot b) \cdot c = a \cdot (b \cdot c).
\]

thm:multiplication-commutative.title: Multiplication Is Commutative
thm:multiplication-commutative.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$, \[ a \cdot b = b \cdot a. \]
thm:multiplication-commutative.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$,
\[
a \cdot b = b \cdot a.
\]
thm:multiplication-commutative.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$,
\[
a \cdot b = b \cdot a.
\]

thm:multiplication-left-cancellation.title: Left Cancellation for Multiplication
thm:multiplication-left-cancellation.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$, \[ a\cdot b=a\cdot c \Longrightarrow b=c. \]
thm:multiplication-left-cancellation.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a\cdot b=a\cdot c
\Longrightarrow
b=c.
\]
thm:multiplication-left-cancellation.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a\cdot b=a\cdot c
\Longrightarrow
b=c.
\]

thm:multiplication-right-cancellation.title: Right Cancellation for Multiplication
thm:multiplication-right-cancellation.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$, \[ b\cdot a=c\cdot a \Longrightarrow b=c. \]
thm:multiplication-right-cancellation.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
b\cdot a=c\cdot a
\Longrightarrow
b=c.
\]
thm:multiplication-right-cancellation.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
b\cdot a=c\cdot a
\Longrightarrow
b=c.
\]

thm:multiplication-cancellation.title: Cancellation for Multiplication
thm:multiplication-cancellation.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. Multiplication on $P$ satisfies cancellation on both sides: \[ a\cdot b=a\cdot c\Longrightarrow b=c \qquad\text{and}\qquad b\cdot a=c\cdot a\Longrightarrow b=c. \]
thm:multiplication-cancellation.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. Multiplication on $P$ satisfies cancellation
on both sides:
\[
a\cdot b=a\cdot c\Longrightarrow b=c
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
b\cdot a=c\cdot a\Longrightarrow b=c.
\]
thm:multiplication-cancellation.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. Multiplication on $P$ satisfies cancellation
on both sides:
\[
a\cdot b=a\cdot c\Longrightarrow b=c
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
b\cdot a=c\cdot a\Longrightarrow b=c.
\]

def:natural-numbers.title: The Natural Numbers
def:natural-numbers.statement_preview: The natural numbers $\mathbb{N}$ are the canonical Peano system \[ (\mathbb{N},1,S), \] where $1 \in \mathbb{N}$ is the distinguished base element and $S:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ is the successor operation.
def:natural-numbers.statement_display:
The natural numbers $\mathbb{N}$ are the canonical Peano system
\[
(\mathbb{N},1,S),
\]
where $1 \in \mathbb{N}$ is the distinguished base element and
$S:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ is the successor operation.
def:natural-numbers.statement_tex:
The natural numbers $\mathbb{N}$ are the canonical Peano system
\[
(\mathbb{N},1,S),
\]
where $1 \in \mathbb{N}$ is the distinguished base element and
$S:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ is the successor operation.

def:strict-less-than-on-peano-system.title: Strict Less Than on a Peano System
def:strict-less-than-on-peano-system.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $a,b \in P$. One writes \[ a<b \] exactly when there exists $c \in P$ such that \[ b=a+c. \]
def:strict-less-than-on-peano-system.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $a,b \in P$. One writes
\[
a<b
\]
exactly when there exists $c \in P$ such that
\[
b=a+c.
\]
def:strict-less-than-on-peano-system.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $a,b \in P$. One writes
\[
a<b
\]
exactly when there exists $c \in P$ such that
\[
b=a+c.
\]

def:less-than-or-equal-on-peano-system.title: Less Than or Equal on a Peano System
def:less-than-or-equal-on-peano-system.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $a,b \in P$. One writes \[ a \le b \] exactly when \[ a<b \qquad\text{or}\qquad a=b. \]
def:less-than-or-equal-on-peano-system.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $a,b \in P$. One writes
\[
a \le b
\]
exactly when
\[
a<b
\qquad\text{or}\qquad
a=b.
\]
def:less-than-or-equal-on-peano-system.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $a,b \in P$. One writes
\[
a \le b
\]
exactly when
\[
a<b
\qquad\text{or}\qquad
a=b.
\]

thm:order-reflexive-on-peano-system.title: Order Is Reflexive on a Peano System
thm:order-reflexive-on-peano-system.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a \in P$, \[ a \le a. \]
thm:order-reflexive-on-peano-system.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a \in P$,
\[
a \le a.
\]
thm:order-reflexive-on-peano-system.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a \in P$,
\[
a \le a.
\]

thm:order-transitive-on-peano-system.title: Order Is Transitive on a Peano System
thm:order-transitive-on-peano-system.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$, \[ \bigl(a \le b \land b \le c\bigr) \Longrightarrow a \le c. \]
thm:order-transitive-on-peano-system.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
\bigl(a \le b \land b \le c\bigr)
\Longrightarrow
a \le c.
\]
thm:order-transitive-on-peano-system.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
\bigl(a \le b \land b \le c\bigr)
\Longrightarrow
a \le c.
\]

thm:order-antisymmetric-on-peano-system.title: Order Is Antisymmetric on a Peano System
thm:order-antisymmetric-on-peano-system.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$, \[ \bigl(a \le b \land b \le a\bigr) \Longrightarrow a=b. \]
thm:order-antisymmetric-on-peano-system.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$,
\[
\bigl(a \le b \land b \le a\bigr)
\Longrightarrow
a=b.
\]
thm:order-antisymmetric-on-peano-system.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$,
\[
\bigl(a \le b \land b \le a\bigr)
\Longrightarrow
a=b.
\]

thm:addition-preserves-order-on-right.title: Addition Preserves Order on the Right
thm:addition-preserves-order-on-right.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$, \[ a \le b \Longrightarrow a+c \le b+c. \]
thm:addition-preserves-order-on-right.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a \le b
\Longrightarrow
a+c \le b+c.
\]
thm:addition-preserves-order-on-right.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a \le b
\Longrightarrow
a+c \le b+c.
\]

thm:addition-preserves-order-on-left.title: Addition Preserves Order on the Left
thm:addition-preserves-order-on-left.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$, \[ a \le b \Longrightarrow c+a \le c+b. \]
thm:addition-preserves-order-on-left.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a \le b
\Longrightarrow
c+a \le c+b.
\]
thm:addition-preserves-order-on-left.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a \le b
\Longrightarrow
c+a \le c+b.
\]

thm:addition-preserves-order.title: Addition Preserves Order
thm:addition-preserves-order.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$, \[ a \le b \Longrightarrow a+c \le b+c \qquad\text{and}\qquad a \le b \Longrightarrow c+a \le c+b. \]
thm:addition-preserves-order.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a \le b \Longrightarrow a+c \le b+c
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
a \le b \Longrightarrow c+a \le c+b.
\]
thm:addition-preserves-order.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,c \in P$,
\[
a \le b \Longrightarrow a+c \le b+c
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
a \le b \Longrightarrow c+a \le c+b.
\]

thm:multiplication-preserves-and-reflect.title: Multiplication Preserves and Reflects Strict Order
thm:multiplication-preserves-and-reflect.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $k,m,n \in P$, \[ m<n \Longleftrightarrow k\cdot m<k\cdot n. \]
thm:multiplication-preserves-and-reflect.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $k,m,n \in P$,
\[
m<n
\Longleftrightarrow
k\cdot m<k\cdot n.
\]
thm:multiplication-preserves-and-reflect.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $k,m,n \in P$,
\[
m<n
\Longleftrightarrow
k\cdot m<k\cdot n.
\]

thm:strict-order-successor-characterizat.title: Strict Order Successor Characterization
thm:strict-order-successor-characterizat.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$, \[ a<b \Longleftrightarrow S(a) \le b. \]
thm:strict-order-successor-characterizat.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$,
\[
a<b
\Longleftrightarrow
S(a) \le b.
\]
thm:strict-order-successor-characterizat.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$,
\[
a<b
\Longleftrightarrow
S(a) \le b.
\]

thm:trichotomy-on-peano-system.title: Trichotomy on a Peano System
thm:trichotomy-on-peano-system.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$, exactly one of the following holds: \[ a<b,\qquad a=b,\qquad b<a. \]
thm:trichotomy-on-peano-system.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$, exactly one of the
following holds:
\[
a<b,\qquad a=b,\qquad b<a.
\]
thm:trichotomy-on-peano-system.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$, exactly one of the
following holds:
\[
a<b,\qquad a=b,\qquad b<a.
\]

thm:natural-number-order-is-total.title: Natural-Number Order Is Total
thm:natural-number-order-is-total.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$, \[ a \le b \qquad\text{or}\qquad b \le a. \] Consequently $\le$ is a total order on $P$.
thm:natural-number-order-is-total.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$,
\[
a \le b
\qquad\text{or}\qquad
b \le a.
\]
Consequently $\le$ is a total order on $P$.
thm:natural-number-order-is-total.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b \in P$,
\[
a \le b
\qquad\text{or}\qquad
b \le a.
\]
Consequently $\le$ is a total order on $P$.

def:exponentiation-on-peano-system.title: Exponentiation on a Peano System
def:exponentiation-on-peano-system.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For each $a \in P$, define the function \[ h_a : P \to P \] to be the unique function satisfying \[ h_a(1)=a \qquad\text{and}\qquad \forall n \in P\;(h_a(S(n))=h_a(n)\cdot a). \] For $a,n \in P$, define \[ a^n \coloneqq h_a(n). \]
def:exponentiation-on-peano-system.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For each $a \in P$, define the function
\[
h_a : P \to P
\]
to be the unique function satisfying
\[
h_a(1)=a
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(h_a(S(n))=h_a(n)\cdot a).
\]
For $a,n \in P$, define
\[
a^n \coloneqq h_a(n).
\]
def:exponentiation-on-peano-system.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For each $a \in P$, define the function
\[
h_a : P \to P
\]
to be the unique function satisfying
\[
h_a(1)=a
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(h_a(S(n))=h_a(n)\cdot a).
\]
For $a,n \in P$, define
\[
a^n \coloneqq h_a(n).
\]

thm:exponentiation-well-defined-on-peano.title: Exponentiation Is Well-Defined on a Peano System
thm:exponentiation-well-defined-on-peano.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a \in P$, there exists a unique function \[ h_a:P\to P \] such that \[ h_a(1)=a \qquad\text{and}\qquad \forall n \in P\;(h_a(S(n))=h_a(n)\cdot a). \] Consequently the rule \[ (a,n)\longmapsto a^n\coloneqq h_a(n) \] defines a unique binary operation on $P$.
thm:exponentiation-well-defined-on-peano.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a \in P$, there exists a unique
function
\[
h_a:P\to P
\]
such that
\[
h_a(1)=a
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(h_a(S(n))=h_a(n)\cdot a).
\]
Consequently the rule
\[
(a,n)\longmapsto a^n\coloneqq h_a(n)
\]
defines a unique binary operation on $P$.
thm:exponentiation-well-defined-on-peano.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a \in P$, there exists a unique
function
\[
h_a:P\to P
\]
such that
\[
h_a(1)=a
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(h_a(S(n))=h_a(n)\cdot a).
\]
Consequently the rule
\[
(a,n)\longmapsto a^n\coloneqq h_a(n)
\]
defines a unique binary operation on $P$.

thm:exponent-sum-law.title: Exponent Sum Law
thm:exponent-sum-law.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For all $a,m,n \in P$, \[ a^{m+n} = a^m \cdot a^n. \]
thm:exponent-sum-law.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For all $a,m,n \in P$,
\[
a^{m+n} = a^m \cdot a^n.
\]
thm:exponent-sum-law.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For all $a,m,n \in P$,
\[
a^{m+n} = a^m \cdot a^n.
\]

thm:exponent-product-law.title: Exponent Product Law
thm:exponent-product-law.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For all $a,b,n \in P$, \[ (a \cdot b)^n = a^n \cdot b^n. \]
thm:exponent-product-law.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For all $a,b,n \in P$,
\[
(a \cdot b)^n = a^n \cdot b^n.
\]
thm:exponent-product-law.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For all $a,b,n \in P$,
\[
(a \cdot b)^n = a^n \cdot b^n.
\]

thm:exponent-power-law.title: Exponent Power Law
thm:exponent-power-law.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For all $a,m,n \in P$, \[ (a^m)^n = a^{m \cdot n}. \]
thm:exponent-power-law.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For all $a,m,n \in P$,
\[
(a^m)^n = a^{m \cdot n}.
\]
thm:exponent-power-law.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For all $a,m,n \in P$,
\[
(a^m)^n = a^{m \cdot n}.
\]

thm:powers-with-common-exponent-preserve.title: Powers with Common Exponent Preserve Strict Order
thm:powers-with-common-exponent-preserve.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,n \in P$, \[ a<b \Longleftrightarrow a^n<b^n. \]
thm:powers-with-common-exponent-preserve.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,n \in P$,
\[
a<b
\Longleftrightarrow
a^n<b^n.
\]
thm:powers-with-common-exponent-preserve.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,b,n \in P$,
\[
a<b
\Longleftrightarrow
a^n<b^n.
\]

thm:powers-with-common-base-preserve-str.title: Powers with Common Base Preserve Strict Order
thm:powers-with-common-base-preserve-str.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,m,n \in P$, \[ a^m<a^n \Longleftrightarrow 1<a \land m<n. \]
thm:powers-with-common-base-preserve-str.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,m,n \in P$,
\[
a^m<a^n
\Longleftrightarrow
1<a \land m<n.
\]
thm:powers-with-common-base-preserve-str.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $a,m,n \in P$,
\[
a^m<a^n
\Longleftrightarrow
1<a \land m<n.
\]

thm:n-less-than-2-to-n.title: $n < 2^n$ for All $n \in \mathbb{N}$
thm:n-less-than-2-to-n.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $2 \coloneqq S(1)$. For every $n \in P$, \[ n < 2^n. \]
thm:n-less-than-2-to-n.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $2 \coloneqq S(1)$. For every $n \in P$,
\[
n < 2^n.
\]
thm:n-less-than-2-to-n.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $2 \coloneqq S(1)$. For every $n \in P$,
\[
n < 2^n.
\]

thm:well-ordering-principle.title: Well-Ordering Principle
thm:well-ordering-principle.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. Every nonempty subset of $P$ has a least element with respect to the order $\le$ on $P$.
thm:well-ordering-principle.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. Every nonempty subset of $P$ has a least
element with respect to the order $\le$ on $P$.

thm:induction-well-ordering-equivalence.title: Induction, Strong Induction, and Well-Ordering Are Equivalent
thm:induction-well-ordering-equivalence.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system equipped with the order $\le$. The following are equivalent: \begin{enumerate} \item The induction principle: for every predicate $\varphi$ on $P$, if $\varphi(1)$ holds and the successor step holds, then $\varphi(n)$ holds for all $n \in P$. \item The strong induction principle: for every predicate $\varphi$ on $P$, if $\var...
thm:induction-well-ordering-equivalence.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system equipped with the order $\le$. The following
are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item The induction principle: for every predicate $\varphi$ on $P$, if
  $\varphi(1)$ holds and the successor step holds, then $\varphi(n)$ holds
  for all $n \in P$.
  \item The strong induction principle: for every predicate $\varphi$ on $P$,
  if $\varphi(k)$ holds for every $k<n$ implies $\varphi(n)$, then
  $\varphi(n)$ holds for every $n \in P$.
  \item The well-ordering principle: every nonempty subset of $P$ has a least
  element with respect to $\le$.
\end{enumerate}
thm:induction-well-ordering-equivalence.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system equipped with the order $\le$. The following
are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item The induction principle: for every predicate $\varphi$ on $P$, if
  $\varphi(1)$ holds and the successor step holds, then $\varphi(n)$ holds
  for all $n \in P$.
  \item The strong induction principle: for every predicate $\varphi$ on $P$,
  if $\varphi(k)$ holds for every $k<n$ implies $\varphi(n)$, then
  $\varphi(n)$ holds for every $n \in P$.
  \item The well-ordering principle: every nonempty subset of $P$ has a least
  element with respect to $\le$.
\end{enumerate}

def:open-interval.title: Open Interval
def:open-interval.statement_preview: Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a\le b$. The \emph{open interval} with endpoints $a$ and $b$ is \[ (a,b)=\{\,x\in\mathbb{R} : a<x<b\,\}. \]

\]

def:closed-interval.title: Closed Interval
def:closed-interval.statement_preview: Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a\le b$. The \emph{closed interval} with endpoints $a$ and $b$ is \[ [a,b]=\{\,x\in\mathbb{R} : a\le x\le b\,\}. \]

def:ray.title: Ray
def:ray.statement_preview: Let $a\in\mathbb{R}$. The four \emph{rays} determined by $a$ are \[ (a,\infty)=\{x:x>a\},\ [a,\infty)=\{x:x\ge a\},\ (-\infty,a)=\{x:x<a\},\ (-\infty,a]=\{x:x\le a\}. \]

\]

def:number-line.title: Number Line
def:number-line.statement_preview: A \emph{number line} for a linearly ordered system $(A,<_A)$ is a linearly ordered set of points $L$ together with an order isomorphism $\lambda:A\to L$, so that for all $a,a'\in A$, \[ a<_A a' \Longleftrightarrow \lambda(a) \text{ lies to the left of } \lambda(a'). \]
def:number-line.statement_display:

\[
a<_A a' \Longleftrightarrow \lambda(a) \text{ lies to the left of } \lambda(a').
\]

def:peano-system.title: Peano System
def:peano-system.statement_preview: A triple $(P,S,1)$ is called a \emph{Peano system} exactly when $P$ is a set, $1 \in P$, $S : P \to P$ is a function, and the following axioms hold: \begin{enumerate} \item[\textbf{P1.}] $1 \in P$. \item[\textbf{P2.}] For every $n \in P$, $S(n) \in P$. \item[\textbf{P3.}] For every $n \in P$, $S(n) \neq 1$. \item[\textbf{P4.}] For all $m,n \in P$, if $S(m)=...
def:peano-system.statement_display:
A triple $(P,S,1)$ is called a \emph{Peano system} exactly when $P$ is a set,
$1 \in P$, $S : P \to P$ is a function, and the following axioms hold:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item[\textbf{P1.}] $1 \in P$.
  \item[\textbf{P2.}] For every $n \in P$, $S(n) \in P$.
  \item[\textbf{P3.}] For every $n \in P$, $S(n) \neq 1$.
  \item[\textbf{P4.}] For all $m,n \in P$, if $S(m)=S(n)$ then $m=n$.
  \item[\textbf{P5.}] If $A \subseteq P$, $1 \in A$, and
  \[
    \forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A),
  \]
  then $A=P$.
\end{enumerate}
def:peano-system.statement_tex:
A triple $(P,S,1)$ is called a \emph{Peano system} exactly when $P$ is a set,
$1 \in P$, $S : P \to P$ is a function, and the following axioms hold:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item[\textbf{P1.}] $1 \in P$.
  \item[\textbf{P2.}] For every $n \in P$, $S(n) \in P$.
  \item[\textbf{P3.}] For every $n \in P$, $S(n) \neq 1$.
  \item[\textbf{P4.}] For all $m,n \in P$, if $S(m)=S(n)$ then $m=n$.
  \item[\textbf{P5.}] If $A \subseteq P$, $1 \in A$, and
  \[
    \forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A),
  \]
  then $A=P$.
\end{enumerate}

ax:peano-induction.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq P$. If $1 \in A$ and if \[ \forall n \in P\;(n \in A \Longrightarrow S(n) \in A), \] then $A=P$.

thm:induction-principle-for-peano-system.title: Induction Principle for a Peano System
thm:induction-principle-for-peano-system.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $\varphi$ be a predicate on $P$. If \[ \varphi(1) \qquad\text{and}\qquad \forall n \in P\;(\varphi(n) \Longrightarrow \varphi(S(n))), \] then \[ \forall n \in P\;\varphi(n). \]
thm:induction-principle-for-peano-system.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $\varphi$ be a predicate on $P$.
If
\[
\varphi(1)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n \in P\;(\varphi(n) \Longrightarrow \varphi(S(n))),
\]
then
\[
\forall n \in P\;\varphi(n).
\]

thm:strong-induction-on-peano-system.title: Strong Induction on a Peano System
thm:strong-induction-on-peano-system.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $\varphi$ be a predicate on $P$. If \[ \forall n \in P\; \bigl(\forall k \in P\;(k < n \Longrightarrow \varphi(k))\bigr) \Longrightarrow \varphi(n), \] then \[ \forall n \in P\;\varphi(n). \]

\bigl(\forall k \in P\;(k < n \Longrightarrow \varphi(k))\bigr)
\Longrightarrow \varphi(n),
\]
then
\[
\forall n \in P\;\varphi(n).
\]

thm:peano-minimality.title: Minimality of a Peano System
thm:peano-minimality.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. If $A\subseteq P$ contains $1$ and is closed under successor, then $A=P$. \[ \bigl(1\in A \land \forall n\in P\;(n\in A \Longrightarrow S(n)\in A)\bigr) \Longrightarrow A=P. \]

under successor, then $A=P$.
\[
\bigl(1\in A \land
\forall n\in P\;(n\in A \Longrightarrow S(n)\in A)\bigr)
\Longrightarrow A=P.
\]

def:predecessor-in-peano-system.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $x,u \in P$. The element $u$ is called a \emph{predecessor} of $x$ exactly when \[ S(u)=x. \]

thm:every-element-is-one-or-a-successor.title: Every Element Is Either $1$ or a Successor
thm:every-element-is-one-or-a-successor.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system. For every $x \in P$, either $x=1$ or there exists $u \in P$ such that $S(u)=x$.

def:presburger-signature.title: Presburger Signature
def:presburger-signature.statement_preview: The \emph{Presburger signature} is the first-order signature \[ \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Pres}} = \{0, S, +, <, =\}, \] where $0$ is a constant symbol, $S$ is a unary function symbol, $+$ is a binary function symbol, and $<$ and $=$ are binary relation symbols.
def:presburger-signature.statement_display:
The \emph{Presburger signature} is the first-order signature
\[
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Pres}} = \{0, S, +, <, =\},
\]
where $0$ is a constant symbol, $S$ is a unary function symbol, $+$ is a
binary function symbol, and $<$ and $=$ are binary relation symbols.
def:presburger-signature.statement_tex:

def:presburger-arithmetic.title: Presburger Arithmetic
def:presburger-arithmetic.statement_preview: \emph{Presburger arithmetic} is the first-order theory of the natural numbers in the Presburger signature. Its intended structure is \[ (\mathbb{N},0,S,+,<,=), \] and its axioms describe the behavior of zero, successor, addition, order, and induction for formulas written in the language $\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Pres}}$.

\]
and its axioms describe the behavior of zero, successor, addition, order, and
induction for formulas written in the language
$\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Pres}}$.

def:iterator-data-on-peano-system.title: Iterator Data
def:iterator-data-on-peano-system.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $W$ be a set, let $c\in W$, and let $g:W\to W$ be a function. The triple $(W,c,g)$ is called \emph{iterator data} for $(P,S,1)$.

def:iterator-relation.title: Iterator Relation
def:iterator-relation.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $(W,c,g)$ be iterator data for it. A relation $R\subseteq P\times W$ is called an \emph{iterator relation} for $(W,c,g)$ exactly when \[ (1,c)\in R \] and \[ \forall n\in P\;\forall w\in W\; \bigl((n,w)\in R\Longrightarrow (S(n),g(w))\in R\bigr). \]
def:iterator-relation.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $(W,c,g)$ be iterator data for it. A
relation $R\subseteq P\times W$ is called an \emph{iterator relation} for
$(W,c,g)$ exactly when
\[
(1,c)\in R
\]
and
\[
\forall n\in P\;\forall w\in W\;
\bigl((n,w)\in R\Longrightarrow (S(n),g(w))\in R\bigr).
\]
def:iterator-relation.statement_tex:

def:minimal-iterator-relation.title: Minimal Iterator Relation
def:minimal-iterator-relation.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $(W,c,g)$ be iterator data for it. The \emph{minimal iterator relation} for $(W,c,g)$ is \[ R^* = \bigcap\{R\subseteq P\times W : R \text{ is an iterator relation for } (W,c,g)\}. \]
def:minimal-iterator-relation.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $(W,c,g)$ be iterator data for it.
The \emph{minimal iterator relation} for $(W,c,g)$ is
\[
R^*
=
\bigcap\{R\subseteq P\times W : R \text{ is an iterator relation for }
(W,c,g)\}.
\]

lem:forced-values-are-unique.title: Forced Values Are Unique
lem:forced-values-are-unique.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $(W,c,g)$ be iterator data for it, and let $R^*$ be the minimal iterator relation. If $(n,w_0)\in R^*$ and $(n,w_1)\in R^*$, then $w_0=w_1$.

lem:uniqueness-of-iterator-functions.title: Uniqueness of Iterator Functions
lem:uniqueness-of-iterator-functions.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $(W,c,g)$ be iterator data for it. If $f,h:P\to W$ satisfy \[ f(1)=c,\qquad h(1)=c, \] and \[ \forall n\in P\;(f(S(n))=g(f(n))) \qquad\text{and}\qquad \forall n\in P\;(h(S(n))=g(h(n))), \] then $f=h$.
lem:uniqueness-of-iterator-functions.statement_display:

and
\[
\forall n\in P\;(f(S(n))=g(f(n)))
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n\in P\;(h(S(n))=g(h(n))),
\]
then $f=h$.

lem:existence-of-iterator-function.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $(W,c,g)$ be iterator data for it. Then there exists a function $f:P\to W$ such that \[ f(1)=c \qquad\text{and}\qquad \forall n\in P\;(f(S(n))=g(f(n))). \]

thm:peano-iterator-theorem.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $(W,c,g)$ be iterator data for it. Then there exists a unique function $f:P\to W$ such that \[ f(1)=c \qquad\text{and}\qquad \forall n\in P\;(f(S(n))=g(f(n))). \]

def:iterator-generated-function.title: Iterator-Generated Function
def:iterator-generated-function.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $(W,c,g)$ be iterator data for it. The unique function $f:P\to W$ satisfying \[ f(1)=c \qquad\text{and}\qquad \forall n\in P\;(f(S(n))=g(f(n))) \] is called the \emph{iterator-generated function determined by $(W,c,g)$}.
def:iterator-generated-function.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, and let $(W,c,g)$ be iterator data for it.

thm:iterator-base-value.title: Iterator Base Clause
thm:iterator-base-value.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $(W,c,g)$ be iterator data for it, and let $f$ be the iterator-generated function determined by $(W,c,g)$. Then \[ f(1)=c. \]
thm:iterator-base-value.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $(W,c,g)$ be iterator data for it, and let
$f$ be the iterator-generated function determined by $(W,c,g)$. Then
\[
f(1)=c.
\]

def:iteration-of-a-self-map.title: Iteration of a Self-Map
def:iteration-of-a-self-map.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $W$ be a set, let $c\in W$, and let $g:W\to W$. The iterator-generated function determined by $(W,c,g)$ is called the \emph{iteration of $g$ from $c$ along $P$}.
def:iteration-of-a-self-map.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $W$ be a set, let $c\in W$, and let
$g:W\to W$. The iterator-generated function determined by $(W,c,g)$ is called
the \emph{iteration of $g$ from $c$ along $P$}.

cor:uniqueness-of-binary-iterator-operat.title: Uniqueness of Binary Iterator Operations
cor:uniqueness-of-binary-iterator-operat.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $A$ be a set, let $W$ be a set, and suppose each $a\in A$ determines iterator data $(W,c_a,g_a)$ for $(P,S,1)$. If $F,G:A\times P\to W$ satisfy \[ F(a,1)=c_a,\qquad G(a,1)=c_a \] and \[ F(a,S(n))=g_a(F(a,n)), \qquad G(a,S(n))=g_a(G(a,n)) \] for all $a\in A$ and all $n\in P$, then $F=G$.
cor:uniqueness-of-binary-iterator-operat.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $A$ be a set, let $W$ be a set, and
suppose each $a\in A$ determines iterator data $(W,c_a,g_a)$ for $(P,S,1)$.
If $F,G:A\times P\to W$ satisfy
\[
F(a,1)=c_a,\qquad G(a,1)=c_a
\]
and
\[
F(a,S(n))=g_a(F(a,n)),
\qquad
G(a,S(n))=g_a(G(a,n))
\]
for all $a\in A$ and all $n\in P$, then $F=G$.
cor:uniqueness-of-binary-iterator-operat.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $A$ be a set, let $W$ be a set, and
suppose each $a\in A$ determines iterator data $(W,c_a,g_a)$ for $(P,S,1)$.

\[

\]

\[
F(a,S(n))=g_a(F(a,n)),
\qquad
G(a,S(n))=g_a(G(a,n))
\]
for all $a\in A$ and all $n\in P$, then $F=G$.

def:stage-dependent-step-rule.title: Stage-Dependent Step Rule
def:stage-dependent-step-rule.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system and let $W$ be a set. A \emph{stage-dependent step rule} on $W$ over $P$ is a function \[ \Phi:P\times W\to W. \]
def:stage-dependent-step-rule.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system and let $W$ be a set. A
\emph{stage-dependent step rule} on $W$ over $P$ is a function
\[
\Phi:P\times W\to W.
\]
def:stage-dependent-step-rule.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system and let $W$ be a set. A
\emph{stage-dependent step rule} on $W$ over $P$ is a function
\[
\Phi:P\times W\to W.
\]

def:general-recursive-function.title: General Recursive Function
def:general-recursive-function.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $W$ be a set, let $c\in W$, and let $\Phi:P\times W\to W$ be a stage-dependent step rule. A function $f:P\to W$ is called the \emph{general recursive function determined by $(W,c,\Phi)$} exactly when \[ f(1)=c \qquad\text{and}\qquad \forall n\in P\;(f(S(n))=\Phi(n,f(n))). \]
def:general-recursive-function.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $W$ be a set, let $c\in W$, and let
$\Phi:P\times W\to W$ be a stage-dependent step rule. A function $f:P\to W$
is called the \emph{general recursive function determined by $(W,c,\Phi)$}
exactly when
\[
f(1)=c
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n\in P\;(f(S(n))=\Phi(n,f(n))).
\]

Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $W$ be a set, let $c\in W$, and let

\[

\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\forall n\in P\;(f(S(n))=\Phi(n,f(n))).
\]

lem:uniqueness-of-general-recursive-func.title: Uniqueness of General Recursive Functions
lem:uniqueness-of-general-recursive-func.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $W$ be a set, let $c\in W$, and let $\Phi:P\times W\to W$ be a stage-dependent step rule. \qquad\text{and}\qquad

\]
then $f=h$.

thm:general-recursion-by-state-encoding.title: General Recursion by State Encoding
thm:general-recursion-by-state-encoding.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $W$ be a set, let $c\in W$, and let $\Phi:P\times W\to W$ be a stage-dependent step rule. There exists a function $H:P\to P\times W$ such that \[ H(1)=(1,c) \] and \[ \forall n\in P\; \bigl(H(S(n))=(S(\pi_1(H(n))),\Phi(\pi_1(H(n)),\pi_2(H(n))))\bigr). \]
thm:general-recursion-by-state-encoding.statement_display:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $W$ be a set, let $c\in W$, and let
$\Phi:P\times W\to W$ be a stage-dependent step rule. There exists a function
$H:P\to P\times W$ such that
\[
H(1)=(1,c)
\]
and
\[
\forall n\in P\;
\bigl(H(S(n))=(S(\pi_1(H(n))),\Phi(\pi_1(H(n)),\pi_2(H(n))))\bigr).
\]
thm:general-recursion-by-state-encoding.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $W$ be a set, let $c\in W$, and let
$\Phi:P\times W\to W$ be a stage-dependent step rule. There exists a function
$H:P\to P\times W$ such that
\[
H(1)=(1,c)
\]
and
\[
\forall n\in P\;
\bigl(H(S(n))=(S(\pi_1(H(n))),\Phi(\pi_1(H(n)),\pi_2(H(n))))\bigr).
\]

thm:general-recursion-theorem-for-peano-.title: General Recursion Theorem
thm:general-recursion-theorem-for-peano-.statement_preview: Let $(P,S,1)$ be a Peano system, let $W$ be a set, let $c\in W$, and let $\Phi:P\times W\to W$ be a stage-dependent step rule. Then there exists a unique function $f:P\to W$ such that \[ f(1)=c \qquad\text{and}\qquad \forall n\in P\;(f(S(n))=\Phi(n,f(n))). \]

\]

thm:uniqueness-of-peano-systems-up-to-is.title: Uniqueness of Peano Systems up to Isomorphism
thm:uniqueness-of-peano-systems-up-to-is.statement_preview: Let $(P,S_P,1_P)$ and $(Q,S_Q,1_Q)$ be Peano systems. Then there exists a unique bijection $\theta:P\to Q$ such that \[ \theta(1_P)=1_Q \] and \[ \forall n\in P\;(\theta(S_P(n))=S_Q(\theta(n))). \] Thus any two Peano systems are isomorphic.
thm:uniqueness-of-peano-systems-up-to-is.statement_display:
Let $(P,S_P,1_P)$ and $(Q,S_Q,1_Q)$ be Peano systems. Then there exists a
unique bijection $\theta:P\to Q$ such that
\[
\theta(1_P)=1_Q
\]
and
\[
\forall n\in P\;(\theta(S_P(n))=S_Q(\theta(n))).
\]
Thus any two Peano systems are isomorphic.
thm:uniqueness-of-peano-systems-up-to-is.statement_tex:
Let $(P,S_P,1_P)$ and $(Q,S_Q,1_Q)$ be Peano systems. Then there exists a
unique bijection $\theta:P\to Q$ such that
\[
\theta(1_P)=1_Q
\]
and
\[
\forall n\in P\;(\theta(S_P(n))=S_Q(\theta(n))).
\]
Thus any two Peano systems are isomorphic.

def:arbitrarily-close-distinct-points.title: Arbitrarily Close Distinct Points
def:arbitrarily-close-distinct-points.statement_preview: A number system has arbitrarily close distinct points if for every point $x$ and every positive tolerance $\varepsilon$, there is a point $y\ne x$ such that \[ |x-y|<\varepsilon. \]
def:arbitrarily-close-distinct-points.statement_display:
A number system has arbitrarily close distinct points if for every point $x$
and every positive tolerance $\varepsilon$, there is a point $y\ne x$ such that
\[
|x-y|<\varepsilon.
\]
def:arbitrarily-close-distinct-points.statement_tex:
A number system has arbitrarily close distinct points if for every point $x$
and every positive tolerance $\varepsilon$, there is a point $y\ne x$ such that
\[
|x-y|<\varepsilon.
\]

thm:q-has-arbitrarily-close-distinct-poi.title: $\mathbb{Q}$ Has Arbitrarily Close Distinct Points
thm:q-has-arbitrarily-close-distinct-poi.statement_preview: For every $x\in\mathbb{Q}$ and every $\varepsilon\in\mathbb{Q}$, \[ 0_{\mathbb{Q}}<\varepsilon \Longrightarrow \exists y\in\mathbb{Q}\ (y\ne x\ \text{and}\ |x-y|<\varepsilon). \]
thm:q-has-arbitrarily-close-distinct-poi.statement_display:
For every $x\in\mathbb{Q}$ and every $\varepsilon\in\mathbb{Q}$,
\[
0_{\mathbb{Q}}<\varepsilon
\Longrightarrow
\exists y\in\mathbb{Q}\ (y\ne x\ \text{and}\ |x-y|<\varepsilon).
\]
thm:q-has-arbitrarily-close-distinct-poi.statement_tex:
For every $x\in\mathbb{Q}$ and every $\varepsilon\in\mathbb{Q}$,
\[
0_{\mathbb{Q}}<\varepsilon
\Longrightarrow
\exists y\in\mathbb{Q}\ (y\ne x\ \text{and}\ |x-y|<\varepsilon).
\]

cor:q-has-no-adjacent-points.title: $\mathbb{Q}$ Has No Adjacent Points
cor:q-has-no-adjacent-points.statement_preview: There do not exist rational numbers $x<y$ with no rational number strictly between them.
cor:q-has-no-adjacent-points.statement_display:
There do not exist rational numbers $x<y$ with no rational number strictly
between them.
cor:q-has-no-adjacent-points.statement_tex:
There do not exist rational numbers $x<y$ with no rational number strictly
between them.

def:nonzero-integers.title: Nonzero Integers
def:nonzero-integers.statement_preview: The set of nonzero integers is \[ \mathbb{Z}^{\ast}:=\mathbb{Z}\setminus\{0_{\mathbb{Z}}\}. \]
def:nonzero-integers.statement_display:
The set of nonzero integers is
\[
\mathbb{Z}^{\ast}:=\mathbb{Z}\setminus\{0_{\mathbb{Z}}\}.
\]
def:nonzero-integers.statement_tex:
The set of nonzero integers is
\[
\mathbb{Z}^{\ast}:=\mathbb{Z}\setminus\{0_{\mathbb{Z}}\}.
\]

def:prefraction.title: Prefraction
def:prefraction.statement_preview: A \emph{prefraction} is an ordered pair $(a,b)\in\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}^{\ast}$, read as the formal quotient $a/b$.
def:prefraction.statement_display:
A \emph{prefraction} is an ordered pair $(a,b)\in\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}^{\ast}$,
read as the formal quotient $a/b$.
def:prefraction.statement_tex:

read as the formal quotient $a/b$.

thm:fraction-equivalence-relation.title: Fraction Equivalence Is an Equivalence Relation
thm:fraction-equivalence-relation.statement_preview: The relation $\sim_{\mathbb{Q}}$ is an equivalence relation on $\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{Q}$.
thm:fraction-equivalence-relation.statement_display:
The relation $\sim_{\mathbb{Q}}$ is an equivalence relation on
$\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{Q}$.
thm:fraction-equivalence-relation.statement_tex:
The relation $\sim_{\mathbb{Q}}$ is an equivalence relation on
$\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{Q}$.

def:rationals.title: Rational Numbers
def:rationals.statement_preview: The rational numbers are the quotient \[ \mathbb{Q}:=\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{Q}/{\sim_{\mathbb{Q}}}. \]
def:rationals.statement_display:
The rational numbers are the quotient
\[
\mathbb{Q}:=\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{Q}/{\sim_{\mathbb{Q}}}.
\]
def:rationals.statement_tex:
The rational numbers are the quotient
\[
\mathbb{Q}:=\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{Q}/{\sim_{\mathbb{Q}}}.
\]

def:rational-class-notation.title: Rational Class Notation
def:rational-class-notation.statement_preview: The notation $[a,b]$ denotes the $\sim_{\mathbb{Q}}$-equivalence class of the prefraction $(a,b)$, where $b\ne 0_{\mathbb{Z}}$.
def:rational-class-notation.statement_display:
The notation $[a,b]$ denotes the $\sim_{\mathbb{Q}}$-equivalence class of the
prefraction $(a,b)$, where $b\ne 0_{\mathbb{Z}}$.
def:rational-class-notation.statement_tex:
The notation $[a,b]$ denotes the $\sim_{\mathbb{Q}}$-equivalence class of the
prefraction $(a,b)$, where $b\ne 0_{\mathbb{Z}}$.

thm:zero-is-a-rational.title: Zero Is a Rational
thm:zero-is-a-rational.statement_preview: The class $0_{\mathbb{Q}}$ is an element of $\mathbb{Q}$.
thm:zero-is-a-rational.statement_display: The class $0_{\mathbb{Q}}$ is an element of $\mathbb{Q}$.
thm:zero-is-a-rational.statement_tex: The class $0_{\mathbb{Q}}$ is an element of $\mathbb{Q}$.

thm:one-is-a-rational.statement_tex: The class $1_{\mathbb{Q}}$ is an element of $\mathbb{Q}$.

thm:embedding-z-into-q-preserves-zero.title: Embedding Preserves Zero
thm:embedding-z-into-q-preserves-zero.statement_preview: The embedding sends integer zero to rational zero: \[ \iota(0_{\mathbb{Z}})=0_{\mathbb{Q}}. \]
thm:embedding-z-into-q-preserves-zero.statement_display:
The embedding sends integer zero to rational zero:
\[
\iota(0_{\mathbb{Z}})=0_{\mathbb{Q}}.
\]
thm:embedding-z-into-q-preserves-zero.statement_tex:

\[
\iota(0_{\mathbb{Z}})=0_{\mathbb{Q}}.
\]

thm:embedding-z-into-q-preserves-one.title: Embedding Preserves One
thm:embedding-z-into-q-preserves-one.statement_preview: The embedding sends integer one to rational one: \[ \iota(1_{\mathbb{Z}})=1_{\mathbb{Q}}. \]
thm:embedding-z-into-q-preserves-one.statement_display:
The embedding sends integer one to rational one:
\[
\iota(1_{\mathbb{Z}})=1_{\mathbb{Q}}.
\]

thm:z-embeds-into-q.title: $\mathbb{Z}$ Embeds into $\mathbb{Q}$
thm:z-embeds-into-q.statement_preview: The map $\iota:\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Q}$ is an injective order-preserving ring homomorphism.
thm:z-embeds-into-q.statement_display:
The map $\iota:\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Q}$ is an injective order-preserving ring
homomorphism.
thm:z-embeds-into-q.statement_tex:

thm:q-is-field-of-fractions-of-z.title: $\mathbb{Q}$ Is the Field of Fractions of $\mathbb{Z}$
thm:q-is-field-of-fractions-of-z.statement_preview: For every $x\in\mathbb{Q}$, there exist $m,n\in\mathbb{Z}$ with $n\ne0_{\mathbb{Z}}$ such that \[ x=\iota(m)\cdot \iota(n)^{-1}. \]
thm:q-is-field-of-fractions-of-z.statement_display:
For every $x\in\mathbb{Q}$, there exist $m,n\in\mathbb{Z}$ with
$n\ne0_{\mathbb{Z}}$ such that
\[
x=\iota(m)\cdot \iota(n)^{-1}.
\]
thm:q-is-field-of-fractions-of-z.statement_tex:
For every $x\in\mathbb{Q}$, there exist $m,n\in\mathbb{Z}$ with
$n\ne0_{\mathbb{Z}}$ such that
\[
x=\iota(m)\cdot \iota(n)^{-1}.
\]

cor:embedding-z-into-q-not-surjective.title: The Embedding of $\mathbb{Z}$ into $\mathbb{Q}$ Is Not Surjective
cor:embedding-z-into-q-not-surjective.statement_preview: The image of $\iota:\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Q}$ is a proper subset of \(\mathbb{Q}\).
cor:embedding-z-into-q-not-surjective.statement_display:
The image of $\iota:\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Q}$ is a proper subset of
\(\mathbb{Q}\).
cor:embedding-z-into-q-not-surjective.statement_tex:
The image of $\iota:\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Q}$ is a proper subset of
\(\mathbb{Q}\).

cor:zero-product-on-q.title: Zero Product on $\mathbb{Q}$
cor:zero-product-on-q.statement_preview: For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Q}$, \[ x\cdot y=0_{\mathbb{Q}} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad x=0_{\mathbb{Q}}\ \text{or}\ y=0_{\mathbb{Q}}. \]
cor:zero-product-on-q.statement_display:
For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Q}$,
\[
x\cdot y=0_{\mathbb{Q}}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
x=0_{\mathbb{Q}}\ \text{or}\ y=0_{\mathbb{Q}}.
\]
cor:zero-product-on-q.statement_tex:
For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Q}$,
\[
x\cdot y=0_{\mathbb{Q}}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
x=0_{\mathbb{Q}}\ \text{or}\ y=0_{\mathbb{Q}}.
\]

cor:negation-of-product-on-q.title: Negation of a Product on $\mathbb{Q}$
cor:negation-of-product-on-q.statement_preview: For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Q}$, \[ (-x)\cdot y=-(x\cdot y) \qquad\text{and}\qquad x\cdot(-y)=-(x\cdot y). \]
cor:negation-of-product-on-q.statement_display:
For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Q}$,
\[
(-x)\cdot y=-(x\cdot y)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
x\cdot(-y)=-(x\cdot y).
\]
cor:negation-of-product-on-q.statement_tex:

\[

\qquad\text{and}\qquad

\]

cor:multiplicative-cancellation-on-q.title: Multiplicative Cancellation on $\mathbb{Q}$
cor:multiplicative-cancellation-on-q.statement_preview: For all $x,y,z\in\mathbb{Q}$, \[ z\ne 0_{\mathbb{Q}}\land x\cdot z=y\cdot z \Longrightarrow x=y. \]
cor:multiplicative-cancellation-on-q.statement_display:
For all $x,y,z\in\mathbb{Q}$,
\[
z\ne 0_{\mathbb{Q}}\land x\cdot z=y\cdot z
\Longrightarrow
x=y.
\]
cor:multiplicative-cancellation-on-q.statement_tex:
For all $x,y,z\in\mathbb{Q}$,
\[
z\ne 0_{\mathbb{Q}}\land x\cdot z=y\cdot z
\Longrightarrow
x=y.
\]

cor:uniqueness-additive-inverse-on-q.title: Uniqueness of Additive Inverses on $\mathbb{Q}$
cor:uniqueness-additive-inverse-on-q.statement_preview: Every additive inverse in $\mathbb{Q}$ is unique.
cor:uniqueness-additive-inverse-on-q.statement_display: Every additive inverse in $\mathbb{Q}$ is unique.
cor:uniqueness-additive-inverse-on-q.statement_tex: Every additive inverse in $\mathbb{Q}$ is unique.

cor:uniqueness-multiplicative-inverse-on.title: Uniqueness of Multiplicative Inverses on $\mathbb{Q}$
cor:uniqueness-multiplicative-inverse-on.statement_preview: Every multiplicative inverse of a nonzero rational number is unique.
cor:uniqueness-multiplicative-inverse-on.statement_display: Every multiplicative inverse of a nonzero rational number is unique.
cor:uniqueness-multiplicative-inverse-on.statement_tex: Every multiplicative inverse of a nonzero rational number is unique.

def:integer-power-on-q.title: Integer Power on $\mathbb{Q}$
def:integer-power-on-q.statement_preview: Let $x\in\mathbb{Q}$. For $n\in\mathbb{W}$, define $x^n$ by the usual recursive power operation. If $x\ne 0_{\mathbb{Q}}$ and $n\in\mathbb{Z}$ is negative, define \[ x^n:=(x^{-1})^{-n}. \]
def:integer-power-on-q.statement_display:
Let $x\in\mathbb{Q}$. For $n\in\mathbb{W}$, define $x^n$ by the usual
recursive power operation. If $x\ne 0_{\mathbb{Q}}$ and $n\in\mathbb{Z}$ is
negative, define
\[
x^n:=(x^{-1})^{-n}.
\]
def:integer-power-on-q.statement_tex:
Let $x\in\mathbb{Q}$. For $n\in\mathbb{W}$, define $x^n$ by the usual
recursive power operation. If $x\ne 0_{\mathbb{Q}}$ and $n\in\mathbb{Z}$ is
negative, define
\[
x^n:=(x^{-1})^{-n}.
\]

cor:exponent-addition-on-q.title: Exponent Addition Law on $\mathbb{Q}$
cor:exponent-addition-on-q.statement_preview: For every nonzero $x\in\mathbb{Q}$ and all $m,n\in\mathbb{Z}$, \[ x^{m+n}=x^m\cdot x^n. \]
cor:exponent-addition-on-q.statement_display:
For every nonzero $x\in\mathbb{Q}$ and all $m,n\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
x^{m+n}=x^m\cdot x^n.
\]
cor:exponent-addition-on-q.statement_tex:
For every nonzero $x\in\mathbb{Q}$ and all $m,n\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
x^{m+n}=x^m\cdot x^n.
\]

cor:power-of-power-on-q.title: Power of a Power on $\mathbb{Q}$
cor:power-of-power-on-q.statement_preview: For every nonzero $x\in\mathbb{Q}$ and all $m,n\in\mathbb{Z}$, \[ (x^m)^n=x^{m\cdot n}. \]
cor:power-of-power-on-q.statement_display:
For every nonzero $x\in\mathbb{Q}$ and all $m,n\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
(x^m)^n=x^{m\cdot n}.
\]
cor:power-of-power-on-q.statement_tex:
For every nonzero $x\in\mathbb{Q}$ and all $m,n\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
(x^m)^n=x^{m\cdot n}.
\]

cor:power-of-product-on-q.title: Power of a Product on $\mathbb{Q}$
cor:power-of-product-on-q.statement_preview: For all nonzero $x,y\in\mathbb{Q}$ and every $n\in\mathbb{Z}$, \[ (x\cdot y)^n=x^n\cdot y^n. \]
cor:power-of-product-on-q.statement_display:
For all nonzero $x,y\in\mathbb{Q}$ and every $n\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
(x\cdot y)^n=x^n\cdot y^n.
\]
cor:power-of-product-on-q.statement_tex:
For all nonzero $x,y\in\mathbb{Q}$ and every $n\in\mathbb{Z}$,
\[
(x\cdot y)^n=x^n\cdot y^n.
\]

def:addition-on-q-classes.title: Addition on $\mathbb{Q}$-Classes
def:addition-on-q-classes.statement_preview: Define addition on representatives by \[ [a,b]+[c,d]:=[a\cdot d+b\cdot c,\ b\cdot d]. \]

lem:nonzero-predicate-well-defined-q.title: Nonzero Predicate Is Well-Defined on $\mathbb{Q}$
lem:nonzero-predicate-well-defined-q.statement_preview: For every prefraction $(a,b)\in\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{Q}$, \[ [a,b]\ne 0_{\mathbb{Q}} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad a\ne 0_{\mathbb{Z}}. \] Thus nonzeroness is independent of the representative.
lem:nonzero-predicate-well-defined-q.statement_display:
For every prefraction $(a,b)\in\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{Q}$,
\[
[a,b]\ne 0_{\mathbb{Q}}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
a\ne 0_{\mathbb{Z}}.
\]
Thus nonzeroness is independent of the representative.
lem:nonzero-predicate-well-defined-q.statement_tex:
For every prefraction $(a,b)\in\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{Q}$,
\[
[a,b]\ne 0_{\mathbb{Q}}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
a\ne 0_{\mathbb{Z}}.
\]
Thus nonzeroness is independent of the representative.

lem:reciprocal-denominator-nonzero-q.title: Reciprocal Lands in $\operatorname{Pre}\mathbb{Q}$
lem:reciprocal-denominator-nonzero-q.statement_preview: If $[a,b]\ne 0_{\mathbb{Q}}$, then $a\ne 0_{\mathbb{Z}}$, so $(b,a)$ is a prefraction.
lem:reciprocal-denominator-nonzero-q.statement_display:
If $[a,b]\ne 0_{\mathbb{Q}}$, then $a\ne 0_{\mathbb{Z}}$, so $(b,a)$ is a
prefraction.
lem:reciprocal-denominator-nonzero-q.statement_tex:

prefraction.

thm:every-nonzero-rational-has-multiplic.statement_preview: For every $x\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $x\ne 0_{\mathbb{Q}}$, there exists $y\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that \[ x\cdot y=1_{\mathbb{Q}} \qquad\text{and}\qquad y\cdot x=1_{\mathbb{Q}}. \]
thm:every-nonzero-rational-has-multiplic.statement_display:
For every $x\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $x\ne 0_{\mathbb{Q}}$, there exists
$y\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that
\[
x\cdot y=1_{\mathbb{Q}}
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
y\cdot x=1_{\mathbb{Q}}.
\]

def:positive-rational.title: Positive Rational
def:positive-rational.statement_preview: A rational number $[a,b]\in\mathbb{Q}$ is \emph{positive} if \[ a\cdot b>0_{\mathbb{Z}}. \]
def:positive-rational.statement_display:
A rational number $[a,b]\in\mathbb{Q}$ is \emph{positive} if
\[
a\cdot b>0_{\mathbb{Z}}.
\]
def:positive-rational.statement_tex:
A rational number $[a,b]\in\mathbb{Q}$ is \emph{positive} if
\[
a\cdot b>0_{\mathbb{Z}}.
\]

lem:positivity-respects-equivalence-q.title: Positivity Respects Fraction Equivalence on $\mathbb{Q}$
lem:positivity-respects-equivalence-q.statement_preview: If $[a,b]=[c,d]$, then \[ a\cdot b>0_{\mathbb{Z}} \Longleftrightarrow c\cdot d>0_{\mathbb{Z}}. \]
lem:positivity-respects-equivalence-q.statement_display:
If $[a,b]=[c,d]$, then
\[
a\cdot b>0_{\mathbb{Z}}
\Longleftrightarrow
c\cdot d>0_{\mathbb{Z}}.
\]
lem:positivity-respects-equivalence-q.statement_tex:
If $[a,b]=[c,d]$, then
\[
a\cdot b>0_{\mathbb{Z}}
\Longleftrightarrow
c\cdot d>0_{\mathbb{Z}}.
\]

thm:positivity-on-q-well-defined.title: Positivity on $\mathbb{Q}$ Is Well-Defined
thm:positivity-on-q-well-defined.statement_preview: The predicate ``$x$ is positive'' is well-defined on equivalence classes in $\mathbb{Q}$.
thm:positivity-on-q-well-defined.statement_display:

$\mathbb{Q}$.
thm:positivity-on-q-well-defined.statement_tex:

def:strict-order-on-q.title: Strict Order on $\mathbb{Q}$
def:strict-order-on-q.statement_preview: For $x,y\in\mathbb{Q}$, define \[ x<y \quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad y-x \text{ is positive}. \]
def:strict-order-on-q.statement_display:
For $x,y\in\mathbb{Q}$, define
\[
x<y \quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad y-x \text{ is positive}.
\]
def:strict-order-on-q.statement_tex:
For $x,y\in\mathbb{Q}$, define
\[
x<y \quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad y-x \text{ is positive}.
\]

def:order-on-q.title: Order on $\mathbb{Q}$
def:order-on-q.statement_preview: For $x,y\in\mathbb{Q}$, define \[ x\le y \quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad x<y \text{ or } x=y. \]
def:order-on-q.statement_display:
For $x,y\in\mathbb{Q}$, define
\[
x\le y \quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad x<y \text{ or } x=y.
\]
def:order-on-q.statement_tex:
For $x,y\in\mathbb{Q}$, define
\[
x\le y \quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad x<y \text{ or } x=y.
\]

thm:sign-trichotomy-on-q.title: Sign Trichotomy on $\mathbb{Q}$
thm:sign-trichotomy-on-q.statement_preview: For every $x\in\mathbb{Q}$, exactly one of \[ x<0_{\mathbb{Q}},\qquad x=0_{\mathbb{Q}},\qquad 0_{\mathbb{Q}}<x \] holds.
thm:sign-trichotomy-on-q.statement_display:
For every $x\in\mathbb{Q}$, exactly one of
\[
x<0_{\mathbb{Q}},\qquad x=0_{\mathbb{Q}},\qquad 0_{\mathbb{Q}}<x
\]
holds.
thm:sign-trichotomy-on-q.statement_tex:
For every $x\in\mathbb{Q}$, exactly one of
\[
x<0_{\mathbb{Q}},\qquad x=0_{\mathbb{Q}},\qquad 0_{\mathbb{Q}}<x
\]
holds.

thm:trichotomy-on-q.title: Trichotomy on $\mathbb{Q}$
thm:trichotomy-on-q.statement_preview: For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Q}$, exactly one of \[ x<y,\qquad x=y,\qquad y<x \] holds.
thm:trichotomy-on-q.statement_display:
For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Q}$, exactly one of
\[
x<y,\qquad x=y,\qquad y<x
\]
holds.
thm:trichotomy-on-q.statement_tex:
For all $x,y\in\mathbb{Q}$, exactly one of
\[
x<y,\qquad x=y,\qquad y<x
\]

def:cauchy-sequence-in-q.title: Cauchy Sequence in $\mathbb{Q}$
def:cauchy-sequence-in-q.statement_preview: A sequence $(x_n)$ in $\mathbb{Q}$ is Cauchy if \[ \forall\varepsilon\in\mathbb{Q}\, \left( 0_{\mathbb{Q}}<\varepsilon \Longrightarrow \exists N\in\mathbb{N}\, \forall m,n\ge N\, \left|x_m-x_n\right|<\varepsilon \right). \]
def:cauchy-sequence-in-q.statement_display:
A sequence $(x_n)$ in $\mathbb{Q}$ is Cauchy if
\[
\forall\varepsilon\in\mathbb{Q}\,
\left(
0_{\mathbb{Q}}<\varepsilon
\Longrightarrow
\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\,
\forall m,n\ge N\,
\left|x_m-x_n\right|<\varepsilon
\right).
\]
def:cauchy-sequence-in-q.statement_tex:
A sequence $(x_n)$ in $\mathbb{Q}$ is Cauchy if
\[
\forall\varepsilon\in\mathbb{Q}\,
\left(
0_{\mathbb{Q}}<\varepsilon
\Longrightarrow
\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\,
\forall m,n\ge N\,
\left|x_m-x_n\right|<\varepsilon
\right).
\]

def:null-sequence-in-q.title: Null Sequence in $\mathbb{Q}$
def:null-sequence-in-q.statement_preview: A sequence $(x_n)$ in $\mathbb{Q}$ is null if \[ \forall\varepsilon\in\mathbb{Q}\, \left( 0_{\mathbb{Q}}<\varepsilon \Longrightarrow \exists N\in\mathbb{N}\, \forall n\ge N\, \left|x_n\right|<\varepsilon \right). \]
def:null-sequence-in-q.statement_display:
A sequence $(x_n)$ in $\mathbb{Q}$ is null if
\[
\forall\varepsilon\in\mathbb{Q}\,
\left(
0_{\mathbb{Q}}<\varepsilon
\Longrightarrow
\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\,
\forall n\ge N\,
\left|x_n\right|<\varepsilon
\right).
\]
def:null-sequence-in-q.statement_tex:
A sequence $(x_n)$ in $\mathbb{Q}$ is null if
\[
\forall\varepsilon\in\mathbb{Q}\,
\left(
0_{\mathbb{Q}}<\varepsilon
\Longrightarrow
\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\,
\forall n\ge N\,
\left|x_n\right|<\varepsilon
\right).
\]

def:upper-bound-in-r.title: Upper Bound in $\mathbb{R}$
def:upper-bound-in-r.statement_preview: Let $S\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. A cut $\gamma\in\mathbb{R}$ is an upper bound of $S$ if \[ \forall\alpha\in S\,(\alpha\le\gamma). \] The set $S$ is bounded above if it has an upper bound in $\mathbb{R}$.

Let $S\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. A cut $\gamma\in\mathbb{R}$ is an upper bound of $S$

\[

\]
The set $S$ is bounded above if it has an upper bound in $\mathbb{R}$.

def:supremum-in-r.title: Supremum in $\mathbb{R}$
def:supremum-in-r.statement_preview: For $S\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, a cut $\sigma$ is the supremum of $S$ if $\sigma$ is an upper bound of $S$ and every upper bound $\gamma$ of $S$ satisfies $\sigma\le\gamma$.

\]

def:rational-cut.title: Rational Cut
def:rational-cut.statement_preview: For $q\in\mathbb{Q}$, define the rational cut \[ q^{\ast}:=\{r\in\mathbb{Q}:r<q\}. \]
def:rational-cut.statement_display:
For $q\in\mathbb{Q}$, define the rational cut
\[
q^{\ast}:=\{r\in\mathbb{Q}:r<q\}.
\]
def:rational-cut.statement_tex:
For $q\in\mathbb{Q}$, define the rational cut
\[
q^{\ast}:=\{r\in\mathbb{Q}:r<q\}.
\]

lem:rational-cut-is-a-cut.title: Rational Cuts Are Cuts
lem:rational-cut-is-a-cut.statement_preview: For every $q\in\mathbb{Q}$, the set $q^{\ast}$ is a Dedekind cut.
lem:rational-cut-is-a-cut.statement_display: For every $q\in\mathbb{Q}$, the set $q^{\ast}$ is a Dedekind cut.
lem:rational-cut-is-a-cut.statement_tex: For every $q\in\mathbb{Q}$, the set $q^{\ast}$ is a Dedekind cut.

thm:zero-one-are-reals.title: Zero and One Are Reals
thm:zero-one-are-reals.statement_preview: The cuts $0_{\mathbb{R}}$ and $1_{\mathbb{R}}$ are elements of $\mathbb{R}$.
thm:zero-one-are-reals.statement_display: The cuts $0_{\mathbb{R}}$ and $1_{\mathbb{R}}$ are elements of $\mathbb{R}$.
thm:zero-one-are-reals.statement_tex: The cuts $0_{\mathbb{R}}$ and $1_{\mathbb{R}}$ are elements of $\mathbb{R}$.

thm:q-embeds-into-r.title: $\mathbb{Q}$ Embeds into $\mathbb{R}$ as an Ordered Field
thm:q-embeds-into-r.statement_preview: The map $\iota$ is an injective order-preserving field homomorphism.
thm:q-embeds-into-r.statement_display: The map $\iota$ is an injective order-preserving field homomorphism.
thm:q-embeds-into-r.statement_tex: The map $\iota$ is an injective order-preserving field homomorphism.

thm:r-nonzero-multiplicative-abelian-gro.title: Nonzero $\mathbb{R}$ Is a Multiplicative Abelian Group
thm:r-nonzero-multiplicative-abelian-gro.statement_preview: The structure $(\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0_{\mathbb{R}}\},\cdot,1_{\mathbb{R}})$ is an abelian group.
thm:r-nonzero-multiplicative-abelian-gro.statement_display:
The structure $(\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0_{\mathbb{R}}\},\cdot,1_{\mathbb{R}})$
is an abelian group.
thm:r-nonzero-multiplicative-abelian-gro.statement_tex:
The structure $(\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0_{\mathbb{R}}\},\cdot,1_{\mathbb{R}})$
is an abelian group.

cor:field-laws-on-r.title: Field Laws Hold on $\mathbb{R}$
cor:field-laws-on-r.statement_preview: Every theorem proved generically for fields holds in $\mathbb{R}$, including zero product, double negation, sign rules, cancellation, uniqueness of inverses, inverse laws, and exponent laws.
cor:field-laws-on-r.statement_display:
Every theorem proved generically for fields holds in $\mathbb{R}$, including
zero product, double negation, sign rules, cancellation, uniqueness of
inverses, inverse laws, and exponent laws.
cor:field-laws-on-r.statement_tex:
Every theorem proved generically for fields holds in $\mathbb{R}$, including
zero product, double negation, sign rules, cancellation, uniqueness of
inverses, inverse laws, and exponent laws.

thm:addition-preserves-order-on-r.title: Addition Preserves Order on $\mathbb{R}$
thm:addition-preserves-order-on-r.statement_preview: For all $\alpha,\beta,\gamma\in\mathbb{R}$, \[ \alpha<\beta \Longrightarrow \alpha+\gamma<\beta+\gamma, \] and the corresponding non-strict statement also holds.
thm:addition-preserves-order-on-r.statement_display:
For all $\alpha,\beta,\gamma\in\mathbb{R}$,
\[
\alpha<\beta \Longrightarrow \alpha+\gamma<\beta+\gamma,
\]
and the corresponding non-strict statement also holds.
thm:addition-preserves-order-on-r.statement_tex:
For all $\alpha,\beta,\gamma\in\mathbb{R}$,
\[
\alpha<\beta \Longrightarrow \alpha+\gamma<\beta+\gamma,
\]
and the corresponding non-strict statement also holds.

thm:positive-multiplication-preserves-or.title: Multiplication by Positives Preserves Order on $\mathbb{R}$

thm:r-is-an-ordered-field.title: $\mathbb{R}$ Is an Ordered Field
thm:r-is-an-ordered-field.statement_preview: The structure $(\mathbb{R},+,\cdot,0_{\mathbb{R}},1_{\mathbb{R}},\le)$ is an ordered field.
thm:r-is-an-ordered-field.statement_display:
The structure $(\mathbb{R},+,\cdot,0_{\mathbb{R}},1_{\mathbb{R}},\le)$ is an
ordered field.
thm:r-is-an-ordered-field.statement_tex:
The structure $(\mathbb{R},+,\cdot,0_{\mathbb{R}},1_{\mathbb{R}},\le)$ is an
ordered field.

cor:ordered-field-laws-on-r.title: Ordered-Field Laws Hold on $\mathbb{R}$
cor:ordered-field-laws-on-r.statement_preview: Every theorem proved generically for ordered fields holds in $\mathbb{R}$.
cor:ordered-field-laws-on-r.statement_display: Every theorem proved generically for ordered fields holds in $\mathbb{R}$.
cor:ordered-field-laws-on-r.statement_tex: Every theorem proved generically for ordered fields holds in $\mathbb{R}$.

thm:r-is-complete-ordered-field.title: $\mathbb{R}$ Is a Complete Ordered Field
thm:r-is-complete-ordered-field.statement_preview: The ordered field $\mathbb{R}$ satisfies the least-upper-bound property.
thm:r-is-complete-ordered-field.statement_display: The ordered field $\mathbb{R}$ satisfies the least-upper-bound property.
thm:r-is-complete-ordered-field.statement_tex: The ordered field $\mathbb{R}$ satisfies the least-upper-bound property.

def:nonnegative-cut.title: Nonnegative Cut
def:nonnegative-cut.statement_preview: A cut $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$ is nonnegative if $0_{\mathbb{R}}\le\alpha$ and positive if $0_{\mathbb{R}}<\alpha$.
def:nonnegative-cut.statement_display:
A cut $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$ is nonnegative if $0_{\mathbb{R}}\le\alpha$ and
positive if $0_{\mathbb{R}}<\alpha$.
def:nonnegative-cut.statement_tex:
A cut $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$ is nonnegative if $0_{\mathbb{R}}\le\alpha$ and
positive if $0_{\mathbb{R}}<\alpha$.

def:product-nonnegative-on-r.title: Product of Nonnegative Cuts
def:product-nonnegative-on-r.statement_preview: For nonnegative cuts $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$, define \[ \alpha\cdot\beta := \{r\in\mathbb{Q}:r<0_{\mathbb{Q}}\} \cup \{p\cdot q:p\in\alpha,\ q\in\beta,\ p\ge 0_{\mathbb{Q}},\ q\ge 0_{\mathbb{Q}}\}. \]
def:product-nonnegative-on-r.statement_display:
For nonnegative cuts $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$, define
\[
\alpha\cdot\beta
:=
\{r\in\mathbb{Q}:r<0_{\mathbb{Q}}\}
\cup
\{p\cdot q:p\in\alpha,\ q\in\beta,\ p\ge 0_{\mathbb{Q}},\ q\ge 0_{\mathbb{Q}}\}.
\]
def:product-nonnegative-on-r.statement_tex:
For nonnegative cuts $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$, define
\[
\alpha\cdot\beta
:=
\{r\in\mathbb{Q}:r<0_{\mathbb{Q}}\}
\cup
\{p\cdot q:p\in\alpha,\ q\in\beta,\ p\ge 0_{\mathbb{Q}},\ q\ge 0_{\mathbb{Q}}\}.
\]

lem:product-nonnegative-is-a-cut.title: Product of Nonnegative Cuts Is a Cut
lem:product-nonnegative-is-a-cut.statement_preview: If $\alpha,\beta\ge 0_{\mathbb{R}}$, then $\alpha\cdot\beta$ is a Dedekind cut.
lem:product-nonnegative-is-a-cut.statement_display: If $\alpha,\beta\ge 0_{\mathbb{R}}$, then $\alpha\cdot\beta$ is a Dedekind cut.
lem:product-nonnegative-is-a-cut.statement_tex: If $\alpha,\beta\ge 0_{\mathbb{R}}$, then $\alpha\cdot\beta$ is a Dedekind cut.

def:multiplication-on-r.title: Multiplication of Cuts
def:multiplication-on-r.statement_preview: For cuts $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$, define $\alpha\cdot\beta$ by sign cases: use $|\alpha|\cdot|\beta|$ when $\alpha$ and $\beta$ have the same sign, use $-(|\alpha|\cdot|\beta|)$ when they have opposite signs, and use $0_{\mathbb{R}}$ when either factor is $0_{\mathbb{R}}$.
def:multiplication-on-r.statement_display:
For cuts $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$, define $\alpha\cdot\beta$ by sign cases:
use $|\alpha|\cdot|\beta|$ when $\alpha$ and $\beta$ have the same sign, use
$-(|\alpha|\cdot|\beta|)$ when they have opposite signs, and use
$0_{\mathbb{R}}$ when either factor is $0_{\mathbb{R}}$.
def:multiplication-on-r.statement_tex:
For cuts $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$, define $\alpha\cdot\beta$ by sign cases:
use $|\alpha|\cdot|\beta|$ when $\alpha$ and $\beta$ have the same sign, use
$-(|\alpha|\cdot|\beta|)$ when they have opposite signs, and use
$0_{\mathbb{R}}$ when either factor is $0_{\mathbb{R}}$.

lem:product-of-cuts-is-a-cut.title: Product of Cuts Is a Cut

thm:one-multiplicative-identity-on-r.title: One Is a Multiplicative Identity on $\mathbb{R}$

def:reciprocal-on-r.title: Reciprocal of a Nonzero Cut
def:reciprocal-on-r.statement_preview: For $\alpha\ne0_{\mathbb{R}}$, define $\alpha^{-1}$ to be $|\alpha|^{-1}$ if $\alpha>0_{\mathbb{R}}$ and $-(|\alpha|^{-1})$ if $\alpha<0_{\mathbb{R}}$.
def:reciprocal-on-r.statement_display:
For $\alpha\ne0_{\mathbb{R}}$, define $\alpha^{-1}$ to be $|\alpha|^{-1}$ if
$\alpha>0_{\mathbb{R}}$ and $-(|\alpha|^{-1})$ if $\alpha<0_{\mathbb{R}}$.
def:reciprocal-on-r.statement_tex:
For $\alpha\ne0_{\mathbb{R}}$, define $\alpha^{-1}$ to be $|\alpha|^{-1}$ if
$\alpha>0_{\mathbb{R}}$ and $-(|\alpha|^{-1})$ if $\alpha<0_{\mathbb{R}}$.

lem:reciprocal-is-a-cut.title: Reciprocal of a Nonzero Cut Is a Cut

thm:reciprocal-multiplicative-inverse-on.title: Reciprocal Is a Multiplicative Inverse on $\mathbb{R}$

thm:trichotomy-on-r.title: Trichotomy on $\mathbb{R}$
thm:trichotomy-on-r.statement_preview: For all $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$, exactly one of \[ \alpha<\beta,\qquad \alpha=\beta,\qquad \beta<\alpha \] holds.
thm:trichotomy-on-r.statement_display:
For all $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$, exactly one of
\[
\alpha<\beta,\qquad \alpha=\beta,\qquad \beta<\alpha
\]
holds.
thm:trichotomy-on-r.statement_tex:
For all $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$, exactly one of
\[
\alpha<\beta,\qquad \alpha=\beta,\qquad \beta<\alpha
\]
holds.

thm:r-totally-ordered-set.title: $\mathbb{R}$ Is a Totally Ordered Set
thm:r-totally-ordered-set.statement_preview: The ordered pair $(\mathbb{R},\le)$ is a total order.
thm:r-totally-ordered-set.statement_display: The ordered pair $(\mathbb{R},\le)$ is a total order.
thm:r-totally-ordered-set.statement_tex: The ordered pair $(\mathbb{R},\le)$ is a total order.

lem:sqrt-two-is-a-cut.title: The Square-Root-of-Two Cut Is a Cut
lem:sqrt-two-is-a-cut.statement_preview: The set $\sqrt{2}$ is a Dedekind cut.
lem:sqrt-two-is-a-cut.statement_display: The set $\sqrt{2}$ is a Dedekind cut.
lem:sqrt-two-is-a-cut.statement_tex: The set $\sqrt{2}$ is a Dedekind cut.

cor:r-fills-the-gap.title: $\mathbb{R}$ Contains the Supremum $\mathbb{Q}$ Was Missing
cor:r-fills-the-gap.statement_preview: Let \[ S=\{q\in\mathbb{Q}:0_{\mathbb{Q}}<q\land q\cdot q<2_{\mathbb{Q}}\}. \] Then \[ \sqrt{2}=\sup \iota(S). \] Thus the bounded-above rational set with no rational supremum has a supremum in $\mathbb{R}$.
cor:r-fills-the-gap.statement_display:
Let
\[
S=\{q\in\mathbb{Q}:0_{\mathbb{Q}}<q\land q\cdot q<2_{\mathbb{Q}}\}.
\]
Then
\[
\sqrt{2}=\sup \iota(S).
\]
Thus the bounded-above rational set with no rational supremum has a supremum
in $\mathbb{R}$.
cor:r-fills-the-gap.statement_tex:
Let
\[
S=\{q\in\mathbb{Q}:0_{\mathbb{Q}}<q\land q\cdot q<2_{\mathbb{Q}}\}.
\]
Then
\[
\sqrt{2}=\sup \iota(S).
\]
Thus the bounded-above rational set with no rational supremum has a supremum
in $\mathbb{R}$.

prop:abs-value-is-distance-to-zero.title: Absolute Value Is Distance to the Origin

def:closed-set.title: Closed Set in $\mathbb{R}$
def:closed-set.statement_preview: A set $F\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is \emph{closed} exactly when its complement $\mathbb{R}\setminus F$ is open.
def:closed-set.statement_display: A set $F\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is \emph{closed} exactly when its complement $\mathbb{R}\setminus F$ is open.
def:closed-set.statement_tex: A set $F\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is \emph{closed} exactly when its complement $\mathbb{R}\setminus F$ is open.

thm:closed-iff-contains-limit-points.title: Closed Sets Contain Their Limit Points
thm:closed-iff-contains-limit-points.statement_preview: A set $F\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is closed if and only if it contains every one of its limit points.
thm:closed-iff-contains-limit-points.statement_display: A set $F\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is closed if and only if it contains every one of its limit points.
thm:closed-iff-contains-limit-points.statement_tex: A set $F\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is closed if and only if it contains every one of its limit points.

def:compact-set.title: Compact Set in $\mathbb{R}$
def:compact-set.statement_preview: A set $K\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is \emph{compact} exactly when every open cover of $K$ admits a finite subcover: \[ \forall \{U_i\}_{i\in I}\text{ open cover of }K\;\exists \text{finite } J\subseteq I\;\Bigl(K\subseteq\bigcup_{i\in J}U_i\Bigr). \]
def:compact-set.statement_display:
A set $K\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is \emph{compact} exactly when every open cover of $K$ admits a finite subcover:
\[
\forall \{U_i\}_{i\in I}\text{ open cover of }K\;\exists \text{finite } J\subseteq I\;\Bigl(K\subseteq\bigcup_{i\in J}U_i\Bigr).
\]
def:compact-set.statement_tex:
A set $K\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is \emph{compact} exactly when every open cover of $K$ admits a finite subcover:
\[
\forall \{U_i\}_{i\in I}\text{ open cover of }K\;\exists \text{finite } J\subseteq I\;\Bigl(K\subseteq\bigcup_{i\in J}U_i\Bigr).
\]

thm:compact-implies-closed-bounded.title: Compact Subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ Are Closed and Bounded
thm:compact-implies-closed-bounded.statement_preview: If $K\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is compact, then $K$ is closed and bounded.
thm:compact-implies-closed-bounded.statement_display: If $K\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is compact, then $K$ is closed and bounded.
thm:compact-implies-closed-bounded.statement_tex: If $K\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is compact, then $K$ is closed and bounded.

def:real-open-cover.title: Open Cover
def:real-open-cover.statement_preview: Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. An \emph{open cover} of $E$ is a family $\{U_{i}\}_{i\in I}$ of open subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ with \[ E\subseteq\bigcup_{i\in I} U_{i}. \]
def:real-open-cover.statement_display:
Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. An \emph{open cover} of $E$ is a family $\{U_{i}\}_{i\in I}$ of open subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ with
\[
E\subseteq\bigcup_{i\in I} U_{i}.
\]
def:real-open-cover.statement_tex:
Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. An \emph{open cover} of $E$ is a family $\{U_{i}\}_{i\in I}$ of open subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ with
\[
E\subseteq\bigcup_{i\in I} U_{i}.
\]

def:finite-subcover.title: Subcover and Finite Subcover
def:finite-subcover.statement_preview: Let $\{U_{i}\}_{i\in I}$ be an open cover of $E$. A \emph{subcover} is a subfamily $\{U_{i}\}_{i\in J}$ with $J\subseteq I$ that still covers $E$. It is a \emph{finite subcover} when $J$ is finite.
def:finite-subcover.statement_display: Let $\{U_{i}\}_{i\in I}$ be an open cover of $E$. A \emph{subcover} is a subfamily $\{U_{i}\}_{i\in J}$ with $J\subseteq I$ that still covers $E$. It is a \emph{finite subcover} when $J$ is finite.
def:finite-subcover.statement_tex: Let $\{U_{i}\}_{i\in I}$ be an open cover of $E$. A \emph{subcover} is a subfamily $\{U_{i}\}_{i\in J}$ with $J\subseteq I$ that still covers $E$. It is a \emph{finite subcover} when $J$ is finite.

thm:closed-bounded-interval-compact.title: Closed Bounded Intervals Are Compact
thm:closed-bounded-interval-compact.statement_preview: Every closed bounded interval $[a,b]\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is compact.
thm:closed-bounded-interval-compact.statement_display: Every closed bounded interval $[a,b]\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is compact.
thm:closed-bounded-interval-compact.statement_tex: Every closed bounded interval $[a,b]\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is compact.

thm:heine-borel.title: Heine--Borel Theorem for $\mathbb{R}$
thm:heine-borel.statement_preview: A set $K\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is compact if and only if $K$ is closed and bounded.
thm:heine-borel.statement_display: A set $K\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is compact if and only if $K$ is closed and bounded.
thm:heine-borel.statement_tex: A set $K\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is compact if and only if $K$ is closed and bounded.

def:interior-point.title: Interior Point
def:interior-point.statement_preview: Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $x$ is an interior point of $E$ exactly when \[ \exists r>0\;(B_{r}(x)\subseteq E). \]
def:interior-point.statement_display:
Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $x$ is an interior point of $E$ exactly when
\[
\exists r>0\;(B_{r}(x)\subseteq E).
\]
def:interior-point.statement_tex:
Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $x$ is an interior point of $E$ exactly when
\[
\exists r>0\;(B_{r}(x)\subseteq E).
\]

def:exterior-point.title: Exterior Point
def:exterior-point.statement_preview: Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $x$ is an exterior point of $E$ exactly when \[ \exists r>0\;(B_{r}(x)\subseteq \mathbb{R}\setminus E). \]
def:exterior-point.statement_display:
Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $x$ is an exterior point of $E$ exactly when
\[
\exists r>0\;(B_{r}(x)\subseteq \mathbb{R}\setminus E).
\]
def:exterior-point.statement_tex:
Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $x$ is an exterior point of $E$ exactly when
\[
\exists r>0\;(B_{r}(x)\subseteq \mathbb{R}\setminus E).
\]

def:boundary-point.title: Boundary Point
def:boundary-point.statement_preview: Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $x$ is a boundary point of $E$ exactly when \[ \forall r>0\;(B_{r}(x)\cap E\neq\emptyset \;\land\; B_{r}(x)\cap(\mathbb{R}\setminus E)\neq\emptyset). \]
def:boundary-point.statement_display:
Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $x$ is a boundary point of $E$ exactly when
\[
\forall r>0\;(B_{r}(x)\cap E\neq\emptyset \;\land\; B_{r}(x)\cap(\mathbb{R}\setminus E)\neq\emptyset).
\]
def:boundary-point.statement_tex:
Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $x$ is a boundary point of $E$ exactly when
\[
\forall r>0\;(B_{r}(x)\cap E\neq\emptyset \;\land\; B_{r}(x)\cap(\mathbb{R}\setminus E)\neq\emptyset).
\]

def:interior-of-set.title: Interior of a Set
def:interior-of-set.statement_preview: The \emph{interior} of $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, written $\operatorname{int}(E)$, is the set of all interior points of $E$.
def:interior-of-set.statement_display: The \emph{interior} of $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, written $\operatorname{int}(E)$, is the set of all interior points of $E$.
