def:interior-of-set.statement_tex: The \emph{interior} of $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, written $\operatorname{int}(E)$, is the set of all interior points of $E$.

def:bounded-subset-of-r.title: Bounded Subset of the Real Line
def:bounded-subset-of-r.statement_preview: A set $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is \emph{bounded} exactly when there exists $M\ge 0$ such that $|x|\le M$ for all $x\in E$; equivalently, $E$ is contained in some closed interval $[-M,M]$.
def:bounded-subset-of-r.statement_display: A set $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is \emph{bounded} exactly when there exists $M\ge 0$ such that $|x|\le M$ for all $x\in E$; equivalently, $E$ is contained in some closed interval $[-M,M]$.
def:bounded-subset-of-r.statement_tex: A set $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is \emph{bounded} exactly when there exists $M\ge 0$ such that $|x|\le M$ for all $x\in E$; equivalently, $E$ is contained in some closed interval $[-M,M]$.

def:union-of-intervals.title: Union of Intervals
def:union-of-intervals.statement_preview: Let $I,J\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be intervals. Their \emph{union} is the set \[ I\cup J=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:x\in I\text{ or }x\in J\}. \]
def:union-of-intervals.statement_display:
Let $I,J\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be intervals. Their \emph{union} is the set
\[
I\cup J=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:x\in I\text{ or }x\in J\}.
\]
def:union-of-intervals.statement_tex:
Let $I,J\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be intervals. Their \emph{union} is the set
\[
I\cup J=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:x\in I\text{ or }x\in J\}.
\]

def:intersection-of-intervals.title: Intersection of Intervals
def:intersection-of-intervals.statement_preview: Let $I,J\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be intervals. Their \emph{intersection} is the set \[ I\cap J=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:x\in I\text{ and }x\in J\}. \]
def:intersection-of-intervals.statement_display:
Let $I,J\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be intervals. Their \emph{intersection} is the set
\[
I\cap J=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:x\in I\text{ and }x\in J\}.
\]
def:intersection-of-intervals.statement_tex:
Let $I,J\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be intervals. Their \emph{intersection} is the set
\[
I\cap J=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:x\in I\text{ and }x\in J\}.
\]

def:complement-of-interval.title: Complement of an Interval
def:complement-of-interval.statement_preview: Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval. Its \emph{complement} in $\mathbb{R}$ is the set \[ I^{c}=\mathbb{R}\setminus I =\{x\in\mathbb{R}:x\notin I\}. \]
def:complement-of-interval.statement_display:
Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval. Its \emph{complement} in
$\mathbb{R}$ is the set
\[
I^{c}=\mathbb{R}\setminus I
=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:x\notin I\}.
\]
def:complement-of-interval.statement_tex:
Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval. Its \emph{complement} in
$\mathbb{R}$ is the set
\[
I^{c}=\mathbb{R}\setminus I
=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:x\notin I\}.
\]

def:difference-of-intervals.title: Difference of Intervals
def:difference-of-intervals.statement_preview: Let $I,J\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be intervals. The \emph{difference} of $I$ by $J$ is the set \[ I\setminus J=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:x\in I\text{ and }x\notin J\} =I\cap J^{c}. \]
def:difference-of-intervals.statement_display:
Let $I,J\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be intervals. The \emph{difference} of $I$ by
$J$ is the set
\[
I\setminus J=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:x\in I\text{ and }x\notin J\}
=I\cap J^{c}.
\]
def:difference-of-intervals.statement_tex:
Let $I,J\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be intervals. The \emph{difference} of $I$ by
$J$ is the set
\[
I\setminus J=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:x\in I\text{ and }x\notin J\}
=I\cap J^{c}.
\]

def:limit-point.title: Limit Point
def:limit-point.statement_preview: Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $x$ is a \emph{limit point} of $E$ exactly when \[ \forall r>0\;\exists y\in E\;\bigl(0<|y-x|<r\bigr). \]
def:limit-point.statement_display:
Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $x$ is a \emph{limit point} of $E$ exactly when
\[
\forall r>0\;\exists y\in E\;\bigl(0<|y-x|<r\bigr).
\]
def:limit-point.statement_tex:
Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $x$ is a \emph{limit point} of $E$ exactly when
\[
\forall r>0\;\exists y\in E\;\bigl(0<|y-x|<r\bigr).
\]

def:isolated-point.title: Isolated Point
def:isolated-point.statement_preview: Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in E$. Then $x$ is an \emph{isolated point} of $E$ exactly when \[ \exists r>0\;\forall y\in E\;\bigl(|y-x|<r \Rightarrow y=x\bigr). \]
def:isolated-point.statement_display:
Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in E$. Then $x$ is an \emph{isolated point} of $E$ exactly when
\[
\exists r>0\;\forall y\in E\;\bigl(|y-x|<r \Rightarrow y=x\bigr).
\]
def:isolated-point.statement_tex:
Let $E\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and $x\in E$. Then $x$ is an \emph{isolated point} of $E$ exactly when
\[
\exists r>0\;\forall y\in E\;\bigl(|y-x|<r \Rightarrow y=x\bigr).
\]

def:open-ball.title: Open Ball on the Real Line
def:open-ball.statement_preview: Let $x\in\mathbb{R}$ and $r>0$. The \emph{open ball} of radius $r$ about $x$ is \[ B_{r}(x)=\{\,y\in\mathbb{R} : |y-x|<r\,\}=(x-r,\,x+r). \]
def:open-ball.statement_display:
Let $x\in\mathbb{R}$ and $r>0$. The \emph{open ball} of radius $r$ about $x$ is
\[
B_{r}(x)=\{\,y\in\mathbb{R} : |y-x|<r\,\}=(x-r,\,x+r).
\]
def:open-ball.statement_tex:
Let $x\in\mathbb{R}$ and $r>0$. The \emph{open ball} of radius $r$ about $x$ is
\[
B_{r}(x)=\{\,y\in\mathbb{R} : |y-x|<r\,\}=(x-r,\,x+r).
\]

def:neighborhood-of-point.title: Neighborhood of a Point
def:neighborhood-of-point.statement_preview: A set $N\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is a \emph{neighborhood} of $x\in\mathbb{R}$ exactly when there exists $r>0$ with $B_{r}(x)\subseteq N$.
def:neighborhood-of-point.statement_display: A set $N\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is a \emph{neighborhood} of $x\in\mathbb{R}$ exactly when there exists $r>0$ with $B_{r}(x)\subseteq N$.
def:neighborhood-of-point.statement_tex: A set $N\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is a \emph{neighborhood} of $x\in\mathbb{R}$ exactly when there exists $r>0$ with $B_{r}(x)\subseteq N$.

def:open-set.title: Open Set in $\mathbb{R}$
def:open-set.statement_preview: A set $U\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is \emph{open} exactly when \[ \forall x\in U\;\exists r>0\;(B_{r}(x)\subseteq U). \]
def:open-set.statement_display:
A set $U\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is \emph{open} exactly when
\[
\forall x\in U\;\exists r>0\;(B_{r}(x)\subseteq U).
\]
def:open-set.statement_tex:
A set $U\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is \emph{open} exactly when
\[
\forall x\in U\;\exists r>0\;(B_{r}(x)\subseteq U).
\]

thm:open-interval-is-open.title: Open Intervals Are Open
thm:open-interval-is-open.statement_preview: Every open interval $(a,b)$, every open ray $(a,\infty)$ and $(-\infty,b)$, and $\mathbb{R}$ itself are open sets.
thm:open-interval-is-open.statement_display: Every open interval $(a,b)$, every open ray $(a,\infty)$ and $(-\infty,b)$, and $\mathbb{R}$ itself are open sets.
thm:open-interval-is-open.statement_tex: Every open interval $(a,b)$, every open ray $(a,\infty)$ and $(-\infty,b)$, and $\mathbb{R}$ itself are open sets.

thm:open-set-closure-operations.title: Unions and Finite Intersections of Open Sets
thm:open-set-closure-operations.statement_preview: An arbitrary union of open subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is open, and a finite intersection of open subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is open.
thm:open-set-closure-operations.statement_display: An arbitrary union of open subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is open, and a finite intersection of open subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is open.
thm:open-set-closure-operations.statement_tex: An arbitrary union of open subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is open, and a finite intersection of open subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is open.

def:distance-on-real-line.title: Distance on the Real Line
def:distance-on-real-line.statement_preview: The \emph{distance} on $\mathbb{R}$ is the map $d:\mathbb{R}\times\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ given by \[ d(x,y)=|x-y|. \]
def:distance-on-real-line.statement_display:
The \emph{distance} on $\mathbb{R}$ is the map $d:\mathbb{R}\times\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ given by
\[
d(x,y)=|x-y|.
\]
def:distance-on-real-line.statement_tex:
The \emph{distance} on $\mathbb{R}$ is the map $d:\mathbb{R}\times\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ given by
\[
d(x,y)=|x-y|.
\]

def:length-of-interval.title: Length of a Bounded Interval
def:length-of-interval.statement_preview: Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a\le b$. The \emph{length} of any bounded interval with endpoints $a$ and $b$ is \[ \ell=b-a. \]
def:length-of-interval.statement_display:
Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a\le b$. The \emph{length} of any bounded interval with endpoints $a$ and $b$ is
\[
\ell=b-a.
\]
def:length-of-interval.statement_tex:
Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a\le b$. The \emph{length} of any bounded interval with endpoints $a$ and $b$ is
\[
\ell=b-a.
\]

thm:distance-is-a-metric.title: The Distance Function Is a Metric
thm:distance-is-a-metric.statement_preview: The map $d(x,y)=|x-y|$ satisfies, for all $x,y,z\in\mathbb{R}$: (i) $d(x,y)\ge 0$ with $d(x,y)=0$ iff $x=y$; (ii) $d(x,y)=d(y,x)$; and (iii) $d(x,z)\le d(x,y)+d(y,z)$. Hence $(\mathbb{R},d)$ is a metric space.
thm:distance-is-a-metric.statement_display: The map $d(x,y)=|x-y|$ satisfies, for all $x,y,z\in\mathbb{R}$: (i) $d(x,y)\ge 0$ with $d(x,y)=0$ iff $x=y$; (ii) $d(x,y)=d(y,x)$; and (iii) $d(x,z)\le d(x,y)+d(y,z)$. Hence $(\mathbb{R},d)$ is a metric space.
thm:distance-is-a-metric.statement_tex: The map $d(x,y)=|x-y|$ satisfies, for all $x,y,z\in\mathbb{R}$: (i) $d(x,y)\ge 0$ with $d(x,y)=0$ iff $x=y$; (ii) $d(x,y)=d(y,x)$; and (iii) $d(x,z)\le d(x,y)+d(y,z)$. Hence $(\mathbb{R},d)$ is a metric space.

thm:closed-set-closure-operations.title: Closure Laws for Closed Sets
thm:closed-set-closure-operations.statement_preview: An arbitrary intersection of closed subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is closed, and a finite union of closed subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is closed.
thm:closed-set-closure-operations.statement_display: An arbitrary intersection of closed subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is closed, and a finite union of closed subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is closed.
thm:closed-set-closure-operations.statement_tex: An arbitrary intersection of closed subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is closed, and a finite union of closed subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is closed.

thm:w-additive-commutative-monoid.title: $(\mathbb{W},+,0)$ Is a Commutative Monoid
thm:w-additive-commutative-monoid.statement_preview: The structure $(\mathbb{W},+_{\mathbb{W}},0)$ is a commutative monoid.
thm:w-additive-commutative-monoid.statement_display: The structure $(\mathbb{W},+_{\mathbb{W}},0)$ is a commutative monoid.
thm:w-additive-commutative-monoid.statement_tex: The structure $(\mathbb{W},+_{\mathbb{W}},0)$ is a commutative monoid.

thm:w-multiplicative-commutative-monoid.title: $(\mathbb{W},\cdot,1)$ Is a Commutative Monoid
thm:w-multiplicative-commutative-monoid.statement_preview: The structure $(\mathbb{W},\cdot_{\mathbb{W}},1)$ is a commutative monoid.
thm:w-multiplicative-commutative-monoid.statement_display: The structure $(\mathbb{W},\cdot_{\mathbb{W}},1)$ is a commutative monoid.
thm:w-multiplicative-commutative-monoid.statement_tex: The structure $(\mathbb{W},\cdot_{\mathbb{W}},1)$ is a commutative monoid.

thm:w-commutative-semiring.title: $(\mathbb{W},+,\cdot,0,1)$ Is a Commutative Semiring
thm:w-commutative-semiring.statement_preview: The structure $(\mathbb{W},+_{\mathbb{W}},\cdot_{\mathbb{W}},0,1)$ is a commutative semiring.
thm:w-commutative-semiring.statement_display:
The structure
$(\mathbb{W},+_{\mathbb{W}},\cdot_{\mathbb{W}},0,1)$ is a commutative
semiring.
thm:w-commutative-semiring.statement_tex:
The structure
$(\mathbb{W},+_{\mathbb{W}},\cdot_{\mathbb{W}},0,1)$ is a commutative
semiring.

thm:w-commutative-ordered-semiring.title: $\mathbb{W}$ Is a Commutative Ordered Semiring
thm:w-commutative-ordered-semiring.statement_preview: With the operations $+_{\mathbb{W}}$ and $\cdot_{\mathbb{W}}$ and the order $\le_{\mathbb{W}}$, the whole numbers form a commutative ordered semiring.
thm:w-commutative-ordered-semiring.statement_display:
With the operations $+_{\mathbb{W}}$ and $\cdot_{\mathbb{W}}$ and the order
$\le_{\mathbb{W}}$, the whole numbers form a commutative ordered semiring.
thm:w-commutative-ordered-semiring.statement_tex:
With the operations $+_{\mathbb{W}}$ and $\cdot_{\mathbb{W}}$ and the order
$\le_{\mathbb{W}}$, the whole numbers form a commutative ordered semiring.

thm:n-embeds-into-w-as-positive-part.title: $\mathbb{N}$ Embeds into $\mathbb{W}$ as the Positive Part
thm:n-embeds-into-w-as-positive-part.statement_preview: The inclusion map identifies $\mathbb{N}$ with the positive part $\mathbb{W}\setminus\{0\}$ and preserves successor, $1$, addition, multiplication, and order.
thm:n-embeds-into-w-as-positive-part.statement_display:
The inclusion map identifies $\mathbb{N}$ with the positive part
$\mathbb{W}\setminus\{0\}$ and preserves successor, $1$, addition,
multiplication, and order.
thm:n-embeds-into-w-as-positive-part.statement_tex:
The inclusion map identifies $\mathbb{N}$ with the positive part
$\mathbb{W}\setminus\{0\}$ and preserves successor, $1$, addition,
multiplication, and order.

thm:w-is-not-a-ring.title: $\mathbb{W}$ Is Not a Ring
thm:w-is-not-a-ring.statement_preview: With the operations $+_{\mathbb{W}}$ and $\cdot_{\mathbb{W}}$, the whole numbers do not form a ring.
thm:w-is-not-a-ring.statement_display:
With the operations $+_{\mathbb{W}}$ and $\cdot_{\mathbb{W}}$, the whole
numbers do not form a ring.
thm:w-is-not-a-ring.statement_tex:
With the operations $+_{\mathbb{W}}$ and $\cdot_{\mathbb{W}}$, the whole
numbers do not form a ring.

def:whole-numbers.title: Whole Numbers
def:whole-numbers.statement_preview: Let $\mathbb{N}$ be the one-based natural-number system developed in the preceding chapter. Let $0$ be a new element with $0\notin\mathbb{N}$. Define \[ \mathbb{W}\coloneqq \mathbb{N}\cup\{0\}. \]
def:whole-numbers.statement_display:
Let $\mathbb{N}$ be the one-based natural-number system developed in the
preceding chapter. Let $0$ be a new element with $0\notin\mathbb{N}$. Define
\[
\mathbb{W}\coloneqq \mathbb{N}\cup\{0\}.
\]
def:whole-numbers.statement_tex:
Let $\mathbb{N}$ be the one-based natural-number system developed in the
preceding chapter. Let $0$ be a new element with $0\notin\mathbb{N}$. Define
\[
\mathbb{W}\coloneqq \mathbb{N}\cup\{0\}.
\]

thm:zero-not-in-n.title: $0\notin\mathbb{N}$
thm:zero-not-in-n.statement_preview: The adjoined element $0$ is not an element of the one-based natural-number system: \[ 0\notin\mathbb{N}. \]
thm:zero-not-in-n.statement_display:
The adjoined element $0$ is not an element of the one-based natural-number
system:
\[
0\notin\mathbb{N}.
\]
thm:zero-not-in-n.statement_tex:
The adjoined element $0$ is not an element of the one-based natural-number
system:
\[
0\notin\mathbb{N}.
\]

def:addition-on-w.title: Addition on $\mathbb{W}$
def:addition-on-w.statement_preview: Define $+_{\mathbb{W}}:\mathbb{W}\times\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{W}$ by \[ 0+_{\mathbb{W}}a=a,\qquad a+_{\mathbb{W}}0=a, \] and, for $a,b\in\mathbb{N}$, \[ a+_{\mathbb{W}}b=a+_{\mathbb{N}}b. \]
def:addition-on-w.statement_display:
Define $+_{\mathbb{W}}:\mathbb{W}\times\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{W}$ by
\[
0+_{\mathbb{W}}a=a,\qquad
a+_{\mathbb{W}}0=a,
\]
and, for $a,b\in\mathbb{N}$,
\[
a+_{\mathbb{W}}b=a+_{\mathbb{N}}b.
\]
def:addition-on-w.statement_tex:
Define $+_{\mathbb{W}}:\mathbb{W}\times\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{W}$ by
\[
0+_{\mathbb{W}}a=a,\qquad
a+_{\mathbb{W}}0=a,
\]
and, for $a,b\in\mathbb{N}$,
\[
a+_{\mathbb{W}}b=a+_{\mathbb{N}}b.
\]

thm:addition-on-w-well-defined.title: Addition on $\mathbb{W}$ Is Well-Defined
thm:addition-on-w-well-defined.statement_preview: The case definition of $+_{\mathbb{W}}$ determines a binary operation on $\mathbb{W}$.
thm:addition-on-w-well-defined.statement_display:
The case definition of $+_{\mathbb{W}}$ determines a binary operation on
$\mathbb{W}$.
thm:addition-on-w-well-defined.statement_tex:
The case definition of $+_{\mathbb{W}}$ determines a binary operation on
$\mathbb{W}$.

thm:zero-additive-identity-on-w.title: Zero Is an Additive Identity on $\mathbb{W}$

thm:multiplication-on-w-well-defined.title: Multiplication on $\mathbb{W}$ Is Well-Defined
thm:multiplication-on-w-well-defined.statement_preview: The case definition of $\cdot_{\mathbb{W}}$ determines a binary operation on $\mathbb{W}$.
thm:multiplication-on-w-well-defined.statement_display:
The case definition of $\cdot_{\mathbb{W}}$ determines a binary operation on
$\mathbb{W}$.
thm:multiplication-on-w-well-defined.statement_tex:
The case definition of $\cdot_{\mathbb{W}}$ determines a binary operation on
$\mathbb{W}$.

thm:zero-absorbing-for-multiplication-on.title: Zero Is Absorbing for Multiplication on $\mathbb{W}$

thm:one-multiplicative-identity-on-w.title: One Is a Multiplicative Identity on $\mathbb{W}$

thm:successor-on-w-well-defined.title: Successor on $\mathbb{W}$ Is Well-Defined
thm:successor-on-w-well-defined.statement_preview: The rule defining $S_{\mathbb{W}}$ determines a function $S_{\mathbb{W}}:\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{W}$.
thm:successor-on-w-well-defined.statement_display:
The rule defining $S_{\mathbb{W}}$ determines a function
$S_{\mathbb{W}}:\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{W}$.
thm:successor-on-w-well-defined.statement_tex:
The rule defining $S_{\mathbb{W}}$ determines a function
$S_{\mathbb{W}}:\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{W}$.

thm:every-nonzero-w-is-successor.title: Every Nonzero Element of $\mathbb{W}$ Is a Successor
thm:every-nonzero-w-is-successor.statement_preview: If $a\in\mathbb{W}$ and $a\neq 0$, then there exists $b\in\mathbb{W}$ such that $a=S_{\mathbb{W}}(b)$.
thm:every-nonzero-w-is-successor.statement_display:
If $a\in\mathbb{W}$ and $a\neq 0$, then there exists
$b\in\mathbb{W}$ such that $a=S_{\mathbb{W}}(b)$.
thm:every-nonzero-w-is-successor.statement_tex:
If $a\in\mathbb{W}$ and $a\neq 0$, then there exists
$b\in\mathbb{W}$ such that $a=S_{\mathbb{W}}(b)$.

thm:w-successor-system-properties.title: $(\mathbb{W},0,S_{\mathbb{W}})$ Satisfies the Successor Properties
thm:w-successor-system-properties.statement_preview: The triple $(\mathbb{W},0,S_{\mathbb{W}})$ satisfies the successor-side properties: \[ 0\in\mathbb{W},\qquad S_{\mathbb{W}}:\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{W}, \] \[ \neg\exists a\in\mathbb{W}\;(S_{\mathbb{W}}(a)=0), \qquad \forall a,b\in\mathbb{W}\; (S_{\mathbb{W}}(a)=S_{\mathbb{W}}(b)\Longrightarrow a=b). \]
thm:w-successor-system-properties.statement_display:
The triple $(\mathbb{W},0,S_{\mathbb{W}})$ satisfies the successor-side
properties:
\[
0\in\mathbb{W},\qquad
S_{\mathbb{W}}:\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{W},
\]
\[
\neg\exists a\in\mathbb{W}\;(S_{\mathbb{W}}(a)=0),
\qquad
\forall a,b\in\mathbb{W}\;
(S_{\mathbb{W}}(a)=S_{\mathbb{W}}(b)\Longrightarrow a=b).
\]
thm:w-successor-system-properties.statement_tex:
The triple $(\mathbb{W},0,S_{\mathbb{W}})$ satisfies the successor-side
properties:
\[
0\in\mathbb{W},\qquad
S_{\mathbb{W}}:\mathbb{W}\to\mathbb{W},
\]
\[
\neg\exists a\in\mathbb{W}\;(S_{\mathbb{W}}(a)=0),
\qquad
\forall a,b\in\mathbb{W}\;
(S_{\mathbb{W}}(a)=S_{\mathbb{W}}(b)\Longrightarrow a=b).
\]

def:order-on-w.title: Order on $\mathbb{W}$
def:order-on-w.statement_preview: Define $\leq_{\mathbb{W}}$ on $\mathbb{W}$ by declaring \[ 0\leq_{\mathbb{W}}a\quad(a\in\mathbb{W}), \] and, for $a,b\in\mathbb{N}$, \[ a\leq_{\mathbb{W}}b \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad a\leq_{\mathbb{N}}b. \]
def:order-on-w.statement_display:
Define $\leq_{\mathbb{W}}$ on $\mathbb{W}$ by declaring
\[
0\leq_{\mathbb{W}}a\quad(a\in\mathbb{W}),
\]
and, for $a,b\in\mathbb{N}$,
\[
a\leq_{\mathbb{W}}b
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
a\leq_{\mathbb{N}}b.
\]
def:order-on-w.statement_tex:
Define $\leq_{\mathbb{W}}$ on $\mathbb{W}$ by declaring
\[
0\leq_{\mathbb{W}}a\quad(a\in\mathbb{W}),
\]
and, for $a,b\in\mathbb{N}$,
\[
a\leq_{\mathbb{W}}b
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
a\leq_{\mathbb{N}}b.
\]

def:strict-order-on-w.title: Strict Order on $\mathbb{W}$
def:strict-order-on-w.statement_preview: Define $<_{\mathbb{W}}$ on $\mathbb{W}$ by declaring \[ 0<_{\mathbb{W}}n\quad(n\in\mathbb{N}), \] and, for $a,b\in\mathbb{N}$, \[ a<_{\mathbb{W}}b \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad a<_{\mathbb{N}}b. \] No element of $\mathbb{W}$ is strictly less than $0$.
def:strict-order-on-w.statement_display:
Define $<_{\mathbb{W}}$ on $\mathbb{W}$ by declaring
\[
0<_{\mathbb{W}}n\quad(n\in\mathbb{N}),
\]
and, for $a,b\in\mathbb{N}$,
\[
a<_{\mathbb{W}}b
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
a<_{\mathbb{N}}b.
\]
No element of $\mathbb{W}$ is strictly less than $0$.
def:strict-order-on-w.statement_tex:
Define $<_{\mathbb{W}}$ on $\mathbb{W}$ by declaring
\[
0<_{\mathbb{W}}n\quad(n\in\mathbb{N}),
\]
and, for $a,b\in\mathbb{N}$,
\[
a<_{\mathbb{W}}b
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
a<_{\mathbb{N}}b.
\]
No element of $\mathbb{W}$ is strictly less than $0$.

thm:order-on-w-extends-order-on-n.title: Order on $\mathbb{W}$ Extends Order on $\mathbb{N}$
thm:order-on-w-extends-order-on-n.statement_preview: For all $a,b\in\mathbb{N}$, \[ a\leq_{\mathbb{W}}b\Longleftrightarrow a\leq_{\mathbb{N}}b, \qquad a<_{\mathbb{W}}b\Longleftrightarrow a<_{\mathbb{N}}b. \]
thm:order-on-w-extends-order-on-n.statement_display:
For all $a,b\in\mathbb{N}$,
\[
a\leq_{\mathbb{W}}b\Longleftrightarrow a\leq_{\mathbb{N}}b,
\qquad
a<_{\mathbb{W}}b\Longleftrightarrow a<_{\mathbb{N}}b.
\]
thm:order-on-w-extends-order-on-n.statement_tex:
For all $a,b\in\mathbb{N}$,
\[
a\leq_{\mathbb{W}}b\Longleftrightarrow a\leq_{\mathbb{N}}b,
\qquad
a<_{\mathbb{W}}b\Longleftrightarrow a<_{\mathbb{N}}b.
\]

thm:zero-least-element-of-w.title: Zero Is the Least Element of $\mathbb{W}$

thm:well-ordering-principle-for-w.title: Well-Ordering Principle for $\mathbb{W}$
thm:well-ordering-principle-for-w.statement_preview: Every nonempty subset of $\mathbb{W}$ has a least element with respect to $\leq_{\mathbb{W}}$.
thm:well-ordering-principle-for-w.statement_display:
Every nonempty subset of $\mathbb{W}$ has a least element with respect to
$\leq_{\mathbb{W}}$.
thm:well-ordering-principle-for-w.statement_tex:
Every nonempty subset of $\mathbb{W}$ has a least element with respect to
$\leq_{\mathbb{W}}$.

prop:uniqueness-of-the-maximum.title: Uniqueness of the Maximum
prop:uniqueness-of-the-maximum.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. If $m_1$ and $m_2$ are both maximum elements of $A$, then $m_1=m_2$.
prop:uniqueness-of-the-maximum.statement_display: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. If $m_1$ and $m_2$ are both maximum elements of $A$, then $m_1=m_2$.
prop:uniqueness-of-the-maximum.statement_tex: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. If $m_1$ and $m_2$ are both maximum elements of $A$, then $m_1=m_2$.

prop:uniqueness-of-the-supremum.title: Uniqueness of the Supremum
prop:uniqueness-of-the-supremum.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. If $s_1$ and $s_2$ are both suprema of $A$, then $s_1=s_2$.
prop:uniqueness-of-the-supremum.statement_display: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. If $s_1$ and $s_2$ are both suprema of $A$, then $s_1=s_2$.
prop:uniqueness-of-the-supremum.statement_tex: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. If $s_1$ and $s_2$ are both suprema of $A$, then $s_1=s_2$.

prop:maximum-implies-supremum.title: Maximum Implies Supremum
prop:maximum-implies-supremum.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $m\in A$. If $m$ is a maximum of $A$, then $m=\sup A$.
prop:maximum-implies-supremum.statement_display: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $m\in A$. If $m$ is a maximum of $A$, then $m=\sup A$.
prop:maximum-implies-supremum.statement_tex: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $m\in A$. If $m$ is a maximum of $A$, then $m=\sup A$.

prop:supremum-in-the-set-is-the-maximum.title: Supremum in the Set Is the Maximum
prop:supremum-in-the-set-is-the-maximum.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $s=\sup A$. If $s\in A$, then $s$ is a maximum of $A$.
prop:supremum-in-the-set-is-the-maximum.statement_display: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $s=\sup A$. If $s\in A$, then $s$ is a maximum of $A$.
prop:supremum-in-the-set-is-the-maximum.statement_tex: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $s=\sup A$. If $s\in A$, then $s$ is a maximum of $A$.

lem:subset-inclusion-preserves-upper-bou.title: Subset Inclusion Preserves Upper Bounds
lem:subset-inclusion-preserves-upper-bou.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. If $u$ is an upper bound of $B$, then $u$ is an upper bound of $A$.
lem:subset-inclusion-preserves-upper-bou.statement_display: Let $A\subseteq B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. If $u$ is an upper bound of $B$, then $u$ is an upper bound of $A$.
lem:subset-inclusion-preserves-upper-bou.statement_tex: Let $A\subseteq B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. If $u$ is an upper bound of $B$, then $u$ is an upper bound of $A$.

thm:supremum-is-monotone-under-inclusion.title: Supremum Is Monotone Under Inclusion
thm:supremum-is-monotone-under-inclusion.statement_preview: Let $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty sets that are bounded above. If $A\subseteq B$, then $\sup A\le \sup B$.
thm:supremum-is-monotone-under-inclusion.statement_display: Let $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty sets that are bounded above. If $A\subseteq B$, then $\sup A\le \sup B$.
thm:supremum-is-monotone-under-inclusion.statement_tex: Let $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty sets that are bounded above. If $A\subseteq B$, then $\sup A\le \sup B$.

prop:upper-bound-comparison-with-the-sup.title: Upper Bound Comparison with the Supremum
prop:upper-bound-comparison-with-the-sup.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above, and let $s=\sup A$. For $u\in\mathbb{R}$, the number $u$ is an upper bound of $A$ if and only if $s\le u$.
prop:upper-bound-comparison-with-the-sup.statement_display: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above, and let $s=\sup A$. For $u\in\mathbb{R}$, the number $u$ is an upper bound of $A$ if and only if $s\le u$.
prop:upper-bound-comparison-with-the-sup.statement_tex: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above, and let $s=\sup A$. For $u\in\mathbb{R}$, the number $u$ is an upper bound of $A$ if and only if $s\le u$.

prop:every-element-lies-below-the-suprem.title: Every Element Lies Below the Supremum
prop:every-element-lies-below-the-suprem.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above, and let $s=\sup A$. Then $x\le s$ for every $x\in A$.
prop:every-element-lies-below-the-suprem.statement_display: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above, and let $s=\sup A$. Then $x\le s$ for every $x\in A$.
prop:every-element-lies-below-the-suprem.statement_tex: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above, and let $s=\sup A$. Then $x\le s$ for every $x\in A$.

thm:infimum-less-than-supremum.title: Infimum Is Less Than or Equal to the Supremum
thm:infimum-less-than-supremum.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty, bounded below, and bounded above. Then $\inf A\le \sup A$.
thm:infimum-less-than-supremum.statement_display: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty, bounded below, and bounded above. Then $\inf A\le \sup A$.
thm:infimum-less-than-supremum.statement_tex: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty, bounded below, and bounded above. Then $\inf A\le \sup A$.

prop:order-comparison-of-suprema.title: Order Comparison of Suprema
prop:order-comparison-of-suprema.statement_preview: Let $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above. If for every $x\in A$ there exists $y\in B$ such that $x\le y$, then $\sup A\le \sup B$.
prop:order-comparison-of-suprema.statement_display: Let $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above. If for every $x\in A$ there exists $y\in B$ such that $x\le y$, then $\sup A\le \sup B$.
prop:order-comparison-of-suprema.statement_tex: Let $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above. If for every $x\in A$ there exists $y\in B$ such that $x\le y$, then $\sup A\le \sup B$.

thm:translation-invariance-supremum.title: Translation Invariance of the Supremum
thm:translation-invariance-supremum.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above, and let $c\in\mathbb{R}$. Then \[ \sup(A+c)=\sup A+c, \qquad A+c=\{a+c:a\in A\}. \]
thm:translation-invariance-supremum.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above, and let $c\in\mathbb{R}$. Then
\[
  \sup(A+c)=\sup A+c,
  \qquad
  A+c=\{a+c:a\in A\}.
\]
thm:translation-invariance-supremum.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above, and let $c\in\mathbb{R}$. Then
\[
  \sup(A+c)=\sup A+c,
  \qquad
  A+c=\{a+c:a\in A\}.
\]

thm:scalar-mult-positive.title: Scalar Multiplication by a Positive Scalar
thm:scalar-mult-positive.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above, and let $k>0$. Then \[ \sup(kA)=k\sup A, \qquad kA=\{ka:a\in A\}. \]
thm:scalar-mult-positive.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above, and let $k>0$. Then
\[
  \sup(kA)=k\sup A,
  \qquad
  kA=\{ka:a\in A\}.
\]
thm:scalar-mult-positive.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above, and let $k>0$. Then
\[
  \sup(kA)=k\sup A,
  \qquad
  kA=\{ka:a\in A\}.
\]

thm:scalar-mult-negative.title: Scalar Multiplication by a Negative Scalar
thm:scalar-mult-negative.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded below, and let $k<0$. Then \[ \sup(kA)=k\inf A, \qquad kA=\{ka:a\in A\}. \]
thm:scalar-mult-negative.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded below, and let $k<0$. Then
\[
  \sup(kA)=k\inf A,
  \qquad
  kA=\{ka:a\in A\}.
\]
thm:scalar-mult-negative.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded below, and let $k<0$. Then
\[
  \sup(kA)=k\inf A,
  \qquad
  kA=\{ka:a\in A\}.
\]

thm:negation-exchange-sup-inf.title: Negation Exchanges Supremum and Infimum
thm:negation-exchange-sup-inf.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded below. Then $-A=\{-a:a\in A\}$ is bounded above and \[ \sup(-A)=-\inf A. \]
thm:negation-exchange-sup-inf.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded below. Then $-A=\{-a:a\in A\}$ is bounded above and
\[
  \sup(-A)=-\inf A.
\]
thm:negation-exchange-sup-inf.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded below. Then $-A=\{-a:a\in A\}$ is bounded above and
\[
  \sup(-A)=-\inf A.
\]

thm:supremum-sum-set.title: Supremum of a Sum Set
thm:supremum-sum-set.statement_preview: Let $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above. Then \[ \sup(A+B)=\sup A+\sup B, \qquad A+B=\{a+b:a\in A,\ b\in B\}. \]
thm:supremum-sum-set.statement_display:
Let $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above. Then
\[
  \sup(A+B)=\sup A+\sup B,
  \qquad
  A+B=\{a+b:a\in A,\ b\in B\}.
\]
thm:supremum-sum-set.statement_tex:
Let $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded above. Then
\[
  \sup(A+B)=\sup A+\sup B,
  \qquad
  A+B=\{a+b:a\in A,\ b\in B\}.
\]

thm:supremum-difference-set.title: Supremum of a Difference Set
thm:supremum-difference-set.statement_preview: Let $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty, with $A$ bounded above and $B$ bounded below. Then \[ \sup(A-B)=\sup A-\inf B, \qquad A-B=\{a-b:a\in A,\ b\in B\}. \]
thm:supremum-difference-set.statement_display:
Let $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty, with $A$ bounded above and $B$ bounded below. Then
\[
  \sup(A-B)=\sup A-\inf B,
  \qquad
  A-B=\{a-b:a\in A,\ b\in B\}.
\]
thm:supremum-difference-set.statement_tex:
Let $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty, with $A$ bounded above and $B$ bounded below. Then
\[
  \sup(A-B)=\sup A-\inf B,
  \qquad
  A-B=\{a-b:a\in A,\ b\in B\}.
\]

thm:supremum-dilation.title: Supremum of a Dilation
thm:supremum-dilation.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded, and let $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$. Then \[ \sup(\lambda A)=\max\{\lambda\sup A,\lambda\inf A\}, \qquad \lambda A=\{\lambda a:a\in A\}. \]
thm:supremum-dilation.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded, and let $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$. Then
\[
  \sup(\lambda A)=\max\{\lambda\sup A,\lambda\inf A\},
  \qquad
  \lambda A=\{\lambda a:a\in A\}.
\]
thm:supremum-dilation.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded, and let $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$. Then
\[
  \sup(\lambda A)=\max\{\lambda\sup A,\lambda\inf A\},
  \qquad
  \lambda A=\{\lambda a:a\in A\}.
\]

thm:supremum-absolute-value-image.title: Supremum of the Absolute-Value Image
thm:supremum-absolute-value-image.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded. Then \[ \sup |A|=\max\{|\sup A|,|\inf A|\}, \qquad |A|=\{|a|:a\in A\}. \]
thm:supremum-absolute-value-image.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded. Then
\[
  \sup |A|=\max\{|\sup A|,|\inf A|\},
  \qquad
  |A|=\{|a|:a\in A\}.
\]
thm:supremum-absolute-value-image.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded. Then
\[
  \sup |A|=\max\{|\sup A|,|\inf A|\},
  \qquad
  |A|=\{|a|:a\in A\}.
\]

prop:bounds-sum-set.title: Bounds for Suprema and Infima Under Set Addition
prop:bounds-sum-set.statement_preview: Let $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded. Then \[ \inf A+\inf B\le \inf(A+B) \qquad\text{and}\qquad \sup(A+B)\le \sup A+\sup B. \]
prop:bounds-sum-set.statement_display:
Let $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded. Then
\[
  \inf A+\inf B\le \inf(A+B)
  \qquad\text{and}\qquad
  \sup(A+B)\le \sup A+\sup B.
\]
prop:bounds-sum-set.statement_tex:
Let $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be nonempty and bounded. Then
\[
  \inf A+\inf B\le \inf(A+B)
  \qquad\text{and}\qquad
  \sup(A+B)\le \sup A+\sup B.
\]

prop:upper-bounds-ambient-order.title: Upper Bounds Depend on the Ambient Order
prop:upper-bounds-ambient-order.statement_preview: Let $(S,\le_S)$ and $(T,\le_T)$ be ordered sets, and let $A\subseteq S\cap T$. Whether $u$ is an upper bound of $A$ must be interpreted relative to the chosen ambient order.
prop:upper-bounds-ambient-order.statement_display: Let $(S,\le_S)$ and $(T,\le_T)$ be ordered sets, and let $A\subseteq S\cap T$. Whether $u$ is an upper bound of $A$ must be interpreted relative to the chosen ambient order.
prop:upper-bounds-ambient-order.statement_tex: Let $(S,\le_S)$ and $(T,\le_T)$ be ordered sets, and let $A\subseteq S\cap T$. Whether $u$ is an upper bound of $A$ must be interpreted relative to the chosen ambient order.

prop:suprema-ambient-set.title: Suprema Depend on the Ambient Set
prop:suprema-ambient-set.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq S\subseteq S'$ be subsets of an ordered set. The supremum of $A$ relative to $S$, if it exists, need not agree with the supremum of $A$ relative to $S'$.
prop:suprema-ambient-set.statement_display: Let $A\subseteq S\subseteq S'$ be subsets of an ordered set. The supremum of $A$ relative to $S$, if it exists, need not agree with the supremum of $A$ relative to $S'$.
prop:suprema-ambient-set.statement_tex: Let $A\subseteq S\subseteq S'$ be subsets of an ordered set. The supremum of $A$ relative to $S$, if it exists, need not agree with the supremum of $A$ relative to $S'$.

thm:ambient-existence-supremum.title: Ambient Existence of Supremum
thm:ambient-existence-supremum.statement_preview: There are ordered sets $S\subseteq S'$ and a subset $A\subseteq S$ such that $A$ has a supremum relative to $S'$ but has no supremum relative to $S$.
thm:ambient-existence-supremum.statement_display: There are ordered sets $S\subseteq S'$ and a subset $A\subseteq S$ such that $A$ has a supremum relative to $S'$ but has no supremum relative to $S$.
thm:ambient-existence-supremum.statement_tex: There are ordered sets $S\subseteq S'$ and a subset $A\subseteq S$ such that $A$ has a supremum relative to $S'$ but has no supremum relative to $S$.

lem:rational-gap-suprema.title: Rational Gap Example for Suprema
lem:rational-gap-suprema.statement_preview: Let \[ A=\{q\in\mathbb{Q}:q^2<2\}. \] Then $A$ is nonempty and bounded above in $\mathbb{Q}$, but $A$ has no supremum relative to $\mathbb{Q}$.
lem:rational-gap-suprema.statement_display:
Let
\[
  A=\{q\in\mathbb{Q}:q^2<2\}.
\]
Then $A$ is nonempty and bounded above in $\mathbb{Q}$, but $A$ has no supremum relative to $\mathbb{Q}$.
lem:rational-gap-suprema.statement_tex:
Let
\[
  A=\{q\in\mathbb{Q}:q^2<2\}.
\]
Then $A$ is nonempty and bounded above in $\mathbb{Q}$, but $A$ has no supremum relative to $\mathbb{Q}$.

def:epsilon-characterization-of-supremum.title: Epsilon Characterization of Supremum
def:epsilon-characterization-of-supremum.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $s\in\mathbb{R}$. The element $s$ satisfies the \emph{epsilon characterization of the supremum} of $A$ if and only if $s$ is an upper bound of $A$ and for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $a\in A$ such that $s-\varepsilon<a$.
def:epsilon-characterization-of-supremum.statement_display: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $s\in\mathbb{R}$. The element $s$ satisfies the \emph{epsilon characterization of the supremum} of $A$ if and only if $s$ is an upper bound of $A$ and for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $a\in A$ such that $s-\varepsilon<a$.
def:epsilon-characterization-of-supremum.statement_tex: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $s\in\mathbb{R}$. The element $s$ satisfies the \emph{epsilon characterization of the supremum} of $A$ if and only if $s$ is an upper bound of $A$ and for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $a\in A$ such that $s-\varepsilon<a$.

def:epsilon-characterization-of-infimum.title: Epsilon Characterization of Infimum
def:epsilon-characterization-of-infimum.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $t\in\mathbb{R}$. The element $t$ satisfies the \emph{epsilon characterization of the infimum} of $A$ if and only if $t$ is a lower bound of $A$ and for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $a\in A$ such that $a<t+\varepsilon$.
def:epsilon-characterization-of-infimum.statement_display: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $t\in\mathbb{R}$. The element $t$ satisfies the \emph{epsilon characterization of the infimum} of $A$ if and only if $t$ is a lower bound of $A$ and for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $a\in A$ such that $a<t+\varepsilon$.
def:epsilon-characterization-of-infimum.statement_tex: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $t\in\mathbb{R}$. The element $t$ satisfies the \emph{epsilon characterization of the infimum} of $A$ if and only if $t$ is a lower bound of $A$ and for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $a\in A$ such that $a<t+\varepsilon$.

def:bound.title: Bound
def:bound.statement_preview: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, let $A\subseteq S$, and let $b\in S$. The element $b$ is a \emph{bound} for $A$ if and only if $b$ is a uniform one-sided comparison point for all elements of $A$: \[ \operatorname{Bound}(b,A) \Longleftrightarrow \bigl[\forall x\in A\;(x\le b)\bigr] \lor \bigl[\forall x\in A\;(b\le x)\bigr]. \]
def:bound.statement_display:
Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, let $A\subseteq S$, and let $b\in S$. The element $b$ is a \emph{bound} for $A$ if and only if $b$ is a uniform one-sided comparison point for all elements of $A$:
\[
\operatorname{Bound}(b,A)
\Longleftrightarrow
\bigl[\forall x\in A\;(x\le b)\bigr]
\lor
\bigl[\forall x\in A\;(b\le x)\bigr].
\]
def:bound.statement_tex:
Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, let $A\subseteq S$, and let $b\in S$. The element $b$ is a \emph{bound} for $A$ if and only if $b$ is a uniform one-sided comparison point for all elements of $A$:
\[
\operatorname{Bound}(b,A)
\Longleftrightarrow
\bigl[\forall x\in A\;(x\le b)\bigr]
\lor
\bigl[\forall x\in A\;(b\le x)\bigr].
\]

def:upper-bound.title: Upper Bound
def:upper-bound.statement_preview: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, let $A\subseteq S$, and let $u\in S$. The element $u$ is an \emph{upper bound} of $A$ if and only if $u$ is a bound for $A$ from above, meaning $x\le u$ for every $x\in A$.
def:upper-bound.statement_display: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, let $A\subseteq S$, and let $u\in S$. The element $u$ is an \emph{upper bound} of $A$ if and only if $u$ is a bound for $A$ from above, meaning $x\le u$ for every $x\in A$.
def:upper-bound.statement_tex: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, let $A\subseteq S$, and let $u\in S$. The element $u$ is an \emph{upper bound} of $A$ if and only if $u$ is a bound for $A$ from above, meaning $x\le u$ for every $x\in A$.

def:bounded-above.title: Bounded Above
def:bounded-above.statement_preview: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set and let $A\subseteq S$. The set $A$ is \emph{bounded above} in $S$ if and only if there exists $u\in S$ such that $u$ is an upper bound of $A$.
def:bounded-above.statement_display: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set and let $A\subseteq S$. The set $A$ is \emph{bounded above} in $S$ if and only if there exists $u\in S$ such that $u$ is an upper bound of $A$.
def:bounded-above.statement_tex: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set and let $A\subseteq S$. The set $A$ is \emph{bounded above} in $S$ if and only if there exists $u\in S$ such that $u$ is an upper bound of $A$.

def:lower-bound.title: Lower Bound
def:lower-bound.statement_preview: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, $A\subseteq S$, and $l\in S$. The element $l$ is a \emph{lower bound} of $A$ if and only if $l$ is a bound for $A$ from below, meaning every $x\in A$ satisfies $l\le x$.
def:lower-bound.statement_display: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, $A\subseteq S$, and $l\in S$. The element $l$ is a \emph{lower bound} of $A$ if and only if $l$ is a bound for $A$ from below, meaning every $x\in A$ satisfies $l\le x$.
def:lower-bound.statement_tex: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, $A\subseteq S$, and $l\in S$. The element $l$ is a \emph{lower bound} of $A$ if and only if $l$ is a bound for $A$ from below, meaning every $x\in A$ satisfies $l\le x$.

def:bounded-below.title: Bounded Below
def:bounded-below.statement_preview: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set and let $A\subseteq S$. The set $A$ is \emph{bounded below} in $S$ if and only if there exists $l\in S$ such that $l$ is a lower bound of $A$.
def:bounded-below.statement_display: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set and let $A\subseteq S$. The set $A$ is \emph{bounded below} in $S$ if and only if there exists $l\in S$ such that $l$ is a lower bound of $A$.
def:bounded-below.statement_tex: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set and let $A\subseteq S$. The set $A$ is \emph{bounded below} in $S$ if and only if there exists $l\in S$ such that $l$ is a lower bound of $A$.

def:bounded.title: Bounded
def:bounded.statement_preview: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set and let $A\subseteq S$. The set $A$ is \emph{bounded} in $S$ if and only if $A$ is bounded above in $S$ and bounded below in $S$.
def:bounded.statement_display: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set and let $A\subseteq S$. The set $A$ is \emph{bounded} in $S$ if and only if $A$ is bounded above in $S$ and bounded below in $S$.
def:bounded.statement_tex: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set and let $A\subseteq S$. The set $A$ is \emph{bounded} in $S$ if and only if $A$ is bounded above in $S$ and bounded below in $S$.

def:minimum.title: Minimum
def:minimum.statement_preview: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, $A\subseteq S$, and $m\in S$. The element $m$ is the \emph{minimum} of $A$ if and only if $m\in A$ and $m$ is a lower bound of $A$.
def:minimum.statement_display: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, $A\subseteq S$, and $m\in S$. The element $m$ is the \emph{minimum} of $A$ if and only if $m\in A$ and $m$ is a lower bound of $A$.
def:minimum.statement_tex: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, $A\subseteq S$, and $m\in S$. The element $m$ is the \emph{minimum} of $A$ if and only if $m\in A$ and $m$ is a lower bound of $A$.

def:maximum.title: Maximum
def:maximum.statement_preview: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set and let $A \subseteq S$. An element $M \in S$ is the maximum of $A$ if and only if $M \in A$ and $M$ is an upper bound of $A$.
def:maximum.statement_display: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set and let $A \subseteq S$. An element $M \in S$ is the maximum of $A$ if and only if $M \in A$ and $M$ is an upper bound of $A$.
def:maximum.statement_tex: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set and let $A \subseteq S$. An element $M \in S$ is the maximum of $A$ if and only if $M \in A$ and $M$ is an upper bound of $A$.

def:supremum.title: Supremum
def:supremum.statement_preview: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, let $A \subseteq S$, and let $s \in S$. The element $s$ is the supremum of $A$ if and only if $s$ is an upper bound of $A$ and every upper bound $u$ of $A$ satisfies $s \le u$.
def:supremum.statement_display: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, let $A \subseteq S$, and let $s \in S$. The element $s$ is the supremum of $A$ if and only if $s$ is an upper bound of $A$ and every upper bound $u$ of $A$ satisfies $s \le u$.
def:supremum.statement_tex: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, let $A \subseteq S$, and let $s \in S$. The element $s$ is the supremum of $A$ if and only if $s$ is an upper bound of $A$ and every upper bound $u$ of $A$ satisfies $s \le u$.

def:infimum.title: Infimum
def:infimum.statement_preview: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, let $A\subseteq S$, and let $i\in S$. The element $i$ is the infimum of $A$ if and only if $i$ is a lower bound of $A$ and every lower bound $l$ of $A$ satisfies $l\le i$.
def:infimum.statement_display: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, let $A\subseteq S$, and let $i\in S$. The element $i$ is the infimum of $A$ if and only if $i$ is a lower bound of $A$ and every lower bound $l$ of $A$ satisfies $l\le i$.
def:infimum.statement_tex: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set, let $A\subseteq S$, and let $i\in S$. The element $i$ is the infimum of $A$ if and only if $i$ is a lower bound of $A$ and every lower bound $l$ of $A$ satisfies $l\le i$.

thm:lub-property-implies-existence-of-su.title: Least Upper Bound Property Implies Existence of Suprema
thm:lub-property-implies-existence-of-su.statement_preview: Let $S$ be an ordered set with the least upper bound property. If $A\subseteq S$ is nonempty and bounded above in $S$, then $A$ has a supremum in $S$.
thm:lub-property-implies-existence-of-su.statement_display: Let $S$ be an ordered set with the least upper bound property. If $A\subseteq S$ is nonempty and bounded above in $S$, then $A$ has a supremum in $S$.
thm:lub-property-implies-existence-of-su.statement_tex: Let $S$ be an ordered set with the least upper bound property. If $A\subseteq S$ is nonempty and bounded above in $S$, then $A$ has a supremum in $S$.

prop:upper-bound-property-of-supremum.title: Upper Bound Property of Supremum
prop:upper-bound-property-of-supremum.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $s=\sup A$. Then $x\le s$ for every $x\in A$.
prop:upper-bound-property-of-supremum.statement_display: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $s=\sup A$. Then $x\le s$ for every $x\in A$.
prop:upper-bound-property-of-supremum.statement_tex: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $s=\sup A$. Then $x\le s$ for every $x\in A$.

thm:archimedean-property.title: Archimedean Property
thm:archimedean-property.statement_preview: For every real number $x$, there exists a natural number $n$ such that $n>x$.
thm:archimedean-property.statement_display: For every real number $x$, there exists a natural number $n$ such that $n>x$.
thm:archimedean-property.statement_tex: For every real number $x$, there exists a natural number $n$ such that $n>x$.

cor:archimedean-reciprocal.title: Archimedean Reciprocal Corollary
cor:archimedean-reciprocal.statement_preview: For every $x>0$ and every $y\in\mathbb{R}$, there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $nx>y$.
cor:archimedean-reciprocal.statement_display: For every $x>0$ and every $y\in\mathbb{R}$, there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $nx>y$.
cor:archimedean-reciprocal.statement_tex: For every $x>0$ and every $y\in\mathbb{R}$, there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $nx>y$.

def:least-upper-bound-property.title: Least Upper Bound Property
def:least-upper-bound-property.statement_preview: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set. The set $S$ has the \emph{Least Upper Bound Property} if and only if every nonempty subset of $S$ that is bounded above in $S$ has a supremum in $S$.
def:least-upper-bound-property.statement_display: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set. The set $S$ has the \emph{Least Upper Bound Property} if and only if every nonempty subset of $S$ that is bounded above in $S$ has a supremum in $S$.
def:least-upper-bound-property.statement_tex: Let $(S,\le)$ be an ordered set. The set $S$ has the \emph{Least Upper Bound Property} if and only if every nonempty subset of $S$ that is bounded above in $S$ has a supremum in $S$.

ax:completeness-of-reals.title: Completeness of $\mathbb{R}$
ax:completeness-of-reals.statement_preview: The ordered field $\mathbb{R}$ has the Least Upper Bound Property.
ax:completeness-of-reals.statement_display: The ordered field $\mathbb{R}$ has the Least Upper Bound Property.
ax:completeness-of-reals.statement_tex: The ordered field $\mathbb{R}$ has the Least Upper Bound Property.

def:dense-subset.title: Dense Subset
def:dense-subset.statement_preview: Let $D\subseteq S\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. The set $D$ is \emph{dense in} $S$ if and only if for every $x\in S$ and every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $d\in D$ such that $|x-d|<\varepsilon$.
def:dense-subset.statement_display: Let $D\subseteq S\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. The set $D$ is \emph{dense in} $S$ if and only if for every $x\in S$ and every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $d\in D$ such that $|x-d|<\varepsilon$.
def:dense-subset.statement_tex: Let $D\subseteq S\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. The set $D$ is \emph{dense in} $S$ if and only if for every $x\in S$ and every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $d\in D$ such that $|x-d|<\varepsilon$.

def:order-dense-subset.title: Order Dense Subset
def:order-dense-subset.statement_preview: Let $X$ be a linearly ordered set and let $A\subseteq X$. The subset $A$ is \emph{order dense in} $X$ if and only if for every $a,b\in X$ with $a<b$ there exists $c\in A$ such that $a<c<b$.
def:order-dense-subset.statement_display: Let $X$ be a linearly ordered set and let $A\subseteq X$. The subset $A$ is \emph{order dense in} $X$ if and only if for every $a,b\in X$ with $a<b$ there exists $c\in A$ such that $a<c<b$.
def:order-dense-subset.statement_tex: Let $X$ be a linearly ordered set and let $A\subseteq X$. The subset $A$ is \emph{order dense in} $X$ if and only if for every $a,b\in X$ with $a<b$ there exists $c\in A$ such that $a<c<b$.

thm:density-of-rationals-in-reals.title: Density of the Rational Numbers in $\mathbb{R}$
thm:density-of-rationals-in-reals.statement_preview: For every $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, there exists $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that $a<q<b$.
thm:density-of-rationals-in-reals.statement_display: For every $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, there exists $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that $a<q<b$.
thm:density-of-rationals-in-reals.statement_tex: For every $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, there exists $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that $a<q<b$.

thm:density-of-irrationals-in-reals.title: Density of the Irrational Numbers in $\mathbb{R}$
thm:density-of-irrationals-in-reals.statement_preview: For every $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, there exists $s\in\mathbb{R}\setminus\mathbb{Q}$ such that $a<s<b$.
thm:density-of-irrationals-in-reals.statement_display: For every $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, there exists $s\in\mathbb{R}\setminus\mathbb{Q}$ such that $a<s<b$.
thm:density-of-irrationals-in-reals.statement_tex: For every $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, there exists $s\in\mathbb{R}\setminus\mathbb{Q}$ such that $a<s<b$.

cor:every-open-interval-contains-rationa.title: Rational and Irrational Points in Every Open Interval
cor:every-open-interval-contains-rationa.statement_preview: Every open interval in $\mathbb{R}$ contains both a rational number and an irrational number.
cor:every-open-interval-contains-rationa.statement_display: Every open interval in $\mathbb{R}$ contains both a rational number and an irrational number.
cor:every-open-interval-contains-rationa.statement_tex: Every open interval in $\mathbb{R}$ contains both a rational number and an irrational number.

thm:nested-interval-theorem.title: Nested Interval Property
thm:nested-interval-theorem.statement_preview: Let $(I_n)$ be a sequence of nonempty closed intervals in $\mathbb{R}$. If $I_{n+1}\subseteq I_n$ for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$, then \[ \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} I_n\neq\varnothing. \]
thm:nested-interval-theorem.statement_display:
Let $(I_n)$ be a sequence of nonempty closed intervals in $\mathbb{R}$. If $I_{n+1}\subseteq I_n$ for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$, then
\[
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} I_n\neq\varnothing.
\]
thm:nested-interval-theorem.statement_tex:
Let $(I_n)$ be a sequence of nonempty closed intervals in $\mathbb{R}$. If $I_{n+1}\subseteq I_n$ for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$, then
\[
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} I_n\neq\varnothing.
\]

thm:step-function-approximation.title: Step Function Approximation
thm:step-function-approximation.statement_preview: Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous and let $\varepsilon>0$. Then there exists a step function $s_\varepsilon:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ such that \[ \sup_{x\in[a,b]} |f(x)-s_\varepsilon(x)|<\varepsilon. \]
thm:step-function-approximation.statement_display:
Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous and let $\varepsilon>0$.
Then there exists a step function $s_\varepsilon:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ such that
\[
\sup_{x\in[a,b]} |f(x)-s_\varepsilon(x)|<\varepsilon.
\]
thm:step-function-approximation.statement_tex:
Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous and let $\varepsilon>0$.
Then there exists a step function $s_\varepsilon:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ such that
\[
\sup_{x\in[a,b]} |f(x)-s_\varepsilon(x)|<\varepsilon.
\]

def:piecewise-linear-function.title: Piecewise Linear Function
def:piecewise-linear-function.statement_preview: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$ and let $g:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$. The function $g$ is piecewise linear if and only if there exist $m \in \mathbb{N}$ with $m \ge 1$ and points $a=x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_m=b$ such that $g|_{[x_{k-1},x_k]}$ is affine for every $k\in\{1,\ldots,m\}$.
def:piecewise-linear-function.statement_display:
Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$ and let $g:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$.
The function $g$ is piecewise linear if and only if there exist
$m \in \mathbb{N}$ with $m \ge 1$ and points
$a=x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_m=b$ such that $g|_{[x_{k-1},x_k]}$ is affine
for every $k\in\{1,\ldots,m\}$.
def:piecewise-linear-function.statement_tex:
Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$ and let $g:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$.
The function $g$ is piecewise linear if and only if there exist
$m \in \mathbb{N}$ with $m \ge 1$ and points
$a=x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_m=b$ such that $g|_{[x_{k-1},x_k]}$ is affine
for every $k\in\{1,\ldots,m\}$.

thm:piecewise-linear-approximation.title: Piecewise Linear Approximation
thm:piecewise-linear-approximation.statement_preview: Let $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous and let $\varepsilon>0$. Then there exists a continuous piecewise linear function $\ell_\varepsilon\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ such that $\sup_{x\in[a,b]}|f(x)-\ell_\varepsilon(x)|<\varepsilon$.
thm:piecewise-linear-approximation.statement_display: Let $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous and let $\varepsilon>0$. Then there exists a continuous piecewise linear function $\ell_\varepsilon\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ such that $\sup_{x\in[a,b]}|f(x)-\ell_\varepsilon(x)|<\varepsilon$.
thm:piecewise-linear-approximation.statement_tex: Let $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous and let $\varepsilon>0$. Then there exists a continuous piecewise linear function $\ell_\varepsilon\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ such that $\sup_{x\in[a,b]}|f(x)-\ell_\varepsilon(x)|<\varepsilon$.

thm:weierstrass-approximation.title: Weierstrass Approximation Theorem
thm:weierstrass-approximation.statement_preview: Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous. For every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists a polynomial $p_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{R}[x]$ such that $\|f-p_\varepsilon\|_{\infty,[a,b]}<\varepsilon$.
thm:weierstrass-approximation.statement_display: Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous. For every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists a polynomial $p_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{R}[x]$ such that $\|f-p_\varepsilon\|_{\infty,[a,b]}<\varepsilon$.
thm:weierstrass-approximation.statement_tex: Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous. For every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists a polynomial $p_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{R}[x]$ such that $\|f-p_\varepsilon\|_{\infty,[a,b]}<\varepsilon$.

def:bernstein-polynomial.title: Bernstein Polynomial
def:bernstein-polynomial.statement_preview: Let $n \in \mathbb{N}$ and let $f:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$. A function $B_n:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ is called the $n$th Bernstein polynomial of $f$ if and only if, for every $x \in [0,1]$, \[ B_n(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{n} f\!\left(\frac{k}{n}\right)\binom{n}{k}x^k(1-x)^{n-k}. \]
def:bernstein-polynomial.statement_display:
Let $n \in \mathbb{N}$ and let $f:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$.
A function $B_n:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ is called the $n$th Bernstein polynomial of $f$
if and only if, for every $x \in [0,1]$,
\[
B_n(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{n} f\!\left(\frac{k}{n}\right)\binom{n}{k}x^k(1-x)^{n-k}.
\]
def:bernstein-polynomial.statement_tex:
Let $n \in \mathbb{N}$ and let $f:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$.
A function $B_n:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ is called the $n$th Bernstein polynomial of $f$
if and only if, for every $x \in [0,1]$,
\[
B_n(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{n} f\!\left(\frac{k}{n}\right)\binom{n}{k}x^k(1-x)^{n-k}.
\]

thm:bernstein-approximation.title: Bernstein Approximation Theorem
thm:bernstein-approximation.statement_preview: Let $f:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous, and for each $n\in\mathbb{N}$ let $B_n$ denote the $n$th Bernstein polynomial of $f$. Then for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $n_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $\|f-B_n\|_\infty<\varepsilon$ for all $n\ge n_\varepsilon$.
thm:bernstein-approximation.statement_display: Let $f:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous, and for each $n\in\mathbb{N}$ let $B_n$ denote the $n$th Bernstein polynomial of $f$. Then for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $n_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $\|f-B_n\|_\infty<\varepsilon$ for all $n\ge n_\varepsilon$.
thm:bernstein-approximation.statement_tex: Let $f:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous, and for each $n\in\mathbb{N}$ let $B_n$ denote the $n$th Bernstein polynomial of $f$. Then for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $n_\varepsilon\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $\|f-B_n\|_\infty<\varepsilon$ for all $n\ge n_\varepsilon$.

def:interval-partition.title: Partition
def:interval-partition.statement_preview: Let $a<b$ and set $I=[a,b]$. A finite ordered set $P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\}$ with $n\in\mathbb{N}$ and $n\ge 1$ is a partition of $I$ if and only if $x_0=a$, $x_n=b$, and $x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_n$. In this case the closed subintervals $[x_{i-1},x_i]$ for $i=1,\ldots,n$ subdivide $I$.
def:interval-partition.statement_display: Let $a<b$ and set $I=[a,b]$. A finite ordered set $P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\}$ with $n\in\mathbb{N}$ and $n\ge 1$ is a partition of $I$ if and only if $x_0=a$, $x_n=b$, and $x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_n$. In this case the closed subintervals $[x_{i-1},x_i]$ for $i=1,\ldots,n$ subdivide $I$.
def:interval-partition.statement_tex: Let $a<b$ and set $I=[a,b]$. A finite ordered set $P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\}$ with $n\in\mathbb{N}$ and $n\ge 1$ is a partition of $I$ if and only if $x_0=a$, $x_n=b$, and $x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_n$. In this case the closed subintervals $[x_{i-1},x_i]$ for $i=1,\ldots,n$ subdivide $I$.

def:tagged-partition.title: Tagged Partition
def:tagged-partition.statement_preview: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a < b$. A list $\dot{\mathcal{P}}=\{([x_{i-1},x_i],t_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ is a tagged partition of $[a,b]$ if and only if the subintervals determine a partition $P=\{[x_{i-1},x_i]\}_{i=1}^n$ of $[a,b]$ with $a=x_0 < x_1 < \cdots < x_n=b$, and each tag satisfies $t_i \in [x_{i-1},x_i]$ for $i=1,\ldots,n$. Equivalently, a tagged partit...
def:tagged-partition.statement_display: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a < b$. A list $\dot{\mathcal{P}}=\{([x_{i-1},x_i],t_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ is a tagged partition of $[a,b]$ if and only if the subintervals determine a partition $P=\{[x_{i-1},x_i]\}_{i=1}^n$ of $[a,b]$ with $a=x_0 < x_1 < \cdots < x_n=b$, and each tag satisfies $t_i \in [x_{i-1},x_i]$ for $i=1,\ldots,n$. Equivalently, a tagged partition is exactly a partition $P$ of $[a,b]$ together with one tag chosen inside every subinterval of $P$.
def:tagged-partition.statement_tex: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a < b$. A list $\dot{\mathcal{P}}=\{([x_{i-1},x_i],t_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ is a tagged partition of $[a,b]$ if and only if the subintervals determine a partition $P=\{[x_{i-1},x_i]\}_{i=1}^n$ of $[a,b]$ with $a=x_0 < x_1 < \cdots < x_n=b$, and each tag satisfies $t_i \in [x_{i-1},x_i]$ for $i=1,\ldots,n$. Equivalently, a tagged partition is exactly a partition $P$ of $[a,b]$ together with one tag chosen inside every subinterval of $P$.

def:gauge.title: Gauge
def:gauge.statement_preview: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$ and set $I=[a,b]$. A gauge on $I$ is a function $\delta:I\to(0,\infty)$.
def:gauge.statement_display: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$ and set $I=[a,b]$. A gauge on $I$ is a function $\delta:I\to(0,\infty)$.
def:gauge.statement_tex: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$ and set $I=[a,b]$. A gauge on $I$ is a function $\delta:I\to(0,\infty)$.

def:delta-fine-partition.title: $\delta$-Fine Partition
def:delta-fine-partition.statement_preview: Let $[a,b]$ be a closed interval, let $\delta:[a,b]\to(0,\infty)$ be a gauge, and let $\dot{\mathcal{P}}=\{([x_{i-1},x_i],t_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ be a tagged partition of $[a,b]$. The tagged partition $\dot{\mathcal{P}}$ is $\delta$-fine if and only if for every $i \in \{1,\dots,n\}$ and every $x \in [x_{i-1},x_i]$ the inequality $|x-t_i|<\delta(t_i)$ holds.
def:delta-fine-partition.statement_display: Let $[a,b]$ be a closed interval, let $\delta:[a,b]\to(0,\infty)$ be a gauge, and let $\dot{\mathcal{P}}=\{([x_{i-1},x_i],t_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ be a tagged partition of $[a,b]$. The tagged partition $\dot{\mathcal{P}}$ is $\delta$-fine if and only if for every $i \in \{1,\dots,n\}$ and every $x \in [x_{i-1},x_i]$ the inequality $|x-t_i|<\delta(t_i)$ holds.
def:delta-fine-partition.statement_tex: Let $[a,b]$ be a closed interval, let $\delta:[a,b]\to(0,\infty)$ be a gauge, and let $\dot{\mathcal{P}}=\{([x_{i-1},x_i],t_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ be a tagged partition of $[a,b]$. The tagged partition $\dot{\mathcal{P}}$ is $\delta$-fine if and only if for every $i \in \{1,\dots,n\}$ and every $x \in [x_{i-1},x_i]$ the inequality $|x-t_i|<\delta(t_i)$ holds.

lem:every-point-covered-by-tag.title: Every Point Is Covered by a Tag
lem:every-point-covered-by-tag.statement_preview: Let $\dot{\mathcal{P}}=\{([x_{i-1},x_i],t_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ be a tagged partition of $[a,b]$ that is $\delta$-fine on $[a,b]$, and let $x\in[a,b]$. Then there exists $i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}$ such that $|x-t_i|<\delta(t_i)$.
lem:every-point-covered-by-tag.statement_display: Let $\dot{\mathcal{P}}=\{([x_{i-1},x_i],t_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ be a tagged partition of $[a,b]$ that is $\delta$-fine on $[a,b]$, and let $x\in[a,b]$. Then there exists $i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}$ such that $|x-t_i|<\delta(t_i)$.
lem:every-point-covered-by-tag.statement_tex: Let $\dot{\mathcal{P}}=\{([x_{i-1},x_i],t_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ be a tagged partition of $[a,b]$ that is $\delta$-fine on $[a,b]$, and let $x\in[a,b]$. Then there exists $i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}$ such that $|x-t_i|<\delta(t_i)$.

thm:cousins-theorem.title: Cousin's Theorem
thm:cousins-theorem.statement_preview: Every gauge $\delta:[a,b]\to(0,\infty)$ on a closed interval $[a,b]$ admits a $\delta$-fine tagged partition of $[a,b]$.
thm:cousins-theorem.statement_display:
Every gauge $\delta:[a,b]\to(0,\infty)$ on a closed interval $[a,b]$
admits a $\delta$-fine tagged partition of $[a,b]$.
thm:cousins-theorem.statement_tex:
Every gauge $\delta:[a,b]\to(0,\infty)$ on a closed interval $[a,b]$
admits a $\delta$-fine tagged partition of $[a,b]$.

def:mesh-of-partition.title: Mesh of a Partition
def:mesh-of-partition.statement_preview: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, and let $P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\}$ be a partition of $[a,b]$. A real number $m$ is the \emph{mesh} (or \emph{norm}) of $P$ if and only if \[ m=\max_{1\le i\le n}(x_i - x_{i-1}). \] The mesh of $P$ is denoted by $\|P\|$ and is the unique $m$ satisfying this equivalence.
def:mesh-of-partition.statement_display:
Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, and let $P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\}$ be a partition of $[a,b]$. A real number $m$ is the \emph{mesh} (or \emph{norm}) of $P$ if and only if
\[
m=\max_{1\le i\le n}(x_i - x_{i-1}).
\]
The mesh of $P$ is denoted by $\|P\|$ and is the unique $m$ satisfying this equivalence.
def:mesh-of-partition.statement_tex:
Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, and let $P=\{x_0,\ldots,x_n\}$ be a partition of $[a,b]$. A real number $m$ is the \emph{mesh} (or \emph{norm}) of $P$ if and only if
\[
m=\max_{1\le i\le n}(x_i - x_{i-1}).
\]
The mesh of $P$ is denoted by $\|P\|$ and is the unique $m$ satisfying this equivalence.

def:refinement-of-partition.title: Refinement
def:refinement-of-partition.statement_preview: Let $[a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $P$ and $Q$ be partitions of $[a,b]$. Then $Q$ is a refinement of $P$ if and only if every point of $P$ is also a point of $Q$; equivalently, $P \subseteq Q$.
def:refinement-of-partition.statement_display: Let $[a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $P$ and $Q$ be partitions of $[a,b]$. Then $Q$ is a refinement of $P$ if and only if every point of $P$ is also a point of $Q$; equivalently, $P \subseteq Q$.
def:refinement-of-partition.statement_tex: Let $[a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $P$ and $Q$ be partitions of $[a,b]$. Then $Q$ is a refinement of $P$ if and only if every point of $P$ is also a point of $Q$; equivalently, $P \subseteq Q$.

def:common-refinement.title: Common Refinement
def:common-refinement.statement_preview: Let $a<b$ and let $P$ and $Q$ be partitions of $[a,b]$. Their common refinement is the partition $R$ obtained by taking the union of their node sets, so $R = P \cup Q$ as sets of points in $[a,b]$.
def:common-refinement.statement_display: Let $a<b$ and let $P$ and $Q$ be partitions of $[a,b]$. Their common refinement is the partition $R$ obtained by taking the union of their node sets, so $R = P \cup Q$ as sets of points in $[a,b]$.
def:common-refinement.statement_tex: Let $a<b$ and let $P$ and $Q$ be partitions of $[a,b]$. Their common refinement is the partition $R$ obtained by taking the union of their node sets, so $R = P \cup Q$ as sets of points in $[a,b]$.

def:bounded-on-a-set.title: Bounded on a Set
def:bounded-on-a-set.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is bounded on $A$ if and only if there exists a constant $M>0$ such that $|f(x)| \le M$ for every $x \in A$.
def:bounded-on-a-set.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is bounded on $A$ if and only if there exists a constant $M>0$ such that $|f(x)| \le M$ for every $x \in A$.
def:bounded-on-a-set.statement_tex: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is bounded on $A$ if and only if there exists a constant $M>0$ such that $|f(x)| \le M$ for every $x \in A$.

thm:boundedness-theorem.title: Boundedness Theorem
thm:boundedness-theorem.statement_preview: Let $a<b$ and let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $[a,b]$. Then there exists $M>0$ such that $|f(x)|\le M$ for every $x\in[a,b]$.
thm:boundedness-theorem.statement_display: Let $a<b$ and let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $[a,b]$. Then there exists $M>0$ such that $|f(x)|\le M$ for every $x\in[a,b]$.
thm:boundedness-theorem.statement_tex: Let $a<b$ and let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $[a,b]$. Then there exists $M>0$ such that $|f(x)|\le M$ for every $x\in[a,b]$.

def:absolute-extrema.title: Absolute Maximum and Absolute Minimum
def:absolute-extrema.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$. A point $x^{*} \in A$ is an absolute maximum of $f$ on $A$ if and only if $f(x^{*}) \ge f(x)$ for every $x \in A$. A point $x_{*} \in A$ is an absolute minimum of $f$ on $A$ if and only if $f(x_{*}) \le f(x)$ for every $x \in A$.
def:absolute-extrema.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$. A point $x^{*} \in A$ is an absolute maximum of $f$ on $A$ if and only if $f(x^{*}) \ge f(x)$ for every $x \in A$. A point $x_{*} \in A$ is an absolute minimum of $f$ on $A$ if and only if $f(x_{*}) \le f(x)$ for every $x \in A$.
def:absolute-extrema.statement_tex: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$. A point $x^{*} \in A$ is an absolute maximum of $f$ on $A$ if and only if $f(x^{*}) \ge f(x)$ for every $x \in A$. A point $x_{*} \in A$ is an absolute minimum of $f$ on $A$ if and only if $f(x_{*}) \le f(x)$ for every $x \in A$.

thm:extreme-value-theorem.title: Extreme Value Theorem
thm:extreme-value-theorem.statement_preview: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, and let $f:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous. There exist points $x_m,x_M \in [a,b]$ such that $f(x_m) \le f(x) \le f(x_M)$ for every $x \in [a,b]$. Equivalently, $f$ attains both an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on $[a,b]$.
thm:extreme-value-theorem.statement_display: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, and let $f:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous. There exist points $x_m,x_M \in [a,b]$ such that $f(x_m) \le f(x) \le f(x_M)$ for every $x \in [a,b]$. Equivalently, $f$ attains both an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on $[a,b]$.
thm:extreme-value-theorem.statement_tex: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, and let $f:[a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous. There exist points $x_m,x_M \in [a,b]$ such that $f(x_m) \le f(x) \le f(x_M)$ for every $x \in [a,b]$. Equivalently, $f$ attains both an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on $[a,b]$.

thm:location-of-roots.title: Location of Roots
thm:location-of-roots.statement_preview: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$ and let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous. If either $f(a)<0<f(b)$ or $f(a)>0>f(b)$, then there exists $c\in(a,b)$ such that $f(c)=0$.
thm:location-of-roots.statement_display: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$ and let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous. If either $f(a)<0<f(b)$ or $f(a)>0>f(b)$, then there exists $c\in(a,b)$ such that $f(c)=0$.
thm:location-of-roots.statement_tex: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$ and let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous. If either $f(a)<0<f(b)$ or $f(a)>0>f(b)$, then there exists $c\in(a,b)$ such that $f(c)=0$.

thm:bolzano-intermediate-value.title: Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem
thm:bolzano-intermediate-value.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f \colon I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $I$, and choose $a,b \in I$ with $f(a) < k < f(b)$. Then there exists $c \in I$ such that $\min\{a,b\} < c < \max\{a,b\}$ and $f(c) = k$.
thm:bolzano-intermediate-value.statement_display: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f \colon I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $I$, and choose $a,b \in I$ with $f(a) < k < f(b)$. Then there exists $c \in I$ such that $\min\{a,b\} < c < \max\{a,b\}$ and $f(c) = k$.
thm:bolzano-intermediate-value.statement_tex: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f \colon I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $I$, and choose $a,b \in I$ with $f(a) < k < f(b)$. Then there exists $c \in I$ such that $\min\{a,b\} < c < \max\{a,b\}$ and $f(c) = k$.

thm:image-of-closed-bounded-interval.title: Image of Closed Bounded Interval Is Closed Bounded Interval
thm:image-of-closed-bounded-interval.statement_preview: Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous. Then $f([a,b])=[\min_{[a,b]} f,\max_{[a,b]} f]$.
thm:image-of-closed-bounded-interval.statement_display: Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous. Then $f([a,b])=[\min_{[a,b]} f,\max_{[a,b]} f]$.
thm:image-of-closed-bounded-interval.statement_tex: Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous. Then $f([a,b])=[\min_{[a,b]} f,\max_{[a,b]} f]$.

thm:preservation-of-intervals.title: Preservation of Intervals
thm:preservation-of-intervals.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval and let $f:I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous. Then $f(I)$ is an interval.
thm:preservation-of-intervals.statement_display: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval and let $f:I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous. Then $f(I)$ is an interval.
thm:preservation-of-intervals.statement_tex: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval and let $f:I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous. Then $f(I)$ is an interval.

thm:darboux-property.title: Darboux Property
thm:darboux-property.statement_preview: Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ satisfy $a<b$, let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous, and fix $c\in\mathbb{R}$. If $f(x)\neq c$ for every $x\in[a,b]$, then either $f(x)>c$ for all $x\in[a,b]$ or $f(x)<c$ for all $x\in[a,b]$.
thm:darboux-property.statement_display: Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ satisfy $a<b$, let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous, and fix $c\in\mathbb{R}$. If $f(x)\neq c$ for every $x\in[a,b]$, then either $f(x)>c$ for all $x\in[a,b]$ or $f(x)<c$ for all $x\in[a,b]$.
thm:darboux-property.statement_tex: Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ satisfy $a<b$, let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous, and fix $c\in\mathbb{R}$. If $f(x)\neq c$ for every $x\in[a,b]$, then either $f(x)>c$ for all $x\in[a,b]$ or $f(x)<c$ for all $x\in[a,b]$.

def:infinite-adherent-points.title: Infinite Adherent Points
def:infinite-adherent-points.statement_preview: Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. The point $+\infty$ is adherent to $X$ if and only if for every $M\in\mathbb{R}$ there exists $x\in X$ such that $x>M$. The point $-\infty$ is adherent to $X$ if and only if for every $M\in\mathbb{R}$ there exists $x\in X$ such that $x<M$.
def:infinite-adherent-points.statement_display:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. The point $+\infty$ is adherent to $X$ if
and only if for every $M\in\mathbb{R}$ there exists $x\in X$ such that
$x>M$. The point $-\infty$ is adherent to $X$ if and only if for every
$M\in\mathbb{R}$ there exists $x\in X$ such that $x<M$.
def:infinite-adherent-points.statement_tex:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. The point $+\infty$ is adherent to $X$ if
and only if for every $M\in\mathbb{R}$ there exists $x\in X$ such that
$x>M$. The point $-\infty$ is adherent to $X$ if and only if for every
$M\in\mathbb{R}$ there exists $x\in X$ such that $x<M$.

def:limit-at-infinity.title: Limit at Infinity
def:limit-at-infinity.statement_preview: Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be such that for every $M \in \mathbb{R}$ there exists $x \in X$ with $x > M$, let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. The limit of $f$ at $+\infty$ equals $L$ if and only if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $M \in \mathbb{R}$ such that for every $x \in X$, $x > M$ implies $|f(x) - L| < \varepsilon$.
def:limit-at-infinity.statement_display:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be such that for every $M \in \mathbb{R}$ there exists $x \in X$ with $x > M$, let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. The limit of $f$ at $+\infty$ equals $L$ if and only if
for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $M \in \mathbb{R}$ such that for every $x \in X$, $x > M$ implies $|f(x) - L| < \varepsilon$.
def:limit-at-infinity.statement_tex:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be such that for every $M \in \mathbb{R}$ there exists $x \in X$ with $x > M$, let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. The limit of $f$ at $+\infty$ equals $L$ if and only if
for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $M \in \mathbb{R}$ such that for every $x \in X$, $x > M$ implies $|f(x) - L| < \varepsilon$.

def:limsup-liminf-function.title: Limits Superior and Inferior of a Function
def:limsup-liminf-function.statement_preview: Let $f:E\to\mathbb{R}$ and let $x_0$ be an accumulation point of $E$. For each $\delta>0$ set \[ U(\delta)=\{\,f(x): x\in E,\ 0<|x-x_0|<\delta\,\}. \] The extended real numbers $\limsup_{x\to x_0} f(x)$ and $\liminf_{x\to x_0} f(x)$ are defined by \[ \limsup_{x\to x_0} f(x)=\inf_{\delta>0}\sup U(\delta), \qquad \liminf_{x\to x_0} f(x)=\sup_{\delta>0}\inf U(...
def:limsup-liminf-function.statement_display:
Let $f:E\to\mathbb{R}$ and let $x_0$ be an accumulation point of $E$. For each $\delta>0$ set
\[
U(\delta)=\{\,f(x): x\in E,\ 0<|x-x_0|<\delta\,\}.
\]
The extended real numbers $\limsup_{x\to x_0} f(x)$ and $\liminf_{x\to x_0} f(x)$ are defined by
\[
\limsup_{x\to x_0} f(x)=\inf_{\delta>0}\sup U(\delta),
\qquad
\liminf_{x\to x_0} f(x)=\sup_{\delta>0}\inf U(\delta),
\]
where each inner supremum or infimum is taken in $\overline{\mathbb{R}}$ and the outer infimum or supremum ranges over all $\delta>0$.
def:limsup-liminf-function.statement_tex:
Let $f:E\to\mathbb{R}$ and let $x_0$ be an accumulation point of $E$. For each $\delta>0$ set
\[
U(\delta)=\{\,f(x): x\in E,\ 0<|x-x_0|<\delta\,\}.
\]
The extended real numbers $\limsup_{x\to x_0} f(x)$ and $\liminf_{x\to x_0} f(x)$ are defined by
\[
\limsup_{x\to x_0} f(x)=\inf_{\delta>0}\sup U(\delta),
\qquad
\liminf_{x\to x_0} f(x)=\sup_{\delta>0}\inf U(\delta),
\]
where each inner supremum or infimum is taken in $\overline{\mathbb{R}}$ and the outer infimum or supremum ranges over all $\delta>0$.

prop:limsup-geq-liminf-function.title: $\limsup\geq\liminf$
prop:limsup-geq-liminf-function.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be a limit point of $A$, and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. Then \[ \limsup_{x\to x_0} f(x) \ge \liminf_{x\to x_0} f(x). \]
prop:limsup-geq-liminf-function.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be a limit point of $A$, and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. Then
\[
\limsup_{x\to x_0} f(x) \ge \liminf_{x\to x_0} f(x).
\]
prop:limsup-geq-liminf-function.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be a limit point of $A$, and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. Then
\[
\limsup_{x\to x_0} f(x) \ge \liminf_{x\to x_0} f(x).
\]

prop:limsup-liminf-limit-criterion.title: Limit Exists iff $\limsup=\liminf$
prop:limsup-liminf-limit-criterion.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be a limit point of $A$ with $x_0 \in \mathbb{R}$, and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. Then $\lim_{x \to x_0} f(x)$ exists and equals $L$ if and only if $\limsup_{x \to x_0} f(x) = \liminf_{x \to x_0} f(x) = L$.
prop:limsup-liminf-limit-criterion.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be a limit point of $A$ with $x_0 \in \mathbb{R}$, and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. Then $\lim_{x \to x_0} f(x)$ exists and equals $L$ if and only if $\limsup_{x \to x_0} f(x) = \liminf_{x \to x_0} f(x) = L$.
prop:limsup-liminf-limit-criterion.statement_tex: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be a limit point of $A$ with $x_0 \in \mathbb{R}$, and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. Then $\lim_{x \to x_0} f(x)$ exists and equals $L$ if and only if $\limsup_{x \to x_0} f(x) = \liminf_{x \to x_0} f(x) = L$.

thm:monotone-one-sided-limits.title: One-Sided Limits of Monotone Functions
thm:monotone-one-sided-limits.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $c$ be interior to $I$, and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ be increasing. Define \[ L_- \coloneqq \sup\{f(x): x\in I,\; x<c\}, \qquad L_+ \coloneqq \inf\{f(x): x\in I,\; c<x\}. \] Then both one-sided limits exist and satisfy \[ \lim_{x\to c^-} f(x) = L_-, \qquad \lim_{x\to c^+} f(x) = L_+. \]
thm:monotone-one-sided-limits.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $c$ be interior to $I$, and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ be increasing. Define
\[
L_- \coloneqq \sup\{f(x): x\in I,\; x<c\},
\qquad
L_+ \coloneqq \inf\{f(x): x\in I,\; c<x\}.
\]
Then both one-sided limits exist and satisfy
\[
\lim_{x\to c^-} f(x) = L_-,
\qquad
\lim_{x\to c^+} f(x) = L_+.
\]
thm:monotone-one-sided-limits.statement_tex:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $c$ be interior to $I$, and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ be increasing. Define
\[
L_- \coloneqq \sup\{f(x): x\in I,\; x<c\},
\qquad
L_+ \coloneqq \inf\{f(x): x\in I,\; c<x\}.
\]
