Then both one-sided limits exist and satisfy
\[
\lim_{x\to c^-} f(x) = L_-,
\qquad
\lim_{x\to c^+} f(x) = L_+.
\]

cor:monotone-continuity-criterion.title: Continuity Criterion for Monotone Functions
cor:monotone-continuity-criterion.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f:I \to \mathbb{R}$ be monotone increasing, and let $c$ be an interior point of $I$. Then $f$ is continuous at $c$ if and only if its one-sided limits satisfy $f(c^-)=f(c)=f(c^+)$, where $f(c^-)=\lim_{x\to c^-} f(x)$ and $f(c^+)=\lim_{x\to c^+} f(x)$.
cor:monotone-continuity-criterion.statement_display: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f:I \to \mathbb{R}$ be monotone increasing, and let $c$ be an interior point of $I$. Then $f$ is continuous at $c$ if and only if its one-sided limits satisfy $f(c^-)=f(c)=f(c^+)$, where $f(c^-)=\lim_{x\to c^-} f(x)$ and $f(c^+)=\lim_{x\to c^+} f(x)$.
cor:monotone-continuity-criterion.statement_tex: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f:I \to \mathbb{R}$ be monotone increasing, and let $c$ be an interior point of $I$. Then $f$ is continuous at $c$ if and only if its one-sided limits satisfy $f(c^-)=f(c)=f(c^+)$, where $f(c^-)=\lim_{x\to c^-} f(x)$ and $f(c^+)=\lim_{x\to c^+} f(x)$.

def:jump-of-function.title: Jump of a Function
def:jump-of-function.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ be increasing, and let $c$ be an interior point of $I$. If the one-sided limits $f(c^-)$ and $f(c^+)$ exist, the jump of $f$ at $c$ is \[ j_f(c)\coloneqq f(c^+)-f(c^-). \]
def:jump-of-function.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ be
increasing, and let $c$ be an interior point of $I$. If the one-sided
limits $f(c^-)$ and $f(c^+)$ exist, the jump of $f$ at $c$ is
\[
j_f(c)\coloneqq f(c^+)-f(c^-).
\]
def:jump-of-function.statement_tex:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ be
increasing, and let $c$ be an interior point of $I$. If the one-sided
limits $f(c^-)$ and $f(c^+)$ exist, the jump of $f$ at $c$ is
\[
j_f(c)\coloneqq f(c^+)-f(c^-).
\]

prop:monotone-discontinuities-first-kind.title: Discontinuities of a Monotone Function Are of First Kind
prop:monotone-discontinuities-first-kind.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f:I \to \mathbb{R}$ be monotone, and let $a \in I$ be such that there exist points of $I$ strictly less and strictly greater than $a$. If $f$ is discontinuous at $a$, then both one-sided limits $\lim_{x\uparrow a} f(x)$ and $\lim_{x\downarrow a} f(x)$ exist in $\mathbb{R}$.
prop:monotone-discontinuities-first-kind.statement_display: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f:I \to \mathbb{R}$ be monotone, and let $a \in I$ be such that there exist points of $I$ strictly less and strictly greater than $a$. If $f$ is discontinuous at $a$, then both one-sided limits $\lim_{x\uparrow a} f(x)$ and $\lim_{x\downarrow a} f(x)$ exist in $\mathbb{R}$.
prop:monotone-discontinuities-first-kind.statement_tex: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f:I \to \mathbb{R}$ be monotone, and let $a \in I$ be such that there exist points of $I$ strictly less and strictly greater than $a$. If $f$ is discontinuous at $a$, then both one-sided limits $\lim_{x\uparrow a} f(x)$ and $\lim_{x\downarrow a} f(x)$ exist in $\mathbb{R}$.

cor:jump-intervals-for-monotone-disconti.title: Jump Intervals Are Disjoint
cor:jump-intervals-for-monotone-disconti.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval and let $f:I \to \mathbb{R}$ be nondecreasing. If $a,b \in I$ are distinct points of discontinuity of $f$, then the open intervals $(f(a^-),f(a^+))$ and $(f(b^-),f(b^+))$ are disjoint.
cor:jump-intervals-for-monotone-disconti.statement_display: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval and let $f:I \to \mathbb{R}$ be nondecreasing. If $a,b \in I$ are distinct points of discontinuity of $f$, then the open intervals $(f(a^-),f(a^+))$ and $(f(b^-),f(b^+))$ are disjoint.
cor:jump-intervals-for-monotone-disconti.statement_tex: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval and let $f:I \to \mathbb{R}$ be nondecreasing. If $a,b \in I$ are distinct points of discontinuity of $f$, then the open intervals $(f(a^-),f(a^+))$ and $(f(b^-),f(b^+))$ are disjoint.

thm:monotone-discontinuities-countable.title: Discontinuities of a Monotone Function Are Countable
thm:monotone-discontinuities-countable.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ be monotone. Then the set $D_f \coloneqq \{c \in I : f \text{ is discontinuous at } c\}$ is at most countable.
thm:monotone-discontinuities-countable.statement_display: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ be monotone. Then the set $D_f \coloneqq \{c \in I : f \text{ is discontinuous at } c\}$ is at most countable.
thm:monotone-discontinuities-countable.statement_tex: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ be monotone. Then the set $D_f \coloneqq \{c \in I : f \text{ is discontinuous at } c\}$ is at most countable.

cor:monotone-continuous-on-dense-set.title: Monotone Function Is Continuous on a Dense Set
cor:monotone-continuous-on-dense-set.statement_preview: Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be monotone. Then the set $\{x\in[a,b]:f \text{ is continuous at } x\}$ is dense in $[a,b]$.
cor:monotone-continuous-on-dense-set.statement_display: Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be monotone. Then the set $\{x\in[a,b]:f \text{ is continuous at } x\}$ is dense in $[a,b]$.
cor:monotone-continuous-on-dense-set.statement_tex: Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be monotone. Then the set $\{x\in[a,b]:f \text{ is continuous at } x\}$ is dense in $[a,b]$.

prop:continuous-injective-iff-strictly-m.title: Continuous Injective Is Strictly Monotone
prop:continuous-injective-iff-strictly-m.statement_preview: Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $[a,b]$. Then $f$ is injective if and only if $f$ is strictly monotone on $[a,b]$.
prop:continuous-injective-iff-strictly-m.statement_display: Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $[a,b]$. Then $f$ is injective if and only if $f$ is strictly monotone on $[a,b]$.
prop:continuous-injective-iff-strictly-m.statement_tex: Let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $[a,b]$. Then $f$ is injective if and only if $f$ is strictly monotone on $[a,b]$.

thm:continuous-inverse-theorem.title: Continuous Inverse Theorem
thm:continuous-inverse-theorem.statement_preview: Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ be strictly monotone and continuous on $I$. Then the inverse function $f^{-1}:f(I)\to I$ exists, is strictly monotone, and is continuous on $f(I)$.
thm:continuous-inverse-theorem.statement_display: Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ be strictly monotone and continuous on $I$. Then the inverse function $f^{-1}:f(I)\to I$ exists, is strictly monotone, and is continuous on $f(I)$.
thm:continuous-inverse-theorem.statement_tex: Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ be strictly monotone and continuous on $I$. Then the inverse function $f^{-1}:f(I)\to I$ exists, is strictly monotone, and is continuous on $f(I)$.

def:relative-neighborhood.title: Relative Neighborhood
def:relative-neighborhood.statement_preview: Let $E \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $a \in E$. A set $U \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is a neighborhood of $a$ in $E$ if and only if there exists $\delta > 0$ such that $U = E \cap (a-\delta, a+\delta)$. Equivalently, every neighborhood of $a$ in $E$ is a set of the form $U_E(a) := E \cap (a-\delta, a+\delta)$ for some $\delta > 0$.
def:relative-neighborhood.statement_display: Let $E \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $a \in E$. A set $U \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is a neighborhood of $a$ in $E$ if and only if there exists $\delta > 0$ such that $U = E \cap (a-\delta, a+\delta)$. Equivalently, every neighborhood of $a$ in $E$ is a set of the form $U_E(a) := E \cap (a-\delta, a+\delta)$ for some $\delta > 0$.
def:relative-neighborhood.statement_tex: Let $E \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $a \in E$. A set $U \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is a neighborhood of $a$ in $E$ if and only if there exists $\delta > 0$ such that $U = E \cap (a-\delta, a+\delta)$. Equivalently, every neighborhood of $a$ in $E$ is a set of the form $U_E(a) := E \cap (a-\delta, a+\delta)$ for some $\delta > 0$.

def:continuous-at-point.title: Continuity at a Point
def:continuous-at-point.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. The function $f$ is \textbf{continuous at $c$} if and only if \[ \forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists \delta > 0 \;\forall x \in A : \bigl(|x-c| < \delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(c)| < \varepsilon\bigr). \]
def:continuous-at-point.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. The function $f$ is \textbf{continuous at $c$} if and only if
\[
\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists \delta > 0 \;\forall x \in A :
\bigl(|x-c| < \delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(c)| < \varepsilon\bigr).
\]
def:continuous-at-point.statement_tex:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. The function $f$ is \textbf{continuous at $c$} if and only if
\[
\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists \delta > 0 \;\forall x \in A :
\bigl(|x-c| < \delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(c)| < \varepsilon\bigr).
\]

def:continuous-at-point-nbhd.title: Continuity at a Point --- Neighbourhood Form
def:continuous-at-point-nbhd.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. The function $f$ is continuous at $c$ if and only if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $\delta > 0$ such that \[ f\bigl(A \cap (c-\delta, c+\delta)\bigr) \subseteq (f(c)-\varepsilon, f(c)+\varepsilon). \]
def:continuous-at-point-nbhd.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. The function $f$ is continuous at $c$ if and only if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $\delta > 0$ such that
\[
f\bigl(A \cap (c-\delta, c+\delta)\bigr) \subseteq (f(c)-\varepsilon, f(c)+\varepsilon).
\]
def:continuous-at-point-nbhd.statement_tex:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. The function $f$ is continuous at $c$ if and only if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $\delta > 0$ such that
\[
f\bigl(A \cap (c-\delta, c+\delta)\bigr) \subseteq (f(c)-\varepsilon, f(c)+\varepsilon).
\]

def:continuous-at-point-seq.title: Continuity at a Point --- Sequential Form
def:continuous-at-point-seq.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $c \in A$, and let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is continuous at $c$ if and only if for every sequence $(x_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ in $A$, the convergence $\lim_{n\to\infty} x_n = c$ implies $\lim_{n\to\infty} f(x_n) = f(c)$.
def:continuous-at-point-seq.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $c \in A$, and let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is continuous at $c$ if and only if for every sequence $(x_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ in $A$, the convergence $\lim_{n\to\infty} x_n = c$ implies $\lim_{n\to\infty} f(x_n) = f(c)$.
def:continuous-at-point-seq.statement_tex: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $c \in A$, and let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is continuous at $c$ if and only if for every sequence $(x_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ in $A$, the convergence $\lim_{n\to\infty} x_n = c$ implies $\lim_{n\to\infty} f(x_n) = f(c)$.

def:point-of-discontinuity.title: Point of Discontinuity
def:point-of-discontinuity.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $a \in A$. The function $f$ is discontinuous at $a$ if and only if $f$ is not continuous at $a$.
def:point-of-discontinuity.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $a \in A$. The function $f$ is discontinuous at $a$ if and only if $f$ is not continuous at $a$.
def:point-of-discontinuity.statement_tex: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $a \in A$. The function $f$ is discontinuous at $a$ if and only if $f$ is not continuous at $a$.

def:sequential-discontinuity-at-a-point.title: Sequential Characterization of Discontinuity
def:sequential-discontinuity-at-a-point.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. The function $f$ is discontinuous at $c$ if and only if there exists a sequence $(x_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}} \subseteq A$ such that $x_n \to c$ while $f(x_n) \not\to f(c)$.
def:sequential-discontinuity-at-a-point.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. The function $f$ is discontinuous at $c$ if and only if there exists a sequence $(x_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}} \subseteq A$ such that $x_n \to c$ while $f(x_n) \not\to f(c)$.
def:sequential-discontinuity-at-a-point.statement_tex: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. The function $f$ is discontinuous at $c$ if and only if there exists a sequence $(x_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}} \subseteq A$ such that $x_n \to c$ while $f(x_n) \not\to f(c)$.

def:neighborhood-discontinuity-at-a-poin.title: Neighbourhood Characterization of Discontinuity
def:neighborhood-discontinuity-at-a-poin.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. The function $f$ is discontinuous at $c$ if and only if there exists a neighborhood $V$ of $f(c)$ such that for every neighborhood $U$ of $c$ relative to $A$ there exists $x \in U \cap A$ with $f(x) \notin V$.
def:neighborhood-discontinuity-at-a-poin.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. The function $f$ is discontinuous at $c$ if and only if there exists a neighborhood $V$ of $f(c)$ such that for every neighborhood $U$ of $c$ relative to $A$ there exists $x \in U \cap A$ with $f(x) \notin V$.
def:neighborhood-discontinuity-at-a-poin.statement_tex: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. The function $f$ is discontinuous at $c$ if and only if there exists a neighborhood $V$ of $f(c)$ such that for every neighborhood $U$ of $c$ relative to $A$ there exists $x \in U \cap A$ with $f(x) \notin V$.

lem:equivalent-discontinuity-at-a-point.title: Equivalent Formulations of Discontinuity at a Point
lem:equivalent-discontinuity-at-a-point.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, and let $c\in A$. The following are equivalent: \begin{enumerate} \item $c$ is a point of discontinuity in the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ sense. \item $c$ is a sequential point of discontinuity. \item $c$ is a neighbourhood point of discontinuity. \end{enumerate}

def:types-of-discontinuity-at-a-point.title: Types of Discontinuity
def:types-of-discontinuity-at-a-point.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $x_0 \in A$ with $f$ discontinuous at $x_0$. \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item \emph{Removable} iff $\exists L \in \mathbb{R}:\;\lim_{x \to x_0} f(x) = L$ and $L \ne f(x_0)$. \item \emph{Jump} iff $\exists L_{-}, L_{+} \in \mathbb{R}:\;\lim_{x \to x_0^-} f(x) = L_{-}$, $\lim_{x \to x_0^+...
def:types-of-discontinuity-at-a-point.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $x_0 \in A$ with $f$ discontinuous at $x_0$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item \emph{Removable} iff $\exists L \in \mathbb{R}:\;\lim_{x \to x_0} f(x) = L$ and $L \ne f(x_0)$.
  \item \emph{Jump} iff $\exists L_{-}, L_{+} \in \mathbb{R}:\;\lim_{x \to x_0^-} f(x) = L_{-}$, $\lim_{x \to x_0^+} f(x) = L_{+}$, and $L_{-} \ne L_{+}$.
  \item \emph{Essential} iff it is not removable.
\end{enumerate}
def:types-of-discontinuity-at-a-point.statement_tex:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $x_0 \in A$ with $f$ discontinuous at $x_0$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item \emph{Removable} iff $\exists L \in \mathbb{R}:\;\lim_{x \to x_0} f(x) = L$ and $L \ne f(x_0)$.
  \item \emph{Jump} iff $\exists L_{-}, L_{+} \in \mathbb{R}:\;\lim_{x \to x_0^-} f(x) = L_{-}$, $\lim_{x \to x_0^+} f(x) = L_{+}$, and $L_{-} \ne L_{+}$.
  \item \emph{Essential} iff it is not removable.
\end{enumerate}

def:oscillation-on-a-set.title: Oscillation on a Set
def:oscillation-on-a-set.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, and let $E\subseteq A$. The oscillation of $f$ on $E$ is the extended real number \[ \omega(f;E)\coloneqq \sup\{\,|f(x_1)-f(x_2)|:x_1,x_2\in E\,\} =\mathrm{diam}(f(E)). \]
def:oscillation-on-a-set.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, and let
$E\subseteq A$. The oscillation of $f$ on $E$ is the extended real number
\[
\omega(f;E)\coloneqq
\sup\{\,|f(x_1)-f(x_2)|:x_1,x_2\in E\,\}
=\mathrm{diam}(f(E)).
\]
def:oscillation-on-a-set.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, and let
$E\subseteq A$. The oscillation of $f$ on $E$ is the extended real number
\[
\omega(f;E)\coloneqq
\sup\{\,|f(x_1)-f(x_2)|:x_1,x_2\in E\,\}
=\mathrm{diam}(f(E)).
\]

def:oscillation-at-a-point.title: Oscillation at a Point
def:oscillation-at-a-point.statement_preview: Let $E \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:E \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $x_0 \in \mathbb{R}$ satisfy $U_\delta(x_0)\cap E \neq \varnothing$ for every $\delta>0$, where $U_\delta(x_0)=\{x \in \mathbb{R}: |x-x_0|<\delta\}$. The oscillation of $f$ at $x_0$ is the extended real number \[ \omega(f;x_0) = \lim_{\delta \to 0^+} \omega\bigl(f;\,U_\delta(x_0)\cap E\bigr), \...
def:oscillation-at-a-point.statement_display:
Let $E \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:E \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $x_0 \in \mathbb{R}$ satisfy $U_\delta(x_0)\cap E \neq \varnothing$ for every $\delta>0$, where $U_\delta(x_0)=\{x \in \mathbb{R}: |x-x_0|<\delta\}$. The oscillation of $f$ at $x_0$ is the extended real number
\[
\omega(f;x_0)
=
\lim_{\delta \to 0^+}
\omega\bigl(f;\,U_\delta(x_0)\cap E\bigr),
\]
with $\omega(f;A)$ denoting the oscillation of $f$ on the set $A$. Because the function $\delta \mapsto \omega\bigl(f;\,U_\delta(x_0)\cap E\bigr)$ is nonincreasing, this right-hand limit exists (possibly $+\infty$).
def:oscillation-at-a-point.statement_tex:
Let $E \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:E \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $x_0 \in \mathbb{R}$ satisfy $U_\delta(x_0)\cap E \neq \varnothing$ for every $\delta>0$, where $U_\delta(x_0)=\{x \in \mathbb{R}: |x-x_0|<\delta\}$. The oscillation of $f$ at $x_0$ is the extended real number
\[
\omega(f;x_0)
=
\lim_{\delta \to 0^+}
\omega\bigl(f;\,U_\delta(x_0)\cap E\bigr),
\]
with $\omega(f;A)$ denoting the oscillation of $f$ on the set $A$. Because the function $\delta \mapsto \omega\bigl(f;\,U_\delta(x_0)\cap E\bigr)$ is nonincreasing, this right-hand limit exists (possibly $+\infty$).

thm:continuity-iff-zero-oscillation.title: Continuity Characterised by Oscillation
thm:continuity-iff-zero-oscillation.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. Then $f$ is continuous at $c$ if and only if $\omega(f;c)=0$.
thm:continuity-iff-zero-oscillation.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. Then $f$ is continuous at $c$ if and only if $\omega(f;c)=0$.
thm:continuity-iff-zero-oscillation.statement_tex: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f \colon A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. Then $f$ is continuous at $c$ if and only if $\omega(f;c)=0$.

prop:discontinuity-set-via-oscillation.title: Discontinuity Set via Oscillation
prop:discontinuity-set-via-oscillation.statement_preview: Let $f:E\to\mathbb{R}$. Then \[ D(f):=\{x\in E: f \text{ is discontinuous at } x\} =\{x\in E: \omega(f;x)>0\} =\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} \{x\in E: \omega(f;x)\ge 1/n\}. \]

def:uniform-continuity.title: Uniform Continuity
def:uniform-continuity.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is uniformly continuous on $A$ if and only if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $\delta > 0$ such that for all $x,u \in A$ the implication $|x-u| < \delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(u)| < \varepsilon$ holds.
def:uniform-continuity.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is uniformly continuous on $A$ if and only if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $\delta > 0$ such that for all $x,u \in A$ the implication $|x-u| < \delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(u)| < \varepsilon$ holds.
def:uniform-continuity.statement_tex: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is uniformly continuous on $A$ if and only if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $\delta > 0$ such that for all $x,u \in A$ the implication $|x-u| < \delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(u)| < \varepsilon$ holds.

thm:algebra-of-uniform-continuity-genera.title: Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a General Domain
thm:algebra-of-uniform-continuity-genera.statement_preview: \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)] \item For every set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, every pair of functions $f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}$ that are uniformly continuous on $A$, and every scalar $c \in \mathbb{R}$, the functions $f+g$, $f-g$, and $cf$ are uniformly continuous on $A$. \item There exist a set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and uniformly continuous functions $...
thm:algebra-of-uniform-continuity-genera.statement_display:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
  \item For every set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, every pair of functions $f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}$ that are uniformly continuous on $A$, and every scalar $c \in \mathbb{R}$, the functions $f+g$, $f-g$, and $cf$ are uniformly continuous on $A$.
  \item There exist a set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and uniformly continuous functions $f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}$ such that the product $fg$ is not uniformly continuous on $A$.
\end{enumerate}
thm:algebra-of-uniform-continuity-genera.statement_tex:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
  \item For every set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, every pair of functions $f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}$ that are uniformly continuous on $A$, and every scalar $c \in \mathbb{R}$, the functions $f+g$, $f-g$, and $cf$ are uniformly continuous on $A$.
  \item There exist a set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and uniformly continuous functions $f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}$ such that the product $fg$ is not uniformly continuous on $A$.
\end{enumerate}

thm:algebra-of-uniform-continuity-bounde.title: Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a Bounded Domain
thm:algebra-of-uniform-continuity-bounde.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be bounded, and let $f,g:A \to \mathbb{R}$ be uniformly continuous on $A$. \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)] \item The product $x \mapsto f(x)g(x)$ is uniformly continuous on $A$. \item If there exists $k>0$ such that $|g(x)| \ge k$ for all $x \in A$, then the quotient $x \mapsto f(x)/g(x)$ is uniformly continuous on $A$. \end{e...
thm:algebra-of-uniform-continuity-bounde.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be bounded, and let $f,g:A \to \mathbb{R}$ be uniformly continuous on $A$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item The product $x \mapsto f(x)g(x)$ is uniformly continuous on $A$.
\item If there exists $k>0$ such that $|g(x)| \ge k$ for all $x \in A$, then the quotient $x \mapsto f(x)/g(x)$ is uniformly continuous on $A$.
\end{enumerate}
thm:algebra-of-uniform-continuity-bounde.statement_tex:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be bounded, and let $f,g:A \to \mathbb{R}$ be uniformly continuous on $A$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item The product $x \mapsto f(x)g(x)$ is uniformly continuous on $A$.
\item If there exists $k>0$ such that $|g(x)| \ge k$ for all $x \in A$, then the quotient $x \mapsto f(x)/g(x)$ is uniformly continuous on $A$.
\end{enumerate}

prop:sequential-uniform-continuity.title: Sequential Characterization of Uniform Continuity
prop:sequential-uniform-continuity.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$. Then $f$ is uniformly continuous on $A$ if and only if for every pair of sequences $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ in $A$ with $\lim_{n \to \infty} (x_n - y_n) = 0$ we have $\lim_{n \to \infty} (f(x_n) - f(y_n)) = 0$.
prop:sequential-uniform-continuity.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$. Then $f$ is uniformly continuous on $A$ if and only if for every pair of sequences $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ in $A$ with $\lim_{n \to \infty} (x_n - y_n) = 0$ we have $\lim_{n \to \infty} (f(x_n) - f(y_n)) = 0$.
prop:sequential-uniform-continuity.statement_tex: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$. Then $f$ is uniformly continuous on $A$ if and only if for every pair of sequences $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ in $A$ with $\lim_{n \to \infty} (x_n - y_n) = 0$ we have $\lim_{n \to \infty} (f(x_n) - f(y_n)) = 0$.

thm:heine-cantor.title: Heine--Cantor Theorem
thm:heine-cantor.statement_preview: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$ and let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $[a,b]$. Then $f$ is uniformly continuous on $[a,b]$.
thm:heine-cantor.statement_display: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$ and let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $[a,b]$. Then $f$ is uniformly continuous on $[a,b]$.
thm:heine-cantor.statement_tex: Let $a,b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$ and let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $[a,b]$. Then $f$ is uniformly continuous on $[a,b]$.

prop:uniform-continuity-cauchy.title: Uniform Continuity Preserves Cauchy Sequences
prop:uniform-continuity-cauchy.statement_preview: Let $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:S \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $(x_n)$ be a sequence in $S$. If $f$ is uniformly continuous on $S$ and $(x_n)$ is a Cauchy sequence in $S$, then $(f(x_n))$ is a Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{R}$.
prop:uniform-continuity-cauchy.statement_display: Let $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:S \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $(x_n)$ be a sequence in $S$. If $f$ is uniformly continuous on $S$ and $(x_n)$ is a Cauchy sequence in $S$, then $(f(x_n))$ is a Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{R}$.
prop:uniform-continuity-cauchy.statement_tex: Let $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:S \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $(x_n)$ be a sequence in $S$. If $f$ is uniformly continuous on $S$ and $(x_n)$ is a Cauchy sequence in $S$, then $(f(x_n))$ is a Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{R}$.

def:lipschitz-condition.title: Lipschitz Condition
def:lipschitz-condition.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $K>0$. The function $f$ satisfies the Lipschitz condition on $A$ with constant $K$ if and only if $|f(x)-f(u)| \le K|x-u|$ for every $x,u \in A$.
def:lipschitz-condition.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $K>0$. The function $f$ satisfies the Lipschitz condition on $A$ with constant $K$ if and only if $|f(x)-f(u)| \le K|x-u|$ for every $x,u \in A$.
def:lipschitz-condition.statement_tex: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $K>0$. The function $f$ satisfies the Lipschitz condition on $A$ with constant $K$ if and only if $|f(x)-f(u)| \le K|x-u|$ for every $x,u \in A$.

prop:lipschitz-implies-uc.title: Lipschitz Implies Uniform Continuity
prop:lipschitz-implies-uc.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$. If there exists $K \ge 0$ such that $|f(x)-f(y)| \le K |x-y|$ for all $x,y \in A$, then $f$ is uniformly continuous on $A$.
prop:lipschitz-implies-uc.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$. If there exists $K \ge 0$ such that $|f(x)-f(y)| \le K |x-y|$ for all $x,y \in A$, then $f$ is uniformly continuous on $A$.
prop:lipschitz-implies-uc.statement_tex: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$. If there exists $K \ge 0$ such that $|f(x)-f(y)| \le K |x-y|$ for all $x,y \in A$, then $f$ is uniformly continuous on $A$.

thm:algebra-of-lipschitz-functions.title: Algebra of Lipschitz Functions
thm:algebra-of-lipschitz-functions.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ be Lipschitz with constants $K_f,K_g\ge 0$ respectively. \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)] \item For every $c\in\mathbb{R}$, the function $cf$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $|c|K_f$. \item The functions $f+g$ and $f-g$ are Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $K_f+K_g$. \item If $B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$,...
thm:algebra-of-lipschitz-functions.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ be Lipschitz with constants $K_f,K_g\ge 0$ respectively.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item For every $c\in\mathbb{R}$, the function $cf$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $|c|K_f$.
\item The functions $f+g$ and $f-g$ are Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $K_f+K_g$.
\item If $B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, $h:B\to\mathbb{R}$ is Lipschitz with constant $K_h\ge 0$, and $f(A)\subseteq B$, then $h\circ f$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $K_hK_f$.
\item If $|f(x)|\le M_f$ and $|g(x)|\le M_g$ for all $x\in A$, then $fg$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $M_gK_f+M_fK_g$.
\item If $|f(x)|\le M_f$ for all $x\in A$, if $|g(x)|\ge k>0$ for all $x\in A$, and if $g$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $K_g$, then $f/g$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $K_f/k + M_fK_g/k^2$.
\end{enumerate}
thm:algebra-of-lipschitz-functions.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ be Lipschitz with constants $K_f,K_g\ge 0$ respectively.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item For every $c\in\mathbb{R}$, the function $cf$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $|c|K_f$.
\item The functions $f+g$ and $f-g$ are Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $K_f+K_g$.
\item If $B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, $h:B\to\mathbb{R}$ is Lipschitz with constant $K_h\ge 0$, and $f(A)\subseteq B$, then $h\circ f$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $K_hK_f$.
\item If $|f(x)|\le M_f$ and $|g(x)|\le M_g$ for all $x\in A$, then $fg$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $M_gK_f+M_fK_g$.
\item If $|f(x)|\le M_f$ for all $x\in A$, if $|g(x)|\ge k>0$ for all $x\in A$, and if $g$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $K_g$, then $f/g$ is Lipschitz on $A$ with constant $K_f/k + M_fK_g/k^2$.
\end{enumerate}

def:bi-lipschitz.title: Bi-Lipschitz Map
def:bi-lipschitz.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. The map $f$ is bi-Lipschitz on $A$ if and only if there exist constants $0<\alpha\le K$ such that for every $x,u\in A$, \[ \alpha|x-u|\le |f(x)-f(u)|\le K|x-u|. \] Any such pair $(\alpha,K)$ is called a bi-Lipschitz constant pair for $f$ on $A$.
def:bi-lipschitz.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. The map $f$ is bi-Lipschitz on $A$ if and only if there exist constants $0<\alpha\le K$ such that for every $x,u\in A$,
\[
\alpha|x-u|\le |f(x)-f(u)|\le K|x-u|.
\]
Any such pair $(\alpha,K)$ is called a bi-Lipschitz constant pair for $f$ on $A$.
def:bi-lipschitz.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. The map $f$ is bi-Lipschitz on $A$ if and only if there exist constants $0<\alpha\le K$ such that for every $x,u\in A$,
\[
\alpha|x-u|\le |f(x)-f(u)|\le K|x-u|.
\]
Any such pair $(\alpha,K)$ is called a bi-Lipschitz constant pair for $f$ on $A$.

thm:bilipschitz-inverse-is-lipschitz.title: Inverse of a Bi-Lipschitz Map Is Lipschitz
thm:bilipschitz-inverse-is-lipschitz.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$, and suppose there exist constants $\alpha,K>0$ such that $\alpha |x-y| \le |f(x)-f(y)| \le K |x-y|$ for all $x,y \in A$. Then $f$ is injective, the inverse mapping $f^{-1}:f(A) \to A$ is well defined, and \[ \frac{1}{K}|u-v| \le |f^{-1}(u)-f^{-1}(v)| \le \frac{1}{\alpha}|u-v| \quad\text{for all } u,v \...
thm:bilipschitz-inverse-is-lipschitz.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$, and suppose there exist constants $\alpha,K>0$ such that
$\alpha |x-y| \le |f(x)-f(y)| \le K |x-y|$ for all $x,y \in A$. Then $f$ is injective, the inverse mapping $f^{-1}:f(A) \to A$ is well defined, and
\[
\frac{1}{K}|u-v| \le |f^{-1}(u)-f^{-1}(v)| \le \frac{1}{\alpha}|u-v|
\quad\text{for all } u,v \in f(A).
\]
thm:bilipschitz-inverse-is-lipschitz.statement_tex:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f:A \to \mathbb{R}$, and suppose there exist constants $\alpha,K>0$ such that
$\alpha |x-y| \le |f(x)-f(y)| \le K |x-y|$ for all $x,y \in A$. Then $f$ is injective, the inverse mapping $f^{-1}:f(A) \to A$ is well defined, and
\[
\frac{1}{K}|u-v| \le |f^{-1}(u)-f^{-1}(v)| \le \frac{1}{\alpha}|u-v|
\quad\text{for all } u,v \in f(A).
\]

thm:constant-multiple-rule.title: Constant Multiple Rule
thm:constant-multiple-rule.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$, and let $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$. If $f$ is differentiable at $c$, then $\alpha f$ is differentiable at $c$, and \[ (\alpha f)'(c) = \alpha f'(c). \]
thm:constant-multiple-rule.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$, and let $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$. If $f$ is differentiable at $c$, then $\alpha f$ is differentiable at $c$, and
\[
(\alpha f)'(c) = \alpha f'(c).
\]

thm:sum-rule.title: Sum Rule
thm:sum-rule.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f, g : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$. If $f$ and $g$ are differentiable at $c$, then $f + g$ is differentiable at $c$, and \[ (f+g)'(c) = f'(c) + g'(c). \]
thm:sum-rule.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f, g : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$. If $f$ and $g$ are differentiable at $c$, then $f + g$ is differentiable at $c$, and
\[
(f+g)'(c) = f'(c) + g'(c).
\]

\[
(f+g)'(c) = f'(c) + g'(c).

thm:product-rule.title: Product Rule
thm:product-rule.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f, g : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$. If $f$ and $g$ are differentiable at $c$, then $fg$ is differentiable at $c$, and \[ (fg)'(c) = f'(c)g(c) + f(c)g'(c). \]
thm:product-rule.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f, g : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$. If $f$ and $g$ are differentiable at $c$, then $fg$ is differentiable at $c$, and
\[
(fg)'(c) = f'(c)g(c) + f(c)g'(c).
\]

thm:quotient-rule.title: Quotient Rule
thm:quotient-rule.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f, g : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$. Suppose that $f$ and $g$ are differentiable at $c$ and that $g(c) \neq 0$. Then $f/g$ is differentiable at $c$, and \[ \left(\frac{f}{g}\right)'(c) = \frac{f'(c)g(c) - f(c)g'(c)}{(g(c))^2}. \]
thm:quotient-rule.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f, g : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$. Suppose that $f$ and $g$ are differentiable at $c$ and that $g(c) \neq 0$. Then $f/g$ is differentiable at $c$, and
\[
\left(\frac{f}{g}\right)'(c) = \frac{f'(c)g(c) - f(c)g'(c)}{(g(c))^2}.
\]

cor:finite-sum-rule.title: Finite Sum Rule
cor:finite-sum-rule.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f_1, \dots, f_n : A \to \mathbb{R}$, let $\alpha_1, \dots, \alpha_n \in \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$. If each $f_i$ is differentiable at $c$, then $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i f_i$ is differentiable at $c$, and \[ \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i f_i\right)'(c) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i f_i'(c). \]
cor:finite-sum-rule.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f_1, \dots, f_n : A \to \mathbb{R}$, let $\alpha_1, \dots, \alpha_n \in \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$. If each $f_i$ is differentiable at $c$, then $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i f_i$ is differentiable at $c$, and
\[
\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i f_i\right)'(c) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i f_i'(c).
\]

cor:extended-product-rule.title: Extended Product Rule
cor:extended-product-rule.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f_1, \dots, f_n : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$. If each $f_i$ is differentiable at $c$, then $\prod_{i=1}^{n} f_i$ is differentiable at $c$, and \[ \left(\prod_{i=1}^{n} f_i\right)'(c) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} f_k'(c) \prod_{\substack{i=1\\i\neq k}}^{n} f_i(c). \]
cor:extended-product-rule.statement_display:

=
\sum_{k=1}^{n}
f_k'(c) \prod_{\substack{i=1\\i\neq k}}^{n} f_i(c).
\]

def:caratheodory-factorization.title: Carathéodory Factorization
def:caratheodory-factorization.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$. We say that $f$ admits a \emph{Carathéodory factorization} at $c$ if there exists a function $\phi : I \to \mathbb{R}$, continuous at $c$, such that \[ f(x) = f(c) + (x-c)\,\phi(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in I. \] In this case, $f$ is differentiable at $c$ and $f'(...
def:caratheodory-factorization.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$. We say that $f$ admits a \emph{Carathéodory factorization} at $c$ if there exists a function $\phi : I \to \mathbb{R}$, continuous at $c$, such that
\[
f(x) = f(c) + (x-c)\,\phi(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in I.
\]
In this case, $f$ is differentiable at $c$ and $f'(c) = \phi(c)$.
def:caratheodory-factorization.statement_tex:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$. We say that $f$ admits a \emph{Carathéodory factorization} at $c$ if there exists a function $\phi : I \to \mathbb{R}$, continuous at $c$, such that
\[
f(x) = f(c) + (x-c)\,\phi(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in I.
\]
In this case, $f$ is differentiable at $c$ and $f'(c) = \phi(c)$.

thm:caratheodory-characterization-of-dif.title: Carathéodory Characterization of Differentiability
thm:caratheodory-characterization-of-dif.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$. The following are equivalent: \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item $f$ is differentiable at $c$. \item There exists a function $\phi : I \to \mathbb{R}$, continuous at $c$, such that \[ f(x) = f(c) + (x-c)\,\phi(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in I. \] \end{enumerat...
thm:caratheodory-characterization-of-dif.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$. The following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f$ is differentiable at $c$.
\item There exists a function $\phi : I \to \mathbb{R}$, continuous at $c$, such that
\[
f(x) = f(c) + (x-c)\,\phi(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in I.
\]
\end{enumerate}
In this case, $\phi(c) = f'(c)$.
thm:caratheodory-characterization-of-dif.statement_tex:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$. The following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f$ is differentiable at $c$.
\item There exists a function $\phi : I \to \mathbb{R}$, continuous at $c$, such that
\[
f(x) = f(c) + (x-c)\,\phi(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in I.
\]
\end{enumerate}
In this case, $\phi(c) = f'(c)$.

thm:chain-rule.title: Chain Rule
thm:chain-rule.statement_preview: Let $I, J \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be intervals, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ and $g : J \to \mathbb{R}$, and suppose that $f(I) \subseteq J$. Let $c \in I$. If $f$ is differentiable at $c$ and $g$ is differentiable at $f(c)$, then the composition $g \circ f$ is differentiable at $c$, and \[ (g \circ f)'(c) = g'(f(c))\,f'(c). \]
thm:chain-rule.statement_display:
Let $I, J \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be intervals, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ and $g : J \to \mathbb{R}$, and suppose that $f(I) \subseteq J$. Let $c \in I$. If $f$ is differentiable at $c$ and $g$ is differentiable at $f(c)$, then the composition $g \circ f$ is differentiable at $c$, and
\[
(g \circ f)'(c) = g'(f(c))\,f'(c).
\]
thm:chain-rule.statement_tex:
Let $I, J \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be intervals, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ and $g : J \to \mathbb{R}$, and suppose that $f(I) \subseteq J$. Let $c \in I$. If $f$ is differentiable at $c$ and $g$ is differentiable at $f(c)$, then the composition $g \circ f$ is differentiable at $c$, and
\[
(g \circ f)'(c) = g'(f(c))\,f'(c).
\]

def:derivative-at-a-point.title: Derivative at a Point
def:derivative-at-a-point.statement_preview: Let $f: D \to \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function and $c \in \operatorname{int}(D)$. We say $f$ is \emph{differentiable at $c$} with \emph{derivative} $L = f'(c)$ if and only if: \[ \forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists \delta > 0 \;\forall x \in D \Bigl(0 < |x - c| < \delta \implies \left| \frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c} - L \right| < \varepsilon\Bigr). \]
def:derivative-at-a-point.statement_display:
Let $f: D \to \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function and $c \in \operatorname{int}(D)$. We say $f$ is \emph{differentiable at $c$} with \emph{derivative} $L = f'(c)$ if and only if:
\[
\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists \delta > 0 \;\forall x \in D
\Bigl(0 < |x - c| < \delta \implies \left| \frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c} - L \right| < \varepsilon\Bigr).
\]
def:derivative-at-a-point.statement_tex:
Let $f: D \to \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function and $c \in \operatorname{int}(D)$. We say $f$ is \emph{differentiable at $c$} with \emph{derivative} $L = f'(c)$ if and only if:
\[
\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists \delta > 0 \;\forall x \in D
\Bigl(0 < |x - c| < \delta \implies \left| \frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c} - L \right| < \varepsilon\Bigr).
\]

def:topological-definition-of-derivative.title: Topological Definition of Derivative at a Point
def:topological-definition-of-derivative.statement_preview: Let $f: D \to \mathbb{R}$ and $c \in \operatorname{int}(D)$. $f$ is differentiable at $c$ with derivative $L$ if for every neighbourhood $V$ of $L$, there exists a neighbourhood $U$ of $c$ such that for all $x \in (U \setminus \{c\}) \cap D$: \[ \frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c} \in V. \]
def:topological-definition-of-derivative.statement_display:
Let $f: D \to \mathbb{R}$ and $c \in \operatorname{int}(D)$. $f$ is differentiable at $c$ with derivative $L$ if for every neighbourhood $V$ of $L$, there exists a neighbourhood $U$ of $c$ such that for all $x \in (U \setminus \{c\}) \cap D$:
\[
\frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c} \in V.
\]
def:topological-definition-of-derivative.statement_tex:
Let $f: D \to \mathbb{R}$ and $c \in \operatorname{int}(D)$. $f$ is differentiable at $c$ with derivative $L$ if for every neighbourhood $V$ of $L$, there exists a neighbourhood $U$ of $c$ such that for all $x \in (U \setminus \{c\}) \cap D$:
\[
\frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c} \in V.
\]

def:sequential-definition-of-derivative-.title: Sequential Definition of Derivative at a Point
def:sequential-definition-of-derivative-.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$. We say that $f$ is differentiable at $c$ with derivative $L$ if \[ \forall (x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}\; \bigl(x_n \to c \Rightarrow \tfrac{f(x_n)-f(c)}{x_n-c} \to L\bigr). \]
def:sequential-definition-of-derivative-.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$. We say that $f$ is differentiable at $c$ with derivative $L$ if
\[
\forall (x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}\;
\bigl(x_n \to c \Rightarrow \tfrac{f(x_n)-f(c)}{x_n-c} \to L\bigr).
\]
def:sequential-definition-of-derivative-.statement_tex:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$. We say that $f$ is differentiable at $c$ with derivative $L$ if
\[
\forall (x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}\;
\bigl(x_n \to c \Rightarrow \tfrac{f(x_n)-f(c)}{x_n-c} \to L\bigr).
\]

thm:derivative-equivalence.title: Equivalence of Definitions of the Derivative at a Point
thm:derivative-equivalence.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and $c \in A$ be a limit point of $A$. The following are equivalent: \begin{enumerate} \item $f$ is differentiable at $c$ in the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ sense with derivative $L$. \item $f$ is differentiable at $c$ in the topological sense with derivative $L$. \item $f$ is differentiable at $c$ in the sequential sense with derivati...

prop:derivative-h-form-equivalence.title: Derivative: $h$-Form Equivalence
prop:derivative-h-form-equivalence.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and $c \in \operatorname{int}(A)$. Then $f$ is differentiable at $c$ with derivative $L$ if and only if \[ \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(c+h) - f(c)}{h} = L. \]
prop:derivative-h-form-equivalence.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and $c \in \operatorname{int}(A)$. Then $f$ is differentiable at $c$ with derivative $L$ if and only if
\[
\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(c+h) - f(c)}{h} = L.
\]
prop:derivative-h-form-equivalence.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and $c \in \operatorname{int}(A)$. Then $f$ is differentiable at $c$ with derivative $L$ if and only if
\[
\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(c+h) - f(c)}{h} = L.
\]

thm:differentiable-implies-continuous.title: Differentiable Implies Continuous
thm:differentiable-implies-continuous.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and $c \in A$. If $f$ is differentiable at $c$, then $f$ is continuous at $c$.
thm:differentiable-implies-continuous.statement_display: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and $c \in A$. If $f$ is differentiable at $c$, then $f$ is continuous at $c$.
thm:differentiable-implies-continuous.statement_tex: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and $c \in A$. If $f$ is differentiable at $c$, then $f$ is continuous at $c$.

thm:uniqueness-of-the-derivative.title: Uniqueness of the Derivative
thm:uniqueness-of-the-derivative.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and $c \in A$. If $f$ is differentiable at $c$, its derivative is unique.
thm:uniqueness-of-the-derivative.statement_display: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and $c \in A$. If $f$ is differentiable at $c$, its derivative is unique.
thm:uniqueness-of-the-derivative.statement_tex: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and $c \in A$. If $f$ is differentiable at $c$, its derivative is unique.

prop:derivative-of-the-identity.title: Derivative of the Identity
prop:derivative-of-the-identity.statement_preview: Let $f(x) = x$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$. Then $f'(c) = 1$ for every $c \in \mathbb{R}$.
prop:derivative-of-the-identity.statement_display: Let $f(x) = x$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$. Then $f'(c) = 1$ for every $c \in \mathbb{R}$.
prop:derivative-of-the-identity.statement_tex: Let $f(x) = x$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$. Then $f'(c) = 1$ for every $c \in \mathbb{R}$.

def:relative-minimum.title: Relative Minimum
def:relative-minimum.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. We say that $f$ has a \emph{relative minimum} at $c$ if there exists $\delta > 0$ such that \[ f(c) \leq f(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in A \cap (c-\delta,c+\delta). \] If, in addition, \[ f(c) < f(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in \bigl(A \cap (c-\delta,c+\delta)\bigr)\setminus\{c\...
def:relative-minimum.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. We say that $f$ has a \emph{relative minimum} at $c$ if there exists $\delta > 0$ such that
\[
f(c) \leq f(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in A \cap (c-\delta,c+\delta).
\]
If, in addition,
\[
f(c) < f(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in \bigl(A \cap (c-\delta,c+\delta)\bigr)\setminus\{c\},
\]
then $f$ has a \emph{strict relative minimum} at $c$.
def:relative-minimum.statement_tex:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. We say that $f$ has a \emph{relative minimum} at $c$ if there exists $\delta > 0$ such that
\[
f(c) \leq f(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in A \cap (c-\delta,c+\delta).
\]
If, in addition,
\[
f(c) < f(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in \bigl(A \cap (c-\delta,c+\delta)\bigr)\setminus\{c\},
\]
then $f$ has a \emph{strict relative minimum} at $c$.

def:relative-maximum.title: Relative Maximum
def:relative-maximum.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. We say that $f$ has a \emph{relative maximum} at $c$ if there exists $\delta > 0$ such that \[ f(x) \leq f(c) \qquad \text{for all } x \in A \cap (c-\delta,c+\delta). \] If, in addition, \[ f(x) < f(c) \qquad \text{for all } x \in \bigl(A \cap (c-\delta,c+\delta)\bigr)\setminus\{c\...
def:relative-maximum.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. We say that $f$ has a \emph{relative maximum} at $c$ if there exists $\delta > 0$ such that
\[
f(x) \leq f(c) \qquad \text{for all } x \in A \cap (c-\delta,c+\delta).
\]
If, in addition,
\[
f(x) < f(c) \qquad \text{for all } x \in \bigl(A \cap (c-\delta,c+\delta)\bigr)\setminus\{c\},
\]
then $f$ has a \emph{strict relative maximum} at $c$.
def:relative-maximum.statement_tex:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. We say that $f$ has a \emph{relative maximum} at $c$ if there exists $\delta > 0$ such that
\[
f(x) \leq f(c) \qquad \text{for all } x \in A \cap (c-\delta,c+\delta).
\]
If, in addition,
\[
f(x) < f(c) \qquad \text{for all } x \in \bigl(A \cap (c-\delta,c+\delta)\bigr)\setminus\{c\},
\]
then $f$ has a \emph{strict relative maximum} at $c$.

def:relative-extremum.title: Relative Extremum
def:relative-extremum.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. We say that $f$ has a \emph{relative extremum} at $c$ if $f$ has either a relative minimum at $c$ or a relative maximum at $c$.
def:relative-extremum.statement_display: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. We say that $f$ has a \emph{relative extremum} at $c$ if $f$ has either a relative minimum at $c$ or a relative maximum at $c$.
def:relative-extremum.statement_tex: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. We say that $f$ has a \emph{relative extremum} at $c$ if $f$ has either a relative minimum at $c$ or a relative maximum at $c$.

thm:interior-extremum-theorem.title: Interior Extremum Theorem
thm:interior-extremum-theorem.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an open interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$. If $f$ has a relative extremum at $c$ and $f$ is differentiable at $c$, then \[ f'(c) = 0. \]
thm:interior-extremum-theorem.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an open interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$.
If $f$ has a relative extremum at $c$ and $f$ is differentiable at $c$, then
\[
f'(c) = 0.
\]
thm:interior-extremum-theorem.statement_tex:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an open interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$.
If $f$ has a relative extremum at $c$ and $f$ is differentiable at $c$, then
\[
f'(c) = 0.
\]

thm:necessary-condition-extremum.title: Necessary Condition for an Interior Extremum
thm:necessary-condition-extremum.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an open interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$. If $f$ has a relative extremum at $c$, then either $f'(c)$ does not exist, or \[ f'(c) = 0. \]
thm:necessary-condition-extremum.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an open interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$. If $f$ has a relative extremum at $c$, then either $f'(c)$ does not exist, or
\[
f'(c) = 0.
\]
thm:necessary-condition-extremum.statement_tex:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an open interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$. If $f$ has a relative extremum at $c$, then either $f'(c)$ does not exist, or
\[
f'(c) = 0.
\]

cor:necessary-condition-extremum.title: Necessary Condition for an Extremum
cor:necessary-condition-extremum.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an open interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$. If $f$ has a relative extremum at $c$, then \[ f'(c) = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad f'(c) \text{ does not exist.} \]
cor:necessary-condition-extremum.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an open interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$. If $f$ has a relative extremum at $c$, then
\[
f'(c) = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad f'(c) \text{ does not exist.}
\]
cor:necessary-condition-extremum.statement_tex:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an open interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$. If $f$ has a relative extremum at $c$, then
\[
f'(c) = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad f'(c) \text{ does not exist.}
\]

def:increasing-at-a-point.title: Increasing at a Point
def:increasing-at-a-point.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. We say that $f$ is \emph{increasing at $c$} if there exists $\delta > 0$ such that for all \[ x \in A \cap (c-\delta,c) \quad \text{and} \quad y \in A \cap (c,c+\delta), \] we have $f(x) \leq f(c) \leq f(y)$.
def:increasing-at-a-point.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. We say that $f$ is \emph{increasing at $c$} if there exists $\delta > 0$ such that for all
\[
x \in A \cap (c-\delta,c)
\quad \text{and} \quad
y \in A \cap (c,c+\delta),
\]
we have $f(x) \leq f(c) \leq f(y)$.
def:increasing-at-a-point.statement_tex:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. We say that $f$ is \emph{increasing at $c$} if there exists $\delta > 0$ such that for all
\[
x \in A \cap (c-\delta,c)
\quad \text{and} \quad
y \in A \cap (c,c+\delta),
\]
we have $f(x) \leq f(c) \leq f(y)$.

def:decreasing-at-a-point.title: Decreasing at a Point
def:decreasing-at-a-point.statement_preview: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. We say that $f$ is \emph{decreasing at $c$} if there exists $\delta > 0$ such that for all \[ x \in A \cap (c-\delta,c) \quad \text{and} \quad y \in A \cap (c,c+\delta), \] we have $f(x) \geq f(c) \geq f(y)$.
def:decreasing-at-a-point.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. We say that $f$ is \emph{decreasing at $c$} if there exists $\delta > 0$ such that for all
\[
x \in A \cap (c-\delta,c)
\quad \text{and} \quad
y \in A \cap (c,c+\delta),
\]
we have $f(x) \geq f(c) \geq f(y)$.
def:decreasing-at-a-point.statement_tex:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in A$. We say that $f$ is \emph{decreasing at $c$} if there exists $\delta > 0$ such that for all
\[
x \in A \cap (c-\delta,c)
\quad \text{and} \quad
y \in A \cap (c,c+\delta),
\]
we have $f(x) \geq f(c) \geq f(y)$.

thm:zero-derivative-implies-constant.title: Zero Derivative Implies Constant
thm:zero-derivative-implies-constant.statement_preview: Let $I := [a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be a closed interval, and let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $I$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$. If \[ f'(x) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } x \in (a,b), \] then $f$ is constant on $I$.
thm:zero-derivative-implies-constant.statement_display:
Let $I := [a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be a closed interval, and let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $I$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$. If
\[
f'(x) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } x \in (a,b),
\]
then $f$ is constant on $I$.
thm:zero-derivative-implies-constant.statement_tex:
Let $I := [a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be a closed interval, and let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $I$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$. If
\[
f'(x) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } x \in (a,b),
\]
then $f$ is constant on $I$.

cor:equal-derivatives-constant-differenc.title: Equal Derivatives Imply Difference Is Constant
cor:equal-derivatives-constant-differenc.statement_preview: Let $I := [a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, and let $f, g : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $I$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$. If \[ f'(x) = g'(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in (a,b), \] then there exists $C \in \mathbb{R}$ such that $f(x) = g(x) + C$ for all $x \in I$.
cor:equal-derivatives-constant-differenc.statement_display:
Let $I := [a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, and let $f, g : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $I$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$. If
\[
f'(x) = g'(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in (a,b),
\]
then there exists $C \in \mathbb{R}$ such that $f(x) = g(x) + C$ for all $x \in I$.
cor:equal-derivatives-constant-differenc.statement_tex:
Let $I := [a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, and let $f, g : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $I$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$. If
\[
f'(x) = g'(x) \qquad \text{for all } x \in (a,b),
\]
then there exists $C \in \mathbb{R}$ such that $f(x) = g(x) + C$ for all $x \in I$.

thm:nondecreasing-iff-nonneg-derivative.title: Nondecreasing Iff Nonnegative Derivative
thm:nondecreasing-iff-nonneg-derivative.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, and let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be differentiable on $I$. Then $f$ is nondecreasing on $I$ if and only if \[ f'(x) \geq 0 \qquad \text{for all } x \in I. \]
thm:nondecreasing-iff-nonneg-derivative.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, and let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be differentiable on $I$. Then $f$ is nondecreasing on $I$ if and only if
\[
f'(x) \geq 0 \qquad \text{for all } x \in I.
\]
thm:nondecreasing-iff-nonneg-derivative.statement_tex:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, and let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be differentiable on $I$. Then $f$ is nondecreasing on $I$ if and only if
\[
f'(x) \geq 0 \qquad \text{for all } x \in I.
\]

thm:nonincreasing-iff-nonpos-derivative.title: Nonincreasing Iff Nonpositive Derivative
thm:nonincreasing-iff-nonpos-derivative.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, and let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be differentiable on $I$. Then $f$ is nonincreasing on $I$ if and only if \[ f'(x) \leq 0 \qquad \text{for all } x \in I. \]
thm:nonincreasing-iff-nonpos-derivative.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, and let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be differentiable on $I$. Then $f$ is nonincreasing on $I$ if and only if
\[
f'(x) \leq 0 \qquad \text{for all } x \in I.
\]
thm:nonincreasing-iff-nonpos-derivative.statement_tex:

lem:positive-derivative-implies-locally-.title: Positive Derivative Implies Locally Increasing
lem:positive-derivative-implies-locally-.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c \in I$, and suppose that $f$ is differentiable at $c$. If $f'(c) > 0$, then there exists $\delta > 0$ such that \[ f(x) < f(c) \quad \text{for all } x \in I \text{ with } c - \delta < x < c, \] and \[ f(x) > f(c) \quad \text{for all } x \in I \text{ with } c < x < c + \delta. \]
lem:positive-derivative-implies-locally-.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c \in I$, and suppose that $f$ is differentiable at $c$. If $f'(c) > 0$, then there exists $\delta > 0$ such that
\[
f(x) < f(c) \quad \text{for all } x \in I \text{ with } c - \delta < x < c,
\]
and
\[
f(x) > f(c) \quad \text{for all } x \in I \text{ with } c < x < c + \delta.
\]
lem:positive-derivative-implies-locally-.statement_tex:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c \in I$, and suppose that $f$ is differentiable at $c$. If $f'(c) > 0$, then there exists $\delta > 0$ such that
\[
f(x) < f(c) \quad \text{for all } x \in I \text{ with } c - \delta < x < c,
\]
and
\[
f(x) > f(c) \quad \text{for all } x \in I \text{ with } c < x < c + \delta.
\]

lem:negative-derivative-implies-locally-.title: Negative Derivative Implies Locally Decreasing
lem:negative-derivative-implies-locally-.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c \in I$, and suppose that $f$ is differentiable at $c$. If $f'(c) < 0$, then there exists $\delta > 0$ such that \[ f(x) > f(c) \quad \text{for all } x \in I \text{ with } c - \delta < x < c, \] and \[ f(x) < f(c) \quad \text{for all } x \in I \text{ with } c < x < c + \delta. \]
lem:negative-derivative-implies-locally-.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c \in I$, and suppose that $f$ is differentiable at $c$. If $f'(c) < 0$, then there exists $\delta > 0$ such that
\[
f(x) > f(c) \quad \text{for all } x \in I \text{ with } c - \delta < x < c,
\]
and
\[
f(x) < f(c) \quad \text{for all } x \in I \text{ with } c < x < c + \delta.
\]
lem:negative-derivative-implies-locally-.statement_tex:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c \in I$, and suppose that $f$ is differentiable at $c$. If $f'(c) < 0$, then there exists $\delta > 0$ such that
\[
f(x) > f(c) \quad \text{for all } x \in I \text{ with } c - \delta < x < c,
\]
and
\[
f(x) < f(c) \quad \text{for all } x \in I \text{ with } c < x < c + \delta.
\]

thm:first-derivative-test-maximum.title: First Derivative Test: Relative Maximum
thm:first-derivative-test-maximum.statement_preview: Let $I := [a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $I$, and let $c \in (a,b)$. Suppose $f$ is differentiable on $(a,c)$ and $(c,b)$. If there exists $\delta > 0$ such that $(c-\delta,c+\delta) \subseteq I$ and \[ f'(x) \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } x \in (c-\delta,c), \qquad f'(x) \leq 0 \quad \text{for all } x \in (c,c+\delta...
thm:first-derivative-test-maximum.statement_display:
Let $I := [a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous on $I$, and let $c \in (a,b)$. Suppose $f$ is differentiable on $(a,c)$ and $(c,b)$. If there exists $\delta > 0$ such that $(c-\delta,c+\delta) \subseteq I$ and
\[
f'(x) \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } x \in (c-\delta,c),
\qquad
f'(x) \leq 0 \quad \text{for all } x \in (c,c+\delta),
\]
then $f$ has a relative maximum at $c$.

thm:darboux.title: Darboux's Theorem
thm:darboux.statement_preview: Let $I := [a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be differentiable on $I$. If $k$ is a real number strictly between $f'(a)$ and $f'(b)$, then there exists $c \in (a,b)$ such that \[ f'(c) = k. \]
thm:darboux.statement_display:
Let $I := [a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be differentiable on $I$. If $k$ is a real number strictly between $f'(a)$ and $f'(b)$, then there exists $c \in (a,b)$ such that
\[
f'(c) = k.
\]
thm:darboux.statement_tex:
Let $I := [a,b] \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be differentiable on $I$. If $k$ is a real number strictly between $f'(a)$ and $f'(b)$, then there exists $c \in (a,b)$ such that
\[
f'(c) = k.
\]

thm:rolles-theorem.title: Rolle's Theorem
thm:rolles-theorem.statement_preview: Let $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a < b$. Suppose that $f : [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous on $[a,b]$, differentiable on $(a,b)$, and satisfies $f(a) = f(b)$. Then there exists $c \in (a,b)$ such that \[ f'(c) = 0. \]
thm:rolles-theorem.statement_display:
Let $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a < b$. Suppose that $f : [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous on $[a,b]$, differentiable on $(a,b)$, and satisfies $f(a) = f(b)$. Then there exists $c \in (a,b)$ such that
\[
f'(c) = 0.
\]
thm:rolles-theorem.statement_tex:
Let $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a < b$. Suppose that $f : [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous on $[a,b]$, differentiable on $(a,b)$, and satisfies $f(a) = f(b)$. Then there exists $c \in (a,b)$ such that
\[
f'(c) = 0.
\]

thm:mean-value-theorem.title: Mean Value Theorem
thm:mean-value-theorem.statement_preview: Let $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a < b$. Suppose that $f : [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous on $[a,b]$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$. Then there exists $c \in (a,b)$ such that \[ f'(c) = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}. \]
thm:mean-value-theorem.statement_display:
Let $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a < b$. Suppose that $f : [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous on $[a,b]$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$. Then there exists $c \in (a,b)$ such that
\[
f'(c) = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}.
\]
thm:mean-value-theorem.statement_tex:
Let $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $a < b$. Suppose that $f : [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous on $[a,b]$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$. Then there exists $c \in (a,b)$ such that
\[
f'(c) = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}.
\]

def:left-hand-derivative.title: Left-Hand Derivative
def:left-hand-derivative.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$ be such that $c$ is a left-hand limit point of $I$. We say that $f$ has a \emph{left-hand derivative} at $c$ if the limit \[ \lim_{x \to c^-} \frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c} \] exists. In that case, the value of this limit is denoted $f'_-(c)$: \[ f'_-(c) := \lim_{x \to c^-...
def:left-hand-derivative.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$ be such that $c$ is a left-hand limit point of $I$. We say that $f$ has a \emph{left-hand derivative} at $c$ if the limit
\[
\lim_{x \to c^-} \frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c}
\]
exists. In that case, the value of this limit is denoted $f'_-(c)$:
\[
f'_-(c) := \lim_{x \to c^-} \frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c}.
\]
def:left-hand-derivative.statement_tex:

\[
\lim_{x \to c^-} \frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c}
\]
exists. In that case, the value of this limit is denoted $f'_-(c)$:
\[
f'_-(c) := \lim_{x \to c^-} \frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c}.
\]

In that case, the value of this limit is denoted $f'_+(c)$: \[ f'_+(c) := \lim_{x \to c...
def:right-hand-derivative.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in I$ be such that $c$ is a right-hand limit point of $I$. We say that $f$ has a \emph{right-hand derivative} at $c$ if the limit
\[
\lim_{x \to c^+} \frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c}
\]
exists. In that case, the value of this limit is denoted $f'_+(c)$:
\[
f'_+(c) := \lim_{x \to c^+} \frac{f(x) - f(c)}{x - c}.

thm:differentiability-and-one-sided-deri.title: Differentiability and One-Sided Derivatives
thm:differentiability-and-one-sided-deri.statement_preview: Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an open interval and let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$. Then $f$ is differentiable at $c \in I$ if and only if both one-sided derivatives $f'_-(c)$ and $f'_+(c)$ exist and are equal. In that case, \[ f'(c) = f'_-(c) = f'_+(c). \]
thm:differentiability-and-one-sided-deri.statement_display:
Let $I \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be an open interval and let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$. Then $f$ is differentiable at $c \in I$ if and only if both one-sided derivatives $f'_-(c)$ and $f'_+(c)$ exist and are equal. In that case,
\[
f'(c) = f'_-(c) = f'_+(c).
\]
thm:differentiability-and-one-sided-deri.statement_tex:

\[
f'(c) = f'_-(c) = f'_+(c).
\]

def:secant-line.title: Secant Line
def:secant-line.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $x_1, x_2 \in A$ with $x_1 \neq x_2$. The \emph{secant line} determined by the points $(x_1, f(x_1))$ and $(x_2, f(x_2))$ is the unique line passing through these two points. Its slope is \[ m_{\mathrm{sec}}(x_1, x_2) := \frac{f(x_2) - f(x_1)}{x_2 - x_1}. \]
def:secant-line.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $x_1, x_2 \in A$ with $x_1 \neq x_2$. The \emph{secant line} determined by the points $(x_1, f(x_1))$ and $(x_2, f(x_2))$ is the unique line passing through these two points. Its slope is
\[
m_{\mathrm{sec}}(x_1, x_2) := \frac{f(x_2) - f(x_1)}{x_2 - x_1}.
\]
def:secant-line.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $x_1, x_2 \in A$ with $x_1 \neq x_2$. The \emph{secant line} determined by the points $(x_1, f(x_1))$ and $(x_2, f(x_2))$ is the unique line passing through these two points. Its slope is
\[
m_{\mathrm{sec}}(x_1, x_2) := \frac{f(x_2) - f(x_1)}{x_2 - x_1}.
\]

def:tangent-line.title: Tangent Line
def:tangent-line.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c \in A$, and suppose that $f$ is differentiable at $c$. The \emph{tangent line} to the graph of $f$ at the point $(c, f(c))$ is the line through $(c, f(c))$ with slope $f'(c)$. Its equation is \[ y = f(c) + f'(c)(x - c). \]
def:tangent-line.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c \in A$, and suppose that $f$ is differentiable at $c$. The \emph{tangent line} to the graph of $f$ at the point $(c, f(c))$ is the line through $(c, f(c))$ with slope $f'(c)$. Its equation is
\[
y = f(c) + f'(c)(x - c).
\]
def:tangent-line.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c \in A$, and suppose that $f$ is differentiable at $c$. The \emph{tangent line} to the graph of $f$ at the point $(c, f(c))$ is the line through $(c, f(c))$ with slope $f'(c)$. Its equation is
\[
y = f(c) + f'(c)(x - c).
\]

def:taylor-polynomial-at-a-point.title: Taylor Polynomial at a Point
def:taylor-polynomial-at-a-point.statement_preview: Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $a\in I$, let $n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ have derivatives $f^{(k)}(a)$ for $0\le k\le n$. The \textbf{Taylor polynomial of order} $n$ for $f$ at $a$ is \[ T_{n,a}f(x) := \sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{f^{(k)}(a)}{k!}(x-a)^k. \]
def:taylor-polynomial-at-a-point.statement_display:
Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $a\in I$, let
$n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ have derivatives
$f^{(k)}(a)$ for $0\le k\le n$. The \textbf{Taylor polynomial of order}
$n$ for $f$ at $a$ is
\[
  T_{n,a}f(x)
  :=
  \sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{f^{(k)}(a)}{k!}(x-a)^k.
\]
def:taylor-polynomial-at-a-point.statement_tex:
Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $a\in I$, let
$n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ have derivatives
$f^{(k)}(a)$ for $0\le k\le n$. The \textbf{Taylor polynomial of order}
$n$ for $f$ at $a$ is
\[
  T_{n,a}f(x)
  :=
  \sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{f^{(k)}(a)}{k!}(x-a)^k.
\]

def:taylor-remainder.title: Taylor Remainder
def:taylor-remainder.statement_preview: Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $a\in I$, let $n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ have a Taylor polynomial $T_{n,a}f$. The \textbf{Taylor remainder of order} $n$ at $a$ is the function $R_{n,a}f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ defined by \[ R_{n,a}f(x):=f(x)-T_{n,a}f(x). \]
def:taylor-remainder.statement_display:
Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $a\in I$, let
$n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ have a Taylor polynomial
$T_{n,a}f$. The \textbf{Taylor remainder of order} $n$ at $a$ is the
function $R_{n,a}f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ defined by
\[
  R_{n,a}f(x):=f(x)-T_{n,a}f(x).
\]
def:taylor-remainder.statement_tex:
Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $a\in I$, let
$n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ have a Taylor polynomial
$T_{n,a}f$. The \textbf{Taylor remainder of order} $n$ at $a$ is the
function $R_{n,a}f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ defined by
\[

\]

def:maclaurin-polynomial.title: Maclaurin Polynomial
def:maclaurin-polynomial.statement_preview: Let $n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let $f$ be defined on an interval containing $0$ with derivatives $f^{(k)}(0)$ for $0\le k\le n$. The \textbf{Maclaurin polynomial of order} $n$ for $f$ is \[ T_{n,0}f(x) := \sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{f^{(k)}(0)}{k!}x^k. \]
def:maclaurin-polynomial.statement_display:
Let $n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let $f$ be defined on an interval containing
$0$ with derivatives $f^{(k)}(0)$ for $0\le k\le n$. The
\textbf{Maclaurin polynomial of order} $n$ for $f$ is
\[
  T_{n,0}f(x)
  :=
  \sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{f^{(k)}(0)}{k!}x^k.
\]
def:maclaurin-polynomial.statement_tex:
Let $n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let $f$ be defined on an interval containing
$0$ with derivatives $f^{(k)}(0)$ for $0\le k\le n$. The
\textbf{Maclaurin polynomial of order} $n$ for $f$ is
\[
  T_{n,0}f(x)
  :=
  \sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{f^{(k)}(0)}{k!}x^k.
\]

thm:taylor-theorem-lagrange-remainder.title: Taylor's Theorem with Lagrange Remainder
thm:taylor-theorem-lagrange-remainder.statement_preview: Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, let $n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$. Suppose that $f,f',\ldots,f^{(n)}$ are continuous on $[a,b]$ and that $f^{(n+1)}$ exists on $(a,b)$. Then, for every $x\in(a,b)$, there exists $c$ strictly between $a$ and $x$ such that \[ f(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{f^{(k)}(a)}{k!}(x-a)^k + \frac{f^{(n+1)}(c)}{(n+1)!}(x...
thm:taylor-theorem-lagrange-remainder.statement_display:
Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, let $n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let
$f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$. Suppose that $f,f',\ldots,f^{(n)}$ are continuous
on $[a,b]$ and that $f^{(n+1)}$ exists on $(a,b)$. Then, for every
$x\in(a,b)$, there exists $c$ strictly between $a$ and $x$ such that
\[
  f(x)
  =
  \sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{f^{(k)}(a)}{k!}(x-a)^k
  +
  \frac{f^{(n+1)}(c)}{(n+1)!}(x-a)^{n+1}.
\]
thm:taylor-theorem-lagrange-remainder.statement_tex:
Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a<b$, let $n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let
$f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$. Suppose that $f,f',\ldots,f^{(n)}$ are continuous
on $[a,b]$ and that $f^{(n+1)}$ exists on $(a,b)$. Then, for every
$x\in(a,b)$, there exists $c$ strictly between $a$ and $x$ such that
\[
  f(x)
  =
  \sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{f^{(k)}(a)}{k!}(x-a)^k
  +
  \frac{f^{(n+1)}(c)}{(n+1)!}(x-a)^{n+1}.
\]

cor:taylor-expansion-peano-remainder.title: Taylor Expansion with Peano Remainder
cor:taylor-expansion-peano-remainder.statement_preview: Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $a\in I$, let $n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ have derivatives up to order $n$ in a neighbourhood of $a$. If $f^{(n)}$ is continuous at $a$, then \[ f(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{f^{(k)}(a)}{k!}(x-a)^k + o\bigl((x-a)^n\bigr) \qquad\text{as }x\to a. \]
cor:taylor-expansion-peano-remainder.statement_display:
Let $I\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an interval, let $a\in I$, let
$n\in\mathbb{N}$, and let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ have derivatives up to order
$n$ in a neighbourhood of $a$. If $f^{(n)}$ is continuous at $a$, then
\[
  f(x)
  =
  \sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{f^{(k)}(a)}{k!}(x-a)^k
  +
  o\bigl((x-a)^n\bigr)
  \qquad\text{as }x\to a.
\]

\]

def:hyperbolic.title: Hyperbolic Functions
def:hyperbolic.statement_preview: For all $x \in \mathbb{R}$: \[ \cosh x \;:=\; \frac{e^x + e^{-x}}{2}, \qquad \sinh x \;:=\; \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{2}, \qquad \tanh x \;:=\; \frac{\sinh x}{\cosh x}. \] Additional: $\operatorname{sech} x := 1/\cosh x$,\; $\operatorname{csch} x := 1/\sinh x$,\; $\coth x := \cosh x / \sinh x$.
def:hyperbolic.statement_display:
For all $x \in \mathbb{R}$:
\[
  \cosh x \;:=\; \frac{e^x + e^{-x}}{2},
  \qquad
  \sinh x \;:=\; \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{2},
  \qquad
  \tanh x \;:=\; \frac{\sinh x}{\cosh x}.
\]
Additional: $\operatorname{sech} x := 1/\cosh x$,\;
$\operatorname{csch} x := 1/\sinh x$,\;
$\coth x := \cosh x / \sinh x$.
def:hyperbolic.statement_tex:
For all $x \in \mathbb{R}$:
\[
  \cosh x \;:=\; \frac{e^x + e^{-x}}{2},
  \qquad
  \sinh x \;:=\; \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{2},
  \qquad
  \tanh x \;:=\; \frac{\sinh x}{\cosh x}.
\]
Additional: $\operatorname{sech} x := 1/\cosh x$,\;
$\operatorname{csch} x := 1/\sinh x$,\;
$\coth x := \cosh x / \sinh x$.

prop:hyperbolic-addition.title: Addition Formulas for Hyperbolic Functions
prop:hyperbolic-addition.statement_preview: For all $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$: \[ \sinh(x+y) = \sinh x \cosh y + \cosh x \sinh y, \qquad \cosh(x+y) = \cosh x \cosh y + \sinh x \sinh y. \]
prop:hyperbolic-addition.statement_display:
For all $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$:
\[
  \sinh(x+y) = \sinh x \cosh y + \cosh x \sinh y,
  \qquad
  \cosh(x+y) = \cosh x \cosh y + \sinh x \sinh y.
\]
prop:hyperbolic-addition.statement_tex:
For all $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$:
\[
  \sinh(x+y) = \sinh x \cosh y + \cosh x \sinh y,
  \qquad
  \cosh(x+y) = \cosh x \cosh y + \sinh x \sinh y.
\]

def:inverse-hyperbolic.title: Inverse Hyperbolic Functions
def:inverse-hyperbolic.statement_preview: Since $\sinh$ is strictly increasing and bijective $\mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$, its inverse $\operatorname{arcsinh}$ is defined on all of $\mathbb{R}$. Since $\cosh$ is strictly increasing on $[0,\infty)$ with range $[1,\infty)$, its inverse $\operatorname{arccosh}$ is defined on $[1,\infty)$. Since $\tanh$ is strictly increasing with range $(-1,1)$, $\oper...
def:inverse-hyperbolic.statement_display:
Since $\sinh$ is strictly increasing and bijective $\mathbb{R} \to
\mathbb{R}$, its inverse $\operatorname{arcsinh}$ is defined on all
of $\mathbb{R}$. Since $\cosh$ is strictly increasing on
$[0,\infty)$ with range $[1,\infty)$, its inverse
$\operatorname{arccosh}$ is defined on $[1,\infty)$. Since $\tanh$
is strictly increasing with range $(-1,1)$, $\operatorname{arctanh}$
is defined on $(-1,1)$.

Explicit logarithmic formulas:
\begin{align*}
  \operatorname{arcsinh} x
  &= \ln\!\left(x + \sqrt{x^2+1}\right),
  \quad x \in \mathbb{R}. \\
  \operatorname{arccosh} x
  &= \ln\!\left(x + \sqrt{x^2-1}\right),
  \quad x \geq 1. \\
  \operatorname{arctanh} x
  &= \tfrac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right),
  \quad |x| < 1.
\end{align*}
def:inverse-hyperbolic.statement_tex:
Since $\sinh$ is strictly increasing and bijective $\mathbb{R} \to
\mathbb{R}$, its inverse $\operatorname{arcsinh}$ is defined on all
of $\mathbb{R}$. Since $\cosh$ is strictly increasing on
$[0,\infty)$ with range $[1,\infty)$, its inverse
$\operatorname{arccosh}$ is defined on $[1,\infty)$. Since $\tanh$
is strictly increasing with range $(-1,1)$, $\operatorname{arctanh}$
is defined on $(-1,1)$.

Explicit logarithmic formulas:
\begin{align*}
  \operatorname{arcsinh} x
  &= \ln\!\left(x + \sqrt{x^2+1}\right),
  \quad x \in \mathbb{R}. \\
  \operatorname{arccosh} x
  &= \ln\!\left(x + \sqrt{x^2-1}\right),
  \quad x \geq 1. \\
  \operatorname{arctanh} x
  &= \tfrac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right),
  \quad |x| < 1.
\end{align*}

def:natural-logarithm.title: Natural Logarithm
def:natural-logarithm.statement_preview: The \textbf{natural logarithm} $\ln : (0,\infty) \to \mathbb{R}$ is the inverse of the exponential function: for $x > 0$, \[ \ln x \;:=\; \text{the unique } y \in \mathbb{R} \text{ such that } e^y = x. \] Equivalently, $\ln x$ is defined by the integral \[ \ln x \;:=\; \int_1^x \frac{1}{t}\, dt. \]
def:natural-logarithm.statement_display:
The \textbf{natural logarithm} $\ln : (0,\infty) \to \mathbb{R}$ is
the inverse of the exponential function: for $x > 0$,
\[
  \ln x \;:=\; \text{the unique } y \in \mathbb{R}
  \text{ such that } e^y = x.
\]
Equivalently, $\ln x$ is defined by the integral
\[
  \ln x \;:=\; \int_1^x \frac{1}{t}\, dt.
\]
def:natural-logarithm.statement_tex:

def:power-function.title: Power Function
def:power-function.statement_preview: Let $n \in \mathbb{N}$. The \textbf{integer power function} is $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$, $f(x) := x^n$. For $r \in \mathbb{R}$ and $x > 0$, the \textbf{real power} is defined by \[ x^r \;:=\; e^{r \ln x}. \]
def:power-function.statement_display:
Let $n \in \mathbb{N}$. The \textbf{integer power function} is
$f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$, $f(x) := x^n$. For $r \in \mathbb{R}$
and $x > 0$, the \textbf{real power} is defined by
\[
  x^r \;:=\; e^{r \ln x}.
\]

def:number-e.title: The Number $e$
def:number-e.statement_preview: The \textbf{Euler number} is \[ e \;:=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!} \;=\; \lim_{n \to \infty} \!\left(1 + \frac{1}{n}\right)^{\!n} \;\approx\; 2.71828\ldots \] Both expressions define the same real number.
def:number-e.statement_display:
The \textbf{Euler number} is
\[
  e \;:=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!}
  \;=\; \lim_{n \to \infty} \!\left(1 + \frac{1}{n}\right)^{\!n}
  \;\approx\; 2.71828\ldots
\]
Both expressions define the same real number.
