def:exponential.title: Exponential Function
def:exponential.statement_preview: The \textbf{exponential function} $\exp : \mathbb{R} \to (0,\infty)$ is defined by the absolutely convergent power series \[ e^x \;:=\; \exp(x) \;:=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{x^n}{n!}, \qquad x \in \mathbb{R}. \]
def:exponential.statement_display:
The \textbf{exponential function} $\exp : \mathbb{R} \to (0,\infty)$
is defined by the absolutely convergent power series
\[
  e^x \;:=\; \exp(x) \;:=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{x^n}{n!},
  \qquad x \in \mathbb{R}.
\]
def:exponential.statement_tex:
The \textbf{exponential function} $\exp : \mathbb{R} \to (0,\infty)$
is defined by the absolutely convergent power series
\[
  e^x \;:=\; \exp(x) \;:=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{x^n}{n!},
  \qquad x \in \mathbb{R}.
\]

def:sine-cosine.title: Sine and Cosine via Power Series
def:sine-cosine.statement_preview: The \textbf{sine} and \textbf{cosine} functions are defined for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$ by the absolutely convergent power series \[ \sin x \;:=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n x^{2n+1}}{(2n+1)!} \;=\; x - \frac{x^3}{3!} + \frac{x^5}{5!} - \cdots \] \[ \cos x \;:=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n x^{2n}}{(2n)!} \;=\; 1 - \frac{x^2}{2!} + \frac{x^4}{4!}...

prop:trig-addition-formulas.title: Addition Formulas
prop:trig-addition-formulas.statement_preview: For all $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$: \[ \sin(x+y) = \sin x \cos y + \cos x \sin y, \qquad \cos(x+y) = \cos x \cos y - \sin x \sin y. \]
prop:trig-addition-formulas.statement_display:
For all $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$:
\[
  \sin(x+y) = \sin x \cos y + \cos x \sin y,
  \qquad
  \cos(x+y) = \cos x \cos y - \sin x \sin y.
\]
prop:trig-addition-formulas.statement_tex:
For all $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$:
\[
  \sin(x+y) = \sin x \cos y + \cos x \sin y,
  \qquad
  \cos(x+y) = \cos x \cos y - \sin x \sin y.
\]

def:other-trig.title: Tangent, Cotangent, Secant, Cosecant
def:other-trig.statement_preview: \[ \tan x := \frac{\sin x}{\cos x},\quad \cot x := \frac{\cos x}{\sin x},\quad \sec x := \frac{1}{\cos x},\quad \csc x := \frac{1}{\sin x}. \] $\tan x$ and $\sec x$ are defined where $\cos x \neq 0$, i.e., $x \neq \pi/2 + k\pi$ for $k \in \mathbb{Z}$.
def:other-trig.statement_display:
\[
  \tan x := \frac{\sin x}{\cos x},\quad
  \cot x := \frac{\cos x}{\sin x},\quad
  \sec x := \frac{1}{\cos x},\quad
  \csc x := \frac{1}{\sin x}.
\]
$\tan x$ and $\sec x$ are defined where $\cos x \neq 0$,
i.e., $x \neq \pi/2 + k\pi$ for $k \in \mathbb{Z}$.

def:inverse-trig.title: Inverse Trigonometric Functions
def:inverse-trig.statement_preview: \begin{itemize} \item $\arcsin : [-1,1] \to [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ is the inverse of $\sin\big|_{[-\pi/2,\pi/2]}$. \item $\arccos : [-1,1] \to [0,\pi]$ is the inverse of $\cos\big|_{[0,\pi]}$. \item $\arctan : \mathbb{R} \to (-\pi/2, \pi/2)$ is the inverse of $\tan\big|_{(-\pi/2,\pi/2)}$. \end{itemize}
def:inverse-trig.statement_display:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $\arcsin : [-1,1] \to [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ is the inverse of
    $\sin\big|_{[-\pi/2,\pi/2]}$.
  \item $\arccos : [-1,1] \to [0,\pi]$ is the inverse of
    $\cos\big|_{[0,\pi]}$.
  \item $\arctan : \mathbb{R} \to (-\pi/2, \pi/2)$ is the inverse
    of $\tan\big|_{(-\pi/2,\pi/2)}$.
\end{itemize}
def:inverse-trig.statement_tex:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $\arcsin : [-1,1] \to [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ is the inverse of
    $\sin\big|_{[-\pi/2,\pi/2]}$.
  \item $\arccos : [-1,1] \to [0,\pi]$ is the inverse of

\end{itemize}

prop:composition-injective.title: Composition of Injections Is Injective
prop:composition-injective.statement_preview: Let $f:A\to B$ and let $g:B\to C$. If $f$ is injective and $g$ is injective, then $g\circ f:A\to C$ is injective.
prop:composition-injective.statement_display:
Let $f:A\to B$ and let $g:B\to C$. If $f$ is injective and $g$ is
injective, then $g\circ f:A\to C$ is injective.
prop:composition-injective.statement_tex:
Let $f:A\to B$ and let $g:B\to C$. If $f$ is injective and $g$ is
injective, then $g\circ f:A\to C$ is injective.

cor:composition-bijective.title: Composition of Bijections Is Bijective
cor:composition-bijective.statement_preview: Let $f:A\to B$ and let $g:B\to C$. If $f$ is bijective and $g$ is bijective, then $g\circ f:A\to C$ is bijective.

prop:inverse-bijection.title: Inverse of a Bijection Is a Bijection

prop:preimage-union-intersection.title: Preimage Distributes over Union and Intersection
prop:preimage-union-intersection.statement_preview: Let $f:A\to B$, $S,T\subseteq B$. Then: \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item $f^{-1}(S\cup T)=f^{-1}(S)\cup f^{-1}(T)$. \item $f^{-1}(S\cap T)=f^{-1}(S)\cap f^{-1}(T)$. \item $f^{-1}(S^c)=(f^{-1}(S))^c$. \end{enumerate}
prop:preimage-union-intersection.statement_display:
Let $f:A\to B$, $S,T\subseteq B$. Then:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item $f^{-1}(S\cup T)=f^{-1}(S)\cup f^{-1}(T)$.
  \item $f^{-1}(S\cap T)=f^{-1}(S)\cap f^{-1}(T)$.
  \item $f^{-1}(S^c)=(f^{-1}(S))^c$.
\end{enumerate}
prop:preimage-union-intersection.statement_tex:
Let $f:A\to B$, $S,T\subseteq B$. Then:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item $f^{-1}(S\cup T)=f^{-1}(S)\cup f^{-1}(T)$.
  \item $f^{-1}(S\cap T)=f^{-1}(S)\cap f^{-1}(T)$.
  \item $f^{-1}(S^c)=(f^{-1}(S))^c$.
\end{enumerate}

thm:limit-sum.title: Sum Rule for Function Limits
thm:limit-sum.statement_preview: Let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. If $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ and $\lim_{x\to c}g(x)=M$, then \[ \lim_{x\to c}(f+g)(x)=L+M. \]
thm:limit-sum.statement_display:
Let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. If
$\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ and $\lim_{x\to c}g(x)=M$, then
\[
  \lim_{x\to c}(f+g)(x)=L+M.
\]
thm:limit-sum.statement_tex:
Let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. If
$\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ and $\lim_{x\to c}g(x)=M$, then
\[
  \lim_{x\to c}(f+g)(x)=L+M.
\]

thm:limit-difference.title: Difference Rule for Function Limits
thm:limit-difference.statement_preview: Let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. If $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ and $\lim_{x\to c}g(x)=M$, then \[ \lim_{x\to c}(f-g)(x)=L-M. \]
thm:limit-difference.statement_display:
Let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. If
$\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ and $\lim_{x\to c}g(x)=M$, then
\[
  \lim_{x\to c}(f-g)(x)=L-M.
\]
thm:limit-difference.statement_tex:
Let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. If

\[
  \lim_{x\to c}(f-g)(x)=L-M.
\]

thm:limit-scalar-multiple.title: Scalar Multiple Rule for Function Limits
thm:limit-scalar-multiple.statement_preview: Let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$, and let $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$. If $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, then \[ \lim_{x\to c}(\alpha f)(x)=\alpha L. \]
thm:limit-scalar-multiple.statement_display:
Let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$, and let
$\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$. If $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, then
\[
  \lim_{x\to c}(\alpha f)(x)=\alpha L.
\]
thm:limit-scalar-multiple.statement_tex:
Let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$, and let
$\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$. If $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, then
\[
  \lim_{x\to c}(\alpha f)(x)=\alpha L.
\]

thm:limit-product.title: Product Rule for Function Limits
thm:limit-product.statement_preview: Let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. If $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ and $\lim_{x\to c}g(x)=M$, then \[ \lim_{x\to c}(fg)(x)=LM. \]
thm:limit-product.statement_display:
Let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. If
$\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ and $\lim_{x\to c}g(x)=M$, then
\[
  \lim_{x\to c}(fg)(x)=LM.
\]
thm:limit-product.statement_tex:
Let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. If
$\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ and $\lim_{x\to c}g(x)=M$, then
\[
  \lim_{x\to c}(fg)(x)=LM.
\]

thm:limit-quotient.title: Quotient Rule for Function Limits
thm:limit-quotient.statement_preview: Let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. If $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, $\lim_{x\to c}g(x)=M$, and $M\neq 0$, then there exists $\delta_0>0$ such that $g(x)\neq 0$ whenever $x\in A$ and $0<|x-c|<\delta_0$, and \[ \lim_{x\to c}\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}=\frac{L}{M}. \]
thm:limit-quotient.statement_display:
Let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. If
$\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, $\lim_{x\to c}g(x)=M$, and $M\neq 0$, then
there exists $\delta_0>0$ such that $g(x)\neq 0$ whenever $x\in A$ and
$0<|x-c|<\delta_0$, and
\[
  \lim_{x\to c}\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}=\frac{L}{M}.
\]
thm:limit-quotient.statement_tex:
Let $f,g:A\to\mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. If
$\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, $\lim_{x\to c}g(x)=M$, and $M\neq 0$, then
there exists $\delta_0>0$ such that $g(x)\neq 0$ whenever $x\in A$ and
$0<|x-c|<\delta_0$, and
\[
  \lim_{x\to c}\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}=\frac{L}{M}.
\]

def:eps-close-function.title: $\varepsilon$-Closeness of a Function to a Value
def:eps-close-function.statement_preview: Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, let $L \in \mathbb{R}$, and let $\varepsilon > 0$. The function $f$ is \textbf{$\varepsilon$-close to $L$} if $|f(x)-L|\leq\varepsilon$ for all $x\in X$.
def:eps-close-function.statement_display:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, let
$L \in \mathbb{R}$, and let $\varepsilon > 0$. The function $f$ is
\textbf{$\varepsilon$-close to $L$} if $|f(x)-L|\leq\varepsilon$ for
all $x\in X$.
def:eps-close-function.statement_tex:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, let
$L \in \mathbb{R}$, and let $\varepsilon > 0$. The function $f$ is
\textbf{$\varepsilon$-close to $L$} if $|f(x)-L|\leq\varepsilon$ for
all $x\in X$.

def:delta-close-function.title: Local $\delta$-Closeness Near a Point
def:delta-close-function.statement_preview: Let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, let $L \in \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be adherent to $X$, and let $\varepsilon, \delta > 0$. The function $f$ is \textbf{$\varepsilon$-close to $L$ near $x_0$ within $\delta$} if \[ \forall x \in X\;\bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta\Rightarrow |f(x)-L|\leq\varepsilon\bigr). \]
def:delta-close-function.statement_display:
Let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, let $L \in \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be
adherent to $X$, and let $\varepsilon, \delta > 0$. The function $f$
is \textbf{$\varepsilon$-close to $L$ near $x_0$ within $\delta$} if
\[
  \forall x \in X\;\bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta\Rightarrow |f(x)-L|\leq\varepsilon\bigr).
\]
def:delta-close-function.statement_tex:
Let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, let $L \in \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be
adherent to $X$, and let $\varepsilon, \delta > 0$. The function $f$
is \textbf{$\varepsilon$-close to $L$ near $x_0$ within $\delta$} if
\[
  \forall x \in X\;\bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta\Rightarrow |f(x)-L|\leq\varepsilon\bigr).
\]

def:limit-function.title: Limit of a Function
def:limit-function.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. A real number $L$ is the \textbf{limit of $f$ at $c$}, written $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$, if \[ \forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists\delta>0\;\forall x\in A\; 0<|x-c|<\delta\Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon. \]
def:limit-function.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
A real number $L$ is the \textbf{limit of $f$ at $c$}, written
$\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$, if
\[
  \forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists\delta>0\;\forall x\in A\;
  0<|x-c|<\delta\Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon.
\]
def:limit-function.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
A real number $L$ is the \textbf{limit of $f$ at $c$}, written
$\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$, if
\[
  \forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists\delta>0\;\forall x\in A\;
  0<|x-c|<\delta\Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon.
\]

def:limit-function-sequential.title: Sequential Definition of Limit
def:limit-function-sequential.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. The limit $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$ in the \textbf{sequential sense} holds if for every sequence $(x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$ with $x_n \to c$, the sequence $(f(x_n))$ converges to $L$.
def:limit-function-sequential.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
The limit $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$ in the \textbf{sequential
sense} holds if for every sequence $(x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$
with $x_n \to c$, the sequence $(f(x_n))$ converges to $L$.
def:limit-function-sequential.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
The limit $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$ in the \textbf{sequential
sense} holds if for every sequence $(x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$
with $x_n \to c$, the sequence $(f(x_n))$ converges to $L$.

def:limit-function-neighbourhood.title: Neighbourhood Form of Limit
def:limit-function-neighbourhood.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. The limit $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ in the \textbf{neighbourhood sense} holds if for every $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood $V_\varepsilon(L)$ there exists a deleted $\delta$-neighbourhood $V_\delta^*(c)$ such that \[ x\in V_\delta^*(c)\cap A \quad\Longrightarrow\quad f(x)\in V_\varepsilon(L). \]
def:limit-function-neighbourhood.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
The limit $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ in the \textbf{neighbourhood
sense} holds if for every $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood
$V_\varepsilon(L)$ there exists a deleted $\delta$-neighbourhood
$V_\delta^*(c)$ such that
\[
  x\in V_\delta^*(c)\cap A
  \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
  f(x)\in V_\varepsilon(L).
\]
def:limit-function-neighbourhood.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
The limit $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ in the \textbf{neighbourhood
sense} holds if for every $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood
$V_\varepsilon(L)$ there exists a deleted $\delta$-neighbourhood
$V_\delta^*(c)$ such that
\[
  x\in V_\delta^*(c)\cap A
  \quad\Longrightarrow\quad

\]

prop:limit-neighbourhood-equiv.title: Neighbourhood Form of the Limit
prop:limit-neighbourhood-equiv.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. Then $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ if and only if for every $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood $V_\varepsilon(L)$ there exists a $\delta$-neighbourhood $V_\delta(c)$ such that $x\in V_\delta^*(c)\cap A$ implies $f(x)\in V_\varepsilon(L)$.
prop:limit-neighbourhood-equiv.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
Then $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ if and only if for every
$\varepsilon$-neighbourhood $V_\varepsilon(L)$ there exists a
$\delta$-neighbourhood $V_\delta(c)$ such that
$x\in V_\delta^*(c)\cap A$ implies $f(x)\in V_\varepsilon(L)$.
prop:limit-neighbourhood-equiv.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
Then $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ if and only if for every
$\varepsilon$-neighbourhood $V_\varepsilon(L)$ there exists a
$\delta$-neighbourhood $V_\delta(c)$ such that
$x\in V_\delta^*(c)\cap A$ implies $f(x)\in V_\varepsilon(L)$.

thm:limit-unique.title: Uniqueness of Limits of Functions
thm:limit-unique.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. Then $f$ has at most one limit at $c$.
thm:limit-unique.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
Then $f$ has at most one limit at $c$.
thm:limit-unique.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
Then $f$ has at most one limit at $c$.

prop:sequential-criterion-limits.title: Sequential Criterion for Function Limits
prop:sequential-criterion-limits.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. Then $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ if and only if for every sequence $(x_n)\subseteq A\setminus\{c\}$ with $x_n\to c$, one has $f(x_n)\to L$.
prop:sequential-criterion-limits.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
Then $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ if and only if for every sequence
$(x_n)\subseteq A\setminus\{c\}$ with $x_n\to c$, one has
$f(x_n)\to L$.
prop:sequential-criterion-limits.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
Then $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ if and only if for every sequence
$(x_n)\subseteq A\setminus\{c\}$ with $x_n\to c$, one has
$f(x_n)\to L$.

cor:limit-three-equiv.title: Three Equivalent Forms of the Function Limit
cor:limit-three-equiv.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. The $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ form of $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, the neighbourhood form, and the sequential form are equivalent descriptions of the same limit condition.
cor:limit-three-equiv.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
The $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ form of $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, the
neighbourhood form, and the sequential form are equivalent descriptions
of the same limit condition.
cor:limit-three-equiv.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
The $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ form of $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, the
neighbourhood form, and the sequential form are equivalent descriptions
of the same limit condition.

thm:limit-implies-local-bounding.title: Limit Implies Local Boundedness
thm:limit-implies-local-bounding.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. If $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ for some $L \in \mathbb{R}$, then there exist $\delta, M > 0$ such that $|f(x)| \leq M$ for all $x \in A$ with $0<|x-c|<\delta$.
thm:limit-implies-local-bounding.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
If $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ for some $L \in \mathbb{R}$,
then there exist $\delta, M > 0$ such that $|f(x)| \leq M$ for all
$x \in A$ with $0<|x-c|<\delta$.
thm:limit-implies-local-bounding.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
If $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ for some $L \in \mathbb{R}$,
then there exist $\delta, M > 0$ such that $|f(x)| \leq M$ for all
$x \in A$ with $0<|x-c|<\delta$.

thm:bounded-limits.title: Bounded Limits
thm:bounded-limits.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$, and let $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$. If $a \leq f(x) \leq b$ for all $x \in A$ with $x \neq c$, and $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, then $a \leq L \leq b$.
thm:bounded-limits.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$, and
let $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$. If $a \leq f(x) \leq b$ for all $x \in A$
with $x \neq c$, and $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, then
$a \leq L \leq b$.
thm:bounded-limits.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$, and
let $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$. If $a \leq f(x) \leq b$ for all $x \in A$
with $x \neq c$, and $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, then
$a \leq L \leq b$.

thm:squeeze-function-limits.title: Squeeze Theorem for Function Limits
thm:squeeze-function-limits.statement_preview: Let $f, g, h : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. Suppose $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L = \lim_{x\to c}g(x)=L$, and suppose there exists $\delta_0 > 0$ such that \[ \forall x \in A,\; 0 < |x - c| < \delta_0 \;\Rightarrow\; f(x) \leq h(x) \leq g(x). \] Then $\lim_{x\to c}h(x)=L$.
thm:squeeze-function-limits.statement_display:
Let $f, g, h : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of
$A$. Suppose $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L =
\lim_{x\to c}g(x)=L$, and suppose there exists
$\delta_0 > 0$ such that
\[
  \forall x \in A,\;
  0 < |x - c| < \delta_0
  \;\Rightarrow\;
  f(x) \leq h(x) \leq g(x).
\]
Then $\lim_{x\to c}h(x)=L$.
thm:squeeze-function-limits.statement_tex:
Let $f, g, h : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of
$A$. Suppose $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L =
\lim_{x\to c}g(x)=L$, and suppose there exists
$\delta_0 > 0$ such that
\[
  \forall x \in A,\;
  0 < |x - c| < \delta_0
  \;\Rightarrow\;
  f(x) \leq h(x) \leq g(x).
\]
Then $\lim_{x\to c}h(x)=L$.

thm:limits-are-local.title: Limits Are Local
thm:limits-are-local.statement_preview: Let $E \subseteq X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be a cluster point of $E$, let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $\delta > 0$. Then \[ \lim_{x\to x_0;\,x\in E}f(x)=L \;\iff\; \lim_{x\to x_0;\,x\in E\cap(x_0-\delta,x_0+\delta)}f(x)=L. \]
thm:limits-are-local.statement_display:
Let $E \subseteq X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be a cluster
point of $E$, let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $\delta > 0$.
Then
\[
  \lim_{x\to x_0;\,x\in E}f(x)=L
  \;\iff\;
  \lim_{x\to x_0;\,x\in E\cap(x_0-\delta,x_0+\delta)}f(x)=L.
\]
thm:limits-are-local.statement_tex:
Let $E \subseteq X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $x_0$ be a cluster
point of $E$, let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $\delta > 0$.
Then
\[
  \lim_{x\to x_0;\,x\in E}f(x)=L
  \;\iff\;
  \lim_{x\to x_0;\,x\in E\cap(x_0-\delta,x_0+\delta)}f(x)=L.
\]

prop:limit-absolute-value.title: Limit of Absolute Value
prop:limit-absolute-value.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. If $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, then $\lim_{x\to c}|f(x)|=|L|$.
prop:limit-absolute-value.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
If $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, then
$\lim_{x\to c}|f(x)|=|L|$.
prop:limit-absolute-value.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
If $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$, then
$\lim_{x\to c}|f(x)|=|L|$.

thm:composition-of-limits.title: Composition of Limits
thm:composition-of-limits.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to B$, let $g : B \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c_1$ be a cluster point of $A$, and let $c_2 \in \mathbb{R}$ be a cluster point of $B$. Suppose $\lim_{x\to c_1}f(x)=c_2$ and $\lim_{y\to c_2}g(y)=L$. If $c_2 \in B$, assume also that $g(c_2) = L$. Then $\lim_{x\to c_1}g(f(x))=L$.
thm:composition-of-limits.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$, let $g : B \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c_1$ be a
cluster point of $A$, and let $c_2 \in \mathbb{R}$ be a cluster
point of $B$. Suppose $\lim_{x\to c_1}f(x)=c_2$ and
$\lim_{y\to c_2}g(y)=L$. If $c_2 \in B$, assume also that
$g(c_2) = L$. Then $\lim_{x\to c_1}g(f(x))=L$.
thm:composition-of-limits.statement_tex:
Let $f : A \to B$, let $g : B \to \mathbb{R}$, let $c_1$ be a
cluster point of $A$, and let $c_2 \in \mathbb{R}$ be a cluster
point of $B$. Suppose $\lim_{x\to c_1}f(x)=c_2$ and
$\lim_{y\to c_2}g(y)=L$. If $c_2 \in B$, assume also that
$g(c_2) = L$. Then $\lim_{x\to c_1}g(f(x))=L$.

thm:monotone-limit-theorem.title: Monotone Limit Theorem
thm:monotone-limit-theorem.statement_preview: Let $a < b$ and let $f : (a,b) \to \mathbb{R}$ be monotone and bounded. Then both one-sided limits exist at every interior point. For increasing $f$ and every $x_0 \in (a,b)$: \[ f(x_0^-) = \sup\{\,f(x) : a < x < x_0\,\}, \qquad f(x_0^+) = \inf\{\,f(x) : x_0 < x < b\,\}. \]
thm:monotone-limit-theorem.statement_display:
Let $a < b$ and let $f : (a,b) \to \mathbb{R}$ be monotone and
bounded. Then both one-sided limits exist at every interior point.
For increasing $f$ and every $x_0 \in (a,b)$:
\[
  f(x_0^-) = \sup\{\,f(x) : a < x < x_0\,\},
  \qquad
  f(x_0^+) = \inf\{\,f(x) : x_0 < x < b\,\}.
\]
thm:monotone-limit-theorem.statement_tex:
Let $a < b$ and let $f : (a,b) \to \mathbb{R}$ be monotone and
bounded. Then both one-sided limits exist at every interior point.
For increasing $f$ and every $x_0 \in (a,b)$:
\[
  f(x_0^-) = \sup\{\,f(x) : a < x < x_0\,\},
  \qquad
  f(x_0^+) = \inf\{\,f(x) : x_0 < x < b\,\}.
\]

prop:cauchy-criterion-limits.title: Cauchy Criterion for Function Limits
prop:cauchy-criterion-limits.statement_preview: Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. Then $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ exists for some $L \in \mathbb{R}$ if and only if \[ \ForallOT{\varepsilon},\; \ExistsIT{\delta},\; \forall x, y \in A :\; 0<|x-c|<\delta \;\wedge\; 0<|y-c|<\delta \;\Rightarrow\; |f(x)-f(y)|<\varepsilon. \]

def:right-hand-limit.title: Right-Hand Limit
def:right-hand-limit.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, let $c\in\mathbb{R}$, and suppose $c$ is a cluster point of $A\cap(c,\infty)$. A real number $L$ is the \textbf{right-hand limit} of $f$ at $c$ if \[ \forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists\delta>0\;\forall x\in A\; 0<x-c<\delta\Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon. \]
def:right-hand-limit.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, let $c\in\mathbb{R}$,
and suppose $c$ is a cluster point of $A\cap(c,\infty)$. A real number
$L$ is the \textbf{right-hand limit} of $f$ at $c$ if
\[
  \forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists\delta>0\;\forall x\in A\;
  0<x-c<\delta\Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon.
\]
def:right-hand-limit.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, let $c\in\mathbb{R}$,
and suppose $c$ is a cluster point of $A\cap(c,\infty)$. A real number
$L$ is the \textbf{right-hand limit} of $f$ at $c$ if
\[
  \forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists\delta>0\;\forall x\in A\;
  0<x-c<\delta\Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon.
\]

def:left-hand-limit.title: Left-Hand Limit
def:left-hand-limit.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, let $c\in\mathbb{R}$, and suppose $c$ is a cluster point of $A\cap(-\infty,c)$. A real number $L$ is the \textbf{left-hand limit} of $f$ at $c$ if \[ \forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists\delta>0\;\forall x\in A\; 0<c-x<\delta\Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon. \]
def:left-hand-limit.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, let $c\in\mathbb{R}$,
and suppose $c$ is a cluster point of $A\cap(-\infty,c)$. A real number

\[
  \forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists\delta>0\;\forall x\in A\;
  0<c-x<\delta\Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon.
\]
def:left-hand-limit.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, let $c\in\mathbb{R}$,

thm:sequential-criterion-right-hand-limi.title: Sequential Criterion for Right-Hand Limits
thm:sequential-criterion-right-hand-limi.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, and suppose $c$ is a cluster point of $A\cap(c,\infty)$. Then $\lim_{x\to c^+}f(x)=L$ if and only if for every sequence $(x_n)\subseteq A\cap(c,\infty)$ with $x_n\to c$, one has $f(x_n)\to L$.
thm:sequential-criterion-right-hand-limi.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, and suppose $c$ is a
cluster point of $A\cap(c,\infty)$. Then $\lim_{x\to c^+}f(x)=L$ if and
only if for every sequence $(x_n)\subseteq A\cap(c,\infty)$ with
$x_n\to c$, one has $f(x_n)\to L$.

Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$, and suppose $c$ is a

Then $\lim_{x\to c^-}f(x)=L$ if and only if for every sequence $(x_n)\subseteq A\cap(-\infty,c)$ with $x_n\to c$, one has $f(x_n)\to L$.

thm:two-sided-limit-iff-matching-one-sid.title: Two-Sided Limit via One-Sided Limits
thm:two-sided-limit-iff-matching-one-sid.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. If $c$ is a cluster point of both $A\cap(c,\infty)$ and $A\cap(-\infty,c)$, then $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ if and only if both $\lim_{x\to c^+}f(x)=L$ and $\lim_{x\to c^-}f(x)=L$.
thm:two-sided-limit-iff-matching-one-sid.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. If $c$ is a
cluster point of both $A\cap(c,\infty)$ and $A\cap(-\infty,c)$, then
$\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ if and only if both $\lim_{x\to c^+}f(x)=L$ and
$\lim_{x\to c^-}f(x)=L$.
thm:two-sided-limit-iff-matching-one-sid.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. If $c$ is a
cluster point of both $A\cap(c,\infty)$ and $A\cap(-\infty,c)$, then
$\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ if and only if both $\lim_{x\to c^+}f(x)=L$ and

def:pointwise-sum-of-functions.title: Pointwise Sum of Functions
def:pointwise-sum-of-functions.statement_preview: Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f,g:X\to\mathbb{R}$. The pointwise sum $f+g:X\to\mathbb{R}$ is the function defined by \[ (f+g)(x)=f(x)+g(x) \qquad (x\in X). \]
def:pointwise-sum-of-functions.statement_display:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f,g:X\to\mathbb{R}$.  The
pointwise sum $f+g:X\to\mathbb{R}$ is the function defined by
\[
  (f+g)(x)=f(x)+g(x)
  \qquad (x\in X).
\]
def:pointwise-sum-of-functions.statement_tex:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f,g:X\to\mathbb{R}$.  The
pointwise sum $f+g:X\to\mathbb{R}$ is the function defined by
\[
  (f+g)(x)=f(x)+g(x)
  \qquad (x\in X).
\]

def:pointwise-difference-of-functions.statement_preview: Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f,g:X\to\mathbb{R}$. The pointwise difference $f-g:X\to\mathbb{R}$ is the function defined by \[ (f-g)(x)=f(x)-g(x) \qquad (x\in X). \]

def:pointwise-product-of-functions.statement_preview: Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f,g:X\to\mathbb{R}$. The pointwise product $fg:X\to\mathbb{R}$ is the function defined by \[ (fg)(x)=f(x)g(x) \qquad (x\in X). \]

def:pointwise-scalar-multiple-of-a-funct.title: Pointwise Scalar Multiple of a Function
def:pointwise-scalar-multiple-of-a-funct.statement_preview: Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f:X\to\mathbb{R}$, and let $c\in\mathbb{R}$. The pointwise scalar multiple $cf:X\to\mathbb{R}$ is the function defined by \[ (cf)(x)=c f(x) \qquad (x\in X). \]

def:pointwise-absolute-value-of-a-functi.statement_preview: Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:X\to\mathbb{R}$. The pointwise absolute value $|f|:X\to\mathbb{R}$ is the function defined by \[ |f|(x)=|f(x)| \qquad (x\in X). \]

def:pointwise-maximum-of-two-functions.title: Pointwise Maximum of Two Functions
def:pointwise-maximum-of-two-functions.statement_preview: Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f,g:X\to\mathbb{R}$. The pointwise maximum $\max(f,g):X\to\mathbb{R}$ is the function defined by \[ \max(f,g)(x)=\max\{f(x),g(x)\} \qquad (x\in X). \]
def:pointwise-maximum-of-two-functions.statement_display:

\[
  \max(f,g)(x)=\max\{f(x),g(x)\}
  \qquad (x\in X).

def:pointwise-quotient-of-functions.title: Pointwise Quotient of Functions
def:pointwise-quotient-of-functions.statement_preview: Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f,g:X\to\mathbb{R}$. Suppose $g(x)\neq 0$ for every $x\in X$. The pointwise quotient $f/g:X\to\mathbb{R}$ is the function defined by \[ (f/g)(x)=\frac{f(x)}{g(x)} \qquad (x\in X). \]
def:pointwise-quotient-of-functions.statement_display:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f,g:X\to\mathbb{R}$.  Suppose
$g(x)\neq 0$ for every $x\in X$.  The pointwise quotient $f/g:X\to\mathbb{R}$
is the function defined by
\[
  (f/g)(x)=\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}
  \qquad (x\in X).
\]
def:pointwise-quotient-of-functions.statement_tex:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f,g:X\to\mathbb{R}$.  Suppose
$g(x)\neq 0$ for every $x\in X$.  The pointwise quotient $f/g:X\to\mathbb{R}$
is the function defined by
\[
  (f/g)(x)=\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}
  \qquad (x\in X).
\]

def:linear-combination-of-real-valued-fu.title: Linear Combination of Real-Valued Functions
def:linear-combination-of-real-valued-fu.statement_preview: Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f,g:X\to\mathbb{R}$, and let $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$. The real linear combination $\alpha f+\beta g:X\to\mathbb{R}$ is the function defined by \[ (\alpha f+\beta g)(x)=\alpha f(x)+\beta g(x) \qquad (x\in X). \]
def:linear-combination-of-real-valued-fu.statement_display:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f,g:X\to\mathbb{R}$, and let
$\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$.  The real linear combination
$\alpha f+\beta g:X\to\mathbb{R}$ is the function defined by
\[
  (\alpha f+\beta g)(x)=\alpha f(x)+\beta g(x)
  \qquad (x\in X).
\]
def:linear-combination-of-real-valued-fu.statement_tex:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, let $f,g:X\to\mathbb{R}$, and let
$\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$.  The real linear combination
$\alpha f+\beta g:X\to\mathbb{R}$ is the function defined by
\[
  (\alpha f+\beta g)(x)=\alpha f(x)+\beta g(x)
  \qquad (x\in X).
\]

def:real-linear-rule.title: Real-Linear Rule
def:real-linear-rule.statement_preview: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be a class of real-valued functions closed under real linear combinations. A rule $T$ on $\mathcal{C}$ is real-linear if, whenever $f,g\in\mathcal{C}$ and $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$, \[ T(\alpha f+\beta g)=\alpha T(f)+\beta T(g) \] whenever both sides are defined.
def:real-linear-rule.statement_display:
Let $\mathcal{C}$ be a class of real-valued functions closed under real
linear combinations.  A rule $T$ on $\mathcal{C}$ is real-linear if,
whenever $f,g\in\mathcal{C}$ and $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$,
\[
  T(\alpha f+\beta g)=\alpha T(f)+\beta T(g)
\]
whenever both sides are defined.
def:real-linear-rule.statement_tex:
Let $\mathcal{C}$ be a class of real-valued functions closed under real
linear combinations.  A rule $T$ on $\mathcal{C}$ is real-linear if,
whenever $f,g\in\mathcal{C}$ and $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$,
\[
  T(\alpha f+\beta g)=\alpha T(f)+\beta T(g)
\]
whenever both sides are defined.

def:function-bounded-above.title: Bounded Above Function
def:function-bounded-above.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is bounded above on $A$ if there exists $M\in\mathbb{R}$ such that \[ f(x)\leq M \qquad (x\in A). \]
def:function-bounded-above.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is bounded above on $A$ if there exists $M\in\mathbb{R}$ such that
\[
  f(x)\leq M
  \qquad (x\in A).
\]
def:function-bounded-above.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is bounded above on $A$ if there exists $M\in\mathbb{R}$ such that
\[
  f(x)\leq M
  \qquad (x\in A).
\]

def:function-bounded-below.title: Bounded Below Function
def:function-bounded-below.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is bounded below on $A$ if there exists $m\in\mathbb{R}$ such that \[ m\leq f(x) \qquad (x\in A). \]
def:function-bounded-below.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is bounded below on $A$ if there exists $m\in\mathbb{R}$ such that
\[
  m\leq f(x)
  \qquad (x\in A).
\]
def:function-bounded-below.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is bounded below on $A$ if there exists $m\in\mathbb{R}$ such that
\[
  m\leq f(x)
  \qquad (x\in A).
\]

def:function-bounded.title: Bounded Function
def:function-bounded.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is bounded on $A$ if there exists $B>0$ such that \[ |f(x)|\leq B \qquad (x\in A). \]
def:function-bounded.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is bounded on $A$ if there exists $B>0$ such that
\[
  |f(x)|\leq B
  \qquad (x\in A).
\]
def:function-bounded.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is bounded on $A$ if there exists $B>0$ such that
\[
  |f(x)|\leq B
  \qquad (x\in A).
\]

def:function-supremum-on-set.title: Supremum of a Function on a Set
def:function-supremum-on-set.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. The supremum of $f$ on $A$ is \[ \sup_{x\in A} f(x) = \sup f(A), \] provided the supremum of $f(A)$ exists.
def:function-supremum-on-set.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The supremum of
$f$ on $A$ is
\[
  \sup_{x\in A} f(x) = \sup f(A),
\]
provided the supremum of $f(A)$ exists.
def:function-supremum-on-set.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The supremum of
$f$ on $A$ is
\[
  \sup_{x\in A} f(x) = \sup f(A),
\]
provided the supremum of $f(A)$ exists.

def:function-infimum-on-set.title: Infimum of a Function on a Set
def:function-infimum-on-set.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. The infimum of $f$ on $A$ is \[ \inf_{x\in A} f(x) = \inf f(A), \] provided the infimum of $f(A)$ exists.
def:function-infimum-on-set.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The infimum of
$f$ on $A$ is
\[
  \inf_{x\in A} f(x) = \inf f(A),
\]
provided the infimum of $f(A)$ exists.
def:function-infimum-on-set.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The infimum of
$f$ on $A$ is
\[
  \inf_{x\in A} f(x) = \inf f(A),
\]
provided the infimum of $f(A)$ exists.

def:function-maximum-point.title: Maximum Point of a Function
def:function-maximum-point.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. A point $x^*\in A$ is a maximum point of $f$ on $A$ if \[ f(x)\leq f(x^*) \qquad (x\in A). \]
def:function-maximum-point.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  A point
$x^*\in A$ is a maximum point of $f$ on $A$ if
\[
  f(x)\leq f(x^*)
  \qquad (x\in A).
\]
def:function-maximum-point.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  A point
$x^*\in A$ is a maximum point of $f$ on $A$ if
\[
  f(x)\leq f(x^*)
  \qquad (x\in A).
\]

def:function-minimum-point.title: Minimum Point of a Function
def:function-minimum-point.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. A point $x_*\in A$ is a minimum point of $f$ on $A$ if \[ f(x_*)\leq f(x) \qquad (x\in A). \]
def:function-minimum-point.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  A point
$x_*\in A$ is a minimum point of $f$ on $A$ if
\[
  f(x_*)\leq f(x)
  \qquad (x\in A).
\]
def:function-minimum-point.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  A point
$x_*\in A$ is a minimum point of $f$ on $A$ if
\[
  f(x_*)\leq f(x)
  \qquad (x\in A).
\]

def:function-increasing.title: Increasing Function
def:function-increasing.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is increasing on $A$ if \[ x_1<x_2\;\Longrightarrow\;f(x_1)\leq f(x_2) \qquad (x_1,x_2\in A). \]
def:function-increasing.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is increasing on $A$ if
\[
  x_1<x_2\;\Longrightarrow\;f(x_1)\leq f(x_2)
  \qquad (x_1,x_2\in A).
\]
def:function-increasing.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is increasing on $A$ if
\[
  x_1<x_2\;\Longrightarrow\;f(x_1)\leq f(x_2)
  \qquad (x_1,x_2\in A).
\]

def:function-strictly-increasing.title: Strictly Increasing Function
def:function-strictly-increasing.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is strictly increasing on $A$ if \[ x_1<x_2\;\Longrightarrow\;f(x_1)<f(x_2) \qquad (x_1,x_2\in A). \]
def:function-strictly-increasing.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is strictly increasing on $A$ if
\[
  x_1<x_2\;\Longrightarrow\;f(x_1)<f(x_2)
  \qquad (x_1,x_2\in A).
\]
def:function-strictly-increasing.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is strictly increasing on $A$ if
\[
  x_1<x_2\;\Longrightarrow\;f(x_1)<f(x_2)
  \qquad (x_1,x_2\in A).
\]

def:function-decreasing.title: Decreasing Function
def:function-decreasing.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is decreasing on $A$ if \[ x_1<x_2\;\Longrightarrow\;f(x_1)\geq f(x_2) \qquad (x_1,x_2\in A). \]
def:function-decreasing.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is decreasing on $A$ if
\[
  x_1<x_2\;\Longrightarrow\;f(x_1)\geq f(x_2)
  \qquad (x_1,x_2\in A).
\]
def:function-decreasing.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is decreasing on $A$ if
\[
  x_1<x_2\;\Longrightarrow\;f(x_1)\geq f(x_2)
  \qquad (x_1,x_2\in A).
\]

def:function-strictly-decreasing.title: Strictly Decreasing Function
def:function-strictly-decreasing.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is strictly decreasing on $A$ if \[ x_1<x_2\;\Longrightarrow\;f(x_1)>f(x_2) \qquad (x_1,x_2\in A). \]
def:function-strictly-decreasing.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is strictly decreasing on $A$ if
\[
  x_1<x_2\;\Longrightarrow\;f(x_1)>f(x_2)
  \qquad (x_1,x_2\in A).
\]
def:function-strictly-decreasing.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is strictly decreasing on $A$ if
\[
  x_1<x_2\;\Longrightarrow\;f(x_1)>f(x_2)
  \qquad (x_1,x_2\in A).
\]

def:function-monotone.title: Monotone Function
def:function-monotone.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is monotone on $A$ if $f$ is increasing on $A$ or decreasing on $A$.
def:function-monotone.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is monotone on $A$ if $f$ is increasing on $A$ or decreasing on $A$.
def:function-monotone.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is monotone on $A$ if $f$ is increasing on $A$ or decreasing on $A$.

def:function-constant.title: Constant Function
def:function-constant.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$. The function $f$ is constant on $A$ if \[ f(x_1)=f(x_2) \qquad (x_1,x_2\in A). \]
def:function-constant.statement_display:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is constant on $A$ if
\[
  f(x_1)=f(x_2)
  \qquad (x_1,x_2\in A).
\]
def:function-constant.statement_tex:
Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$.  The function
$f$ is constant on $A$ if
\[
  f(x_1)=f(x_2)
  \qquad (x_1,x_2\in A).
\]

def:epsilon-neighbourhood.title: $\varepsilon$-Neighbourhood
def:epsilon-neighbourhood.statement_preview: Let $x\in\mathbb{R}$ and let $\varepsilon>0$. The \textbf{$\varepsilon$-neighbourhood} of $x$ is \[ V_\varepsilon(x):=(x-\varepsilon,x+\varepsilon) =\{y\in\mathbb{R}: |y-x|<\varepsilon\}. \]
def:epsilon-neighbourhood.statement_display:
Let $x\in\mathbb{R}$ and let $\varepsilon>0$. The
\textbf{$\varepsilon$-neighbourhood} of $x$ is
\[
  V_\varepsilon(x):=(x-\varepsilon,x+\varepsilon)
  =\{y\in\mathbb{R}: |y-x|<\varepsilon\}.
\]
def:epsilon-neighbourhood.statement_tex:
Let $x\in\mathbb{R}$ and let $\varepsilon>0$. The
\textbf{$\varepsilon$-neighbourhood} of $x$ is
\[
  V_\varepsilon(x):=(x-\varepsilon,x+\varepsilon)
  =\{y\in\mathbb{R}: |y-x|<\varepsilon\}.
\]

def:deleted-epsilon-neighbourhood.title: Deleted $\varepsilon$-Neighbourhood
def:deleted-epsilon-neighbourhood.statement_preview: Let $x\in\mathbb{R}$ and let $\varepsilon>0$. The \textbf{deleted $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood} of $x$ is \[ V_\varepsilon^*(x):=V_\varepsilon(x)\setminus\{x\} =\{y\in\mathbb{R}:0<|y-x|<\varepsilon\}. \]
def:deleted-epsilon-neighbourhood.statement_display:
Let $x\in\mathbb{R}$ and let $\varepsilon>0$. The
\textbf{deleted $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood} of $x$ is
\[
  V_\varepsilon^*(x):=V_\varepsilon(x)\setminus\{x\}
  =\{y\in\mathbb{R}:0<|y-x|<\varepsilon\}.
\]
def:deleted-epsilon-neighbourhood.statement_tex:
Let $x\in\mathbb{R}$ and let $\varepsilon>0$. The
\textbf{deleted $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood} of $x$ is
\[
  V_\varepsilon^*(x):=V_\varepsilon(x)\setminus\{x\}
  =\{y\in\mathbb{R}:0<|y-x|<\varepsilon\}.
\]

def:cluster-point-r.title: Cluster Point
def:cluster-point-r.statement_preview: Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $x \in \mathbb{R}$. The point $x$ is a \textbf{cluster point} of $X$ if \[ \forall\varepsilon > 0,\; \exists\, y \in X \setminus \{x\} :\; |y-x|<\varepsilon. \]
def:cluster-point-r.statement_display:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $x \in \mathbb{R}$. The point $x$
is a \textbf{cluster point} of $X$ if
\[
  \forall\varepsilon > 0,\;
  \exists\, y \in X \setminus \{x\} :\;
  |y-x|<\varepsilon.
\]
def:cluster-point-r.statement_tex:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $x \in \mathbb{R}$. The point $x$
is a \textbf{cluster point} of $X$ if
\[
  \forall\varepsilon > 0,\;
  \exists\, y \in X \setminus \{x\} :\;
  |y-x|<\varepsilon.
\]

thm:cluster-point-sequential.title: Sequential Characterization of Cluster Points
thm:cluster-point-sequential.statement_preview: $c\text{ is a cluster point of }A$ if and only if there exists $(a_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$ with $a_n \to c$.
thm:cluster-point-sequential.statement_display:
$c\text{ is a cluster point of }A$ if and only if there exists
$(a_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$ with $a_n \to c$.
thm:cluster-point-sequential.statement_tex:
$c\text{ is a cluster point of }A$ if and only if there exists
$(a_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$ with $a_n \to c$.

def:adherent-point-r.title: Adherent Point
def:adherent-point-r.statement_preview: Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $x\in\mathbb{R}$. The point $x$ is an \textbf{adherent point} of $X$ if \[ \forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists y\in X\;(|y-x|<\varepsilon). \]
def:adherent-point-r.statement_display:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $x\in\mathbb{R}$. The point $x$ is
an \textbf{adherent point} of $X$ if
\[
  \forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists y\in X\;(|y-x|<\varepsilon).
\]
def:adherent-point-r.statement_tex:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $x\in\mathbb{R}$. The point $x$ is
an \textbf{adherent point} of $X$ if
\[
  \forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists y\in X\;(|y-x|<\varepsilon).
\]

def:isolated-point-r.title: Isolated Point
def:isolated-point-r.statement_preview: Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $x\in X$. The point $x$ is an \textbf{isolated point} of $X$ if \[ \exists\varepsilon>0\;\bigl(V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X=\{x\}\bigr). \]
def:isolated-point-r.statement_display:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $x\in X$. The point $x$ is an
\textbf{isolated point} of $X$ if
\[
  \exists\varepsilon>0\;\bigl(V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X=\{x\}\bigr).
\]
def:isolated-point-r.statement_tex:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $x\in X$. The point $x$ is an
\textbf{isolated point} of $X$ if
\[
  \exists\varepsilon>0\;\bigl(V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X=\{x\}\bigr).
\]

def:interior-point-r.title: Interior Point
def:interior-point-r.statement_preview: Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $x\in\mathbb{R}$. The point $x$ is an \textbf{interior point} of $X$ if \[ \exists\varepsilon>0\;\bigl(V_\varepsilon(x)\subseteq X\bigr). \]
def:interior-point-r.statement_display:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $x\in\mathbb{R}$. The point $x$ is
an \textbf{interior point} of $X$ if
\[
  \exists\varepsilon>0\;\bigl(V_\varepsilon(x)\subseteq X\bigr).
\]
def:interior-point-r.statement_tex:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $x\in\mathbb{R}$. The point $x$ is
an \textbf{interior point} of $X$ if
\[
  \exists\varepsilon>0\;\bigl(V_\varepsilon(x)\subseteq X\bigr).
\]

def:boundary-point-r.title: Boundary Point
def:boundary-point-r.statement_preview: Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $x\in\mathbb{R}$. The point $x$ is a \textbf{boundary point} of $X$ if \[ \forall\varepsilon>0\;\bigl(V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X\neq\emptyset\bigr) \wedge\bigl(V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X^c\neq\emptyset\bigr). \]
def:boundary-point-r.statement_display:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $x\in\mathbb{R}$. The point $x$ is
a \textbf{boundary point} of $X$ if
\[
  \forall\varepsilon>0\;\bigl(V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X\neq\emptyset\bigr)
  \wedge\bigl(V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X^c\neq\emptyset\bigr).
\]
def:boundary-point-r.statement_tex:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and let $x\in\mathbb{R}$. The point $x$ is
a \textbf{boundary point} of $X$ if
\[
  \forall\varepsilon>0\;\bigl(V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X\neq\emptyset\bigr)
  \wedge\bigl(V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X^c\neq\emptyset\bigr).
\]

def:boundary-r.title: Boundary
def:boundary-r.statement_preview: Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. The \textbf{boundary} of $X$ is \[ \partial X:=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:\forall\varepsilon>0\;(V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X\neq\emptyset\wedge V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X^c\neq\emptyset)\}. \]
def:boundary-r.statement_display:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. The \textbf{boundary} of $X$ is
\[
  \partial X:=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:\forall\varepsilon>0\;(V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X\neq\emptyset\wedge V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X^c\neq\emptyset)\}.
\]
def:boundary-r.statement_tex:
Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. The \textbf{boundary} of $X$ is
\[
  \partial X:=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:\forall\varepsilon>0\;(V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X\neq\emptyset\wedge V_\varepsilon(x)\cap X^c\neq\emptyset)\}.
\]

lem:closure-elementary.title: Elementary Properties of Closures
lem:closure-elementary.statement_preview: Let $X, Y \subseteq \mathbb{R}$. Then: (i) $X \subseteq \overline{X}$; (ii) $\overline{X \cup Y} = \overline{X} \cup \overline{Y}$; (iii) $\overline{X \cap Y} \subseteq \overline{X} \cap \overline{Y}$; (iv) $X \subseteq Y \Rightarrow \overline{X} \subseteq \overline{Y}$.

cor:closed-iff-seq-limits.title: Closed iff Sequentially Closed
cor:closed-iff-seq-limits.statement_preview: $X\text{ is closed} \iff \forall (a_n)\subseteq X,\; a_n \to x \Rightarrow x \in X$.
cor:closed-iff-seq-limits.statement_display:
$X\text{ is closed} \iff \forall (a_n)\subseteq X,\;
a_n \to x \Rightarrow x \in X$.
cor:closed-iff-seq-limits.statement_tex:
$X\text{ is closed} \iff \forall (a_n)\subseteq X,\;
a_n \to x \Rightarrow x \in X$.

cor:interval-all-limit-points.title: Every Point of an Interval Is a Cluster Point
cor:interval-all-limit-points.statement_preview: If $I$ is an interval, then every $x\in I$ is a cluster point of $I$.
cor:interval-all-limit-points.statement_display: If $I$ is an interval, then every $x\in I$ is a cluster point of $I$.
cor:interval-all-limit-points.statement_tex: If $I$ is an interval, then every $x\in I$ is a cluster point of $I$.

def:bounded-set-r.title: Bounded Subset of $\mathbb{R}$
def:bounded-set-r.statement_preview: $X\text{ is bounded} \iff \exists M>0,\;\forall x\in X: |x|\leq M$.
def:bounded-set-r.statement_display:
$X\text{ is bounded} \iff \exists M>0,\;\forall x\in X:
|x|\leq M$.
def:bounded-set-r.statement_tex:
$X\text{ is bounded} \iff \exists M>0,\;\forall x\in X:
|x|\leq M$.

thm:heine-borel-subsets-real-line.title: Heine--Borel Theorem for $\mathbb{R}$
thm:heine-borel-subsets-real-line.statement_preview: $X\text{ is closed} \wedge X\text{ is bounded}$ $\iff$ every $(a_n)\subseteq X$ has a subsequence converging to a limit in $X$.
thm:heine-borel-subsets-real-line.statement_display:
$X\text{ is closed} \wedge X\text{ is bounded}$
$\iff$ every $(a_n)\subseteq X$ has a subsequence converging to
a limit in $X$.
thm:heine-borel-subsets-real-line.statement_tex:
$X\text{ is closed} \wedge X\text{ is bounded}$
$\iff$ every $(a_n)\subseteq X$ has a subsequence converging to
a limit in $X$.

def:true-near.title: True Near a Point
def:true-near.statement_preview: Property $P(f(x))$ is \textbf{true near $x_0$} if $\exists\delta>0,\;\forall x : 0<|x-x_0|< \delta) \Rightarrow P(f(x))$.
def:true-near.statement_display:
Property $P(f(x))$ is \textbf{true near $x_0$} if
$\exists\delta>0,\;\forall x : 0<|x-x_0|<
\delta) \Rightarrow P(f(x))$.
def:true-near.statement_tex:
Property $P(f(x))$ is \textbf{true near $x_0$} if
$\exists\delta>0,\;\forall x : 0<|x-x_0|<
\delta) \Rightarrow P(f(x))$.

def:newton-sequence-sqrt-two.title: Newton Sequence for $\sqrt{2}$
def:newton-sequence-sqrt-two.statement_preview: The \textbf{Newton sequence for} $\sqrt{2}$ is the real sequence $(x_n)$ defined by \[ x_1:=\frac{3}{2}, \qquad x_{n+1}:=\frac{1}{2}\left(x_n+\frac{2}{x_n}\right) \quad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]
def:newton-sequence-sqrt-two.statement_display:
The \textbf{Newton sequence for} $\sqrt{2}$ is the real sequence $(x_n)$
defined by
\[
  x_1:=\frac{3}{2},
  \qquad
  x_{n+1}:=\frac{1}{2}\left(x_n+\frac{2}{x_n}\right)
  \quad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
def:newton-sequence-sqrt-two.statement_tex:
The \textbf{Newton sequence for} $\sqrt{2}$ is the real sequence $(x_n)$
defined by
\[
  x_1:=\frac{3}{2},
  \qquad
  x_{n+1}:=\frac{1}{2}\left(x_n+\frac{2}{x_n}\right)
  \quad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

thm:newton-approximation-sqrt-two.title: Newton Approximation of $\sqrt{2}$
thm:newton-approximation-sqrt-two.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be the Newton sequence for $\sqrt{2}$. Then $(x_n)$ converges and \[ \lim_{n\to\infty}x_n=\sqrt{2}. \]
thm:newton-approximation-sqrt-two.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be the Newton sequence for $\sqrt{2}$. Then $(x_n)$ converges
and
\[
  \lim_{n\to\infty}x_n=\sqrt{2}.
\]
thm:newton-approximation-sqrt-two.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be the Newton sequence for $\sqrt{2}$. Then $(x_n)$ converges
and
\[
  \lim_{n\to\infty}x_n=\sqrt{2}.
\]

def:factorial-partial-sums.title: Factorial Partial Sums
def:factorial-partial-sums.statement_preview: The \textbf{factorial partial-sum sequence} is the real sequence $(s_n)$ defined by \[ s_n:=\sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{1}{k!} \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]
def:factorial-partial-sums.statement_display:
The \textbf{factorial partial-sum sequence} is the real sequence $(s_n)$
defined by
\[
  s_n:=\sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{1}{k!}
  \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
def:factorial-partial-sums.statement_tex:
The \textbf{factorial partial-sum sequence} is the real sequence $(s_n)$
defined by
\[
  s_n:=\sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{1}{k!}
  \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

thm:factorial-partial-sums-approximate-e.title: Factorial Partial Sums Approximate $e$
thm:factorial-partial-sums-approximate-e.statement_preview: Let $(s_n)$ be the factorial partial-sum sequence. Then $(s_n)$ converges to the Euler number $e$: \[ \lim_{n\to\infty}s_n=e. \]
thm:factorial-partial-sums-approximate-e.statement_display:
Let $(s_n)$ be the factorial partial-sum sequence. Then $(s_n)$ converges
to the Euler number $e$:
\[
  \lim_{n\to\infty}s_n=e.
\]
thm:factorial-partial-sums-approximate-e.statement_tex:
Let $(s_n)$ be the factorial partial-sum sequence. Then $(s_n)$ converges
to the Euler number $e$:
\[
  \lim_{n\to\infty}s_n=e.
\]

def:compound-interest-sequence.title: Compound-Interest Sequence
def:compound-interest-sequence.statement_preview: The \textbf{compound-interest sequence} is the real sequence $(c_n)$ defined by \[ c_n:=\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)^n \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]
def:compound-interest-sequence.statement_display:
The \textbf{compound-interest sequence} is the real sequence $(c_n)$
defined by
\[
  c_n:=\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)^n
  \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
def:compound-interest-sequence.statement_tex:
The \textbf{compound-interest sequence} is the real sequence $(c_n)$
defined by
\[
  c_n:=\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)^n
  \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

thm:compound-interest-approximation-e.title: Compound-Interest Approximation of $e$
thm:compound-interest-approximation-e.statement_preview: Let $(c_n)$ be the compound-interest sequence. Then \[ \lim_{n\to\infty}c_n=e. \]
thm:compound-interest-approximation-e.statement_display:
Let $(c_n)$ be the compound-interest sequence. Then
\[
  \lim_{n\to\infty}c_n=e.
\]
thm:compound-interest-approximation-e.statement_tex:
Let $(c_n)$ be the compound-interest sequence. Then
\[
  \lim_{n\to\infty}c_n=e.
\]

def:decimal-truncation-sequence.title: Decimal Truncation Sequence
def:decimal-truncation-sequence.statement_preview: Let $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$. The \textbf{decimal truncation sequence} of $\alpha$ is the real sequence $(d_n)$ defined by \[ d_n:=\frac{\lfloor 10^n\alpha\rfloor}{10^n} \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]
def:decimal-truncation-sequence.statement_display:
Let $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$. The \textbf{decimal truncation sequence} of
$\alpha$ is the real sequence $(d_n)$ defined by
\[
  d_n:=\frac{\lfloor 10^n\alpha\rfloor}{10^n}
  \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
def:decimal-truncation-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$. The \textbf{decimal truncation sequence} of
$\alpha$ is the real sequence $(d_n)$ defined by
\[
  d_n:=\frac{\lfloor 10^n\alpha\rfloor}{10^n}
  \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

thm:decimal-truncations-converge.title: Decimal Truncations Converge
thm:decimal-truncations-converge.statement_preview: Let $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$, and let $(d_n)$ be the decimal truncation sequence of $\alpha$. Then \[ \lim_{n\to\infty}d_n=\alpha. \]
thm:decimal-truncations-converge.statement_display:
Let $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$, and let $(d_n)$ be the decimal truncation
sequence of $\alpha$. Then
\[
  \lim_{n\to\infty}d_n=\alpha.
\]
thm:decimal-truncations-converge.statement_tex:
Let $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$, and let $(d_n)$ be the decimal truncation
sequence of $\alpha$. Then
\[
  \lim_{n\to\infty}d_n=\alpha.
\]

def:cauchy-sequence.title: Cauchy Sequence
def:cauchy-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is a \textbf{Cauchy sequence} if \[ \forall \varepsilon>0 \;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall m,n\ge N \;\bigl(|x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon\bigr). \]
def:cauchy-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is a
\textbf{Cauchy sequence} if
\[
  \forall \varepsilon>0 \;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall m,n\ge N
  \;\bigl(|x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon\bigr).
\]
def:cauchy-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is a
\textbf{Cauchy sequence} if
\[
  \forall \varepsilon>0 \;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall m,n\ge N
  \;\bigl(|x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon\bigr).
\]

thm:convergent-sequences-are-cauchy.title: Convergent Sequences Are Cauchy
thm:convergent-sequences-are-cauchy.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. If $(x_n)$ converges, then $(x_n)$ is Cauchy.
thm:convergent-sequences-are-cauchy.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. If $(x_n)$ converges, then $(x_n)$ is
Cauchy.
thm:convergent-sequences-are-cauchy.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. If $(x_n)$ converges, then $(x_n)$ is
Cauchy.

thm:cauchy-sequences-are-bounded.title: Cauchy Sequences Are Bounded
thm:cauchy-sequences-are-bounded.statement_preview: Every Cauchy real sequence is bounded.
thm:cauchy-sequences-are-bounded.statement_display: Every Cauchy real sequence is bounded.
thm:cauchy-sequences-are-bounded.statement_tex: Every Cauchy real sequence is bounded.

thm:cauchy-sequence-with-convergent-subs.title: Cauchy Sequence with a Convergent Subsequence
thm:cauchy-sequence-with-convergent-subs.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence. If a subsequence of $(x_n)$ converges to $L\in\mathbb{R}$, then $(x_n)$ converges to $L$.
thm:cauchy-sequence-with-convergent-subs.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence. If a subsequence of $(x_n)$
converges to $L\in\mathbb{R}$, then $(x_n)$ converges to $L$.
thm:cauchy-sequence-with-convergent-subs.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence. If a subsequence of $(x_n)$
converges to $L\in\mathbb{R}$, then $(x_n)$ converges to $L$.

thm:cauchy-criterion-real-sequences.title: Cauchy Criterion for Real Sequences
thm:cauchy-criterion-real-sequences.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. Then $(x_n)$ converges if and only if $(x_n)$ is Cauchy.
thm:cauchy-criterion-real-sequences.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. Then $(x_n)$ converges if and only if
$(x_n)$ is Cauchy.
thm:cauchy-criterion-real-sequences.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. Then $(x_n)$ converges if and only if
$(x_n)$ is Cauchy.

thm:cauchy-criterion-via-tails.title: Cauchy Criterion via Tails
thm:cauchy-criterion-via-tails.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. Then $(x_n)$ is Cauchy if and only if for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that the $N$-tail has pairwise term distances less than $\varepsilon$.
thm:cauchy-criterion-via-tails.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. Then $(x_n)$ is Cauchy if and only if
for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that the
$N$-tail has pairwise term distances less than $\varepsilon$.
thm:cauchy-criterion-via-tails.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. Then $(x_n)$ is Cauchy if and only if
for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that the
$N$-tail has pairwise term distances less than $\varepsilon$.

thm:cauchy-tail-diameter-criterion.title: Cauchy Tail Diameter Criterion
thm:cauchy-tail-diameter-criterion.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. Then $(x_n)$ is Cauchy if and only if every sufficiently late tail has arbitrarily small pairwise diameter.
thm:cauchy-tail-diameter-criterion.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. Then $(x_n)$ is Cauchy if and only if
every sufficiently late tail has arbitrarily small pairwise diameter.
thm:cauchy-tail-diameter-criterion.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. Then $(x_n)$ is Cauchy if and only if
every sufficiently late tail has arbitrarily small pairwise diameter.

thm:cauchy-successive-differences-vanish.title: Cauchy Sequences Have Vanishing Successive Differences
thm:cauchy-successive-differences-vanish.statement_preview: If $(x_n)$ is a Cauchy real sequence, then \[ |x_{n+1}-x_n|\to 0. \]
thm:cauchy-successive-differences-vanish.statement_display:
If $(x_n)$ is a Cauchy real sequence, then
\[
  |x_{n+1}-x_n|\to 0.
\]
thm:cauchy-successive-differences-vanish.statement_tex:
If $(x_n)$ is a Cauchy real sequence, then
\[
  |x_{n+1}-x_n|\to 0.
\]

thm:scalar-multiple-cauchy-sequence.title: Scalar Multiples of Cauchy Sequences
thm:scalar-multiple-cauchy-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence, and let $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $(\alpha x_n)$ is Cauchy.
thm:scalar-multiple-cauchy-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence, and let $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$.
Then $(\alpha x_n)$ is Cauchy.
thm:scalar-multiple-cauchy-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence, and let $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$.
Then $(\alpha x_n)$ is Cauchy.

thm:sum-cauchy-sequences.title: Sums of Cauchy Sequences
thm:sum-cauchy-sequences.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences. Then $(x_n+y_n)$ is Cauchy.
thm:sum-cauchy-sequences.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences. Then $(x_n+y_n)$ is
Cauchy.
thm:sum-cauchy-sequences.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences. Then $(x_n+y_n)$ is
Cauchy.

thm:difference-cauchy-sequences.title: Differences of Cauchy Sequences
thm:difference-cauchy-sequences.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences. Then $(x_n-y_n)$ is Cauchy.
thm:difference-cauchy-sequences.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences. Then $(x_n-y_n)$ is
Cauchy.
thm:difference-cauchy-sequences.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences. Then $(x_n-y_n)$ is
Cauchy.

thm:linear-combination-cauchy-sequences.title: Linear Combinations of Cauchy Sequences
thm:linear-combination-cauchy-sequences.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences, and let $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $(\alpha x_n+\beta y_n)$ is Cauchy.
thm:linear-combination-cauchy-sequences.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences, and let
$\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $(\alpha x_n+\beta y_n)$ is Cauchy.
thm:linear-combination-cauchy-sequences.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences, and let
$\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $(\alpha x_n+\beta y_n)$ is Cauchy.

thm:product-cauchy-sequences.title: Products of Cauchy Sequences
thm:product-cauchy-sequences.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences. Then $(x_ny_n)$ is Cauchy.
thm:product-cauchy-sequences.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences. Then $(x_ny_n)$ is
Cauchy.
thm:product-cauchy-sequences.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences. Then $(x_ny_n)$ is
Cauchy.

thm:reciprocal-cauchy-sequence.title: Reciprocals of Cauchy Sequences
thm:reciprocal-cauchy-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence. If there exist $c>0$ and $N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $|x_n|\ge c$ for every $n\ge N_0$, then $(1/x_n)$ is Cauchy.
thm:reciprocal-cauchy-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence. If there exist $c>0$ and
$N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $|x_n|\ge c$ for every $n\ge N_0$, then
$(1/x_n)$ is Cauchy.
thm:reciprocal-cauchy-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence. If there exist $c>0$ and
$N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $|x_n|\ge c$ for every $n\ge N_0$, then
$(1/x_n)$ is Cauchy.

thm:quotient-cauchy-sequences.title: Quotients of Cauchy Sequences
thm:quotient-cauchy-sequences.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences. If there exist $c>0$ and $N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $|y_n|\ge c$ for every $n\ge N_0$, then $(x_n/y_n)$ is Cauchy.
thm:quotient-cauchy-sequences.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences. If there exist $c>0$
and $N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $|y_n|\ge c$ for every $n\ge N_0$,
then $(x_n/y_n)$ is Cauchy.
thm:quotient-cauchy-sequences.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be Cauchy real sequences. If there exist $c>0$
and $N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $|y_n|\ge c$ for every $n\ge N_0$,
then $(x_n/y_n)$ is Cauchy.

thm:absolute-value-cauchy-sequence.title: Absolute Values of Cauchy Sequences
thm:absolute-value-cauchy-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence. Then $(|x_n|)$ is Cauchy.
thm:absolute-value-cauchy-sequence.statement_display: Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence. Then $(|x_n|)$ is Cauchy.
thm:absolute-value-cauchy-sequence.statement_tex: Let $(x_n)$ be a Cauchy real sequence. Then $(|x_n|)$ is Cauchy.

def:cluster-value-sequence.title: Cluster Value of a Sequence
def:cluster-value-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. The number $L$ is a \textbf{cluster value} of $(x_n)$ if for every $\varepsilon>0$ and every $N\in\mathbb{N}$ there exists $n\ge N$ such that \[ |x_n-L|<\varepsilon. \]
def:cluster-value-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. The number $L$ is
a \textbf{cluster value} of $(x_n)$ if for every $\varepsilon>0$ and every
$N\in\mathbb{N}$ there exists $n\ge N$ such that
\[
  |x_n-L|<\varepsilon.
\]
def:cluster-value-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. The number $L$ is
a \textbf{cluster value} of $(x_n)$ if for every $\varepsilon>0$ and every
$N\in\mathbb{N}$ there exists $n\ge N$ such that
\[
  |x_n-L|<\varepsilon.
\]

thm:cluster-values-are-subsequential-lim.title: Cluster Values Are Subsequential Limits
thm:cluster-values-are-subsequential-lim.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $L$ is a cluster value of $(x_n)$ if and only if $L$ is a subsequential limit of $(x_n)$.
thm:cluster-values-are-subsequential-lim.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $L$ is a
cluster value of $(x_n)$ if and only if $L$ is a subsequential limit of
$(x_n)$.
thm:cluster-values-are-subsequential-lim.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $L$ is a
cluster value of $(x_n)$ if and only if $L$ is a subsequential limit of
$(x_n)$.

thm:bounded-sequences-have-cluster-value.title: Bounded Sequences Have Cluster Values
thm:bounded-sequences-have-cluster-value.statement_preview: Every bounded real sequence has at least one cluster value.
thm:bounded-sequences-have-cluster-value.statement_display: Every bounded real sequence has at least one cluster value.
thm:bounded-sequences-have-cluster-value.statement_tex: Every bounded real sequence has at least one cluster value.

thm:limsup-liminf-extremal-cluster-value.title: Limsup and Liminf Are Extremal Cluster Values
thm:limsup-liminf-extremal-cluster-value.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. Then $\limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n$ is the largest cluster value of $(x_n)$, and $\liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n$ is the smallest cluster value of $(x_n)$.
thm:limsup-liminf-extremal-cluster-value.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. Then
$\limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n$ is the largest cluster value of $(x_n)$, and
$\liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n$ is the smallest cluster value of $(x_n)$.
thm:limsup-liminf-extremal-cluster-value.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. Then
$\limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n$ is the largest cluster value of $(x_n)$, and
$\liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n$ is the smallest cluster value of $(x_n)$.

def:pointwise-sum-sequence.title: Pointwise Sum of Sequences
def:pointwise-sum-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences. The \textbf{pointwise sum} of $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(x_n+y_n)$ defined by \[ (x+y)_n := x_n+y_n \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]
def:pointwise-sum-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences. The \textbf{pointwise sum}
of $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(x_n+y_n)$ defined by
\[
(x+y)_n := x_n+y_n
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
def:pointwise-sum-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences. The \textbf{pointwise sum}
of $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(x_n+y_n)$ defined by
\[
(x+y)_n := x_n+y_n
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

def:pointwise-difference-sequence.title: Pointwise Difference of Sequences
def:pointwise-difference-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences. The \textbf{pointwise difference} of $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(x_n-y_n)$ defined by \[ (x-y)_n := x_n-y_n \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]
def:pointwise-difference-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences. The \textbf{pointwise difference}
of $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(x_n-y_n)$ defined by
\[
(x-y)_n := x_n-y_n
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
def:pointwise-difference-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences. The \textbf{pointwise difference}
of $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(x_n-y_n)$ defined by
\[
(x-y)_n := x_n-y_n
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

def:scalar-multiple-sequence.title: Scalar Multiple of a Sequence
def:scalar-multiple-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$. The \textbf{scalar multiple} $\alpha(x_n)$ is the real sequence $(\alpha x_n)$ defined by \[ (\alpha x)_n := \alpha x_n \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]
def:scalar-multiple-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$. The
\textbf{scalar multiple} $\alpha(x_n)$ is the real sequence
$(\alpha x_n)$ defined by
\[
(\alpha x)_n := \alpha x_n
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
def:scalar-multiple-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$. The
\textbf{scalar multiple} $\alpha(x_n)$ is the real sequence
$(\alpha x_n)$ defined by
\[
(\alpha x)_n := \alpha x_n
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

def:linear-combination-of-real-sequences.title: Linear Combination of Real Sequences
def:linear-combination-of-real-sequences.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences, and let $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$. The real linear combination $\alpha(x_n)+\beta(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(\alpha x_n+\beta y_n)$ defined by \[ (\alpha x+\beta y)_n := \alpha x_n+\beta y_n \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]
def:linear-combination-of-real-sequences.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences, and let
$\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$.  The real linear combination
$\alpha(x_n)+\beta(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(\alpha x_n+\beta y_n)$
defined by
\[
(\alpha x+\beta y)_n := \alpha x_n+\beta y_n
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
def:linear-combination-of-real-sequences.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences, and let
$\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$.  The real linear combination
$\alpha(x_n)+\beta(y_n)$ is the real sequence $(\alpha x_n+\beta y_n)$
defined by
\[
(\alpha x+\beta y)_n := \alpha x_n+\beta y_n
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

def:pointwise-negation-sequence.title: Pointwise Negation of a Sequence
def:pointwise-negation-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The \textbf{pointwise negation} of $(x_n)$ is the real sequence $(-x_n)$ defined by \[ (-x)_n := -x_n \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]
def:pointwise-negation-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The \textbf{pointwise negation} of
$(x_n)$ is the real sequence $(-x_n)$ defined by
\[
(-x)_n := -x_n
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
def:pointwise-negation-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The \textbf{pointwise negation} of
$(x_n)$ is the real sequence $(-x_n)$ defined by
\[
(-x)_n := -x_n
\qquad
\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

def:pointwise-product-sequence.title: Pointwise Product of Sequences

def:convergent-sequence.title: Convergence of a Sequence
def:convergent-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$. The sequence $(x_n)$ \textbf{converges} to $L \in \mathbb{R}$ if \[ \forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists N \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall n \ge N \;\bigl(|x_n - L| < \varepsilon\bigr). \] The value $L$ is called the \textbf{limit} of $(x_n)$, written $x_n \to L$ or $\displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n = L$. A sequence that converge...
def:convergent-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$. The sequence $(x_n)$ \textbf{converges}
to $L \in \mathbb{R}$ if
\[
    \forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists N \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall n \ge N
    \;\bigl(|x_n - L| < \varepsilon\bigr).
\]
The value $L$ is called the \textbf{limit} of $(x_n)$, written
$x_n \to L$ or $\displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n = L$.
A sequence that converges to some $L \in \mathbb{R}$ is called
\textbf{convergent}; otherwise it is called \textbf{divergent}.
def:convergent-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$. The sequence $(x_n)$ \textbf{converges}
to $L \in \mathbb{R}$ if
\[
    \forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists N \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall n \ge N
    \;\bigl(|x_n - L| < \varepsilon\bigr).
\]
The value $L$ is called the \textbf{limit} of $(x_n)$, written
$x_n \to L$ or $\displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n = L$.
A sequence that converges to some $L \in \mathbb{R}$ is called
\textbf{convergent}; otherwise it is called \textbf{divergent}.

def:convergent-sequence-neighborhood.title: Convergence --- Neighborhood Formulation
def:convergent-sequence-neighborhood.statement_preview: Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. The sequence $(x_n)$ \textbf{converges} to $L$ if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ the $\varepsilon$-neighborhood \[ V_\varepsilon(L) \;:=\; (L - \varepsilon,\, L + \varepsilon) \] contains all but finitely many terms of $(x_n)$; that is, \[ \forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists N \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall...
def:convergent-sequence-neighborhood.statement_display:
Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. The sequence
$(x_n)$ \textbf{converges} to $L$ if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ the
$\varepsilon$-neighborhood
\[
    V_\varepsilon(L) \;:=\; (L - \varepsilon,\, L + \varepsilon)
\]
contains all but finitely many terms of $(x_n)$; that is,
\[
    \forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists N \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall n \ge N
    \;\bigl(x_n \in V_\varepsilon(L)\bigr).
\]
def:convergent-sequence-neighborhood.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. The sequence
$(x_n)$ \textbf{converges} to $L$ if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ the
$\varepsilon$-neighborhood
\[
    V_\varepsilon(L) \;:=\; (L - \varepsilon,\, L + \varepsilon)
\]
contains all but finitely many terms of $(x_n)$; that is,
\[
    \forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists N \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall n \ge N
    \;\bigl(x_n \in V_\varepsilon(L)\bigr).
\]

thm:equivalence-of-convergence-formulati.title: Equivalence of Convergence Formulations
thm:equivalence-of-convergence-formulati.statement_preview: Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. The following statements are equivalent. \begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\alph*)}] \item $x_n \to L$. \item $\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists K \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall n \ge K \;\bigl(|x_n - L| < \varepsilon\bigr)$. \item $\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists K \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall n \ge K \;\...
thm:equivalence-of-convergence-formulati.statement_display:
Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. The following
statements are equivalent.
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\alph*)}]
    \item $x_n \to L$.
    \item $\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists K \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall n \ge K
          \;\bigl(|x_n - L| < \varepsilon\bigr)$.
    \item $\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists K \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall n \ge K
          \;\bigl(L - \varepsilon < x_n < L + \varepsilon\bigr)$.
    \item $\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists K \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall n \ge K
          \;\bigl(x_n \in V_\varepsilon(L)\bigr)$.
\end{enumerate}
thm:equivalence-of-convergence-formulati.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. The following
statements are equivalent.
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\alph*)}]
    \item $x_n \to L$.
    \item $\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists K \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall n \ge K
          \;\bigl(|x_n - L| < \varepsilon\bigr)$.
    \item $\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists K \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall n \ge K
          \;\bigl(L - \varepsilon < x_n < L + \varepsilon\bigr)$.
    \item $\forall \varepsilon > 0 \;\exists K \in \mathbb{N} \;\forall n \ge K
          \;\bigl(x_n \in V_\varepsilon(L)\bigr)$.
\end{enumerate}

thm:uniqueness-of-limits.statement_tex:

thm:limit-preserves-eventual-order.title: Limit Preserves Eventual Order
thm:limit-preserves-eventual-order.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be real sequences, and let $L,M\in\mathbb{R}$. Suppose that $x_n\to L$ and $y_n\to M$. If there exists $N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x_n\le y_n$ for every $n\ge N_0$, then $L\le M$.

$L\le M$.

thm:strict-limit-separation-gives-eventu.title: Strict Limit Separation Gives Eventual Order

thm:eventual-strict-comparison-preserves.title: Eventual Strict Comparison Preserves Weak Limit Order

thm:constant-squeeze-theorem.title: Constant Squeeze Theorem
thm:constant-squeeze-theorem.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. If there exists $N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $L\le x_n\le L$ for every $n\ge N_0$, then $x_n\to L$.
thm:constant-squeeze-theorem.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. If there
exists $N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $L\le x_n\le L$ for every
$n\ge N_0$, then $x_n\to L$.

thm:sequence-squeeze-theorem.title: Sequence Squeeze Theorem
thm:sequence-squeeze-theorem.statement_preview: Let $(a_n)$, $(x_n)$, and $(b_n)$ be real sequences, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. Suppose that $a_n\to L$ and $b_n\to L$. If there exists $N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $a_n\le x_n\le b_n$ for every $n\ge N_0$, then $x_n\to L$.

$n\ge N_0$, then $x_n\to L$.

$n\ge N_0$, then $x_n\to L$.

thm:absolute-value-squeeze-theorem.title: Absolute-Value Squeeze Theorem
thm:absolute-value-squeeze-theorem.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(u_n)$ be real sequences, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. Suppose that $u_n\to 0$. If there exists $N_0\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $|x_n-L|\le u_n$ for every $n\ge N_0$, then $x_n\to L$.

$|x_n-L|\le u_n$ for every $n\ge N_0$, then $x_n\to L$.
thm:absolute-value-squeeze-theorem.statement_tex:

def:constant-sequence.title: Constant Sequence
def:constant-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is \textbf{constant} exactly when there exists $c \in \mathbb{R}$ such that $x_n = c$ for every $n \in \mathbb{N}$.

def:null-sequence.title: Null Sequence
def:null-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is a \textbf{null sequence} exactly when for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $N \in \mathbb{N}$ such that $|x_n| < \varepsilon$ for every $n \ge N$.
