thm:tail-equality-preserves-convergence.title: Tail Equality Preserves Convergence

def:sequence.title: Sequence
def:sequence.statement_preview: Let $X$ be a nonempty set. A \textbf{sequence} in $X$ is a function \[ x : \mathbb{N} \to X. \] The value $x(n)$ is written $x_n$ and called the \textbf{$n$-th term} of the sequence. The sequence itself is denoted $(x_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$, or simply $(x_n)$ when the index set is clear.
def:sequence.statement_display:
Let $X$ be a nonempty set. A \textbf{sequence} in $X$ is a function
\[
    x : \mathbb{N} \to X.
\]
The value $x(n)$ is written $x_n$ and called the \textbf{$n$-th term} of the
sequence. The sequence itself is denoted $(x_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$, or
simply $(x_n)$ when the index set is clear.
def:sequence.statement_tex:
Let $X$ be a nonempty set. A \textbf{sequence} in $X$ is a function
\[
    x : \mathbb{N} \to X.
\]
The value $x(n)$ is written $x_n$ and called the \textbf{$n$-th term} of the
sequence. The sequence itself is denoted $(x_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$, or
simply $(x_n)$ when the index set is clear.

def:divergent-sequence.title: Divergent Sequence
def:divergent-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is \textbf{divergent} if there is no $L\in\mathbb{R}$ such that $x_n\to L$.
def:divergent-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is
\textbf{divergent} if there is no $L\in\mathbb{R}$ such that
$x_n\to L$.
def:divergent-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is
\textbf{divergent} if there is no $L\in\mathbb{R}$ such that
$x_n\to L$.

def:diverges-to-positive-infinity.title: Divergence to Positive Infinity
def:diverges-to-positive-infinity.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ \textbf{diverges to} $+\infty$ if \[ \forall M\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall n\ge N \;\bigl(M<x_n\bigr). \]
def:diverges-to-positive-infinity.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ \textbf{diverges to}
$+\infty$ if
\[
  \forall M\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall n\ge N
  \;\bigl(M<x_n\bigr).
\]
def:diverges-to-positive-infinity.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ \textbf{diverges to}
$+\infty$ if
\[
  \forall M\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall n\ge N
  \;\bigl(M<x_n\bigr).
\]

def:diverges-to-negative-infinity.title: Divergence to Negative Infinity
def:diverges-to-negative-infinity.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ \textbf{diverges to} $-\infty$ if \[ \forall M\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall n\ge N \;\bigl(x_n<M\bigr). \]
def:diverges-to-negative-infinity.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ \textbf{diverges to}
$-\infty$ if
\[
  \forall M\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall n\ge N
  \;\bigl(x_n<M\bigr).
\]
def:diverges-to-negative-infinity.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ \textbf{diverges to}
$-\infty$ if
\[
  \forall M\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\;\forall n\ge N
  \;\bigl(x_n<M\bigr).
\]

def:oscillatory-sequence.title: Oscillatory Sequence
def:oscillatory-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is \textbf{oscillatory} if it is divergent, does not diverge to $+\infty$, and does not diverge to $-\infty$.
def:oscillatory-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is
\textbf{oscillatory} if it is divergent, does not diverge to $+\infty$,
and does not diverge to $-\infty$.
def:oscillatory-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is
\textbf{oscillatory} if it is divergent, does not diverge to $+\infty$,
and does not diverge to $-\infty$.

thm:divergence-to-infinity-implies-real-.title: Divergence to Infinity Implies Real Divergence
thm:divergence-to-infinity-implies-real-.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. If $x_n\to+\infty$ or $x_n\to-\infty$, then $(x_n)$ is divergent.
thm:divergence-to-infinity-implies-real-.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. If $x_n\to+\infty$ or $x_n\to-\infty$,
then $(x_n)$ is divergent.
thm:divergence-to-infinity-implies-real-.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. If $x_n\to+\infty$ or $x_n\to-\infty$,
then $(x_n)$ is divergent.

thm:two-subsequential-limits-force-diver.title: Two Subsequential Limits Force Divergence
thm:two-subsequential-limits-force-diver.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. If $(x_n)$ has two subsequential limits $L,K\in\mathbb{R}$ with $L\ne K$, then $(x_n)$ is divergent.
thm:two-subsequential-limits-force-diver.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. If $(x_n)$ has two subsequential limits
$L,K\in\mathbb{R}$ with $L\ne K$, then $(x_n)$ is divergent.
thm:two-subsequential-limits-force-diver.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. If $(x_n)$ has two subsequential limits
$L,K\in\mathbb{R}$ with $L\ne K$, then $(x_n)$ is divergent.

thm:unbounded-above-has-positive-infinit.title: Unbounded Above Sequence Has a Positive-Infinity Subsequence
thm:unbounded-above-has-positive-infinit.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. If $(x_n)$ is not bounded above, then there exists a subsequence $(x_{\sigma(k)})$ such that $x_{\sigma(k)}\to+\infty$.
thm:unbounded-above-has-positive-infinit.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. If $(x_n)$ is not bounded above, then
there exists a subsequence $(x_{\sigma(k)})$ such that
$x_{\sigma(k)}\to+\infty$.
thm:unbounded-above-has-positive-infinit.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. If $(x_n)$ is not bounded above, then
there exists a subsequence $(x_{\sigma(k)})$ such that
$x_{\sigma(k)}\to+\infty$.

thm:unbounded-below-has-negative-infinit.title: Unbounded Below Sequence Has a Negative-Infinity Subsequence
thm:unbounded-below-has-negative-infinit.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. If $(x_n)$ is not bounded below, then there exists a subsequence $(x_{\sigma(k)})$ such that $x_{\sigma(k)}\to-\infty$.
thm:unbounded-below-has-negative-infinit.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. If $(x_n)$ is not bounded below, then
there exists a subsequence $(x_{\sigma(k)})$ such that
$x_{\sigma(k)}\to-\infty$.
thm:unbounded-below-has-negative-infinit.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. If $(x_n)$ is not bounded below, then
there exists a subsequence $(x_{\sigma(k)})$ such that
$x_{\sigma(k)}\to-\infty$.

thm:bounded-divergence-produces-two-subs.title: Bounded Divergence Produces Two Subsequential Limits
thm:bounded-divergence-produces-two-subs.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. If $(x_n)$ is divergent, then there exist $L,K\in\mathbb{R}$ with $L\ne K$ such that $L$ and $K$ are subsequential limits of $(x_n)$.
thm:bounded-divergence-produces-two-subs.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. If $(x_n)$ is divergent, then
there exist $L,K\in\mathbb{R}$ with $L\ne K$ such that $L$ and $K$ are
subsequential limits of $(x_n)$.
thm:bounded-divergence-produces-two-subs.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. If $(x_n)$ is divergent, then
there exist $L,K\in\mathbb{R}$ with $L\ne K$ such that $L$ and $K$ are
subsequential limits of $(x_n)$.

def:tail-supremum-sequence.title: Tail Supremum Sequence
def:tail-supremum-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. The \textbf{tail supremum sequence} of $(x_n)$ is the real sequence $(s_n)$ defined by \[ s_n:=\sup\{x_k:k\ge n\} \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]
def:tail-supremum-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. The \textbf{tail supremum
sequence} of $(x_n)$ is the real sequence $(s_n)$ defined by
\[
  s_n:=\sup\{x_k:k\ge n\}
  \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
def:tail-supremum-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. The \textbf{tail supremum
sequence} of $(x_n)$ is the real sequence $(s_n)$ defined by
\[
  s_n:=\sup\{x_k:k\ge n\}
  \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

def:tail-infimum-sequence.title: Tail Infimum Sequence
def:tail-infimum-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. The \textbf{tail infimum sequence} of $(x_n)$ is the real sequence $(i_n)$ defined by \[ i_n:=\inf\{x_k:k\ge n\} \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]
def:tail-infimum-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. The \textbf{tail infimum
sequence} of $(x_n)$ is the real sequence $(i_n)$ defined by
\[
  i_n:=\inf\{x_k:k\ge n\}
  \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
def:tail-infimum-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. The \textbf{tail infimum
sequence} of $(x_n)$ is the real sequence $(i_n)$ defined by
\[
  i_n:=\inf\{x_k:k\ge n\}
  \qquad\text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

def:limsup-sequence.title: Limit Superior of a Sequence
def:limsup-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let $(s_n)$ be its tail supremum sequence. The \textbf{limit superior} of $(x_n)$ is \[ \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n:=\lim_{n\to\infty}s_n. \]
def:limsup-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let $(s_n)$ be its tail
supremum sequence. The \textbf{limit superior} of $(x_n)$ is
\[
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n:=\lim_{n\to\infty}s_n.
\]
def:limsup-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let $(s_n)$ be its tail
supremum sequence. The \textbf{limit superior} of $(x_n)$ is
\[
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n:=\lim_{n\to\infty}s_n.
\]

def:liminf-sequence.title: Limit Inferior of a Sequence
def:liminf-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let $(i_n)$ be its tail infimum sequence. The \textbf{limit inferior} of $(x_n)$ is \[ \liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n:=\lim_{n\to\infty}i_n. \]
def:liminf-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let $(i_n)$ be its tail
infimum sequence. The \textbf{limit inferior} of $(x_n)$ is
\[
  \liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n:=\lim_{n\to\infty}i_n.
\]
def:liminf-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let $(i_n)$ be its tail
infimum sequence. The \textbf{limit inferior} of $(x_n)$ is
\[
  \liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n:=\lim_{n\to\infty}i_n.
\]

thm:tail-suprema-are-decreasing.title: Tail Suprema Are Decreasing
thm:tail-suprema-are-decreasing.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let $(s_n)$ be its tail supremum sequence. Then $(s_n)$ is decreasing.
thm:tail-suprema-are-decreasing.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let $(s_n)$ be its tail
supremum sequence. Then $(s_n)$ is decreasing.
thm:tail-suprema-are-decreasing.statement_tex:

thm:tail-infima-are-increasing.title: Tail Infima Are Increasing
thm:tail-infima-are-increasing.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let $(i_n)$ be its tail infimum sequence. Then $(i_n)$ is increasing.
thm:tail-infima-are-increasing.statement_display:

infimum sequence. Then $(i_n)$ is increasing.

thm:liminf-below-limsup.title: Liminf Is Below Limsup
thm:liminf-below-limsup.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. Then \[ \liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n\le \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n. \]
thm:liminf-below-limsup.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. Then
\[
  \liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n\le \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n.
\]
thm:liminf-below-limsup.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. Then
\[
  \liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n\le \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n.
\]

thm:convergence-iff-liminf-equals-limsup.title: Convergence iff Liminf Equals Limsup
thm:convergence-iff-liminf-equals-limsup.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $x_n\to L$ if and only if \[ \liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n = \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n = L. \]
thm:convergence-iff-liminf-equals-limsup.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. Then
$x_n\to L$ if and only if
\[
  \liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n
  =
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n
  =
  L.
\]
thm:convergence-iff-liminf-equals-limsup.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. Then
$x_n\to L$ if and only if
\[
  \liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n
  =
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n
  =
  L.
\]

thm:limsup-largest-subsequential-limit.title: Limsup Is the Largest Subsequential Limit
thm:limsup-largest-subsequential-limit.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let $S=\limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n$. Then $S$ is a subsequential limit of $(x_n)$, and every subsequential limit of $(x_n)$ is less than or equal to $S$.
thm:limsup-largest-subsequential-limit.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let
$S=\limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n$. Then $S$ is a subsequential limit of
$(x_n)$, and every subsequential limit of $(x_n)$ is less than or equal
to $S$.
thm:limsup-largest-subsequential-limit.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let
$S=\limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n$. Then $S$ is a subsequential limit of
$(x_n)$, and every subsequential limit of $(x_n)$ is less than or equal
to $S$.

thm:liminf-smallest-subsequential-limit.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence, and let $I=\liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n$. Then $I$ is a subsequential limit of $(x_n)$, and every subsequential limit of $(x_n)$ is greater than or equal to $I$.

thm:liminf-smallest-subsequential-limit.statement_tex:

thm:oscillation-criterion-via-liminf-lim.title: Oscillation Criterion via Liminf and Limsup
thm:oscillation-criterion-via-liminf-lim.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. Then \[ \liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n < \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n \] if and only if $(x_n)$ has two distinct subsequential limits.
thm:oscillation-criterion-via-liminf-lim.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. Then
\[
  \liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n
  <
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n
\]
if and only if $(x_n)$ has two distinct subsequential limits.
thm:oscillation-criterion-via-liminf-lim.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. Then
\[
  \liminf_{n\to\infty}x_n
  <
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n
\]
if and only if $(x_n)$ has two distinct subsequential limits.

thm:limsup-comparison-under-eventual-ord.title: Limsup Comparison Under Eventual Order
thm:limsup-comparison-under-eventual-ord.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be bounded real sequences. If there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x_n\le y_n$ for every $n\ge N$, then \[ \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n \le \limsup_{n\to\infty}y_n. \]
thm:limsup-comparison-under-eventual-ord.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be bounded real sequences. If there exists
$N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x_n\le y_n$ for every $n\ge N$, then
\[
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n
  \le
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}y_n.
\]
thm:limsup-comparison-under-eventual-ord.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ be bounded real sequences. If there exists
$N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x_n\le y_n$ for every $n\ge N$, then
\[
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n
  \le
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}y_n.
\]

  \le

thm:limsup-squeeze-under-eventual-order.title: Limsup Squeeze Under Eventual Order
thm:limsup-squeeze-under-eventual-order.statement_preview: Let $(a_n)$, $(x_n)$, and $(b_n)$ be bounded real sequences. If there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that \[ a_n\le x_n\le b_n \qquad\text{for every }n\ge N, \] then \[ \limsup_{n\to\infty}a_n \le \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n \le \limsup_{n\to\infty}b_n. \]
thm:limsup-squeeze-under-eventual-order.statement_display:
Let $(a_n)$, $(x_n)$, and $(b_n)$ be bounded real sequences. If there
exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
  a_n\le x_n\le b_n
  \qquad\text{for every }n\ge N,
\]
then
\[
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}a_n
  \le
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n
  \le
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}b_n.
\]
thm:limsup-squeeze-under-eventual-order.statement_tex:
Let $(a_n)$, $(x_n)$, and $(b_n)$ be bounded real sequences. If there
exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
  a_n\le x_n\le b_n
  \qquad\text{for every }n\ge N,
\]
then
\[
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}a_n
  \le
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}x_n
  \le
  \limsup_{n\to\infty}b_n.
\]

def:increasing-sequence.title: Increasing Sequence
def:increasing-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is \textbf{increasing} if \[ x_n \le x_{n+1} \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]
def:increasing-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is
\textbf{increasing} if
\[
  x_n \le x_{n+1}
  \qquad
  \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
def:increasing-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is
\textbf{increasing} if
\[
  x_n \le x_{n+1}
  \qquad
  \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

def:decreasing-sequence.title: Decreasing Sequence
def:decreasing-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is \textbf{decreasing} if \[ x_{n+1} \le x_n \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]
def:decreasing-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is
\textbf{decreasing} if
\[
  x_{n+1} \le x_n
  \qquad
  \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
def:decreasing-sequence.statement_tex:

  x_{n+1} \le x_n
  \qquad

\]

def:strictly-increasing-sequence.title: Strictly Increasing Sequence
def:strictly-increasing-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is \textbf{strictly increasing} if \[ x_n < x_{n+1} \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]
def:strictly-increasing-sequence.statement_display:

  x_n < x_{n+1}

def:strictly-decreasing-sequence.title: Strictly Decreasing Sequence
def:strictly-decreasing-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is \textbf{strictly decreasing} if \[ x_{n+1} < x_n \qquad \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}. \]
def:strictly-decreasing-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is
\textbf{strictly decreasing} if
\[
  x_{n+1} < x_n
  \qquad
  \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
def:strictly-decreasing-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is
\textbf{strictly decreasing} if
\[
  x_{n+1} < x_n
  \qquad
  \text{for every } n\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

def:monotone-sequence.title: Monotone Sequence
def:monotone-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is \textbf{monotone} if it is increasing or decreasing.
def:monotone-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is
\textbf{monotone} if it is increasing or decreasing.
def:monotone-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is
\textbf{monotone} if it is increasing or decreasing.

def:eventually-increasing-sequence.title: Eventually Increasing Sequence
def:eventually-increasing-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is \textbf{eventually increasing} if there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that \[ x_n \le x_{n+1} \qquad \text{for every } n\ge N. \]
def:eventually-increasing-sequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is
\textbf{eventually increasing} if there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
  x_n \le x_{n+1}
  \qquad
  \text{for every } n\ge N.
\]
def:eventually-increasing-sequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is
\textbf{eventually increasing} if there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
  x_n \le x_{n+1}
  \qquad
  \text{for every } n\ge N.
\]

def:eventually-decreasing-sequence.title: Eventually Decreasing Sequence
def:eventually-decreasing-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is \textbf{eventually decreasing} if there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that \[ x_{n+1} \le x_n \qquad \text{for every } n\ge N. \]

def:eventually-monotone-sequence.title: Eventually Monotone Sequence
def:eventually-monotone-sequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ is \textbf{eventually monotone} if it is eventually increasing or eventually decreasing.
def:eventually-monotone-sequence.statement_display:

thm:monotone-convergence-theorem.title: Monotone Convergence Theorem
thm:monotone-convergence-theorem.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)] \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing and bounded above, then $(x_n)$ converges and \[ \lim_{n\to\infty}x_n=\sup\{x_n:n\in\mathbb{N}\}. \] \item If $(x_n)$ is decreasing and bounded below, then $(x_n)$ converges and \[ \lim_{n\to\infty}x_n=\inf\{x_n:n\in\mathbb{N}\}. \] \end{enumerate}
thm:monotone-convergence-theorem.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing and bounded above, then $(x_n)$ converges and
  \[
    \lim_{n\to\infty}x_n=\sup\{x_n:n\in\mathbb{N}\}.
  \]
  \item If $(x_n)$ is decreasing and bounded below, then $(x_n)$ converges and
  \[
    \lim_{n\to\infty}x_n=\inf\{x_n:n\in\mathbb{N}\}.
  \]
\end{enumerate}
thm:monotone-convergence-theorem.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing and bounded above, then $(x_n)$ converges and
  \[
    \lim_{n\to\infty}x_n=\sup\{x_n:n\in\mathbb{N}\}.
  \]
  \item If $(x_n)$ is decreasing and bounded below, then $(x_n)$ converges and
  \[
    \lim_{n\to\infty}x_n=\inf\{x_n:n\in\mathbb{N}\}.
  \]
\end{enumerate}

thm:strict-monotonicity-implies-monotoni.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)] \item If $(x_n)$ is strictly increasing, then $(x_n)$ is increasing. \item If $(x_n)$ is strictly decreasing, then $(x_n)$ is decreasing. \end{enumerate}

thm:bounded-monotone-sequence-equivalenc.title: Bounded Monotone Sequence Equivalences
thm:bounded-monotone-sequence-equivalenc.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)] \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing, then $(x_n)$ converges if and only if $(x_n)$ is bounded above. \item If $(x_n)$ is decreasing, then $(x_n)$ converges if and only if $(x_n)$ is bounded below. \item If $(x_n)$ is monotone, then $(x_n)$ converges if and only if $(x_n)$ is bounded. \end{enumerat...
thm:bounded-monotone-sequence-equivalenc.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing, then $(x_n)$ converges if and only if
  $(x_n)$ is bounded above.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is decreasing, then $(x_n)$ converges if and only if
  $(x_n)$ is bounded below.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is monotone, then $(x_n)$ converges if and only if
  $(x_n)$ is bounded.
\end{enumerate}

  \item If $(x_n)$ is monotone, then $(x_n)$ converges if and only if
  $(x_n)$ is bounded.
\end{enumerate}

thm:eventually-monotone-convergence-theo.title: Eventually Monotone Convergence Theorem
thm:eventually-monotone-convergence-theo.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)] \item If $(x_n)$ is eventually increasing and bounded above, then $(x_n)$ converges. \item If $(x_n)$ is eventually decreasing and bounded below, then $(x_n)$ converges. \item If $(x_n)$ is eventually monotone and bounded, then $(x_n)$ converges. \end{enumerate}
thm:eventually-monotone-convergence-theo.statement_display:

thm:unbounded-monotone-divergence.title: Unbounded Monotone Divergence
thm:unbounded-monotone-divergence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)] \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing and is not bounded above, then $x_n\to+\infty$. \item If $(x_n)$ is decreasing and is not bounded below, then $x_n\to-\infty$. \end{enumerate}
thm:unbounded-monotone-divergence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing and is not bounded above, then
  $x_n\to+\infty$.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is decreasing and is not bounded below, then
  $x_n\to-\infty$.
\end{enumerate}

thm:algebraic-transformations-preserve-m.title: Algebraic Transformations Preserve Monotonicity
thm:algebraic-transformations-preserve-m.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence and let $c,\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$. \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)] \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing, then $(x_n+c)$ is increasing. \item If $(x_n)$ is decreasing, then $(x_n+c)$ is decreasing. \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing and $\alpha>0$, then $(\alpha x_n)$ is increasing. \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing and $\alpha<0$, then $(\...
thm:algebraic-transformations-preserve-m.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence and let $c,\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing, then $(x_n+c)$ is increasing.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is decreasing, then $(x_n+c)$ is decreasing.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing and $\alpha>0$, then $(\alpha x_n)$ is increasing.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing and $\alpha<0$, then $(\alpha x_n)$ is decreasing.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing, then $(-x_n)$ is decreasing.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is decreasing, then $(-x_n)$ is increasing.
\end{enumerate}
thm:algebraic-transformations-preserve-m.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence and let $c,\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing, then $(x_n+c)$ is increasing.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is decreasing, then $(x_n+c)$ is decreasing.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing and $\alpha>0$, then $(\alpha x_n)$ is increasing.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing and $\alpha<0$, then $(\alpha x_n)$ is decreasing.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is increasing, then $(-x_n)$ is decreasing.
  \item If $(x_n)$ is decreasing, then $(-x_n)$ is increasing.
\end{enumerate}

thm:increasing-sequence-limit-as-supremu.title: Increasing Sequence Limit as Supremum
thm:increasing-sequence-limit-as-supremu.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be an increasing real sequence that is bounded above. Then \[ \lim_{n\to\infty}x_n=\sup\{x_n:n\in\mathbb{N}\}. \]

thm:decreasing-sequence-limit-as-infimum.title: Decreasing Sequence Limit as Infimum
thm:decreasing-sequence-limit-as-infimum.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a decreasing real sequence that is bounded below. Then \[ \lim_{n\to\infty}x_n=\inf\{x_n:n\in\mathbb{N}\}. \]

thm:tail-suprema-and-infima-converge.title: Tail Suprema and Infima Converge
thm:tail-suprema-and-infima-converge.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. Define \[ s_n=\sup\{x_k:k\ge n\}, \qquad i_n=\inf\{x_k:k\ge n\}. \] Then $(s_n)$ and $(i_n)$ converge.
thm:tail-suprema-and-infima-converge.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a bounded real sequence. Define
\[
  s_n=\sup\{x_k:k\ge n\},
  \qquad
  i_n=\inf\{x_k:k\ge n\}.
\]
Then $(s_n)$ and $(i_n)$ converge.

\]

thm:bounded-sequence-has-limsup-and-limi.title: Bounded Sequence Has Limsup and Liminf
thm:bounded-sequence-has-limsup-and-limi.statement_preview: Every bounded real sequence has a limit superior and a limit inferior.
thm:bounded-sequence-has-limsup-and-limi.statement_display: Every bounded real sequence has a limit superior and a limit inferior.
thm:bounded-sequence-has-limsup-and-limi.statement_tex: Every bounded real sequence has a limit superior and a limit inferior.

def:strictly-increasing-index-map.title: Strictly Increasing Index Map
def:strictly-increasing-index-map.statement_preview: Let $\sigma:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ be a function. The function $\sigma$ is a \textbf{strictly increasing index map} if \[ k<\ell \Longrightarrow \sigma(k)<\sigma(\ell) \qquad \text{for all } k,\ell\in\mathbb{N}. \]
def:strictly-increasing-index-map.statement_display:
Let $\sigma:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ be a function. The function $\sigma$
is a \textbf{strictly increasing index map} if
\[
  k<\ell \Longrightarrow \sigma(k)<\sigma(\ell)
  \qquad
  \text{for all } k,\ell\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
def:strictly-increasing-index-map.statement_tex:
Let $\sigma:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ be a function. The function $\sigma$
is a \textbf{strictly increasing index map} if
\[
  k<\ell \Longrightarrow \sigma(k)<\sigma(\ell)
  \qquad
  \text{for all } k,\ell\in\mathbb{N}.
\]

def:subsequence.title: Subsequence
def:subsequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. A \textbf{subsequence} of $(x_n)$ is a sequence $(x_{\sigma(k)})_{k\in\mathbb{N}}$, where $\sigma:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ is a strictly increasing index map.
def:subsequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. A \textbf{subsequence} of $(x_n)$ is a
sequence $(x_{\sigma(k)})_{k\in\mathbb{N}}$, where
$\sigma:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ is a strictly increasing index map.
def:subsequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. A \textbf{subsequence} of $(x_n)$ is a
sequence $(x_{\sigma(k)})_{k\in\mathbb{N}}$, where
$\sigma:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ is a strictly increasing index map.

def:subsequential-limit.title: Subsequential Limit
def:subsequential-limit.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. The real number $L$ is a \textbf{subsequential limit} of $(x_n)$ if there exists a subsequence $(x_{\sigma(k)})$ such that \[ \lim_{k\to\infty} x_{\sigma(k)}=L. \]
def:subsequential-limit.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. The real number
$L$ is a \textbf{subsequential limit} of $(x_n)$ if there exists a
subsequence $(x_{\sigma(k)})$ such that
\[
  \lim_{k\to\infty} x_{\sigma(k)}=L.
\]
def:subsequential-limit.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. The real number
$L$ is a \textbf{subsequential limit} of $(x_n)$ if there exists a
subsequence $(x_{\sigma(k)})$ such that
\[
  \lim_{k\to\infty} x_{\sigma(k)}=L.
\]

def:has-convergent-subsequence.title: Has Convergent Subsequence
def:has-convergent-subsequence.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ \textbf{has a convergent subsequence} if there exist a strictly increasing index map $\sigma:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ and a real number $L$ such that \[ \lim_{k\to\infty}x_{\sigma(k)}=L. \]
def:has-convergent-subsequence.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ \textbf{has a
convergent subsequence} if there exist a strictly increasing index map
$\sigma:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ and a real number $L$ such that
\[
  \lim_{k\to\infty}x_{\sigma(k)}=L.
\]
def:has-convergent-subsequence.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. The sequence $(x_n)$ \textbf{has a
convergent subsequence} if there exist a strictly increasing index map
$\sigma:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ and a real number $L$ such that
\[
  \lim_{k\to\infty}x_{\sigma(k)}=L.
\]

thm:subsequence-indices-dominate-identit.title: Subsequence Indices Dominate the Identity
thm:subsequence-indices-dominate-identit.statement_preview: Let $\sigma:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ be a strictly increasing index map. Then \[ k\le \sigma(k) \qquad \text{for every } k\in\mathbb{N}. \]
thm:subsequence-indices-dominate-identit.statement_display:
Let $\sigma:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ be a strictly increasing index map.
Then
\[
  k\le \sigma(k)
  \qquad
  \text{for every } k\in\mathbb{N}.
\]
thm:subsequence-indices-dominate-identit.statement_tex:

thm:subsequences-preserve-limits.title: Subsequences Preserve Limits
thm:subsequences-preserve-limits.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. If $x_n\to L$, then every subsequence of $(x_n)$ converges to $L$.
thm:subsequences-preserve-limits.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. If $x_n\to L$,
then every subsequence of $(x_n)$ converges to $L$.
thm:subsequences-preserve-limits.statement_tex:

thm:subsequential-limit-of-convergent-se.title: Subsequential Limit of a Convergent Sequence
thm:subsequential-limit-of-convergent-se.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence and let $L\in\mathbb{R}$. If $x_n\to L$, then $L$ is the only possible subsequential limit of $(x_n)$.

thm:divergence-by-two-subsequential-limi.title: Divergence by Two Subsequential Limits
thm:divergence-by-two-subsequential-limi.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence. If $(x_n)$ has two distinct subsequential limits, then $(x_n)$ does not converge.
thm:divergence-by-two-subsequential-limi.statement_display:

thm:boundedness-passes-to-subsequences.statement_preview: Every subsequence of a bounded real sequence is bounded.

thm:monotonicity-passes-to-subsequences.statement_preview: Every subsequence of an increasing real sequence is increasing, and every subsequence of a decreasing real sequence is decreasing.

thm:monotonicity-passes-to-subsequences.statement_tex:

thm:subsequence-of-subsequence.statement_preview: Every subsequence of a subsequence of $(x_n)$ is a subsequence of $(x_n)$.

thm:eventual-properties-pass-to-subseque.title: Eventual Properties Pass to Subsequences
thm:eventual-properties-pass-to-subseque.statement_preview: Let $P(n)$ be a property of indices. If there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $P(n)$ holds for every $n\ge N$, then for every strictly increasing index map $\sigma$ there exists $K\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $P(\sigma(k))$ holds for every $k\ge K$.
thm:eventual-properties-pass-to-subseque.statement_display:
Let $P(n)$ be a property of indices. If there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such
that $P(n)$ holds for every $n\ge N$, then for every strictly increasing
index map $\sigma$ there exists $K\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $P(\sigma(k))$
holds for every $k\ge K$.
thm:eventual-properties-pass-to-subseque.statement_tex:
Let $P(n)$ be a property of indices. If there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such
that $P(n)$ holds for every $n\ge N$, then for every strictly increasing
index map $\sigma$ there exists $K\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $P(\sigma(k))$
holds for every $k\ge K$.

thm:frequent-properties-yield-subsequenc.title: Frequent Properties Yield Subsequences
thm:frequent-properties-yield-subsequenc.statement_preview: Let $P(n)$ be a property of indices. If for every $N\in\mathbb{N}$ there exists $n\ge N$ such that $P(n)$ holds, then there exists a strictly increasing index map $\sigma:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ such that $P(\sigma(k))$ holds for every $k\in\mathbb{N}$.
thm:frequent-properties-yield-subsequenc.statement_display:
Let $P(n)$ be a property of indices. If for every $N\in\mathbb{N}$ there
exists $n\ge N$ such that $P(n)$ holds, then there exists a strictly
increasing index map $\sigma:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ such that
$P(\sigma(k))$ holds for every $k\in\mathbb{N}$.
thm:frequent-properties-yield-subsequenc.statement_tex:

increasing index map $\sigma:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ such that
$P(\sigma(k))$ holds for every $k\in\mathbb{N}$.

thm:subsequential-limits-respect-bounds.title: Subsequential Limits Respect Bounds
thm:subsequential-limits-respect-bounds.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence and let $L$ be a subsequential limit of $(x_n)$. If $m\le x_n\le M$ for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$, then $m\le L\le M$.
thm:subsequential-limits-respect-bounds.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence and let $L$ be a subsequential limit of
$(x_n)$. If $m\le x_n\le M$ for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$, then
$m\le L\le M$.
thm:subsequential-limits-respect-bounds.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a real sequence and let $L$ be a subsequential limit of
$(x_n)$. If $m\le x_n\le M$ for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$, then
$m\le L\le M$.

thm:squeeze-passes-to-subsequences.title: Squeeze Passes to Subsequences
thm:squeeze-passes-to-subsequences.statement_preview: Let $(a_n)$, $(x_n)$, and $(b_n)$ be real sequences. If $a_n\le x_n\le b_n$ eventually, then for every subsequence $(x_{\sigma(k)})$ there exists $K\in\mathbb{N}$ such that \[ a_{\sigma(k)}\le x_{\sigma(k)}\le b_{\sigma(k)} \qquad \text{for every } k\ge K. \]
thm:squeeze-passes-to-subsequences.statement_display:
Let $(a_n)$, $(x_n)$, and $(b_n)$ be real sequences. If
$a_n\le x_n\le b_n$ eventually, then for every subsequence
$(x_{\sigma(k)})$ there exists $K\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
  a_{\sigma(k)}\le x_{\sigma(k)}\le b_{\sigma(k)}
  \qquad
  \text{for every } k\ge K.
\]
thm:squeeze-passes-to-subsequences.statement_tex:
Let $(a_n)$, $(x_n)$, and $(b_n)$ be real sequences. If
$a_n\le x_n\le b_n$ eventually, then for every subsequence
$(x_{\sigma(k)})$ there exists $K\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
  a_{\sigma(k)}\le x_{\sigma(k)}\le b_{\sigma(k)}
  \qquad
  \text{for every } k\ge K.
\]

thm:monotone-subsequence-theorem.title: Monotone Subsequence Theorem
thm:monotone-subsequence-theorem.statement_preview: Every real sequence has a monotone subsequence.
thm:monotone-subsequence-theorem.statement_display: Every real sequence has a monotone subsequence.
thm:monotone-subsequence-theorem.statement_tex: Every real sequence has a monotone subsequence.

thm:bolzano-weierstrass-sequences.title: Bolzano-Weierstrass Theorem for Sequences
thm:bolzano-weierstrass-sequences.statement_preview: Every bounded real sequence has a convergent subsequence.
thm:bolzano-weierstrass-sequences.statement_display: Every bounded real sequence has a convergent subsequence.
thm:bolzano-weierstrass-sequences.statement_tex: Every bounded real sequence has a convergent subsequence.

thm:sequential-compactness-closed-bounde.title: Sequential Compactness of Closed Bounded Intervals
thm:sequential-compactness-closed-bounde.statement_preview: Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a\le b$. Every sequence in $[a,b]$ has a convergent subsequence whose limit belongs to $[a,b]$.
thm:sequential-compactness-closed-bounde.statement_display:
Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a\le b$. Every sequence in $[a,b]$ has a
convergent subsequence whose limit belongs to $[a,b]$.
thm:sequential-compactness-closed-bounde.statement_tex:
Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ with $a\le b$. Every sequence in $[a,b]$ has a
convergent subsequence whose limit belongs to $[a,b]$.

def:m-tail.title: $M$-Tail of a Sequence
def:m-tail.statement_preview: Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $M \in \mathbb{N}$. The \textbf{$M$-tail} of $(x_n)$ is the sequence \[ (x_n)_{n \ge M} \;:=\; \bigl(x_M,\, x_{M+1},\, x_{M+2},\, \ldots\bigr). \]
def:m-tail.statement_display:
Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $M \in \mathbb{N}$. The
\textbf{$M$-tail} of $(x_n)$ is the sequence
\[
    (x_n)_{n \ge M} \;:=\; \bigl(x_M,\, x_{M+1},\, x_{M+2},\, \ldots\bigr).
\]
def:m-tail.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $M \in \mathbb{N}$. The
\textbf{$M$-tail} of $(x_n)$ is the sequence
\[
    (x_n)_{n \ge M} \;:=\; \bigl(x_M,\, x_{M+1},\, x_{M+2},\, \ldots\bigr).
\]

thm:convergence-of-tail.title: Convergence of a Tail
thm:convergence-of-tail.statement_preview: Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $m \in \mathbb{N}$. The $m$-tail $(x_{m+n})_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ converges if and only if $(x_n)$ converges. Moreover, in this case, \[ \lim_{n \to \infty} x_{m+n} \;=\; \lim_{n \to \infty} x_n. \]
thm:convergence-of-tail.statement_display:
Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $m \in \mathbb{N}$. The
$m$-tail $(x_{m+n})_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ converges if and only if
$(x_n)$ converges. Moreover, in this case,
\[
    \lim_{n \to \infty} x_{m+n} \;=\; \lim_{n \to \infty} x_n.
\]
thm:convergence-of-tail.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $m \in \mathbb{N}$. The
$m$-tail $(x_{m+n})_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ converges if and only if
$(x_n)$ converges. Moreover, in this case,
\[
    \lim_{n \to \infty} x_{m+n} \;=\; \lim_{n \to \infty} x_n.
\]

thm:convergence-by-domination.title: Convergence by Domination
thm:convergence-by-domination.statement_preview: Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. Let $(a_n)$ be a sequence of positive real numbers with $\displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n = 0$. If there exist a constant $c > 0$ and an index $m \in \mathbb{N}$ such that \[ |x_n - L| \;\le\; c\, a_n \qquad \forall n \ge m, \] then $\displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n = L$.
thm:convergence-by-domination.statement_display:
Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. Let
$(a_n)$ be a sequence of positive real numbers with
$\displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n = 0$. If there exist a constant
$c > 0$ and an index $m \in \mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
    |x_n - L| \;\le\; c\, a_n \qquad \forall n \ge m,
\]
then $\displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n = L$.
thm:convergence-by-domination.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n) \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $L \in \mathbb{R}$. Let
$(a_n)$ be a sequence of positive real numbers with
$\displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n = 0$. If there exist a constant
$c > 0$ and an index $m \in \mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
    |x_n - L| \;\le\; c\, a_n \qquad \forall n \ge m,
\]
then $\displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n = L$.

thm:ratio-limit-less-than-one-implies-nu.title: Ratio Limit Less Than One Implies Null
thm:ratio-limit-less-than-one-implies-nu.statement_preview: Let $(x_n)$ be a sequence of positive real numbers. Suppose that the limit \[ L := \lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{x_{n+1}}{x_n} \] exists. If $L<1$, then $(x_n)$ converges and \[ \lim_{n\to\infty} x_n = 0. \]
thm:ratio-limit-less-than-one-implies-nu.statement_display:
Let $(x_n)$ be a sequence of positive real numbers. Suppose that the
limit
\[
    L := \lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{x_{n+1}}{x_n}
\]
exists. If $L<1$, then $(x_n)$ converges and
\[
    \lim_{n\to\infty} x_n = 0.
\]
thm:ratio-limit-less-than-one-implies-nu.statement_tex:
Let $(x_n)$ be a sequence of positive real numbers. Suppose that the
limit
\[
    L := \lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{x_{n+1}}{x_n}
\]
exists. If $L<1$, then $(x_n)$ converges and
\[
    \lim_{n\to\infty} x_n = 0.
\]

def:lattice-chain.title: Chain
def:lattice-chain.statement_preview: Let $(S, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set. A subset $C \subseteq S$ is a \emph{chain} if every pair of elements in $C$ is comparable: \[ \forall x, y \in C, \quad x \leq y \;\lor\; y \leq x. \] Equivalently, the restriction of $\leq$ to $C$ is a \hyperref[def:lattice-order-total-order]{total order} on $C$.
def:lattice-chain.statement_display:
Let $(S, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  A subset $C \subseteq S$ is a
\emph{chain} if every pair of elements in $C$ is comparable:
\[
\forall x, y \in C, \quad x \leq y \;\lor\; y \leq x.
\]
Equivalently, the restriction of $\leq$ to $C$ is a
\hyperref[def:lattice-order-total-order]{total order} on $C$.
def:lattice-chain.statement_tex:
Let $(S, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  A subset $C \subseteq S$ is a
\emph{chain} if every pair of elements in $C$ is comparable:
\[
\forall x, y \in C, \quad x \leq y \;\lor\; y \leq x.
\]
Equivalently, the restriction of $\leq$ to $C$ is a
\hyperref[def:lattice-order-total-order]{total order} on $C$.

def:antichain.title: Antichain
def:antichain.statement_preview:
Let $(S, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set. A subset $A \subseteq S$ is an \emph{antichain} if no two distinct elements of $A$ are comparable: \[  orall x, y \in A, \quad x 
eq y \Longrightarrow 
eg(x \leq y) \land 
eg(y \leq x). \]
def:antichain.statement_display:
Let $(S, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  A subset $A \subseteq S$ is
an \emph{antichain} if no two distinct elements of $A$ are comparable:
\[
\forall x, y \in A, \quad x \neq y \Longrightarrow \neg(x \leq y) \land \neg(y \leq x).
\]
def:antichain.statement_tex:
Let $(S, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  A subset $A \subseteq S$ is
an \emph{antichain} if no two distinct elements of $A$ are comparable:
\[
\forall x, y \in A, \quad x \neq y \Longrightarrow \neg(x \leq y) \land \neg(y \leq x).
\]

def:consistency.title: Consistency
def:consistency.statement_preview: Let $(S, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set. Elements $x, y \in S$ are \emph{consistent} (or \emph{compatible}) if they admit a common upper bound: \[ \exists z \in S, \quad x \leq z \;\land\; y \leq z. \]
def:consistency.statement_display:
Let $(S, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  Elements $x, y \in S$ are
\emph{consistent} (or \emph{compatible}) if they admit a common upper
bound:
\[
\exists z \in S, \quad x \leq z \;\land\; y \leq z.
\]
def:consistency.statement_tex:
Let $(S, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  Elements $x, y \in S$ are
\emph{consistent} (or \emph{compatible}) if they admit a common upper
bound:
\[
\exists z \in S, \quad x \leq z \;\land\; y \leq z.
\]

def:covering-relation.title: Covering Relation
def:covering-relation.statement_preview: Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set and let $x, y \in P$ with $x < y$. The element $y$ \emph{covers} $x$, written $x \prec y$, if there is no element of $P$ strictly between them: \[ x \prec y \;\coloneqq\; x < y \;\land\; \neg\,\bigl(\exists z \in P,\; x < z < y\bigr). \]
def:covering-relation.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set and let $x, y \in P$ with
$x < y$.  The element $y$ \emph{covers} $x$, written $x \prec y$, if
there is no element of $P$ strictly between them:
\[
x \prec y
\;\coloneqq\;
x < y
\;\land\;
\neg\,\bigl(\exists z \in P,\; x < z < y\bigr).
\]
def:covering-relation.statement_tex:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set and let $x, y \in P$ with
$x < y$.  The element $y$ \emph{covers} $x$, written $x \prec y$, if
there is no element of $P$ strictly between them:
\[
x \prec y
\;\coloneqq\;
x < y
\;\land\;
\neg\,\bigl(\exists z \in P,\; x < z < y\bigr).
\]

lem:order-iso-finite-characterizations.title: Characterizations of Order-Isomorphisms for Finite Posets
lem:order-iso-finite-characterizations.statement_preview: Let $(P, \leq_P)$ and $(Q, \leq_Q)$ be finite partially ordered sets and let $\varphi : P \to Q$ be a bijection. The following are equivalent: \begin{enumerate} \item $\varphi$ is an order-isomorphism. \item For all $x, y \in P$:\; $x <_P y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \varphi(x) <_Q \varphi(y)$. \item For all $x, y \in P$:\; $x \prec_P y \;\Longleftrightarrow\;...

prop:hasse-diagram-characterization.title: Hasse Diagram Characterization for Finite Posets
prop:hasse-diagram-characterization.statement_preview: Let $(P, \leq_P)$ and $(Q, \leq_Q)$ be finite partially ordered sets. Then $P$ and $Q$ are order-isomorphic if and only if their Hasse diagrams are isomorphic as directed graphs — that is, identical up to relabeling of elements.
prop:hasse-diagram-characterization.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq_P)$ and $(Q, \leq_Q)$ be finite partially ordered sets.
Then $P$ and $Q$ are order-isomorphic if and only if their Hasse diagrams
are isomorphic as directed graphs — that is, identical up to relabeling
of elements.
prop:hasse-diagram-characterization.statement_tex:
Let $(P, \leq_P)$ and $(Q, \leq_Q)$ be finite partially ordered sets.
Then $P$ and $Q$ are order-isomorphic if and only if their Hasse diagrams
are isomorphic as directed graphs — that is, identical up to relabeling
of elements.

def:down-set.title: Down-Set
def:down-set.statement_preview: Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set. A subset $Q \subseteq P$ is a \emph{down-set} (or \emph{decreasing set}, or \emph{order ideal}) if it is closed downward: \[ \forall x \in Q,\; \forall y \in P,\quad y \leq x \;\Longrightarrow\; y \in Q. \]
def:down-set.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  A subset $Q \subseteq P$ is
a \emph{down-set} (or \emph{decreasing set}, or \emph{order ideal}) if it
is closed downward:
\[
\forall x \in Q,\; \forall y \in P,\quad y \leq x \;\Longrightarrow\; y \in Q.
\]
def:down-set.statement_tex:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  A subset $Q \subseteq P$ is
a \emph{down-set} (or \emph{decreasing set}, or \emph{order ideal}) if it
is closed downward:
\[
\forall x \in Q,\; \forall y \in P,\quad y \leq x \;\Longrightarrow\; y \in Q.
\]

def:up-set.title: Up-Set
def:up-set.statement_preview: Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set. A subset $Q \subseteq P$ is an \emph{up-set} (or \emph{increasing set}, or \emph{order filter}) if it is closed upward: \[ \forall x \in Q,\; \forall y \in P,\quad x \leq y \;\Longrightarrow\; y \in Q. \]
def:up-set.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  A subset $Q \subseteq P$ is
an \emph{up-set} (or \emph{increasing set}, or \emph{order filter}) if it
is closed upward:
\[
\forall x \in Q,\; \forall y \in P,\quad x \leq y \;\Longrightarrow\; y \in Q.
\]
def:up-set.statement_tex:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  A subset $Q \subseteq P$ is

is closed upward:
\[
\forall x \in Q,\; \forall y \in P,\quad x \leq y \;\Longrightarrow\; y \in Q.
\]

def:dual-ordered-set.title: Dual Ordered Set
def:dual-ordered-set.statement_preview: Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set. The \emph{dual} (or \emph{opposite}) of $P$, denoted $P^{\mathrm{op}}$, is the set $P$ equipped with the reversed order: \[ x \leq_{P^{\mathrm{op}}} y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; y \leq_P x. \]
def:dual-ordered-set.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  The \emph{dual} (or
\emph{opposite}) of $P$, denoted $P^{\mathrm{op}}$, is the set $P$
equipped with the reversed order:
\[
x \leq_{P^{\mathrm{op}}} y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; y \leq_P x.
\]
def:dual-ordered-set.statement_tex:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  The \emph{dual} (or
\emph{opposite}) of $P$, denoted $P^{\mathrm{op}}$, is the set $P$
equipped with the reversed order:
\[
x \leq_{P^{\mathrm{op}}} y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; y \leq_P x.
\]

prop:duality-principle.title: Duality Principle
prop:duality-principle.statement_preview: Let $\Phi$ be a statement about partially ordered sets, expressed purely in terms of $\leq$, that holds in every partially ordered set. Then the dual statement $\Phi^{\mathrm{op}}$, obtained by replacing every occurrence of $\leq$ with $\geq$, also holds in every partially ordered set.
prop:duality-principle.statement_display:
Let $\Phi$ be a statement about partially ordered sets, expressed purely
in terms of $\leq$, that holds in every partially ordered set.  Then the
dual statement $\Phi^{\mathrm{op}}$, obtained by replacing every
occurrence of $\leq$ with $\geq$, also holds in every partially ordered
set.
prop:duality-principle.statement_tex:
Let $\Phi$ be a statement about partially ordered sets, expressed purely
in terms of $\leq$, that holds in every partially ordered set.  Then the
dual statement $\Phi^{\mathrm{op}}$, obtained by replacing every
occurrence of $\leq$ with $\geq$, also holds in every partially ordered
set.

def:bottom-element.title: Bottom Element
def:bottom-element.statement_preview: Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set. An element $\bot \in P$ is a \emph{bottom element} (or \emph{least element}) of $P$ if \[ \forall x \in P, \quad \bot \leq x. \] If a bottom element exists it is unique; it is denoted $\bot_P$ or simply $\bot$.
def:bottom-element.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  An element $\bot \in P$ is
a \emph{bottom element} (or \emph{least element}) of $P$ if
\[
\forall x \in P, \quad \bot \leq x.
\]
If a bottom element exists it is unique; it is denoted $\bot_P$ or
simply $\bot$.
def:bottom-element.statement_tex:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  An element $\bot \in P$ is

\]
If a bottom element exists it is unique; it is denoted $\bot_P$ or

def:top-element.title: Top Element
def:top-element.statement_preview: Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set. An element $\top \in P$ is a \emph{top element} (or \emph{greatest element}) of $P$ if \[ \forall x \in P, \quad x \leq \top. \] If a top element exists it is unique; it is denoted $\top_P$ or simply $\top$.
def:top-element.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  An element $\top \in P$ is

\[

\]
If a top element exists it is unique; it is denoted $\top_P$ or simply
$\top$.

def:maximal-element.title: Maximal Element
def:maximal-element.statement_preview: Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set and let $Q \subseteq P$. An element $a \in Q$ is \emph{maximal in $Q$} if no element of $Q$ strictly exceeds it: \[ \forall x \in Q, \quad a \leq x \;\Longrightarrow\; a = x. \] The set of maximal elements of $Q$ is denoted $\operatorname{Max}(Q)$.
def:maximal-element.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set and let $Q \subseteq P$.  An
element $a \in Q$ is \emph{maximal in $Q$} if no element of $Q$ strictly
exceeds it:
\[
\forall x \in Q, \quad a \leq x \;\Longrightarrow\; a = x.
\]
The set of maximal elements of $Q$ is denoted $\operatorname{Max}(Q)$.
def:maximal-element.statement_tex:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set and let $Q \subseteq P$.  An
element $a \in Q$ is \emph{maximal in $Q$} if no element of $Q$ strictly
exceeds it:
\[
\forall x \in Q, \quad a \leq x \;\Longrightarrow\; a = x.
\]
The set of maximal elements of $Q$ is denoted $\operatorname{Max}(Q)$.

def:minimal-element.title: Minimal Element
def:minimal-element.statement_preview: Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set and let $Q \subseteq P$. An element $a \in Q$ is \emph{minimal in $Q$} if no element of $Q$ is strictly below it: \[ \forall x \in Q, \quad x \leq a \;\Longrightarrow\; x = a. \]

def:greatest-element.title: Greatest Element
def:greatest-element.statement_preview: Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set and let $Q \subseteq P$. An element $m \in Q$ is the \emph{greatest element} (or \emph{maximum}) of $Q$ if it dominates every element of $Q$: \[ \forall x \in Q, \quad x \leq m. \] If it exists it is unique; it is denoted $\max Q$.
def:greatest-element.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set and let $Q \subseteq P$.  An
element $m \in Q$ is the \emph{greatest element} (or \emph{maximum}) of
$Q$ if it dominates every element of $Q$:
\[
\forall x \in Q, \quad x \leq m.
\]
If it exists it is unique; it is denoted $\max Q$.

def:least-element.title: Least Element
def:least-element.statement_preview: Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set and let $Q \subseteq P$. def:least-element.statement_display:

\[
\forall x \in Q, \quad m \leq x.
\]

prop:finite-poset-maximal.title: Every Nonempty Finite Poset Has a Maximal Element
prop:finite-poset-maximal.statement_preview: Let $(P, \leq)$ be a finite partially ordered set. Then every nonempty subset $Q \subseteq P$ has at least one maximal element. Furthermore, for every $x \in P$ there exists $y \in \operatorname{Max}(P)$ with $x \leq y$.
prop:finite-poset-maximal.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a finite partially ordered set.  Then every nonempty
subset $Q \subseteq P$ has at least one maximal element.  Furthermore,
for every $x \in P$ there exists $y \in \operatorname{Max}(P)$ with
$x \leq y$.
prop:finite-poset-maximal.statement_tex:
