$x \leq y$.

prop:predicates-power-sets-iso.title: Predicates and Power Sets Are Order-Isomorphic

def:lifting.title: Lifting
def:lifting.statement_preview: Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set. The \emph{lifting} of $P$ is the poset $P_{\bot} \coloneqq P \cup \{\bot\}$, where $\bot \notin P$, with the order: \[ \bot \leq x \quad \text{for all } x \in P_{\bot}, \qquad x \leq_{P_{\bot}} y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; x \leq_P y \quad \text{for all } x, y \in P. \] Thus $P_{\bot}$ is $P$ with a new \hyperref[def...
def:lifting.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  The \emph{lifting} of $P$
is the poset $P_{\bot} \coloneqq P \cup \{\bot\}$, where $\bot \notin P$,
with the order:
\[
\bot \leq x \quad \text{for all } x \in P_{\bot},
\qquad
x \leq_{P_{\bot}} y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; x \leq_P y
\quad \text{for all } x, y \in P.
\]
Thus $P_{\bot}$ is $P$ with a new
\hyperref[def:bottom-element]{bottom element} $\bot$ adjoined.
def:lifting.statement_tex:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  The \emph{lifting} of $P$
is the poset $P_{\bot} \coloneqq P \cup \{\bot\}$, where $\bot \notin P$,
with the order:
\[
\bot \leq x \quad \text{for all } x \in P_{\bot},
\qquad
x \leq_{P_{\bot}} y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; x \leq_P y
\quad \text{for all } x, y \in P.
\]
Thus $P_{\bot}$ is $P$ with a new
\hyperref[def:bottom-element]{bottom element} $\bot$ adjoined.

def:co-lifting.title: Co-Lifting
def:co-lifting.statement_preview: Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set. The \emph{co-lifting} of $P$ is the poset $P^{\top} \coloneqq P \cup \{\top\}$, where $\top \notin P$, with the order: \[ x \leq \top \quad \text{for all } x \in P^{\top}, \qquad x \leq_{P^{\top}} y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; x \leq_P y \quad \text{for all } x, y \in P. \] Thus $P^{\top}$ is $P$ with a new \hyperref[...
def:co-lifting.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  The \emph{co-lifting} of $P$
is the poset $P^{\top} \coloneqq P \cup \{\top\}$, where $\top \notin P$,
with the order:
\[
x \leq \top \quad \text{for all } x \in P^{\top},
\qquad
x \leq_{P^{\top}} y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; x \leq_P y
\quad \text{for all } x, y \in P.
\]
Thus $P^{\top}$ is $P$ with a new
\hyperref[def:top-element]{top element} $\top$ adjoined.
def:co-lifting.statement_tex:
Let $(P, \leq)$ be a partially ordered set.  The \emph{co-lifting} of $P$
is the poset $P^{\top} \coloneqq P \cup \{\top\}$, where $\top \notin P$,
with the order:
\[
x \leq \top \quad \text{for all } x \in P^{\top},
\qquad
x \leq_{P^{\top}} y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; x \leq_P y
\quad \text{for all } x, y \in P.
\]
Thus $P^{\top}$ is $P$ with a new
\hyperref[def:top-element]{top element} $\top$ adjoined.

def:order-preserving-map.title: Order-Preserving Map
def:order-preserving-map.statement_preview: Let $(P, \leq_P)$ and $(Q, \leq_Q)$ be partially ordered sets. A map $\varphi : P \to Q$ is \emph{order-preserving} (or \emph{monotone}) if \[ \forall x, y \in P, \quad x \leq_P y \;\Longrightarrow\; \varphi(x) \leq_Q \varphi(y). \]
def:order-preserving-map.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq_P)$ and $(Q, \leq_Q)$ be partially ordered sets.  A map
$\varphi : P \to Q$ is \emph{order-preserving} (or \emph{monotone}) if
\[
\forall x, y \in P, \quad
x \leq_P y \;\Longrightarrow\; \varphi(x) \leq_Q \varphi(y).
\]
def:order-preserving-map.statement_tex:
Let $(P, \leq_P)$ and $(Q, \leq_Q)$ be partially ordered sets.  A map
$\varphi : P \to Q$ is \emph{order-preserving} (or \emph{monotone}) if
\[
\forall x, y \in P, \quad
x \leq_P y \;\Longrightarrow\; \varphi(x) \leq_Q \varphi(y).
\]

def:lattice-order-embedding.title: Order-Embedding
def:lattice-order-embedding.statement_preview: Let $(P, \leq_P)$ and $(Q, \leq_Q)$ be partially ordered sets. A map $\varphi : P \to Q$ is an \emph{order-embedding} if \[ \forall x, y \in P, \quad x \leq_P y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \varphi(x) \leq_Q \varphi(y). \] When $\varphi$ is an order-embedding, one writes $\varphi : P \hookrightarrow Q$.
def:lattice-order-embedding.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq_P)$ and $(Q, \leq_Q)$ be partially ordered sets.  A map
$\varphi : P \to Q$ is an \emph{order-embedding} if
\[
\forall x, y \in P, \quad
x \leq_P y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \varphi(x) \leq_Q \varphi(y).
\]
When $\varphi$ is an order-embedding, one writes $\varphi : P \hookrightarrow Q$.
def:lattice-order-embedding.statement_tex:
Let $(P, \leq_P)$ and $(Q, \leq_Q)$ be partially ordered sets.  A map
$\varphi : P \to Q$ is an \emph{order-embedding} if
\[
\forall x, y \in P, \quad
x \leq_P y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \varphi(x) \leq_Q \varphi(y).
\]
When $\varphi$ is an order-embedding, one writes $\varphi : P \hookrightarrow Q$.

def:order-isomorphism.title: Order-Isomorphism
def:order-isomorphism.statement_preview: An \hyperref[def:lattice-order-embedding]{order-embedding} $\varphi : P \to Q$ that is surjective is an \emph{order-isomorphism}. When an order-isomorphism exists, $P$ and $Q$ are said to be \emph{order-isomorphic}, written $P \cong Q$.
def:order-isomorphism.statement_display:
An \hyperref[def:lattice-order-embedding]{order-embedding} $\varphi : P \to Q$
that is surjective is an \emph{order-isomorphism}.  When an
order-isomorphism exists, $P$ and $Q$ are said to be
\emph{order-isomorphic}, written $P \cong Q$.
def:order-isomorphism.statement_tex:
An \hyperref[def:lattice-order-embedding]{order-embedding} $\varphi : P \to Q$
that is surjective is an \emph{order-isomorphism}.  When an
order-isomorphism exists, $P$ and $Q$ are said to be
\emph{order-isomorphic}, written $P \cong Q$.

lem:order-embedding-injective.title: Every Order-Embedding Is Injective
lem:order-embedding-injective.statement_preview: Let $(P, \leq_P)$ and $(Q, \leq_Q)$ be partially ordered sets, and let $\varphi : P \to Q$ be an order-embedding. Then $\varphi$ is injective.
lem:order-embedding-injective.statement_display:
Let $(P, \leq_P)$ and $(Q, \leq_Q)$ be partially ordered sets, and let
$\varphi : P \to Q$ be an order-embedding.  Then $\varphi$ is injective.
lem:order-embedding-injective.statement_tex:
Let $(P, \leq_P)$ and $(Q, \leq_Q)$ be partially ordered sets, and let
$\varphi : P \to Q$ be an order-embedding.  Then $\varphi$ is injective.

def:lattice-order-partial-order.title: Partial Order
def:lattice-order-partial-order.statement_preview: A relation $\leq$ on a set $S$ is a \emph{partial order} if it satisfies, for all $a, b, c \in S$: \begin{enumerate} \item \textbf{Reflexivity:} $a \leq a$. \item \textbf{Anti-symmetry:} $a \leq b \land b \leq a \Longrightarrow a = b$. \item \textbf{Transitivity:} $a \leq b \land b \leq c \Longrightarrow a \leq c$. \end{enumerate} The pair $(S, \leq)$ is ca...

def:lattice-order-strict-partial-order.title: Strict Partial Order
def:lattice-order-strict-partial-order.statement_preview: A relation $<$ on a set $S$ is a \emph{strict partial order} if it satisfies, for all $a, b, c \in S$: \begin{enumerate} \item \textbf{Irreflexivity:} $\neg(a < a)$. \item \textbf{Asymmetry:} $a < b \Longrightarrow \neg(b < a)$. \item \textbf{Transitivity:} $a < b \land b < c \Longrightarrow a < c$. \end{enumerate}

def:lattice-order-total-order.title: Total Order
def:lattice-order-total-order.statement_preview: A \hyperref[def:lattice-order-partial-order]{partial order} $\leq$ on a set $S$ is a \emph{total order} (or \emph{linear order}) if it additionally satisfies the \emph{law of trichotomy}: for all $a, b \in S$, exactly one of \[ a < b, \qquad a = b, \qquad b < a \] holds. The pair $(S, \leq)$ is called a \emph{totally ordered set} or a \emph{chain}.
def:lattice-order-total-order.statement_display:
A \hyperref[def:lattice-order-partial-order]{partial order} $\leq$ on a set $S$ is a
\emph{total order} (or \emph{linear order}) if it additionally satisfies
the \emph{law of trichotomy}: for all $a, b \in S$, exactly one of
\[
a < b, \qquad a = b, \qquad b < a
\]
holds.  The pair $(S, \leq)$ is called a \emph{totally ordered set}
or a \emph{chain}.
def:lattice-order-total-order.statement_tex:
A \hyperref[def:lattice-order-partial-order]{partial order} $\leq$ on a set $S$ is a
\emph{total order} (or \emph{linear order}) if it additionally satisfies
the \emph{law of trichotomy}: for all $a, b \in S$, exactly one of
\[
a < b, \qquad a = b, \qquad b < a
\]
holds.  The pair $(S, \leq)$ is called a \emph{totally ordered set}
or a \emph{chain}.

def:partial-map.title: Partial Map
def:partial-map.statement_preview: Let $A$ and $B$ be sets. A \emph{partial map} $f : A \rightharpoonup B$ is a relation $f \subseteq A \times B$ such that every element of $A$ has at most one image: \[ \forall x \in A,\; \forall y_1, y_2 \in B,\quad (x, y_1) \in f \;\land\; (x, y_2) \in f \;\Longrightarrow\; y_1 = y_2. \]
def:partial-map.statement_display:
Let $A$ and $B$ be sets.  A \emph{partial map} $f : A \rightharpoonup B$
is a relation $f \subseteq A \times B$ such that every element of $A$ has
at most one image:
\[
\forall x \in A,\; \forall y_1, y_2 \in B,\quad
(x, y_1) \in f \;\land\; (x, y_2) \in f
\;\Longrightarrow\; y_1 = y_2.
\]
def:partial-map.statement_tex:
Let $A$ and $B$ be sets.  A \emph{partial map} $f : A \rightharpoonup B$
is a relation $f \subseteq A \times B$ such that every element of $A$ has
at most one image:
\[
\forall x \in A,\; \forall y_1, y_2 \in B,\quad
(x, y_1) \in f \;\land\; (x, y_2) \in f
\;\Longrightarrow\; y_1 = y_2.
\]

def:total-map.title: Total Map
def:total-map.statement_preview: A \hyperref[def:partial-map]{partial map} $f : A \rightharpoonup B$ is a \emph{total map} if it is defined on every element of $A$: \[ \forall x \in A,\; \exists y \in B,\quad (x, y) \in f. \] A total map is written $f : A \to B$ and is precisely a function in the usual sense.
def:total-map.statement_display:
A \hyperref[def:partial-map]{partial map} $f : A \rightharpoonup B$ is a
\emph{total map} if it is defined on every element of $A$:
\[
\forall x \in A,\; \exists y \in B,\quad (x, y) \in f.
\]
A total map is written $f : A \to B$ and is precisely a function in the
usual sense.
def:total-map.statement_tex:
A \hyperref[def:partial-map]{partial map} $f : A \rightharpoonup B$ is a
\emph{total map} if it is defined on every element of $A$:
\[
\forall x \in A,\; \exists y \in B,\quad (x, y) \in f.
\]
A total map is written $f : A \to B$ and is precisely a function in the
usual sense.

def:list.title: List
def:list.statement_preview: Suppose $n$ is a non-negative integer. A \textbf{list} of length $n$ is an ordered collection of $n$ elements.
def:list.statement_display: Suppose $n$ is a non-negative integer. A \textbf{list} of length $n$ is an ordered collection of $n$ elements.
def:list.statement_tex: Suppose $n$ is a non-negative integer. A \textbf{list} of length $n$ is an ordered collection of $n$ elements.

def:fn.title: $\mathbf{F}^n$
def:fn.statement_preview: Let $\mathbf{F}$ denote $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$, and let $n$ be a positive integer. Then $\mathbf{F}^n$ is defined to be the set of all lists of length $n$ of elements of $\mathbf{F}$: \[ \mathbf{F}^n = \{(x_1,\dots,x_n) : x_1,\dots,x_n \in \mathbf{F}\}. \] The entries in a list in $\mathbf{F}^n$ are called the coordinates of the list.
def:fn.statement_display:
Let $\mathbf{F}$ denote $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$, and let $n$ be a positive integer.
Then $\mathbf{F}^n$ is defined to be the set of all lists of length $n$ of elements of $\mathbf{F}$:
\[
\mathbf{F}^n
=
\{(x_1,\dots,x_n) : x_1,\dots,x_n \in \mathbf{F}\}.
\]
The entries in a list in $\mathbf{F}^n$ are called the coordinates of the list.
def:fn.statement_tex:
Let $\mathbf{F}$ denote $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$, and let $n$ be a positive integer.
Then $\mathbf{F}^n$ is defined to be the set of all lists of length $n$ of elements of $\mathbf{F}$:
\[
\mathbf{F}^n
=
\{(x_1,\dots,x_n) : x_1,\dots,x_n \in \mathbf{F}\}.
\]
The entries in a list in $\mathbf{F}^n$ are called the coordinates of the list.

def:addition-in-fn.title: Addition in $\mathbf{F}^n$
def:addition-in-fn.statement_preview: Let \[ x=(x_1,\dots,x_n), \qquad y=(y_1,\dots,y_n) \] be vectors in $\mathbf{F}^n$. The sum of $x$ and $y$ is defined by \[ x+y = (x_1+y_1,\dots,x_n+y_n). \]
def:addition-in-fn.statement_display:
Let
\[
x=(x_1,\dots,x_n), \qquad y=(y_1,\dots,y_n)
\]
be vectors in $\mathbf{F}^n$.
The sum of $x$ and $y$ is defined by
\[
x+y
=
(x_1+y_1,\dots,x_n+y_n).
\]
def:addition-in-fn.statement_tex:
Let
\[
x=(x_1,\dots,x_n), \qquad y=(y_1,\dots,y_n)
\]
be vectors in $\mathbf{F}^n$.
The sum of $x$ and $y$ is defined by
\[
x+y
=
(x_1+y_1,\dots,x_n+y_n).
\]

def:zero-vector-in-fn.title: Zero Vector in $\mathbf{F}^n$
def:zero-vector-in-fn.statement_preview: The symbol $0$ denotes the list of length $n$ all of whose coordinates are $0$: \[ 0=(0,\dots,0)\in \mathbf{F}^n. \]
def:zero-vector-in-fn.statement_display:
The symbol $0$ denotes the list of length $n$ all of whose coordinates are $0$:
\[
0=(0,\dots,0)\in \mathbf{F}^n.
\]
def:zero-vector-in-fn.statement_tex:
The symbol $0$ denotes the list of length $n$ all of whose coordinates are $0$:
\[
0=(0,\dots,0)\in \mathbf{F}^n.
\]

def:additive-inverse-in-fn.title: Additive Inverse in $\mathbf{F}^n$
def:additive-inverse-in-fn.statement_preview: Let \[ x=(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in \mathbf{F}^n. \] The additive inverse of $x$ is defined by \[ -x=(-x_1,\dots,-x_n). \]
def:additive-inverse-in-fn.statement_display:
Let
\[
x=(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in \mathbf{F}^n.
\]
The additive inverse of $x$ is defined by
\[
-x=(-x_1,\dots,-x_n).
\]
def:additive-inverse-in-fn.statement_tex:
Let
\[
x=(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in \mathbf{F}^n.
\]
The additive inverse of $x$ is defined by
\[
-x=(-x_1,\dots,-x_n).
\]

def:scalar-multiplication-in-fn.title: Scalar Multiplication in $\mathbf{F}^n$
def:scalar-multiplication-in-fn.statement_preview: Let \[ \lambda \in \mathbf{F} \quad\text{and}\quad x=(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in \mathbf{F}^n. \] The product of $\lambda$ and $x$ is defined by \[ \lambda x = (\lambda x_1,\dots,\lambda x_n). \]
def:scalar-multiplication-in-fn.statement_display:
Let
\[
\lambda \in \mathbf{F}
\quad\text{and}\quad
x=(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in \mathbf{F}^n.
\]
The product of $\lambda$ and $x$ is defined by
\[
\lambda x
=
(\lambda x_1,\dots,\lambda x_n).
\]
def:scalar-multiplication-in-fn.statement_tex:
Let
\[
\lambda \in \mathbf{F}
\quad\text{and}\quad
x=(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in \mathbf{F}^n.
\]
The product of $\lambda$ and $x$ is defined by
\[
\lambda x
=
(\lambda x_1,\dots,\lambda x_n).
\]

def:vector-space.title: Vector Space
def:vector-space.statement_preview: A \textbf{vector space} over $\mathbf{F}$ is a set $V$ together with an addition on $V$ and a scalar multiplication \[ \mathbf{F} \times V \to V \] such that the following properties hold for all $u,v,w \in V$ and all $a,b \in \mathbf{F}$: \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item $u + v \in V$; \item $u + v = v + u$; \item $(u + v) + w = u + (v + w)$; \item...

def:vector-and-point.title: Vector and Point
def:vector-and-point.statement_preview: An element of a vector space $V$ is called a \textbf{vector}. When $V = \mathbf{F}^n$, an element of $\mathbf{F}^n$ may also be called a \textbf{point}.
def:vector-and-point.statement_display:
An element of a vector space $V$ is called a \textbf{vector}.

When $V = \mathbf{F}^n$, an element of $\mathbf{F}^n$ may also be called
a \textbf{point}.
def:vector-and-point.statement_tex:
An element of a vector space $V$ is called a \textbf{vector}.

When $V = \mathbf{F}^n$, an element of $\mathbf{F}^n$ may also be called
a \textbf{point}.

def:real-vector-space.title: Real Vector Space
def:real-vector-space.statement_preview: A \textbf{real vector space} is a vector space over $\mathbb{R}$.
def:real-vector-space.statement_display: A \textbf{real vector space} is a vector space over $\mathbb{R}$.
def:real-vector-space.statement_tex: A \textbf{real vector space} is a vector space over $\mathbb{R}$.

def:complex-vector-space.title: Complex Vector Space
def:complex-vector-space.statement_preview: A \textbf{complex vector space} is a vector space over $\mathbb{C}$.
def:complex-vector-space.statement_display: A \textbf{complex vector space} is a vector space over $\mathbb{C}$.
def:complex-vector-space.statement_tex: A \textbf{complex vector space} is a vector space over $\mathbb{C}$.

thm:unique-additive-identity.title: Unique Additive Identity
thm:unique-additive-identity.statement_preview: A vector space has a unique additive identity.
thm:unique-additive-identity.statement_display: A vector space has a unique additive identity.
thm:unique-additive-identity.statement_tex: A vector space has a unique additive identity.

thm:unique-additive-inverse.title: Unique Additive Inverse
thm:unique-additive-inverse.statement_preview: Every vector in a vector space has a unique additive inverse.
thm:unique-additive-inverse.statement_display: Every vector in a vector space has a unique additive inverse.
thm:unique-additive-inverse.statement_tex: Every vector in a vector space has a unique additive inverse.

def:subspace.title: Subspace
def:subspace.statement_preview: Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$. A subset $U \subseteq V$ is called a \textbf{subspace} of $V$ if $U$ is itself a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$ with the same addition and scalar multiplication as on $V$.
def:subspace.statement_display:
Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$.
A subset $U \subseteq V$ is called a \textbf{subspace} of $V$ if $U$ is itself
a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$ with the same addition and scalar multiplication
as on $V$.
def:subspace.statement_tex:
Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$.
A subset $U \subseteq V$ is called a \textbf{subspace} of $V$ if $U$ is itself
a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$ with the same addition and scalar multiplication
as on $V$.

thm:conditions-for-a-subspace.title: Conditions for a Subspace
thm:conditions-for-a-subspace.statement_preview: Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$ and let $U \subseteq V$. Then $U$ is a subspace of $V$ if and only if the following three conditions hold: \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item $0 \in U$; \item for all $u,v \in U$, one has $u+v \in U$; \item for all $a \in \mathbf{F}$ and all $u \in U$, one has $au \in U$. \end{enumerate}

def:sum-of-subspaces.title: Sum of Subspaces
def:sum-of-subspaces.statement_preview: Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$, and let $U_1,\dots,U_m$ be subspaces of $V$. The \textbf{sum} of $U_1,\dots,U_m$ is the set \[ U_1+\cdots+U_m = \{u_1+\cdots+u_m : u_1 \in U_1,\dots,u_m \in U_m\}. \]
def:sum-of-subspaces.statement_display:
Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$, and let
$U_1,\dots,U_m$ be subspaces of $V$.
The \textbf{sum} of $U_1,\dots,U_m$ is the set
\[
U_1+\cdots+U_m
=
\{u_1+\cdots+u_m : u_1 \in U_1,\dots,u_m \in U_m\}.
\]
def:sum-of-subspaces.statement_tex:
Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$, and let
$U_1,\dots,U_m$ be subspaces of $V$.
The \textbf{sum} of $U_1,\dots,U_m$ is the set
\[
U_1+\cdots+U_m
=
\{u_1+\cdots+u_m : u_1 \in U_1,\dots,u_m \in U_m\}.
\]

thm:sum-of-subspaces-smallest-containing.title: Sum of Subspaces Is the Smallest Containing Subspace
thm:sum-of-subspaces-smallest-containing.statement_preview: Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$, and let $U_1,\dots,U_m$ be subspaces of $V$. Then $U_1+\cdots+U_m$ is a subspace of $V$ containing each of $U_1,\dots,U_m$. Moreover, if $W$ is a subspace of $V$ containing each of $U_1,\dots,U_m$, then \[ U_1+\cdots+U_m \subseteq W. \]
thm:sum-of-subspaces-smallest-containing.statement_display:
Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$, and let
$U_1,\dots,U_m$ be subspaces of $V$.
Then $U_1+\cdots+U_m$ is a subspace of $V$ containing each of
$U_1,\dots,U_m$.
Moreover, if $W$ is a subspace of $V$ containing each of
$U_1,\dots,U_m$, then
\[
U_1+\cdots+U_m \subseteq W.
\]
thm:sum-of-subspaces-smallest-containing.statement_tex:
Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$, and let
$U_1,\dots,U_m$ be subspaces of $V$.
Then $U_1+\cdots+U_m$ is a subspace of $V$ containing each of
$U_1,\dots,U_m$.
Moreover, if $W$ is a subspace of $V$ containing each of
$U_1,\dots,U_m$, then
\[
U_1+\cdots+U_m \subseteq W.
\]

def:direct-sum.title: Direct Sum
def:direct-sum.statement_preview: Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$, and let $U_1,\dots,U_m$ be subspaces of $V$ such that \[ V=U_1+\cdots+U_m. \] Then $V$ is called the \textbf{direct sum} of $U_1,\dots,U_m$ if each vector $v \in V$ can be written in the form \[ v=u_1+\cdots+u_m, \qquad u_1 \in U_1,\dots,u_m \in U_m, \] in exactly one way. In this case, we write \[ V=U_1\oplus\cd...
def:direct-sum.statement_display:
Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$, and let
$U_1,\dots,U_m$ be subspaces of $V$ such that
\[
V=U_1+\cdots+U_m.
\]
Then $V$ is called the \textbf{direct sum} of $U_1,\dots,U_m$ if each
vector $v \in V$ can be written in the form
\[
v=u_1+\cdots+u_m,
\qquad
u_1 \in U_1,\dots,u_m \in U_m,
\]
in exactly one way.
In this case, we write
\[
V=U_1\oplus\cdots\oplus U_m.
\]
def:direct-sum.statement_tex:
Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$, and let
$U_1,\dots,U_m$ be subspaces of $V$ such that
\[
V=U_1+\cdots+U_m.
\]
Then $V$ is called the \textbf{direct sum} of $U_1,\dots,U_m$ if each
vector $v \in V$ can be written in the form
\[
v=u_1+\cdots+u_m,
\qquad
u_1 \in U_1,\dots,u_m \in U_m,
\]
in exactly one way.
In this case, we write
\[
V=U_1\oplus\cdots\oplus U_m.
\]

thm:condition-for-direct-sum.title: Condition for a Direct Sum
thm:condition-for-direct-sum.statement_preview: Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$, and let $U_1,\dots,U_m$ be subspaces of $V$ such that \[ V=U_1+\cdots+U_m. \] Then the following are equivalent: \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)] \item \(V=U_1\oplus\cdots\oplus U_m\); \item if \(u_1 \in U_1,\dots,u_m \in U_m\) and \[ u_1+\cdots+u_m=0, \] then \[ u_1=\cdots=u_m=0. \] \end{enumerate}
thm:condition-for-direct-sum.statement_display:
Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$, and let
$U_1,\dots,U_m$ be subspaces of $V$ such that
\[
V=U_1+\cdots+U_m.
\]
Then the following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item \(V=U_1\oplus\cdots\oplus U_m\);
  \item if \(u_1 \in U_1,\dots,u_m \in U_m\) and
        \[
        u_1+\cdots+u_m=0,
        \]
        then
        \[
        u_1=\cdots=u_m=0.
        \]
\end{enumerate}
thm:condition-for-direct-sum.statement_tex:
Let $V$ be a vector space over $\mathbf{F}$, and let
$U_1,\dots,U_m$ be subspaces of $V$ such that
\[
V=U_1+\cdots+U_m.
\]
Then the following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item \(V=U_1\oplus\cdots\oplus U_m\);
  \item if \(u_1 \in U_1,\dots,u_m \in U_m\) and
        \[
        u_1+\cdots+u_m=0,
        \]
        then
        \[
        u_1=\cdots=u_m=0.
        \]
\end{enumerate}

thm:direct-sum-of-two-subspaces.title: Direct Sum of Two Subspaces
thm:direct-sum-of-two-subspaces.statement_preview: Let $U$ and $W$ be subspaces of a vector space $V$. Then \[ U+W=U\oplus W \] if and only if \[ U \cap W = \{0\}. \]
thm:direct-sum-of-two-subspaces.statement_display:
Let $U$ and $W$ be subspaces of a vector space $V$.
Then
\[
U+W=U\oplus W
\]
if and only if
\[
U \cap W = \{0\}.
\]
thm:direct-sum-of-two-subspaces.statement_tex:
Let $U$ and $W$ be subspaces of a vector space $V$.
Then
\[
U+W=U\oplus W
\]
if and only if
\[
U \cap W = \{0\}.
\]

def:point-slope-form.title: Point-slope form
def:point-slope-form.statement_preview: Let $P_1=(x_1,y_1)$ be a point in the Euclidean plane and let $m\in\mathbb{R}$. The point-slope form of the line through $P_1$ with slope $m$ is \[ y-y_1=m(x-x_1). \]
def:point-slope-form.statement_display:
Let $P_1=(x_1,y_1)$ be a point in the Euclidean plane and let
$m\in\mathbb{R}$. The point-slope form of the line through $P_1$ with slope
$m$ is
\[
  y-y_1=m(x-x_1).
\]
def:point-slope-form.statement_tex:
Let $P_1=(x_1,y_1)$ be a point in the Euclidean plane and let
$m\in\mathbb{R}$. The point-slope form of the line through $P_1$ with slope
$m$ is
\[
  y-y_1=m(x-x_1).
\]

def:two-point-form.title: Two-point form
def:two-point-form.statement_preview: Let $P_1=(x_1,y_1)$ and $P_2=(x_2,y_2)$ be distinct points with $x_1\neq x_2$. The two-point form of the line through $P_1$ and $P_2$ is \[ y-y_1=\left(\frac{y_2-y_1}{x_2-x_1}\right)(x-x_1). \]
def:two-point-form.statement_display:
Let $P_1=(x_1,y_1)$ and $P_2=(x_2,y_2)$ be distinct points with
$x_1\neq x_2$. The two-point form of the line through $P_1$ and $P_2$ is
\[
  y-y_1=\left(\frac{y_2-y_1}{x_2-x_1}\right)(x-x_1).
\]
def:two-point-form.statement_tex:
Let $P_1=(x_1,y_1)$ and $P_2=(x_2,y_2)$ be distinct points with
$x_1\neq x_2$. The two-point form of the line through $P_1$ and $P_2$ is
\[
  y-y_1=\left(\frac{y_2-y_1}{x_2-x_1}\right)(x-x_1).
\]

def:general-linear-form.title: General linear form
def:general-linear-form.statement_preview: The general linear form of a line in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is \[ Ax+By+C=0, \] where $A,B,C\in\mathbb{R}$ and $A^2+B^2\neq 0$.
def:general-linear-form.statement_display:
The general linear form of a line in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is
\[
  Ax+By+C=0,
\]
where $A,B,C\in\mathbb{R}$ and $A^2+B^2\neq 0$.
def:general-linear-form.statement_tex:
The general linear form of a line in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is
\[
  Ax+By+C=0,
\]
where $A,B,C\in\mathbb{R}$ and $A^2+B^2\neq 0$.

def:vector-parametric-form.title: Vector parametric form
def:vector-parametric-form.statement_preview: A line in $\mathbb{R}^2$ may be represented as the image of $\mathbf r:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}^2$ given by \[ \mathbf r(t)=\mathbf r_0+t\mathbf v, \] where $\mathbf r_0\in\mathbb{R}^2$ is a point on the line and $\mathbf v\in\mathbb{R}^2$ is a nonzero direction vector.
def:vector-parametric-form.statement_display:
A line in $\mathbb{R}^2$ may be represented as the image of
$\mathbf r:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}^2$ given by
\[
  \mathbf r(t)=\mathbf r_0+t\mathbf v,
\]
where $\mathbf r_0\in\mathbb{R}^2$ is a point on the line and
$\mathbf v\in\mathbb{R}^2$ is a nonzero direction vector.
def:vector-parametric-form.statement_tex:
A line in $\mathbb{R}^2$ may be represented as the image of
$\mathbf r:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}^2$ given by
\[
  \mathbf r(t)=\mathbf r_0+t\mathbf v,
\]
where $\mathbf r_0\in\mathbb{R}^2$ is a point on the line and
$\mathbf v\in\mathbb{R}^2$ is a nonzero direction vector.

def:intercept-form.title: Intercept form
def:intercept-form.statement_preview: If a line meets the $x$-axis at $(a,0)$ and the $y$-axis at $(0,b)$ with $a,b\neq 0$, its intercept form is \[ \frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1. \]
def:intercept-form.statement_display:
If a line meets the $x$-axis at $(a,0)$ and the $y$-axis at $(0,b)$ with
$a,b\neq 0$, its intercept form is
\[
  \frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1.
\]
def:intercept-form.statement_tex:
If a line meets the $x$-axis at $(a,0)$ and the $y$-axis at $(0,b)$ with
$a,b\neq 0$, its intercept form is
\[
  \frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=1.
\]

def:affine-parametric-line.title: Affine parametric line in $\mathbb{R}^n$
def:affine-parametric-line.statement_preview: Given distinct points $\mathbf x_1,\mathbf x_2\in\mathbb{R}^n$, the line through them is \[ \mathbf x(t)=\mathbf x_1+t(\mathbf x_2-\mathbf x_1), \qquad t\in\mathbb{R}. \]
def:affine-parametric-line.statement_display:
Given distinct points $\mathbf x_1,\mathbf x_2\in\mathbb{R}^n$, the line
through them is
\[
  \mathbf x(t)=\mathbf x_1+t(\mathbf x_2-\mathbf x_1),
  \qquad t\in\mathbb{R}.
\]
def:affine-parametric-line.statement_tex:
Given distinct points $\mathbf x_1,\mathbf x_2\in\mathbb{R}^n$, the line
through them is
\[
  \mathbf x(t)=\mathbf x_1+t(\mathbf x_2-\mathbf x_1),
  \qquad t\in\mathbb{R}.
\]

def:normal-form-line.title: Normal form
def:normal-form-line.statement_preview: A line in $\mathbb{R}^2$ may be written in normal form as \[ x\cos\alpha+y\sin\alpha-p=0, \] where $p\ge 0$ is the distance from the origin to the line.
def:normal-form-line.statement_display:
A line in $\mathbb{R}^2$ may be written in normal form as
\[
  x\cos\alpha+y\sin\alpha-p=0,
\]
where $p\ge 0$ is the distance from the origin to the line.
def:normal-form-line.statement_tex:
A line in $\mathbb{R}^2$ may be written in normal form as
\[
  x\cos\alpha+y\sin\alpha-p=0,
\]
where $p\ge 0$ is the distance from the origin to the line.

def:symmetric-form-line.title: Symmetric form
def:symmetric-form-line.statement_preview: Given a point $(x_1,y_1)$ and direction cosines $(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)$, the symmetric form of a line is \[ \frac{x-x_1}{\cos\theta} = \frac{y-y_1}{\sin\theta} = r. \]
def:symmetric-form-line.statement_display:
Given a point $(x_1,y_1)$ and direction cosines
$(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)$, the symmetric form of a line is
\[
  \frac{x-x_1}{\cos\theta}
  =
  \frac{y-y_1}{\sin\theta}
  =
  r.
\]
def:symmetric-form-line.statement_tex:
Given a point $(x_1,y_1)$ and direction cosines
$(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)$, the symmetric form of a line is
\[
  \frac{x-x_1}{\cos\theta}
  =
  \frac{y-y_1}{\sin\theta}
  =
  r.
\]

thm:euclid-i-1.title: Euclid I.1: Constructing an equilateral triangle
thm:euclid-i-1.statement_preview: Given a finite straight line segment $AB$, there exists an equilateral triangle $ABC$ constructed on $AB$.
thm:euclid-i-1.statement_display:
Given a finite straight line segment $AB$, there exists an equilateral
triangle $ABC$ constructed on $AB$.
thm:euclid-i-1.statement_tex:
Given a finite straight line segment $AB$, there exists an equilateral
triangle $ABC$ constructed on $AB$.

thm:euclid-i-2.title: Euclid I.2: Copying a segment from a given point
thm:euclid-i-2.statement_preview: Given a point $A$ and a finite straight line segment $BC$, there exists a straight line segment beginning at $A$ and equal to $BC$.
thm:euclid-i-2.statement_display:
Given a point $A$ and a finite straight line segment $BC$, there exists a
straight line segment beginning at $A$ and equal to $BC$.
thm:euclid-i-2.statement_tex:
Given a point $A$ and a finite straight line segment $BC$, there exists a
straight line segment beginning at $A$ and equal to $BC$.

thm:euclid-i-3.title: Euclid I.3: Cutting off an equal segment
thm:euclid-i-3.statement_preview: Given two unequal finite straight line segments, there exists a construction which cuts off from the greater a straight line segment equal to the less.
thm:euclid-i-3.statement_display:
Given two unequal finite straight line segments, there exists a construction
which cuts off from the greater a straight line segment equal to the less.
thm:euclid-i-3.statement_tex:
Given two unequal finite straight line segments, there exists a construction
which cuts off from the greater a straight line segment equal to the less.

ax:euclid-common-notion-things-equal-to-.title: Euclid Common Notion I: Equality is transitive through a common equal
ax:euclid-common-notion-things-equal-to-.statement_preview: Things which are equal to the same thing are also equal to one another.
ax:euclid-common-notion-things-equal-to-.statement_display: Things which are equal to the same thing are also equal to one another.
ax:euclid-common-notion-things-equal-to-.statement_tex: Things which are equal to the same thing are also equal to one another.

ax:euclid-common-notion-add-equals.title: Euclid Common Notion II: Adding equals to equals
ax:euclid-common-notion-add-equals.statement_preview: If equals be added to equals, the wholes are equal.
ax:euclid-common-notion-add-equals.statement_display: If equals be added to equals, the wholes are equal.
ax:euclid-common-notion-add-equals.statement_tex: If equals be added to equals, the wholes are equal.

ax:euclid-common-notion-subtract-equals.title: Euclid Common Notion III: Subtracting equals from equals
ax:euclid-common-notion-subtract-equals.statement_preview: If equals be subtracted from equals, the remainders are equal.
ax:euclid-common-notion-subtract-equals.statement_display: If equals be subtracted from equals, the remainders are equal.
ax:euclid-common-notion-subtract-equals.statement_tex: If equals be subtracted from equals, the remainders are equal.

ax:euclid-common-notion-coinciding-thing.title: Euclid Common Notion IV: Coincidence implies equality
ax:euclid-common-notion-coinciding-thing.statement_preview: Things which coincide with one another are equal to one another.
ax:euclid-common-notion-coinciding-thing.statement_display: Things which coincide with one another are equal to one another.
ax:euclid-common-notion-coinciding-thing.statement_tex: Things which coincide with one another are equal to one another.

ax:euclid-common-notion-whole-greater-th.title: Euclid Common Notion V: Whole and part
ax:euclid-common-notion-whole-greater-th.statement_preview: The whole is greater than the part.
ax:euclid-common-notion-whole-greater-th.statement_display: The whole is greater than the part.
ax:euclid-common-notion-whole-greater-th.statement_tex: The whole is greater than the part.

def:euclid-point.title: Point
def:euclid-point.statement_preview: A point is that which has no part.
def:euclid-point.statement_display: A point is that which has no part.
def:euclid-point.statement_tex: A point is that which has no part.

def:euclid-ends-of-a-line.title: Ends of a line
def:euclid-ends-of-a-line.statement_preview: The ends of a line are points.
def:euclid-ends-of-a-line.statement_display: The ends of a line are points.
def:euclid-ends-of-a-line.statement_tex: The ends of a line are points.

def:euclid-straight-line.title: Straight line
def:euclid-straight-line.statement_preview: A straight line is a line which lies evenly with the points on itself.
def:euclid-straight-line.statement_display: A straight line is a line which lies evenly with the points on itself.
def:euclid-straight-line.statement_tex: A straight line is a line which lies evenly with the points on itself.

def:euclid-surface.title: Surface
def:euclid-surface.statement_preview: A surface is that which has length and breadth only.
def:euclid-surface.statement_display: A surface is that which has length and breadth only.
def:euclid-surface.statement_tex: A surface is that which has length and breadth only.

def:euclid-edges-of-a-surface.title: Edges of a surface
def:euclid-edges-of-a-surface.statement_preview: The edges of a surface are lines.
def:euclid-edges-of-a-surface.statement_display: The edges of a surface are lines.
def:euclid-edges-of-a-surface.statement_tex: The edges of a surface are lines.

def:euclid-plane-surface.title: Plane surface
def:euclid-plane-surface.statement_preview: A plane surface is a surface which lies evenly with the straight lines on itself.
def:euclid-plane-surface.statement_display: A plane surface is a surface which lies evenly with the straight lines on itself.
def:euclid-plane-surface.statement_tex: A plane surface is a surface which lies evenly with the straight lines on itself.

def:euclid-plane-angle.title: Plane angle
def:euclid-plane-angle.statement_preview: A plane angle is the inclination to one another of two lines in a plane which meet one another and do not lie in a straight line.
def:euclid-plane-angle.statement_display:
A plane angle is the inclination to one another of two lines in a plane which
meet one another and do not lie in a straight line.
def:euclid-plane-angle.statement_tex:
A plane angle is the inclination to one another of two lines in a plane which
meet one another and do not lie in a straight line.

def:euclid-rectilinear-angle.title: Rectilinear angle
def:euclid-rectilinear-angle.statement_preview: And when the lines containing the angle are straight, the angle is called rectilinear.
def:euclid-rectilinear-angle.statement_display:
And when the lines containing the angle are straight, the angle is called
rectilinear.
def:euclid-rectilinear-angle.statement_tex:
And when the lines containing the angle are straight, the angle is called
rectilinear.

def:euclid-right-angle-and-perpendicular.title: Right angle and perpendicular
def:euclid-right-angle-and-perpendicular.statement_preview: When a straight line standing on a straight line makes the adjacent angles equal to one another, each of the equal angles is right, and the straight line standing on the other is called a perpendicular to that on which it stands.
def:euclid-right-angle-and-perpendicular.statement_display:
When a straight line standing on a straight line makes the adjacent angles equal
to one another, each of the equal angles is right, and the straight line
standing on the other is called a perpendicular to that on which it stands.
def:euclid-right-angle-and-perpendicular.statement_tex:
When a straight line standing on a straight line makes the adjacent angles equal
to one another, each of the equal angles is right, and the straight line
standing on the other is called a perpendicular to that on which it stands.

def:euclid-obtuse-angle.title: Obtuse angle
def:euclid-obtuse-angle.statement_preview: An obtuse angle is an angle greater than a right angle.
def:euclid-obtuse-angle.statement_display: An obtuse angle is an angle greater than a right angle.
def:euclid-obtuse-angle.statement_tex: An obtuse angle is an angle greater than a right angle.

def:euclid-acute-angle.title: Acute angle
def:euclid-acute-angle.statement_preview: An acute angle is an angle less than a right angle.
def:euclid-acute-angle.statement_display: An acute angle is an angle less than a right angle.
def:euclid-acute-angle.statement_tex: An acute angle is an angle less than a right angle.

def:euclid-boundary.title: Boundary
def:euclid-boundary.statement_preview: A boundary is that which is an extremity of anything.
def:euclid-boundary.statement_display: A boundary is that which is an extremity of anything.
def:euclid-boundary.statement_tex: A boundary is that which is an extremity of anything.

def:euclid-figure.title: Figure
def:euclid-figure.statement_preview: A figure is that which is contained by any boundary or boundaries.
def:euclid-figure.statement_display: A figure is that which is contained by any boundary or boundaries.
def:euclid-figure.statement_tex: A figure is that which is contained by any boundary or boundaries.

def:euclid-circle.title: Circle
def:euclid-circle.statement_preview: A circle is a plane figure contained by one line such that all the straight lines falling upon it from one point among those lying within the figure equal one another.
def:euclid-circle.statement_display:
A circle is a plane figure contained by one line such that all the straight
lines falling upon it from one point among those lying within the figure equal
one another.
def:euclid-circle.statement_tex:
A circle is a plane figure contained by one line such that all the straight
lines falling upon it from one point among those lying within the figure equal
one another.

def:euclid-center-of-a-circle.title: Center of a circle
def:euclid-center-of-a-circle.statement_preview: And the point is called the center of the circle.
def:euclid-center-of-a-circle.statement_display: And the point is called the center of the circle.
def:euclid-center-of-a-circle.statement_tex: And the point is called the center of the circle.

def:euclid-diameter.title: Diameter
def:euclid-diameter.statement_preview: A diameter of the circle is any straight line drawn through the center and terminated in both directions by the circumference of the circle, and such a straight line also bisects the circle.
def:euclid-diameter.statement_display:
A diameter of the circle is any straight line drawn through the center and
terminated in both directions by the circumference of the circle, and such a
straight line also bisects the circle.
def:euclid-diameter.statement_tex:
A diameter of the circle is any straight line drawn through the center and
terminated in both directions by the circumference of the circle, and such a
straight line also bisects the circle.

def:euclid-semicircle.title: Semicircle
def:euclid-semicircle.statement_preview: A semicircle is the figure contained by the diameter and the circumference cut off by it. And the center of the semicircle is the same as that of the circle.
def:euclid-semicircle.statement_display:
A semicircle is the figure contained by the diameter and the circumference cut
off by it. And the center of the semicircle is the same as that of the circle.
def:euclid-semicircle.statement_tex:
A semicircle is the figure contained by the diameter and the circumference cut
off by it. And the center of the semicircle is the same as that of the circle.

def:euclid-rectilinear-figures.title: Rectilinear figures
def:euclid-rectilinear-figures.statement_preview: Rectilinear figures are those which are contained by straight lines, trilateral figures being those contained by three, quadrilateral those contained by four, and multilateral those contained by more than four straight lines.
def:euclid-rectilinear-figures.statement_display:
Rectilinear figures are those which are contained by straight lines, trilateral
figures being those contained by three, quadrilateral those contained by four,
and multilateral those contained by more than four straight lines.
def:euclid-rectilinear-figures.statement_tex:
Rectilinear figures are those which are contained by straight lines, trilateral
figures being those contained by three, quadrilateral those contained by four,
and multilateral those contained by more than four straight lines.

def:euclid-triangle-side-classes.title: Equilateral, isosceles, and scalene triangles
def:euclid-triangle-side-classes.statement_preview: Of trilateral figures, an equilateral triangle is that which has its three sides equal, an isosceles triangle that which has two of its sides alone equal, and a scalene triangle that which has its three sides unequal.
def:euclid-triangle-side-classes.statement_display:
Of trilateral figures, an equilateral triangle is that which has its three
sides equal, an isosceles triangle that which has two of its sides alone equal,
and a scalene triangle that which has its three sides unequal.
def:euclid-triangle-side-classes.statement_tex:
Of trilateral figures, an equilateral triangle is that which has its three
sides equal, an isosceles triangle that which has two of its sides alone equal,
and a scalene triangle that which has its three sides unequal.

def:euclid-triangle-angle-classes.title: Right-angled, obtuse-angled, and acute-angled triangles
def:euclid-triangle-angle-classes.statement_preview: Further, of trilateral figures, a right-angled triangle is that which has a right angle, an obtuse-angled triangle that which has an obtuse angle, and an acute-angled triangle that which has its three angles acute.
def:euclid-triangle-angle-classes.statement_display:
Further, of trilateral figures, a right-angled triangle is that which has a
right angle, an obtuse-angled triangle that which has an obtuse angle, and an
acute-angled triangle that which has its three angles acute.
def:euclid-triangle-angle-classes.statement_tex:
Further, of trilateral figures, a right-angled triangle is that which has a
right angle, an obtuse-angled triangle that which has an obtuse angle, and an
acute-angled triangle that which has its three angles acute.

def:euclid-quadrilateral-classes.title: Quadrilateral classes
def:euclid-quadrilateral-classes.statement_preview: Of quadrilateral figures, a square is that which is both equilateral and right-angled; an oblong that which is right-angled but not equilateral; a rhombus that which is equilateral but not right-angled; and a rhomboid that which has its opposite sides and angles equal to one another but is neither equilateral nor right-angled. And let quadrilaterals other t...
def:euclid-quadrilateral-classes.statement_display:
Of quadrilateral figures, a square is that which is both equilateral and
right-angled; an oblong that which is right-angled but not equilateral; a
rhombus that which is equilateral but not right-angled; and a rhomboid that
which has its opposite sides and angles equal to one another but is neither
equilateral nor right-angled. And let quadrilaterals other than these be called
trapezia.
def:euclid-quadrilateral-classes.statement_tex:
Of quadrilateral figures, a square is that which is both equilateral and
right-angled; an oblong that which is right-angled but not equilateral; a
rhombus that which is equilateral but not right-angled; and a rhomboid that
which has its opposite sides and angles equal to one another but is neither
equilateral nor right-angled. And let quadrilaterals other than these be called
trapezia.

def:euclid-parallel-straight-lines.title: Parallel straight lines
def:euclid-parallel-straight-lines.statement_preview: Parallel straight lines are straight lines which, being in the same plane and being produced indefinitely in both directions, do not meet one another in either direction.
def:euclid-parallel-straight-lines.statement_display:
Parallel straight lines are straight lines which, being in the same plane and
being produced indefinitely in both directions, do not meet one another in
either direction.
def:euclid-parallel-straight-lines.statement_tex:
Parallel straight lines are straight lines which, being in the same plane and
being produced indefinitely in both directions, do not meet one another in
either direction.

ax:euclid-postulate-draw-straight-line.title: Euclid Postulate I: Drawing a straight line
ax:euclid-postulate-draw-straight-line.statement_preview: Let it have been postulated to draw a straight line from any point to any point.
ax:euclid-postulate-draw-straight-line.statement_display: Let it have been postulated to draw a straight line from any point to any point.
ax:euclid-postulate-draw-straight-line.statement_tex: Let it have been postulated to draw a straight line from any point to any point.

ax:euclid-postulate-produce-straight-lin.title: Euclid Postulate II: Producing a finite straight line
ax:euclid-postulate-produce-straight-lin.statement_preview: Let it have been postulated to produce a finite straight line continuously in a straight line.
ax:euclid-postulate-produce-straight-lin.statement_display:
Let it have been postulated to produce a finite straight line continuously in a
straight line.
ax:euclid-postulate-produce-straight-lin.statement_tex:
Let it have been postulated to produce a finite straight line continuously in a
straight line.

ax:euclid-postulate-describe-circle.title: Euclid Postulate III: Describing a circle
ax:euclid-postulate-describe-circle.statement_preview: Let it have been postulated to describe a circle with any center and distance.

ax:euclid-postulate-right-angles-equal.statement_preview: Let it have been postulated that all right angles are equal to one another.

ax:euclid-postulate-parallel.title: Euclid Postulate V: Parallel postulate
ax:euclid-postulate-parallel.statement_preview: Let it have been postulated that, if a straight line falling on two straight lines makes the interior angles on the same side less than two right angles, the two straight lines, if produced indefinitely, meet on that side on which are the angles less than the two right angles.

def:computational-geometry-convex-set.title: Convex Set
def:computational-geometry-convex-set.statement_preview: A set \(S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2\) is \emph{convex} if for every pair of points \(a,b\in S\) and every \(\lambda\in[0,1]\), \[ \lambda a+(1-\lambda)b\in S. \] Equivalently, \(S\) contains the line segment between any two of its points.
def:computational-geometry-convex-set.statement_display:
A set \(S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2\) is \emph{convex} if for every pair of points
\(a,b\in S\) and every \(\lambda\in[0,1]\),
\[
  \lambda a+(1-\lambda)b\in S.
\]
Equivalently, \(S\) contains the line segment between any two of its points.
def:computational-geometry-convex-set.statement_tex:
A set \(S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2\) is \emph{convex} if for every pair of points
\(a,b\in S\) and every \(\lambda\in[0,1]\),
\[
  \lambda a+(1-\lambda)b\in S.
\]
Equivalently, \(S\) contains the line segment between any two of its points.

def:computational-geometry-convex-combin.title: Convex Combination
def:computational-geometry-convex-combin.statement_preview: A \emph{convex combination} of points \(q_1,\dots,q_n\in\mathbb{R}^2\) is a point of the form \[ \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i q_i, \qquad \lambda_i\ge 0 \text{ for all } i, \qquad \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i=1. \] The numbers \(\lambda_i\) are called the weights.
def:computational-geometry-convex-combin.statement_display:
A \emph{convex combination} of points
\(q_1,\dots,q_n\in\mathbb{R}^2\) is a point of the form
\[
  \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i q_i,
  \qquad
  \lambda_i\ge 0 \text{ for all } i,
  \qquad
  \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i=1.
\]
The numbers \(\lambda_i\) are called the weights.
def:computational-geometry-convex-combin.statement_tex:
A \emph{convex combination} of points
\(q_1,\dots,q_n\in\mathbb{R}^2\) is a point of the form
\[
  \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i q_i,
  \qquad
  \lambda_i\ge 0 \text{ for all } i,
  \qquad
  \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i=1.
\]
The numbers \(\lambda_i\) are called the weights.

def:computational-geometry-convex-hull.title: Convex Hull
def:computational-geometry-convex-hull.statement_preview: The \emph{convex hull} of a set \(P\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2\), written \(\conv(P)\), is the set of all convex combinations of finitely many points of \(P\): \[ \conv(P) = \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i q_i \;\middle|\; n\ge 1,\ q_i\in P,\ \lambda_i\ge 0,\ \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i=1 \right\}. \] Equivalently, \(\conv(P)\) is the smallest convex set containing \...
def:computational-geometry-convex-hull.statement_display:
The \emph{convex hull} of a set \(P\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2\), written
\(\conv(P)\), is the set of all convex combinations of finitely many points of
\(P\):
\[
  \conv(P)
  =
  \left\{
    \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i q_i
    \;\middle|\;
    n\ge 1,\ q_i\in P,\ \lambda_i\ge 0,\ \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i=1
  \right\}.
\]
Equivalently, \(\conv(P)\) is the smallest convex set containing \(P\), i.e. the
intersection of all convex sets that contain \(P\).
def:computational-geometry-convex-hull.statement_tex:
The \emph{convex hull} of a set \(P\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2\), written
\(\conv(P)\), is the set of all convex combinations of finitely many points of
\(P\):
\[
  \conv(P)
  =
  \left\{
    \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i q_i
    \;\middle|\;
    n\ge 1,\ q_i\in P,\ \lambda_i\ge 0,\ \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i=1
  \right\}.
\]
Equivalently, \(\conv(P)\) is the smallest convex set containing \(P\), i.e. the
intersection of all convex sets that contain \(P\).

lem:computational-geometry-convex-hull-s.title: Convex hull as the smallest convex set
lem:computational-geometry-convex-hull-s.statement_preview: Let \(P\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2\). Then \(\conv(P)\) is convex, contains \(P\), and is contained in every convex set that contains \(P\). Hence \[ \conv(P) = \bigcap\{K\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2 : K\text{ is convex and }P\subseteq K\}. \]
lem:computational-geometry-convex-hull-s.statement_display:
Let \(P\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2\). Then \(\conv(P)\) is convex, contains \(P\), and
is contained in every convex set that contains \(P\). Hence
\[
  \conv(P)
  =
  \bigcap\{K\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2 : K\text{ is convex and }P\subseteq K\}.
\]
lem:computational-geometry-convex-hull-s.statement_tex:
Let \(P\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2\). Then \(\conv(P)\) is convex, contains \(P\), and
is contained in every convex set that contains \(P\). Hence
\[
  \conv(P)
  =
  \bigcap\{K\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2 : K\text{ is convex and }P\subseteq K\}.
\]

def:computational-geometry-extreme-point.title: Extreme Point
def:computational-geometry-extreme-point.statement_preview: Let \(S\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2\) be convex. A point \(p\in S\) is an \emph{extreme point} of \(S\) if whenever \[ p=\lambda a+(1-\lambda)b, \qquad a,b\in S,\quad \lambda\in(0,1), \] it follows that \(a=b=p\). For a finite point set \(P\), the extreme points of \(\conv(P)\) are called the vertices of the convex hull.
def:computational-geometry-extreme-point.statement_display:
Let \(S\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2\) be convex. A point \(p\in S\) is an
\emph{extreme point} of \(S\) if whenever
\[
  p=\lambda a+(1-\lambda)b,
  \qquad
  a,b\in S,\quad \lambda\in(0,1),
\]
it follows that \(a=b=p\). For a finite point set \(P\), the extreme points of
\(\conv(P)\) are called the vertices of the convex hull.
def:computational-geometry-extreme-point.statement_tex:
Let \(S\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2\) be convex. A point \(p\in S\) is an
\emph{extreme point} of \(S\) if whenever
\[
  p=\lambda a+(1-\lambda)b,
  \qquad
  a,b\in S,\quad \lambda\in(0,1),
\]
it follows that \(a=b=p\). For a finite point set \(P\), the extreme points of
\(\conv(P)\) are called the vertices of the convex hull.

def:computational-geometry-supporting-li.title: Supporting Line
def:computational-geometry-supporting-li.statement_preview: A line \(\ell\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2\) is a \emph{supporting line} of a convex set \(S\) at a point \(p\in S\) if \(p\in\ell\) and \(S\) lies entirely in one of the two closed half-planes bounded by \(\ell\). Equivalently, there exists \(d\in\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}\) such that \[ \ell=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^2:\langle d,x\rangle=\langle d,p\rangle\} \] and \[ \la...
def:computational-geometry-supporting-li.statement_display:
A line \(\ell\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2\) is a \emph{supporting line} of a convex set
\(S\) at a point \(p\in S\) if \(p\in\ell\) and \(S\) lies entirely in one of the
two closed half-planes bounded by \(\ell\). Equivalently, there exists
\(d\in\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}\) such that
\[
  \ell=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^2:\langle d,x\rangle=\langle d,p\rangle\}
\]
and
\[
  \langle d,q\rangle\le \langle d,p\rangle
  \qquad
  \text{for all } q\in S.
\]
def:computational-geometry-supporting-li.statement_tex:
A line \(\ell\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2\) is a \emph{supporting line} of a convex set
\(S\) at a point \(p\in S\) if \(p\in\ell\) and \(S\) lies entirely in one of the
two closed half-planes bounded by \(\ell\). Equivalently, there exists
\(d\in\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}\) such that
\[
  \ell=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^2:\langle d,x\rangle=\langle d,p\rangle\}
\]
and
\[
  \langle d,q\rangle\le \langle d,p\rangle
  \qquad
  \text{for all } q\in S.
\]

lem:computational-geometry-linear-functi.title: Linear functionals preserve convex combinations
lem:computational-geometry-linear-functi.statement_preview: Let \(d\in\mathbb{R}^2\), and let \[ z=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i q_i \] be a convex combination of points \(q_1,\dots,q_n\in\mathbb{R}^2\). Then \[ \langle d,z\rangle = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i\langle d,q_i\rangle. \] Consequently, if \(\langle d,q_i\rangle\le M\) for every \(i\), then \[ \langle d,z\rangle\le M. \]
lem:computational-geometry-linear-functi.statement_display:
Let \(d\in\mathbb{R}^2\), and let
\[
  z=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i q_i
\]
be a convex combination of points \(q_1,\dots,q_n\in\mathbb{R}^2\). Then
\[
  \langle d,z\rangle
  =
  \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i\langle d,q_i\rangle.
\]
Consequently, if \(\langle d,q_i\rangle\le M\) for every \(i\), then
\[
  \langle d,z\rangle\le M.
\]
lem:computational-geometry-linear-functi.statement_tex:
Let \(d\in\mathbb{R}^2\), and let
\[
  z=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i q_i
\]
be a convex combination of points \(q_1,\dots,q_n\in\mathbb{R}^2\). Then
\[
  \langle d,z\rangle
  =
  \sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i\langle d,q_i\rangle.
\]
Consequently, if \(\langle d,q_i\rangle\le M\) for every \(i\), then
\[
  \langle d,z\rangle\le M.
\]

thm:computational-geometry-supporting-fu.title: Supporting functional characterization of hull vertices
thm:computational-geometry-supporting-fu.statement_preview: Let \(P\subset\mathbb{R}^2\) be finite and in general position. A point \(p\in P\) is a vertex of \(\conv(P)\) if and only if there exists a direction \(d\in\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}\) such that \[ \langle d,p\rangle>\langle d,q\rangle \qquad \text{for all } q\in P\setminus\{p\}. \] Equivalently, \(p\) is the unique point of \(P\) attaining \[ \max_{q\in P...
thm:computational-geometry-supporting-fu.statement_display:
Let \(P\subset\mathbb{R}^2\) be finite and in general position. A point \(p\in P\)
is a vertex of \(\conv(P)\) if and only if there exists a direction
\(d\in\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}\) such that
\[
  \langle d,p\rangle>\langle d,q\rangle
  \qquad
  \text{for all } q\in P\setminus\{p\}.
\]
Equivalently, \(p\) is the unique point of \(P\) attaining
\[
  \max_{q\in P}\langle d,q\rangle.
\]
Thus every hull vertex is a maximum of \(P\) under some linear functional.
thm:computational-geometry-supporting-fu.statement_tex:
Let \(P\subset\mathbb{R}^2\) be finite and in general position. A point \(p\in P\)
is a vertex of \(\conv(P)\) if and only if there exists a direction
\(d\in\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}\) such that
\[
  \langle d,p\rangle>\langle d,q\rangle
  \qquad
  \text{for all } q\in P\setminus\{p\}.
\]
Equivalently, \(p\) is the unique point of \(P\) attaining
\[
  \max_{q\in P}\langle d,q\rangle.
\]
Thus every hull vertex is a maximum of \(P\) under some linear functional.

thm:computational-geometry-monotone-slop.title: Monotone slope characterization of the upper hull
thm:computational-geometry-monotone-slop.statement_preview: Let \(P\subset\mathbb{R}^2\) be finite and in general position. Let \[ v_0,v_1,\dots,v_m \] be the vertices of the upper hull of \(P\), listed in increasing \(x\)-coordinate, and define \[ s_k = \frac{y(v_{k+1})-y(v_k)}{x(v_{k+1})-x(v_k)}, \qquad k=0,1,\dots,m-1. \] Then \[ s_0>s_1>\cdots>s_{m-1}. \] Conversely, if points \(w_0,\dots,w_m\) satisfy \[ x(w_0)...
thm:computational-geometry-monotone-slop.statement_display:
Let \(P\subset\mathbb{R}^2\) be finite and in general position. Let
\[
  v_0,v_1,\dots,v_m
\]
be the vertices of the upper hull of \(P\), listed in increasing
\(x\)-coordinate, and define
\[
  s_k
  =
  \frac{y(v_{k+1})-y(v_k)}{x(v_{k+1})-x(v_k)},
  \qquad
  k=0,1,\dots,m-1.
\]
Then
\[
  s_0>s_1>\cdots>s_{m-1}.
\]
Conversely, if points \(w_0,\dots,w_m\) satisfy
\[
  x(w_0)<x(w_1)<\cdots<x(w_m)
\]
and their consecutive slopes are strictly decreasing, then every \(w_k\) is a
vertex of
\[
  \conv(\{w_0,\dots,w_m\}).
\]
The lower hull is characterized similarly, with strictly increasing consecutive
slopes.
thm:computational-geometry-monotone-slop.statement_tex:
Let \(P\subset\mathbb{R}^2\) be finite and in general position. Let
\[
  v_0,v_1,\dots,v_m
\]
be the vertices of the upper hull of \(P\), listed in increasing
\(x\)-coordinate, and define
\[
  s_k
  =
  \frac{y(v_{k+1})-y(v_k)}{x(v_{k+1})-x(v_k)},
  \qquad
  k=0,1,\dots,m-1.
\]
Then
\[
  s_0>s_1>\cdots>s_{m-1}.
\]
Conversely, if points \(w_0,\dots,w_m\) satisfy
\[
  x(w_0)<x(w_1)<\cdots<x(w_m)
\]
and their consecutive slopes are strictly decreasing, then every \(w_k\) is a
vertex of
\[
  \conv(\{w_0,\dots,w_m\}).
\]
The lower hull is characterized similarly, with strictly increasing consecutive
slopes.

thm:computational-geometry-greedy-slope-.title: Greedy slope construction of the upper hull
thm:computational-geometry-greedy-slope-.statement_preview: Let \(P\subset\mathbb{R}^2\) be finite and in general position. Define \[ v_0=\argmin_{p\in P}x(p). \] Given \(v_k\) with \[ x(v_k)<\max_{p\in P}x(p), \] define \[ v_{k+1} = \argmax_{\substack{q\in P\\x(q)>x(v_k)}} \frac{y(q)-y(v_k)}{x(q)-x(v_k)}. \] Terminate once \[ v_k=\argmax_{p\in P}x(p). \] Then the points \[ v_0,v_1,\dots,v_m \] produced by this cons...
thm:computational-geometry-greedy-slope-.statement_display:
Let \(P\subset\mathbb{R}^2\) be finite and in general position. Define
\[
  v_0=\argmin_{p\in P}x(p).
\]
Given \(v_k\) with
\[
  x(v_k)<\max_{p\in P}x(p),
\]
define
\[
  v_{k+1}
  =
  \argmax_{\substack{q\in P\\x(q)>x(v_k)}}
  \frac{y(q)-y(v_k)}{x(q)-x(v_k)}.
\]
Terminate once
\[
  v_k=\argmax_{p\in P}x(p).
\]
Then the points
\[
  v_0,v_1,\dots,v_m
\]
produced by this construction are exactly the vertices of the upper hull of
\(P\), and the slopes encountered along the way are strictly decreasing.
thm:computational-geometry-greedy-slope-.statement_tex:
Let \(P\subset\mathbb{R}^2\) be finite and in general position. Define
\[
  v_0=\argmin_{p\in P}x(p).
\]
Given \(v_k\) with
\[
  x(v_k)<\max_{p\in P}x(p),
\]
define
\[
  v_{k+1}
  =
  \argmax_{\substack{q\in P\\x(q)>x(v_k)}}
  \frac{y(q)-y(v_k)}{x(q)-x(v_k)}.
\]
Terminate once
\[
  v_k=\argmax_{p\in P}x(p).
\]
Then the points
\[
  v_0,v_1,\dots,v_m
\]
produced by this construction are exactly the vertices of the upper hull of
\(P\), and the slopes encountered along the way are strictly decreasing.

def:ordinary-differential-equations-mode.title: Malthusian Population Growth Model
def:ordinary-differential-equations-mode.statement_preview: The \textbf{Malthusian population growth model} assumes that the rate of change of a population is proportional to the current population. If $P(t)$ denotes the population at time $t$, then the model is \[ P'(t)=kP(t), \] where $k$ is a constant called the \textbf{growth rate} or \textbf{proportionality constant}.
def:ordinary-differential-equations-mode.statement_display:
The \textbf{Malthusian population growth model} assumes that the rate of change of a population is proportional to the current population.

If $P(t)$ denotes the population at time $t$, then the model is
\[
P'(t)=kP(t),
\]
where $k$ is a constant called the \textbf{growth rate} or \textbf{proportionality constant}.
def:ordinary-differential-equations-mode.statement_tex:

If $P(t)$ denotes the population at time $t$, then the model is
\[
P'(t)=kP(t),
\]
where $k$ is a constant called the \textbf{growth rate} or \textbf{proportionality constant}.

def:ordinary-differential-equations-mode.title: Decay
def:ordinary-differential-equations-mode.statement_preview: A quantity is said to undergo \textbf{decay} if it decreases over time. In a differential equation model, if $Q(t)$ denotes the quantity at time $t$, then decay is often modeled by an equation of the form \[ Q'(t)=-kQ(t), \] where $k>0$ is a constant.
def:ordinary-differential-equations-mode.statement_display:
A quantity is said to undergo \textbf{decay} if it decreases over time.

In a differential equation model, if $Q(t)$ denotes the quantity at time $t$, then decay is often modeled by an equation of the form
\[
Q'(t)=-kQ(t),
\]
where $k>0$ is a constant.

In a differential equation model, if $Q(t)$ denotes the quantity at time $t$, then decay is often modeled by an equation of the form

def:ordinary-differential-equations-mode.title: Newton's Law of Cooling
def:ordinary-differential-equations-mode.statement_preview: \textbf{Newton's Law of Cooling} states that the rate at which an object's temperature changes is proportional to the difference between the object's temperature and the surrounding ambient temperature. If $T(t)$ denotes the temperature of the object at time $t$, and $T_a$ denotes the ambient temperature, then the model is \[ T'(t)=-k\bigl(T(t)-T_a\bigr), \...
def:ordinary-differential-equations-mode.statement_display:
\textbf{Newton's Law of Cooling} states that the rate at which an object's temperature changes is proportional to the difference between the object's temperature and the surrounding ambient temperature.

If $T(t)$ denotes the temperature of the object at time $t$, and $T_a$ denotes the ambient temperature, then the model is
\[
T'(t)=-k\bigl(T(t)-T_a\bigr),
\]
where $k>0$ is a constant called the \textbf{cooling constant}.
def:ordinary-differential-equations-mode.statement_tex:
\textbf{Newton's Law of Cooling} states that the rate at which an object's temperature changes is proportional to the difference between the object's temperature and the surrounding ambient temperature.

If $T(t)$ denotes the temperature of the object at time $t$, and $T_a$ denotes the ambient temperature, then the model is
\[
T'(t)=-k\bigl(T(t)-T_a\bigr),
\]
where $k>0$ is a constant called the \textbf{cooling constant}.

def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.title: General Solution
def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.statement_preview: A \textbf{general solution} of a differential equation is a family of solutions containing one or more arbitrary constants. For an $n$th-order ordinary differential equation, the general solution typically contains $n$ arbitrary constants: \[ y(t)=\Phi(t,C_1,C_2,\dots,C_n). \] Each choice of the constants $C_1,C_2,\dots,C_n$ gives a particular solution of t...
def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.statement_display:
A \textbf{general solution} of a differential equation is a family of solutions containing one or more arbitrary constants.

For an $n$th-order ordinary differential equation, the general solution typically contains $n$ arbitrary constants:
\[
y(t)=\Phi(t,C_1,C_2,\dots,C_n).
\]
Each choice of the constants $C_1,C_2,\dots,C_n$ gives a particular solution of the differential equation.
def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.statement_tex:
A \textbf{general solution} of a differential equation is a family of solutions containing one or more arbitrary constants.

For an $n$th-order ordinary differential equation, the general solution typically contains $n$ arbitrary constants:
\[
y(t)=\Phi(t,C_1,C_2,\dots,C_n).
\]
Each choice of the constants $C_1,C_2,\dots,C_n$ gives a particular solution of the differential equation.

def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.title: Family of Solutions
def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.statement_preview: A \textbf{family of solutions} of a differential equation is a collection of solution functions described by one or more parameters. For example, a family of solutions may be written in the form \[ y(t)=\Phi(t,C), \] or more generally, \[ y(t)=\Phi(t,C_1,C_2,\dots,C_n), \] where $C,C_1,\dots,C_n$ are parameters. Each choice of the parameters gives one parti...
def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.statement_display:
A \textbf{family of solutions} of a differential equation is a collection of solution functions described by one or more parameters.

For example, a family of solutions may be written in the form
\[
y(t)=\Phi(t,C),
\]
or more generally,
\[
y(t)=\Phi(t,C_1,C_2,\dots,C_n),
\]
where $C,C_1,\dots,C_n$ are parameters. Each choice of the parameters gives one particular solution.
def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.statement_tex:
A \textbf{family of solutions} of a differential equation is a collection of solution functions described by one or more parameters.

For example, a family of solutions may be written in the form
\[
y(t)=\Phi(t,C),
\]
or more generally,
\[
y(t)=\Phi(t,C_1,C_2,\dots,C_n),
\]
where $C,C_1,\dots,C_n$ are parameters. Each choice of the parameters gives one particular solution.

def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.title: Initial Condition
def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.statement_preview: An \textbf{initial condition} specifies the value of the unknown function at a particular value of the independent variable. For a first-order differential equation involving an unknown function $y=y(t)$, an initial condition has the form \[ y(t_0)=y_0, \] where $t_0$ is the initial time and $y_0$ is the initial value.
def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.statement_display:
An \textbf{initial condition} specifies the value of the unknown function at a particular value of the independent variable.

For a first-order differential equation involving an unknown function $y=y(t)$, an initial condition has the form
\[
y(t_0)=y_0,
\]
where $t_0$ is the initial time and $y_0$ is the initial value.

where $t_0$ is the initial time and $y_0$ is the initial value.

def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.title: Initial Value Problem
def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.statement_preview: An \textbf{initial value problem}, or \textbf{IVP}, is a differential equation together with one or more initial conditions. For a first-order differential equation, an initial value problem has the form \[ y'=f(t,y), \qquad y(t_0)=y_0. \] The differential equation describes how the function changes, while the initial condition specifies the value of the so...
def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.statement_display:
An \textbf{initial value problem}, or \textbf{IVP}, is a differential equation together with one or more initial conditions.

For a first-order differential equation, an initial value problem has the form
\[
y'=f(t,y),
\qquad
y(t_0)=y_0.
\]
The differential equation describes how the function changes, while the initial condition specifies the value of the solution at the initial time $t_0$.
def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.statement_tex:

\[
y'=f(t,y),
\qquad
y(t_0)=y_0.
\]
The differential equation describes how the function changes, while the initial condition specifies the value of the solution at the initial time $t_0$.

def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.title: Existence
def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.statement_preview: An initial value problem is said to have \textbf{existence} if there is at least one function that satisfies both the differential equation and the initial condition. For a first-order initial value problem \[ y'=f(t,y), \qquad y(t_0)=y_0, \] existence means that there is a function $y(t)$, defined on some interval containing $t_0$, such that \[ y'(t)=f(t,y...
def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.statement_display:
An initial value problem is said to have \textbf{existence} if there is at least one function that satisfies both the differential equation and the initial condition.

For a first-order initial value problem
\[
y'=f(t,y),
\qquad
y(t_0)=y_0,
\]
existence means that there is a function $y(t)$, defined on some interval containing $t_0$, such that
\[
y'(t)=f(t,y(t))
\]
and
\[
y(t_0)=y_0.
\]
def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.statement_tex:
An initial value problem is said to have \textbf{existence} if there is at least one function that satisfies both the differential equation and the initial condition.

\[

\]
and
\[
y(t_0)=y_0.
\]

def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.title: Uniqueness
def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.statement_preview: An initial value problem is said to have \textbf{uniqueness} if it has at most one solution on the interval under consideration. For a first-order initial value problem \[ y'=f(t,y), \qquad y(t_0)=y_0, \] uniqueness means that if $y_1(t)$ and $y_2(t)$ are both solutions on the same interval $I$ containing $t_0$, then \[ y_1(t)=y_2(t) \] for every $t\in I$.
def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.statement_display:

\[

\]
for every $t\in I$.

def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.title: Interval of Existence
def:ordinary-differential-equations-solu.statement_preview: An \textbf{interval of existence} for a solution of an initial value problem is an interval on which the solution is defined and satisfies the differential equation. For an initial value problem \[ y'=f(t,y), \qquad y(t_0)=y_0, \] an interval $I$ is an interval of existence if $t_0\in I$ and there is a solution $y:I\to\mathbb{R}$ such that \[ y'(t)=f(t,y(t)...

\[
y'(t)=f(t,y(t))
\]
for every $t\in I$, and
\[
y(t_0)=y_0.
\]

def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.title: Differential Equation
def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.statement_preview: A \textbf{differential equation} is an equation whose unknown is a function and whose terms involve one or more derivatives of that function. For an unknown function $y=y(t)$, a differential equation may be written in the form \[ F(t,y,y',y'',\dots,y^{(n)})=0. \] Here $t$ is the independent variable, $y(t)$ is the unknown function, and \[ y',y'',\dots,y^{(n...
def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.statement_display:
A \textbf{differential equation} is an equation whose unknown is a function and whose terms involve one or more derivatives of that function.

For an unknown function $y=y(t)$, a differential equation may be written in the form
\[
F(t,y,y',y'',\dots,y^{(n)})=0.
\]
Here $t$ is the independent variable, $y(t)$ is the unknown function, and
\[
y',y'',\dots,y^{(n)}
\]
are derivatives of $y$.
def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.statement_tex:
A \textbf{differential equation} is an equation whose unknown is a function and whose terms involve one or more derivatives of that function.

For an unknown function $y=y(t)$, a differential equation may be written in the form
\[
F(t,y,y',y'',\dots,y^{(n)})=0.
\]
Here $t$ is the independent variable, $y(t)$ is the unknown function, and
\[
y',y'',\dots,y^{(n)}
\]
are derivatives of $y$.

def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.title: Order of a Differential Equation
def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.statement_preview: The \textbf{order} of a differential equation is the highest derivative of the unknown function that appears in the equation. For example, if an equation involving an unknown function $y=y(t)$ can be written in the form \[ F(t,y,y',y'',\dots,y^{(n)})=0 \] and $y^{(n)}$ is the highest derivative that appears, then the differential equation has \textbf{order}...
def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.statement_display:
The \textbf{order} of a differential equation is the highest derivative of the unknown function that appears in the equation.

For example, if an equation involving an unknown function $y=y(t)$ can be written in the form
\[
F(t,y,y',y'',\dots,y^{(n)})=0
\]
and $y^{(n)}$ is the highest derivative that appears, then the differential equation has \textbf{order} $n$.
def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.statement_tex:
The \textbf{order} of a differential equation is the highest derivative of the unknown function that appears in the equation.

For example, if an equation involving an unknown function $y=y(t)$ can be written in the form
\[
F(t,y,y',y'',\dots,y^{(n)})=0
\]
and $y^{(n)}$ is the highest derivative that appears, then the differential equation has \textbf{order} $n$.

def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.title: Solution of a Differential Equation
def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.statement_preview: A \textbf{solution} of a differential equation is a function that satisfies the equation on a specified interval. More precisely, if a differential equation is written in the form \[ F(t,y,y',y'',\dots,y^{(n)})=0, \] then a solution on an interval $I$ is a function $y:I\to\mathbb{R}$ such that $y$ has all derivatives required by the equation and \[ F(t,y(t)...
def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.statement_display:
A \textbf{solution} of a differential equation is a function that satisfies the equation on a specified interval.

More precisely, if a differential equation is written in the form
\[
F(t,y,y',y'',\dots,y^{(n)})=0,
\]
then a solution on an interval $I$ is a function $y:I\to\mathbb{R}$ such that $y$ has all derivatives required by the equation and
\[
F(t,y(t),y'(t),y''(t),\dots,y^{(n)}(t))=0
\]
for every $t\in I$.
def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.statement_tex:
A \textbf{solution} of a differential equation is a function that satisfies the equation on a specified interval.

More precisely, if a differential equation is written in the form
\[
F(t,y,y',y'',\dots,y^{(n)})=0,
\]
then a solution on an interval $I$ is a function $y:I\to\mathbb{R}$ such that $y$ has all derivatives required by the equation and
\[
F(t,y(t),y'(t),y''(t),\dots,y^{(n)}(t))=0
\]
for every $t\in I$.

def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.title: Autonomous Differential Equation
def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.statement_preview: An \textbf{autonomous differential equation} is a differential equation in which the independent variable does not appear explicitly. For a first-order ordinary differential equation, an autonomous equation has the form \[ y'=f(y), \] where the rate of change of $y$ depends only on the current value of $y$, not directly on time $t$.
def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.statement_display:
An \textbf{autonomous differential equation} is a differential equation in which the independent variable does not appear explicitly.

For a first-order ordinary differential equation, an autonomous equation has the form
\[
y'=f(y),
\]
where the rate of change of $y$ depends only on the current value of $y$, not directly on time $t$.
def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.statement_tex:

def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.title: Non-Autonomous Differential Equation
def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.statement_preview: A \textbf{non-autonomous differential equation} is a differential equation in which the independent variable appears explicitly. For a first-order ordinary differential equation, a non-autonomous equation has the form \[ y'=f(t,y), \] where the rate of change of $y$ may depend both on the current value of $y$ and on the independent variable $t$.
def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.statement_display:
A \textbf{non-autonomous differential equation} is a differential equation in which the independent variable appears explicitly.

For a first-order ordinary differential equation, a non-autonomous equation has the form
\[

\]
where the rate of change of $y$ may depend both on the current value of $y$ and on the independent variable $t$.
