def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.title: Linear Differential Equation
def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.statement_preview: A differential equation is called \textbf{linear} if the unknown function and its derivatives appear only to the first power and are not multiplied together. An $n$th-order linear ordinary differential equation for an unknown function $y=y(t)$ has the form \[ a_n(t)y^{(n)}+a_{n-1}(t)y^{(n-1)}+\cdots+a_1(t)y'+a_0(t)y=g(t), \] where the coefficient functions...
def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.statement_display:
A differential equation is called \textbf{linear} if the unknown function and its derivatives appear only to the first power and are not multiplied together.

An $n$th-order linear ordinary differential equation for an unknown function $y=y(t)$ has the form
\[
a_n(t)y^{(n)}+a_{n-1}(t)y^{(n-1)}+\cdots+a_1(t)y'+a_0(t)y=g(t),
\]
where the coefficient functions
\[
a_0(t),a_1(t),\dots,a_n(t)
\]
and the forcing term $g(t)$ are given functions of the independent variable $t$.
def:ordinary-differential-equations-term.statement_tex:
A differential equation is called \textbf{linear} if the unknown function and its derivatives appear only to the first power and are not multiplied together.

An $n$th-order linear ordinary differential equation for an unknown function $y=y(t)$ has the form
\[
a_n(t)y^{(n)}+a_{n-1}(t)y^{(n-1)}+\cdots+a_1(t)y'+a_0(t)y=g(t),
\]
where the coefficient functions
\[
a_0(t),a_1(t),\dots,a_n(t)
\]
and the forcing term $g(t)$ are given functions of the independent variable $t$.

def:numerical-analysis-ieee-754-binary-f.title: IEEE 754 Binary Floating-Point Datum
def:numerical-analysis-ieee-754-binary-f.statement_preview: An \emph{IEEE 754 binary floating-point datum} is a binary encoding partitioned into three fields: \begin{itemize} \item a \emph{sign field} consisting of one bit, \item an \emph{exponent field} consisting of a fixed number of bits, \item a \emph{fraction field} consisting of a fixed number of bits. \end{itemize} Its interpretation depends on the exponent-f...
def:numerical-analysis-ieee-754-binary-f.statement_display:
An \emph{IEEE 754 binary floating-point datum} is a binary encoding partitioned into
three fields:

\begin{itemize}
    \item a \emph{sign field} consisting of one bit,
    \item an \emph{exponent field} consisting of a fixed number of bits,
    \item a \emph{fraction field} consisting of a fixed number of bits.
\end{itemize}

Its interpretation depends on the exponent-field pattern and the fraction-field pattern.
def:numerical-analysis-ieee-754-binary-f.statement_tex:
An \emph{IEEE 754 binary floating-point datum} is a binary encoding partitioned into
three fields:

\begin{itemize}
    \item a \emph{sign field} consisting of one bit,
    \item an \emph{exponent field} consisting of a fixed number of bits,
    \item a \emph{fraction field} consisting of a fixed number of bits.
\end{itemize}

Its interpretation depends on the exponent-field pattern and the fraction-field pattern.

def:numerical-analysis-normalized-ieee-7.title: Normalized IEEE 754 Binary Number
def:numerical-analysis-normalized-ieee-7.statement_preview: Let an IEEE 754 binary floating-point format have exponent bias $\mathrm{bias}$. A finite encoded datum is called \emph{normalized} if its exponent field is neither all zeros nor all ones. If $s$ is the sign bit, if $e$ is the unsigned integer determined by the exponent field, and if $f$ is the binary fraction determined by the fraction field, then the repr...
def:numerical-analysis-normalized-ieee-7.statement_display:
Let an IEEE 754 binary floating-point format have exponent bias $\mathrm{bias}$.
A finite encoded datum is called \emph{normalized} if its exponent field is neither
all zeros nor all ones.

If $s$ is the sign bit, if $e$ is the unsigned integer determined by the exponent field,
and if $f$ is the binary fraction determined by the fraction field, then the represented
value is
\[
(-1)^s (1.f)_2 2^{e-\mathrm{bias}}.
\]

\]

def:numerical-analysis-ieee-754-infinity.title: IEEE 754 Infinity Encoding
def:numerical-analysis-ieee-754-infinity.statement_preview: An IEEE 754 binary floating-point datum encodes \emph{infinity} if its exponent field is all ones and its fraction field is all zeros. The sign bit determines whether the represented value is $+\infty$ or $-\infty$.
def:numerical-analysis-ieee-754-infinity.statement_display:

The sign bit determines whether the represented value is $+\infty$ or $-\infty$.

def:numerical-analysis-ieee-754-binary16.title: IEEE 754 Binary16 Format
def:numerical-analysis-ieee-754-binary16.statement_preview: The \emph{IEEE 754 binary16 format} is the 16-bit binary floating-point interchange format with the following field layout: \begin{itemize} \item $1$ sign bit, \item $5$ exponent bits, \item $10$ fraction bits, \item exponent bias $15$. \end{itemize} For a normalized finite binary16 datum, the represented value is \[ (-1)^s (1.f)_2 2^{e-15}. \]

def:box.title: Box / Product Interval
def:box.statement_preview: Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}^n$ with $a\le b$ coordinatewise (i.e., $a_i\le b_i$ for all $i$). The \emph{box} (or \emph{interval vector}) determined by $(a,b)$ is \[ [a,b] \;:=\; \{x\in\mathbb{R}^n : a\le x \le b\} \;=\;\prod_{i=1}^n [a_i,b_i]. \]
def:box.statement_display:
Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}^n$ with $a\le b$ coordinatewise (i.e., $a_i\le b_i$ for all $i$).
The \emph{box} (or \emph{interval vector}) determined by $(a,b)$ is
\[
[a,b] \;:=\; \{x\in\mathbb{R}^n : a\le x \le b\}
\;=\;\prod_{i=1}^n [a_i,b_i].
\]
def:box.statement_tex:
Let $a,b\in\mathbb{R}^n$ with $a\le b$ coordinatewise (i.e., $a_i\le b_i$ for all $i$).
The \emph{box} (or \emph{interval vector}) determined by $(a,b)$ is
\[
[a,b] \;:=\; \{x\in\mathbb{R}^n : a\le x \le b\}
\;=\;\prod_{i=1}^n [a_i,b_i].
\]

def:box-contain.title: Box Containment
def:box-contain.statement_preview: For $a\le b$ and $c\le d$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, we write $[a,b]\subseteq[c,d]$ iff \[ (\forall x\in\mathbb{R}^n)\ \bigl(x\in[a,b]\Rightarrow x\in[c,d]\bigr). \] Equivalently (and most useful computationally), \[ [a,b]\subseteq[c,d]\quad \Longleftrightarrow\quad c\le a\ \land\ b\le d \qquad\text{(coordinatewise).} \]
def:box-contain.statement_display:
For $a\le b$ and $c\le d$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, we write $[a,b]\subseteq[c,d]$ iff
\[
(\forall x\in\mathbb{R}^n)\ \bigl(x\in[a,b]\Rightarrow x\in[c,d]\bigr).
\]
Equivalently (and most useful computationally),
\[
[a,b]\subseteq[c,d]\quad \Longleftrightarrow\quad c\le a\ \land\ b\le d
\qquad\text{(coordinatewise).}
\]
def:box-contain.statement_tex:
For $a\le b$ and $c\le d$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, we write $[a,b]\subseteq[c,d]$ iff
\[
(\forall x\in\mathbb{R}^n)\ \bigl(x\in[a,b]\Rightarrow x\in[c,d]\bigr).
\]
Equivalently (and most useful computationally),
\[
[a,b]\subseteq[c,d]\quad \Longleftrightarrow\quad c\le a\ \land\ b\le d
\qquad\text{(coordinatewise).}
\]

def:box-add.title: Minkowski Addition of Boxes
def:box-add.statement_preview: For boxes $X=[a,b]$ and $Y=[c,d]$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, their \emph{Minkowski sum} is \[ X\oplus Y \;:=\; \{x+y : x\in X,\ y\in Y\}. \] For boxes, this closes in the class of boxes and satisfies the endpoint rule \[ [a,b]\oplus[c,d] \;=\; [a+c,\; b+d]. \]
def:box-add.statement_display:
For boxes $X=[a,b]$ and $Y=[c,d]$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, their \emph{Minkowski sum} is
\[
X\oplus Y \;:=\; \{x+y : x\in X,\ y\in Y\}.
\]
For boxes, this closes in the class of boxes and satisfies the endpoint rule
\[
[a,b]\oplus[c,d] \;=\; [a+c,\; b+d].
\]
def:box-add.statement_tex:
For boxes $X=[a,b]$ and $Y=[c,d]$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, their \emph{Minkowski sum} is
\[

\]
For boxes, this closes in the class of boxes and satisfies the endpoint rule
\[

def:box-sub.title: Box Subtraction / Difference
def:box-sub.statement_preview: For boxes $X=[a,b]$ and $Y=[c,d]$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, define \[ X\ominus Y \;:=\; \{x-y : x\in X,\ y\in Y\}. \] Then \[ [a,b]\ominus[c,d] \;=\; [a-d,\; b-c]. \]
def:box-sub.statement_display:
For boxes $X=[a,b]$ and $Y=[c,d]$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, define
\[
X\ominus Y \;:=\; \{x-y : x\in X,\ y\in Y\}.
\]
Then
\[
[a,b]\ominus[c,d] \;=\; [a-d,\; b-c].
\]
def:box-sub.statement_tex:

\]
Then
\[

def:box-smul.title: Scalar Multiplication of a Box
def:box-smul.statement_preview: For $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$ and a box $[a,b]\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n$, define \[ \lambda[a,b] \;:=\; \{\lambda x : x\in[a,b]\}. \] Then \[ \lambda[a,b]= \begin{cases} [\lambda a,\ \lambda b], & \lambda\ge 0,\\[4pt] [\lambda b,\ \lambda a], & \lambda<0, \end{cases} \] where the inequalities are coordinatewise.
def:box-smul.statement_display:
For $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$ and a box $[a,b]\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n$, define
\[
\lambda[a,b] \;:=\; \{\lambda x : x\in[a,b]\}.
\]
Then
\[
\lambda[a,b]=
\begin{cases}
[\lambda a,\ \lambda b], & \lambda\ge 0,\\[4pt]
[\lambda b,\ \lambda a], & \lambda<0,
\end{cases}
\]
where the inequalities are coordinatewise.
def:box-smul.statement_tex:
For $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$ and a box $[a,b]\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n$, define
\[
\lambda[a,b] \;:=\; \{\lambda x : x\in[a,b]\}.
\]
Then
\[
\lambda[a,b]=
\begin{cases}
[\lambda a,\ \lambda b], & \lambda\ge 0,\\[4pt]
[\lambda b,\ \lambda a], & \lambda<0,
\end{cases}
\]
where the inequalities are coordinatewise.

def:box-hadamard.title: Coordinatewise / Hadamard Product of Boxes
def:box-hadamard.statement_preview: For $x,y\in\mathbb{R}^n$, write $(x\odot y)_i := x_i y_i$. For boxes $X=[a,b]$ and $Y=[c,d]$, define \[ X\odot Y \;:=\; \{x\odot y : x\in X,\ y\in Y\}. \] Then $X\odot Y$ is again a box, computed coordinatewise: \[ [a,b]\odot[c,d] \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^n \Bigl[\min S_i,\ \max S_i\Bigr], \qquad S_i:=\{a_ic_i,\ a_id_i,\ b_ic_i,\ b_id_i\}. \]
def:box-hadamard.statement_display:
For $x,y\in\mathbb{R}^n$, write $(x\odot y)_i := x_i y_i$.
For boxes $X=[a,b]$ and $Y=[c,d]$, define
\[
X\odot Y \;:=\; \{x\odot y : x\in X,\ y\in Y\}.
\]
Then $X\odot Y$ is again a box, computed coordinatewise:
\[
[a,b]\odot[c,d] \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^n \Bigl[\min S_i,\ \max S_i\Bigr],
\qquad
S_i:=\{a_ic_i,\ a_id_i,\ b_ic_i,\ b_id_i\}.
\]
def:box-hadamard.statement_tex:
For $x,y\in\mathbb{R}^n$, write $(x\odot y)_i := x_i y_i$.
For boxes $X=[a,b]$ and $Y=[c,d]$, define
\[
X\odot Y \;:=\; \{x\odot y : x\in X,\ y\in Y\}.
\]
Then $X\odot Y$ is again a box, computed coordinatewise:
\[
[a,b]\odot[c,d] \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^n \Bigl[\min S_i,\ \max S_i\Bigr],
\qquad
S_i:=\{a_ic_i,\ a_id_i,\ b_ic_i,\ b_id_i\}.
\]

def:interval-add.title: Interval Addition
def:interval-add.statement_preview: For closed intervals $[a,b]$ and $[c,d]$, their \emph{sum} is \[ [a,b] + [c,d] \;=\; [a+c,\; b+d]. \]
def:interval-add.statement_display:
For closed intervals $[a,b]$ and $[c,d]$, their \emph{sum} is
\[
  [a,b] + [c,d] \;=\; [a+c,\; b+d].
\]
def:interval-add.statement_tex:
For closed intervals $[a,b]$ and $[c,d]$, their \emph{sum} is
\[
  [a,b] + [c,d] \;=\; [a+c,\; b+d].
\]

def:interval-sub.title: Interval Subtraction
def:interval-sub.statement_preview: For closed intervals $[a,b]$ and $[c,d]$, their \emph{difference} is \[ [a,b] - [c,d] \;=\; [a-d,\; b-c]. \]
def:interval-sub.statement_display:
For closed intervals $[a,b]$ and $[c,d]$, their \emph{difference} is
\[
  [a,b] - [c,d] \;=\; [a-d,\; b-c].

def:interval-mult.title: Interval Multiplication
def:interval-mult.statement_preview: For closed intervals $[a,b]$ and $[c,d]$, their \emph{product} is \[ [a,b] \cdot [c,d] \;=\; \bigl[\min(ac, ad, bc, bd),\; \max(ac, ad, bc, bd)\bigr]. \]

\[

  \bigl[\min(ac, ad, bc, bd),\; \max(ac, ad, bc, bd)\bigr].
\]

def:interval-contain.title: Interval Containment
def:interval-contain.statement_preview: $[a,b] \subseteq [c,d]$ if and only if $c \leq a$ and $b \leq d$.
def:interval-contain.statement_display: $[a,b] \subseteq [c,d]$ if and only if $c \leq a$ and $b \leq d$.
def:interval-contain.statement_tex: $[a,b] \subseteq [c,d]$ if and only if $c \leq a$ and $b \leq d$.

def:boolean-ring.title: Boolean Ring
def:boolean-ring.statement_preview: A \textbf{Boolean ring} is an idempotent ring with unit. That is, a Boolean ring is a ring $(R, +, \cdot, -, 0, 1)$ satisfying the additional axiom: \[ p \cdot p = p \qquad \text{for all } p \in R. \tag{11} \] An element $p$ satisfying $p \cdot p = p$ is called \textbf{idempotent}. A ring in which every element is idempotent is called an \textbf{idempotent...
def:boolean-ring.statement_display:
A \textbf{Boolean ring} is an idempotent ring with unit.

That is, a Boolean ring is a ring $(R, +, \cdot, -, 0, 1)$
satisfying the additional axiom:
\[
p \cdot p = p \qquad \text{for all } p \in R. \tag{11}
\]
An element $p$ satisfying $p \cdot p = p$ is called
\textbf{idempotent}.
A ring in which every element is idempotent is called an
\textbf{idempotent ring}.

The official axiom set for a Boolean ring consists of
axioms~(1)--(3), (5), (6), (8)--(11), where axiom~(7)
$($the additive inverse law$)$ is replaced by
axiom~(10) $($characteristic~$2)$, which is derived as a theorem.
Axiom numbering follows Halmos--Givant~\cite{HalmosGivant2009}.
def:boolean-ring.statement_tex:

\[
p \cdot p = p \qquad \text{for all } p \in R. \tag{11}
\]
An element $p$ satisfying $p \cdot p = p$ is called
\textbf{idempotent}.
A ring in which every element is idempotent is called an
\textbf{idempotent ring}.

The official axiom set for a Boolean ring consists of
axioms~(1)--(3), (5), (6), (8)--(11), where axiom~(7)
$($the additive inverse law$)$ is replaced by
axiom~(10) $($characteristic~$2)$, which is derived as a theorem.
Axiom numbering follows Halmos--Givant~\cite{HalmosGivant2009}.

def:idempotent-element.title: Idempotent Element
def:idempotent-element.statement_preview: An element $p$ of a ring $R$ is called \textbf{idempotent} if \[ p \cdot p = p. \] A ring in which every element is idempotent is called an \textbf{idempotent ring}.

def:characteristic-2.title: Characteristic 2
def:characteristic-2.statement_preview: A ring $R$ is said to have \textbf{characteristic~$2$} if \[ p + p = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p \in R. \tag{10} \]

lem:self-additive-inverse.statement_preview: In any Boolean ring $R$, \[ p + p = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p \in R. \]

def:ring-of-sets.title: Ring
def:ring-of-sets.statement_preview: A \textbf{ring} is a non-empty set $R$ together with two binary operations $+$ and $\cdot$ on $R$, a unary operation $-$ on $R$, and two distinguished elements $0, 1 \in R$, satisfying the following axioms for all $p, q, r \in R$: \begin{enumerate} \item[(1)] $p + (q + r) = (p + q) + r$ \hfill\textit{(additive associativity)} \item[(2)] $p \cdot (q \cdot r)...

def:lambda-terms.title: Lambda Terms
def:lambda-terms.statement_preview: The set $\Lambda$ of $\lambda$-terms is defined inductively as follows: \begin{enumerate} \item Every variable $x$ is a $\lambda$-term. \item If $M$ is a $\lambda$-term and $x$ is a variable, then \[ \lambda x.\,M \] is a $\lambda$-term. \item If $M$ and $N$ are $\lambda$-terms, then \[ MN \] is a $\lambda$-term. \end{enumerate}
def:lambda-terms.statement_display:
The set $\Lambda$ of $\lambda$-terms is defined inductively as follows:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Every variable $x$ is a $\lambda$-term.
    \item If $M$ is a $\lambda$-term and $x$ is a variable, then
    \[
    \lambda x.\,M
    \]
    is a $\lambda$-term.
    \item If $M$ and $N$ are $\lambda$-terms, then
    \[
    MN
    \]
    is a $\lambda$-term.
\end{enumerate}
def:lambda-terms.statement_tex:
The set $\Lambda$ of $\lambda$-terms is defined inductively as follows:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Every variable $x$ is a $\lambda$-term.
    \item If $M$ is a $\lambda$-term and $x$ is a variable, then
    \[
    \lambda x.\,M
    \]
    is a $\lambda$-term.
    \item If $M$ and $N$ are $\lambda$-terms, then
    \[
    MN
    \]
    is a $\lambda$-term.
\end{enumerate}

def:first-order-language.title: First-Order Language $\mathcal{L}$
def:first-order-language.statement_preview: A \textbf{first-order language} $\mathcal{L}$ consists of three (possibly empty) sets of non-logical symbols: \begin{itemize} \item a set $\mathcal{R}$ of \textbf{relation symbols} (also called \emph{predicate symbols}), each assigned a fixed arity $n \geq 1$; \item a set $\mathcal{F}$ of \textbf{function symbols}, each assigned a fixed \textbf{arity} $n \g...
def:first-order-language.statement_display:
A \textbf{first-order language} $\mathcal{L}$ consists of three (possibly
empty) sets of non-logical symbols:
\begin{itemize}
  \item a set $\mathcal{R}$ of \textbf{relation symbols} (also called
        \emph{predicate symbols}), each assigned a fixed arity $n \geq 1$;
  \item a set $\mathcal{F}$ of \textbf{function symbols}, each assigned a
        fixed \textbf{arity} $n \geq 0$ (0-ary function symbols are
        \emph{constant symbols});
  \item a set $\mathcal{C}$ of \textbf{constant symbols} (equivalently,
        0-ary function symbols — listed separately for emphasis).
\end{itemize}
Together with the logical symbols common to all first-order languages
(variables, connectives $\lnot, \land, \lor, \to, \leftrightarrow$,
quantifiers $\forall, \exists$, equality $=$, parentheses), the triple
$(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{R}, \mathcal{C})$ determines $\mathcal{L}$.
def:first-order-language.statement_tex:
A \textbf{first-order language} $\mathcal{L}$ consists of three (possibly
empty) sets of non-logical symbols:
\begin{itemize}
  \item a set $\mathcal{R}$ of \textbf{relation symbols} (also called
        \emph{predicate symbols}), each assigned a fixed arity $n \geq 1$;
  \item a set $\mathcal{F}$ of \textbf{function symbols}, each assigned a
        fixed \textbf{arity} $n \geq 0$ (0-ary function symbols are
        \emph{constant symbols});
  \item a set $\mathcal{C}$ of \textbf{constant symbols} (equivalently,
        0-ary function symbols — listed separately for emphasis).
\end{itemize}
Together with the logical symbols common to all first-order languages
(variables, connectives $\lnot, \land, \lor, \to, \leftrightarrow$,
quantifiers $\forall, \exists$, equality $=$, parentheses), the triple
$(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{R}, \mathcal{C})$ determines $\mathcal{L}$.

def:l-structure.title: $\mathcal{L}$-Structure
def:l-structure.statement_preview: Let $\mathcal{L} = (\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{R}, \mathcal{C})$ be a first-order language. An \textbf{$\mathcal{L}$-structure} $\mathcal{M}$ consists of: \begin{enumerate} \item a non-empty set $M$ called the \textbf{domain} (or \textbf{universe}) of $\mathcal{M}$, written $|\mathcal{M}|$; \item for each $n$-ary relation symbol $R \in \mathcal{R}$, a relation $...

def:variable-assignment.title: Variable Assignment
def:variable-assignment.statement_preview: Let $\mathcal{M}$ be an $\mathcal{L}$-structure with domain $M$, and let $\mathrm{Var} = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, \ldots\}$ be a fixed countable set of first-order variables. A \textbf{variable assignment} (or \textbf{environment}) for $\mathcal{M}$ is a function $s : \mathrm{Var} \to M$. For a variable assignment $s$, a variable $v_i$, and an element $a \in M$, wr...
def:variable-assignment.statement_display:
Let $\mathcal{M}$ be an $\mathcal{L}$-structure with domain $M$, and let
$\mathrm{Var} = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, \ldots\}$ be a fixed countable set of
first-order variables.

A \textbf{variable assignment} (or \textbf{environment}) for $\mathcal{M}$
is a function $s : \mathrm{Var} \to M$.

For a variable assignment $s$, a variable $v_i$, and an element $a \in M$,
write $s[v_i \mapsto a]$ for the assignment that agrees with $s$ on all
variables except $v_i$, where it returns $a$:
\[
  s[v_i \mapsto a](v_j) =
  \begin{cases}
    a      & \text{if } j = i, \\
    s(v_j) & \text{if } j \neq i.
  \end{cases}
\]
def:variable-assignment.statement_tex:
Let $\mathcal{M}$ be an $\mathcal{L}$-structure with domain $M$, and let
$\mathrm{Var} = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, \ldots\}$ be a fixed countable set of
first-order variables.

A \textbf{variable assignment} (or \textbf{environment}) for $\mathcal{M}$
is a function $s : \mathrm{Var} \to M$.

For a variable assignment $s$, a variable $v_i$, and an element $a \in M$,
write $s[v_i \mapsto a]$ for the assignment that agrees with $s$ on all
variables except $v_i$, where it returns $a$:
\[
  s[v_i \mapsto a](v_j) =
  \begin{cases}
    a      & \text{if } j = i, \\
    s(v_j) & \text{if } j \neq i.
  \end{cases}
\]

def:term-evaluation.title: Term Evaluation
def:term-evaluation.statement_preview: Let $\mathcal{M}$ be an $\mathcal{L}$-structure and $s : \mathrm{Var} \to M$ a variable assignment. The \textbf{evaluation} $s(t) \in M$ of an $\mathcal{L}$-term $t$ under $s$ is defined inductively: \begin{enumerate} \item If $t = v_i$ is a variable, then $s(t) = s(v_i)$. \item If $t = c$ is a constant symbol, then $s(t) = c^{\mathcal{M}}$. \item If $t = f...
def:term-evaluation.statement_display:
Let $\mathcal{M}$ be an $\mathcal{L}$-structure and $s : \mathrm{Var} \to M$
a variable assignment.
The \textbf{evaluation} $s(t) \in M$ of an $\mathcal{L}$-term $t$ under
$s$ is defined inductively:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item If $t = v_i$ is a variable, then $s(t) = s(v_i)$.
  \item If $t = c$ is a constant symbol, then $s(t) = c^{\mathcal{M}}$.
  \item If $t = f(t_1, \ldots, t_n)$ for an $n$-ary function symbol $f$,
        then $s(t) = f^{\mathcal{M}}(s(t_1), \ldots, s(t_n))$.
\end{enumerate}
def:term-evaluation.statement_tex:
Let $\mathcal{M}$ be an $\mathcal{L}$-structure and $s : \mathrm{Var} \to M$
a variable assignment.
The \textbf{evaluation} $s(t) \in M$ of an $\mathcal{L}$-term $t$ under
$s$ is defined inductively:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item If $t = v_i$ is a variable, then $s(t) = s(v_i)$.
  \item If $t = c$ is a constant symbol, then $s(t) = c^{\mathcal{M}}$.
  \item If $t = f(t_1, \ldots, t_n)$ for an $n$-ary function symbol $f$,
        then $s(t) = f^{\mathcal{M}}(s(t_1), \ldots, s(t_n))$.
\end{enumerate}
